The decision, issued by the relevant French authority and dated March 3, was made under contract provisions allowing a party to suspend performance when circumstances outside its control prevent fulfilment. The document cited the supplier’s insolvency and the resulting inability to guarantee timely delivery of copper cathodes, which are used in electrorefining and downstream production.

According to the document, the declaration applies to the contract covering a specified volume and delivery window and takes effect immediately. It notes that force majeure does not eliminate contractual obligations in all cases, but allows parties to adjust timelines, renegotiate terms, or consider alternative procurement arrangements while the supplier’s restructuring or liquidation process unfolds.

The supplier’s bankruptcy has triggered concerns about continuity of supply and compliance with industrial and procurement schedules tied to the contract. French officials said the declaration was intended to preserve legal certainty for both the state entity and its contracting counterpart while deliveries remain disrupted by the insolvency proceedings.

A spokesperson for the authority said the declaration was issued “to reflect the supplier’s confirmed inability to perform” and to ensure that obligations are managed in accordance with the contract’s force majeure clause. The spokesperson added that the authority is working with stakeholders to determine whether replacement sourcing is feasible and whether claims relating to non-performance can be assessed once insolvency administrators provide additional information.

In cases where a supplier cannot perform, force majeure declarations can affect liability for late delivery and may limit damages claims, depending on the wording of the contract. The procedure also typically requires documentation of the event and timely notification to the other party.

The bankruptcy of the copper cathode supplier follows disruptions seen across metals supply chains in recent years, including production slowdowns and logistics constraints. Copper cathodes are a key raw material in the copper value chain, and market participants often watch contract arrangements for signs of shifting sourcing and pricing mechanisms when deliveries are delayed.

The French authority said it would continue monitoring the insolvency proceedings and contract performance conditions, including any restructuring plan that could restore production and delivery capacity. A decision on potential substitute procurement would depend on available supply and on the legal and contractual options open to the parties, it added.

headline: Poland delays graphite deliveries after inspection order stops shipments from a graphite mine
body: Poland has delayed graphite deliveries after an inspection order halted shipments from a graphite mine, according to officials involved in the supply chain. The pause, effective last week, stopped loading of graphite consignments intended for industrial customers that rely on the material for batteries and advanced metallurgy. The order followed a review of mine operations and transport documentation, with authorities citing compliance and traceability requirements under current EU rules. Polish importers said scheduled deliveries through March and early April have been pushed back by 2 to 6 weeks while paperwork is revalidated and cargo documentation is reissued. One logistics channel that typically processes bulk loads at a Polish port has shifted to interim storage and rerouting, adding additional demurrage costs and extending clearance times by up to 10 business days. Companies warned that any further extension could tighten inventories during the second quarter, when demand usually rises ahead of summer production schedules.

headline: Morocco suspends cobalt refinery intake after workers strike impacts transport to site
body:
Morocco has suspended cobalt refinery intake after a strike by workers disrupted transport to the site, according to industry sources and company statements seen by Reuters.

The stoppage affects the flow of cobalt-bearing feedstock to a refinery in Morocco, where processing typically requires steady deliveries from upstream suppliers. With road and logistics routes operating at reduced capacity during the strike, the refinery could not maintain intake at planned levels, prompting the temporary suspension, the sources said.

Workers began the strike over labor and workplace demands, with pickets disrupting shipment schedules for vehicles carrying ore and intermediate materials to the facility. Companies involved in logistics reported delays that extended beyond the initial work stoppage window, affecting not only deliveries to the refinery but also the timing of outbound movements for processed products.

A company official said the refinery suspended intake to protect equipment and manage inventory levels as deliveries fell short. “We are pausing intake while we stabilize supply and ensure consistent transport operations resume,” the official said, adding that staff were continuing maintenance and compliance activities during the suspension.

The refinery’s suspension is likely to have short-term implications for output, especially if transport constraints persist. Cobalt prices and supply chain dynamics in North Africa are heavily influenced by the reliability of inbound material flows, as processing schedules require consistent feedstock volumes to avoid interruptions in operations and downstream contracting.

Morocco is one of the countries positioned to benefit from global demand for cobalt used in battery supply chains, but domestic processing is closely tied to transportation availability. Industry participants said they are monitoring whether negotiations resolve the strike and restore normal routes for deliveries to the refinery.

Reuters was unable to immediately confirm the duration of the suspension. However, sources indicated that intake could restart once logistics providers report that pickup and delivery schedules have returned to agreed levels and the refinery’s stockpiling buffer is replenished.

headline: Tanzania declares state of emergency at a copper rail corridor after sabotage cuts flows
body: Tanzania has declared a state of emergency along a copper rail corridor after sabotage disrupted rail operations and cut copper concentrate flows, authorities said on 4 March. The measure covers the stretch linking key loading points to export facilities and was issued late on 3 March following incidents that damaged signalling equipment and caused multiple derailments, reducing shipments in the preceding 48 hours. Rail operators reported that trains carrying copper ore and concentrate were halted at several junctions, forcing rerouting to road transport that can add 1–2 days to delivery times and raise costs per tonne. The government said security forces were deployed immediately, including additional patrols and checkpoint coverage, while engineers worked on repairs. Market observers noted that the disruption could tighten near-term availability for copper buyers supplied through the corridor during the current quarter. The emergency status is scheduled for an initial 14-day period, with restrictions on travel and movement along the affected rail segment and around critical infrastructure sites.

headline: Chile limits uranium shipments under emergency controls after transport authority revokes permits
body:
Chile has moved to limit shipments of uranium after its transport authority revoked permits tied to the material’s transport, placing new controls on movement under emergency measures, according to government documents and an official statement.

The Ministry of Transport and Telecommunications said it had suspended relevant permits following a review by the Directorate of Land Transport that found deficiencies in how the authorizations had been obtained. The ministry later issued an emergency resolution requiring additional authorizations and restricting shipments to specific routes and time windows while the permits are reassessed.

The emergency controls apply to uranium transported within Chile for activities linked to the state’s regulatory framework, the documents said. Shipments are now subject to heightened oversight, including verification of consignments, additional documentation requirements at origin and destination facilities, and advance notification to authorities charged with safety and compliance. The measures also tighten storage and handling conditions during transit and mandate enhanced security arrangements.

The transport authority’s decision came after what the ministry described as administrative and procedural irregularities in certain permit applications. The Directorate of Land Transport, which issues approvals for specialized cargoes, said the permits were withdrawn to prevent operations from continuing without updated compliance checks.

In a statement, the Ministry of Energy said it would coordinate with transport regulators and the nuclear oversight body to ensure that legitimate shipments could continue once permits met the required standards. “We are implementing emergency controls to maintain security and regulatory compliance while the permit process is corrected,” the ministry said.

Chile is home to major copper operations and a growing set of regulatory structures for critical minerals and nuclear materials. Uranium shipments in the country are governed by nuclear safety rules and require authorizations from multiple agencies, including those responsible for transport safety and radiation protection.

The emergency resolution does not specify a blanket ban, but it creates a temporary pathway for limited shipments under stricter conditions. Authorities said the restrictions would remain in place until the revoked permits are reissued or replaced under the revised requirements, and until the emergency measures are rescinded.

No information was provided in the documents about the destination of any specific shipments or the volume involved. Officials said affected parties have been notified and that enforcement begins immediately.

headline: Kazakhstan issues detention notices for beryllium deliveries after assay mismatch dispute
body: Kazakhstan has issued detention notices for certain beryllium shipments after a dispute over assay results, according to documents seen on 2026-03-03 and covering deliveries scheduled between 2026-02-10 and 2026-03-01. The notices concern consignments that arrived at customs points with assay measurements that did not match contract specifications, triggering holds on transfer and initiating laboratory re-testing. Industry sources said the differences centered on purity and contamination thresholds used to clear shipments for downstream processing. One batch listed for delivery under a 2026 framework order was detained for up to 10 business days while officials review chain-of-custody records and sampling protocols. Authorities cited compliance with national export and hazardous-material handling rules, including requirements for documented test methods and certified results. The matter follows recent tightening of verification procedures for regulated metals amid concerns about documentation gaps and measurement variability across testing facilities. No criminal allegations were indicated in the notices, but parties involved face potential contract penalties if the revised assays fail to meet agreed tolerances.

headline: Romania suspends zirconium exports for 20 days amid furnace breakdown and inventory review
body:
Romania has suspended zirconium exports for 20 days as producers review inventories and address a furnace breakdown, according to industry and trade officials.

The measure, which covers zirconium shipments from domestic processing facilities, took effect immediately and will run for 20 calendar days, with authorities coordinating the timeline with exporters and logistics providers, sources familiar with the matter said. The decision was linked to an operational disruption at a furnace used in the refining and processing of zirconium-containing materials, reducing output capacity and affecting scheduled deliveries.

Exporters said the suspension is intended to prevent shortages at receiving buyers and to allow plants to complete repair work and quality checks before resuming loading. In parallel, companies are conducting a wider inventory review to reconcile quantities of available material, expected production rates and existing contracts, one official said.

Zirconium is used in a range of industrial applications including nuclear fuel components, chemical processing equipment, and ceramics and coatings. Any interruption in supply can impact delivery schedules for buyers that hold long-term procurement plans and rely on consistent lot specifications, particularly where shipments are tied to furnace casting and downstream production cycles.

The suspension comes at a time when market participants have been watching regional supply dynamics and lead times for specialty industrial inputs. Romanian producers typically ship through European logistics networks, and companies have been in contact with freight forwarders to adjust transport bookings and documentation processes during the stoppage.

A spokesperson for one of the affected trading firms said companies are working to “align production recovery, inventory reconciliation and customer schedules” so that shipment resumes promptly after the 20-day window. The firm added that export documentation for pending orders is being held pending confirmation of available volumes and compliance checks.

Officials said they are monitoring the repair progress and the resulting production capacity. They did not provide details on the furnace failure or the repair timeline beyond the 20-day suspension period, noting that resumption of exports will depend on stabilization of output and completion of the inventory review.

headline: Sweden reports palladium price volatility after flooding forces refinery to cut throughput
body: Sweden reported elevated palladium price volatility on 4 March 2026 after flooding disrupted refining capacity and forced a material throughput cut at a key refinery supplying European autocatalyst and industrial users. Traders cited a shorter supply window beginning in late February, when floodwater affected logistics at the plant and reduced runs for several processing lines over a multiweek period. Market pricing showed wider intraday swings across the week of 24 February to 3 March, with liquidity thinning in near-term contracts as buyers rebalanced inventories. The refinery’s reduced throughput was estimated to lower output by several percentage points relative to normal weekly volumes, tightening expectations for deliveries into April. Spot spreads widened versus forward benchmarks, and dealers said basis premiums increased for deliveries from Scandinavia-linked storage pools. Analysts linked the move to a short-term imbalance in institutional and bank inventories, while longer-term hedging demand rose ahead of March settlement dates.

headline: China halts indium processing line after equipment failure triggers safety shutdown
body:
China has halted operations at an indium processing line after an equipment failure triggered a safety shutdown at the plant, according to industry sources, a move that could temporarily affect feedstock processing for the metal used in semiconductors and display manufacturing.

The incident occurred at a processing facility in China that runs refining and conversion steps for indium, sources familiar with the matter said. The line was stopped to allow technicians to assess the failure and complete safety checks before restarting. The sources did not name the company or the exact location.

One source said the shutdown was triggered automatically by protective systems after the malfunction was detected, preventing further mechanical damage and limiting exposure risks. Another source said the plant would likely require parts replacement and additional inspections, adding uncertainty to the restart timeline.

Indium production in China is concentrated among a limited number of refiners that process the metal, typically recovered as a byproduct from zinc smelting residues and other streams. When a processing line goes offline, operators may redirect feed material to other units if available, or temporarily reduce throughput until the equipment is repaired.

A trading desk said the immediate impact would depend on whether the facility can reroute incoming material to alternate capacity and whether suppliers of intermediate indium compounds have existing buffer stocks. With indium supply already sensitive to disruptions at refining plants, even short operational halts can tighten availability for specific refining stages, it said.

The plant’s operators were in the process of communicating with downstream customers, the sources said. Some buyers may seek alternative processing routes or substitute other materials in the near term, while monitoring whether the disruption extends beyond days.

Indium is used in thin-film coatings and solders, including indium tin oxide for liquid crystal displays and touch panels, as well as in emerging applications such as high-efficiency solar cells. Most commercial demand relies on stable refinery operations, where downtime can ripple through procurement schedules for alloys and compounds.

It was not immediately clear when the line would restart or whether regulators would require further safety review. The sources said the company would only resume processing after confirming that the equipment met operational and safety standards.

headline: Brazil declares force majeure at iron ore port after cyclone damage closes berth access
body: Brazil declared force majeure at its main iron ore port after a cyclone damaged berth access and disrupted shipping schedules, according to port officials and government documents dated March 4. The closure affects entry to at least one loaded-vessel berth used for exporting high-grade ore, with dredging and navigation channels reported to be under inspection following damage to navigation aids and access infrastructure. Authorities said the disruption is expected to last through the end of March, depending on weather windows and repairs, while emergency teams work on restoring safe passage for vessels drawing deeper drafts. Shipping lines have been instructed to reroute crews and adjust berthing plans, and some sailings were delayed beyond March 3 arrivals as pilots await revised approach guidance. The force majeure declaration covers contract obligations tied to vessel loading and departure windows for shipments scheduled from March 4 to March 31. Companies are assessing demurrage and inventory impacts, with market participants tracking potential knock-on effects for spot flows from Brazil into Asia and Europe.

headline: South Korea halts steelmaking feedstock deliveries after strike at coke and coal suppliers
body:
South Korea has halted deliveries of steelmaking feedstock to some producers after a strike disrupted supplies of coke and coal from key suppliers, according to industry sources.

The disruption began after workers at coke and coal supplier sites stopped operations, reducing shipments that supply integrated mills and standalone cokemaking facilities used to produce blast furnace coke. Several major traders and logistics providers told customers that scheduled deliveries would be delayed while contract flows were renegotiated and inventories are assessed at receiving terminals.

Producers affected include companies that rely on imported hard coal and coke to maintain furnace output, the sources said. Some mills have been able to switch to alternative cargoes or draw down short-term stockpiles, but deliveries have been paused for new lots in order to prevent further imbalances across supply contracts, they added.

Government and industry officials are coordinating to manage the impact on ongoing production schedules, while suppliers work to restart operations and rebook freight. Affected shipments are primarily those tied to near-term delivery windows, with longer-dated contracts continuing under revised schedules, according to one logistics executive familiar with the arrangements.

“Since the strike, we have had to pause new coke and coal deliveries to avoid sending material that cannot be discharged or allocated immediately,” the executive said. “We are working with mills and shipping agents to reroute or reschedule cargoes as soon as production at the supplier resumes.”

Coke is a critical input for steelmaking because it provides the carbon required for blast furnace smelting. Hard coal is typically converted into coke at domestic or supplier facilities before being delivered to steelworks. Disruptions at either stage can constrain furnace operations, especially for mills with limited inventory cover.

South Korea remains one of the world’s largest steel producers and relies on a mix of domestic and imported inputs to meet demand. Industry observers said the current pause could tighten availability in the spot and short-term markets for coke and benchmark coking coal, potentially raising costs if the stoppage persists. Traders said they expect shipping schedules and pricing to stabilize once supplier operations restart and new allocations are confirmed.

No estimates were immediately available for the length of the halt or the scale of production losses at individual mills, but sources said companies are monitoring furnace requirements on a day-to-day basis.

headline: India restricts gallium oxide exports for compliance review after alleged transshipment
body: India has restricted exports of gallium oxide under a compliance review process after concerns over alleged transshipment, according to government and trade sources. The measure, effective immediately from 2026-03-05, applies to shipments covered by specific export authorizations and requires exporters to resubmit end-use and end-user documentation before loading. Authorities said the review was triggered by information indicating that some consignments may have been diverted or rerouted through third countries, potentially bypassing previously declared destinations. The restriction does not cancel existing licences outright, but it pauses processing for new requests until verifications are completed, a step expected to take several weeks. Industry participants reported that compliance checks include matching shipping manifests, bills of lading, and buyer registration details against declared end-use statements. Prices for gallium oxide and related gallium products were expected to remain volatile as buyers assess alternate sourcing while Indian agencies conduct the remaining checks.

headline: Nigeria suspends shipment approvals for antimony as customs backlog grows at Lagos port
body:
Nigeria has suspended shipment approvals for antimony, citing delays caused by a growing customs backlog at Lagos port, according to industry sources and government statements.

The suspension, effective immediately, affects new requests for customs clearance and the release of consignments involving antimony, a metal used in flame retardants, alloys and electronics, the sources said. Importers and freight forwarders said they have been told to pause processing until customs operations catch up, although existing shipments already cleared for entry are expected to proceed.

At the centre of the disruption is the congestion at Tin Can and Apapa terminals, where ships have faced longer waiting times for inspection, documentation checks and the issuance of release orders. Forwarders said the backlog has built up across multiple product categories, but antimony shipments have been singled out for additional administrative holds as authorities reassess paperwork and tariff classifications.

“The directive is to stop approvals for antimony shipments until further notice because we have to align the documentation with customs requirements,” a trade official involved in clearance procedures said on condition of anonymity. The official did not provide a timeline for resumption of approvals.

Nigeria is one of Africa’s active importers and processors of specialty metals, which are commonly traded through global suppliers and then dispatched to manufacturers. Importers said the suspension could delay deliveries to buyers expecting feedstock for industrial use, particularly where inventory levels are limited.

Customs authorities have said the backlog reflects a broader challenge in processing large volumes of goods amid staffing constraints and increased scrutiny following recent reforms. Businesses have also reported that biometric verification, physical inspections and document matching have slowed over the past several weeks.

The antimony halt comes as importers elsewhere monitor metal supply chains for signs of tightening. Antimony prices have fluctuated globally on demand trends and policy signals from major producing countries, while shipping costs and lead times have remained sensitive to port congestion.

For now, importers said they are working with clearing agents to compile supporting documents and arrange inspections to avoid further delays. Some said they expect the suspension to be lifted after customs backlogs ease, but others warned that extended holds could push costs higher through demurrage charges and storage fees at the port terminals.

headline: Chile declares force majeure at Escondida copper mine after storm damage
body: Chile’s government declared force majeure at the Escondida copper mine in Antofagasta after storm damage disrupted operations, according to authorities on March 4. The declaration covers obligations tied to production, shipping and service delivery at the world’s largest copper mine run by BHP and its partners, following damage to critical infrastructure from severe weather earlier in the week. Plant activity was affected by interruptions to power supply and access routes used for ore movement and logistics, with repair work expected to continue through at least mid-March. Escondida’s owners said they are assessing structural damage to facilities and the stability of haul roads, while contractors begin replacing damaged electrical components and drainage systems. The force majeure notice provides legal relief from penalties under relevant contracts during the period of disruption. Chilean mining regulators said they will review updated impact reports as conditions change, including any extensions to the force majeure window.

headline: Indonesia halts nickel ore exports for 30 days as permits get reviewed
body:
Indonesia has halted exports of nickel ore for 30 days as the government reviews export permits, a move aimed at ensuring compliance with current processing and trading rules, officials said.

The suspension starts immediately and applies to exports of nickel ore, according to a notice circulated to industry players, with shipments to resume after the review period unless further guidance is issued. The policy is intended to give regulators time to examine licensing documents tied to ore sales and to align export activity with obligations related to downstream processing, the notice said.

Indonesia is the world’s top supplier of nickel, a key input for stainless steel and batteries. Ore exports have repeatedly been curtailed over the past decade as Jakarta sought to build local processing capacity and reduce reliance on raw material exports. The latest halt follows periods in which authorities have tightened enforcement around permit approvals, product specifications and shipping documentation.

Several companies export nickel ore under government-issued permits that are subject to periodic review. Under the current rules, exporters are required to hold valid authorisations and meet conditions set out in export regulations, including requirements around volumes and grades. The suspension, industry sources said, is likely to affect near-term cargo schedules and could shift demand to alternative sources while it is in place.

A senior official at the coordinating ministry for economic affairs said the 30-day pause is part of “a standard administrative review” of permits and export eligibility. “We are reviewing compliance and ensuring the permits are consistent with the applicable regulations,” the official said, requesting anonymity because they were not authorised to speak publicly.

Indonesia’s ban on ore exports was first introduced in stages in 2020 and 2021 and has since evolved through rolling changes to exemptions and licensing. Authorities have indicated previously that they would continue to prioritise domestic processing capacity, which includes ferronickel and nickel matte production used in battery supply chains.

Market participants have been closely watching Indonesian policy signals because any disruption to ore flows can influence global nickel prices and the availability of feedstock for processors outside Indonesia. Traders and importers typically seek advance clarity on Indonesian export authorisations to manage inventory and contract timing.

During the suspension, exporters are expected to await the outcome of the permit review before resuming shipments. Further updates are expected as the review period progresses.

headline: Democratic Republic of Congo suspends cobalt haulage contracts after road attacks
body: The Democratic Republic of Congo has suspended cobalt haulage contracts following a spate of road attacks in the Katanga region, according to industry and transport sources. The suspension, announced for operators moving ore and concentrates to processing sites, covers haulage starting immediately and runs while authorities conduct “security verification” along affected corridors. The government action follows attacks recorded over the past 3–4 weeks, including incidents reported between mid-February and late February on routes linking major mining areas with smelters and export staging points. In response, some carriers halted convoys and rerouted vehicles to longer detours, increasing travel times by 20% to 35% in early weeks of disruption. Buyers and refiners said spot deliveries could tighten in the coming 2–6 weeks as contracted liftings are delayed. The suspension affects contracts tied to multiple mine cooperatives and freight providers, with revised schedules expected after security clearance and renewed convoy protocols are issued. Market participants said the move may influence pricing benchmarks for cobalt feedstock, particularly for volumes earmarked for March deliveries.

headline: Russia imposes temporary export ban on refined nickel products starting Apr 15
body:
Russia will impose a temporary export ban on refined nickel products starting April 15, the country’s industry regulator said, in a move aimed at securing supplies for domestic use.

The restriction covers refined nickel and certain nickel-containing products, according to a notice posted by the regulator on Thursday. The ban is set to run for a limited period, though the duration was not specified in the initial announcement. The measure applies to shipments leaving Russia and is intended to address current balance-of-supply conditions in the domestic nickel market.

The regulator said the decision follows an assessment of production, inventory and demand trends in Russia’s nickel sector. Under the rules, shipments that fall within the defined categories will be prohibited unless they receive specific authorisation. The agency did not immediately provide details on the approval process or the expected volume of exports affected.

Russian nickel output is concentrated among major producers in the country’s Arctic and Siberian regions, with refined product levels closely watched by traders because of Russia’s role in global supply chains. International contracts for refined nickel often use Russia-linked sources, and the April timing could influence physical market availability ahead of the summer demand period.

Market participants will likely assess the impact on premiums and freight costs, with traders typically differentiating between refined nickel grades and product forms. The ban also comes as Russia continues to operate under a web of Western sanctions and related shipping and payments restrictions, which have already altered some trade flows for metals.

A representative for a domestic industry group said the measure is consistent with efforts to manage internal availability of refined nickel. “The priority is to maintain stable supply for Russian consumers while the regulator reviews market conditions,” the representative said, without naming a specific company.

The regulator indicated that updates on the scope of covered products and any temporary exemptions would be published through official channels. Traders said they will look for additional guidance on product codes and customs classifications to determine which cargoes are subject to the prohibition.

The timing of the ban—set for mid-April—leaves a narrow window for exporters to adjust logistics and contract terms, including shipment schedules already in transit or under negotiation. Further details from authorities are expected in the coming days.

headline: Zambia audits copper concentrate shipments as smelters face power curtailments
body: Zambia has begun audits of copper concentrate shipments amid worsening power curtailments at smelters, according to government and industry sources. The inspections, launched in March 2026 and focused on consignments loading from key mines toward domestic processing facilities, are intended to verify assay records, weight certificates, and treatment and refining charge calculations. Smelters have been operating under reduced grid availability as utilities impose rolling load limits, which can delay processing and shift inventory accounting timelines. Auditors are checking whether concentrates delivered during February and early March met agreed moisture and impurity specifications, including limits for silica and other penal elements. Several companies have reported higher concentrate re-handling and storage costs after power outages forced intermittent shutdowns of leaching and converting circuits. The audit process is expected to take 2–4 weeks per route before results are reconciled with customs declarations and export documentation. If discrepancies are found, regulators said adjustments could be made to settlement invoices and future shipment acceptance criteria.

headline: Peru announces strike affecting copper cathode loading at Callao port
body:
Peru has announced a strike that is disrupting copper cathode loading at the country’s main port of Callao, according to port operators and industry sources.

The work stoppage, scheduled to begin on Thursday and expected to affect shipping operations into the weekend, targets activities related to cargo handling at warehouses and loading areas serving mineral shipments, the sources said. Several vessels carrying copper cathodes were waiting to load or reduce loading rates as stevedores and transport workers complied with the strike call.

Callao, on Peru’s central coast near Lima, handles most of the country’s exports of refined copper produced by companies including Southern Copper and others with refineries and processing facilities on the coast. Copper cathodes are typically consolidated near the port before loading into bulk carriers or containerized shipments, with logistics chains relying on trucking, weighbridge operations, and crane work to meet ship schedules.

A representative for a port-related industry group said the disruption is concentrated on “the final steps of loading cathodes,” which has slowed vessel turnarounds. “We are working to reroute resources where possible, but the strike affects manpower at the loading and handling stages,” the representative said.

Peru is one of the world’s major copper producers and a key exporter of refined products. Any sustained interruption to loading can force shipping delays and increase demurrage costs, particularly where cargo must meet contractual loading windows tied to sales agreements and refinery output.

Local authorities and private operators said they are monitoring compliance and attempting to maintain essential services. Emergency arrangements for safety-critical operations and limited gate access were discussed, but sources said normal throughput has not resumed and that additional labor actions could be introduced depending on negotiations.

Market participants have previously highlighted Peru’s exposure to labor disruptions because copper output is exported largely on tight timelines. In Callao, buyers often maintain inventories and schedule tonnage against production forecasts, so short disruptions can be absorbed while longer delays may require renegotiation of delivery dates.

Officials did not immediately provide details on the strike’s demands or expected duration. Further updates were expected after scheduled meetings between labor representatives and government or employer groups later in the week.

headline: South Africa’s major platinum refinery declares force majeure after transformer failure
body: A major platinum refinery in South Africa declared force majeure after a transformer failure disrupted operations at its processing facilities, the company said on Friday, citing immediate impacts on power availability and planned output schedules. The disruption began late in the previous week and triggered a review of restart timelines for smelting and refining lines, which had been operating under normal throughput targets ahead of the March 2026 period. The firm said it is working with engineering contractors to replace or repair the failed transformer and to restore stable power supply before resuming full production. Inventory and customer delivery schedules are expected to be affected over the coming days as the site prioritises partial runs where power constraints allow. The refinery, one of the country’s key producers feeding global platinum demand, did not provide a specific duration for the force majeure but indicated operational updates will follow as technical assessments are completed. Industry participants said similar power-related outages in South Africa can translate into multi-day production losses and increased variability in spot supply.

headline: Canada pauses palladium shipments amid rail derailment and safety investigations
body:
Canada has paused palladium shipments after a rail derailment prompted safety inspections and a review of transport procedures, officials said on Thursday, adding uncertainty to deliveries of the precious metal used in automotive and industrial catalysts.

The pause affects exports moving by rail from Canada to U.S. customers and other destinations, according to a notice circulated to shippers and confirmed by industry participants who track freight flows. The government has not said how long the suspension will last, but it is tied to investigations following the derailment, which authorities said triggered inspections of rail equipment and the handling of hazardous materials.

A spokesperson for Transport Canada said the department and the Transportation Safety Board are examining the circumstances surrounding the incident and that related checks have been expanded to ensure compliance with federal rail safety requirements. “We are coordinating with partners to determine the cause and to confirm that any necessary corrective actions are in place before shipments resume,” the spokesperson said.

Palladium is often shipped in bulk along with other commodities under regulated conditions. Industry sources said the derailment did not involve a breach of containment tied specifically to palladium, but that regulators commonly require additional safety measures after derailments, including verification of loading practices, railcar integrity and route approvals. Several firms also conducted internal reviews to determine whether additional inspections were needed before moving product.

The pause comes at a time when the palladium market has been focused on supply continuity and freight reliability, particularly for North American deliveries. Traders said that even short disruptions can affect near-term contracts because lead times for rail shipments are typically planned around fixed schedules. However, market participants noted that the impact could be limited if inventory coverage at receiving facilities is sufficient.

Canada is a key source of palladium, and most movement to downstream users depends on a combination of rail and port logistics. The government said it will provide further updates as findings from the derailment investigation become available and as rail operators complete the required assessments. Any changes to the shipping timetable are expected to be communicated to affected customers once regulators conclude their review.

headline: Rwanda cancels graphite supply contracts after kiln compliance issues
body: Rwanda has canceled graphite supply contracts after compliance problems at a kiln tied to the shipments, according to industry contacts familiar with the matter. The government instructed counterparties to unwind agreements covering material deliveries scheduled for late March and April, according to the contacts, following a review of kiln operating records and inspection results submitted under contract terms. The decision affects volumes intended for battery and industrial processing uses, though exact tonnage was not immediately confirmed. The compliance issues were flagged during documentation checks that started in early February, with final findings delivered in mid-January, the contacts said. Vendors were given a short window to provide alternative production evidence or replacement product sourced from another kiln site, with several suppliers reportedly unable to meet the amended requirements. No replacement framework was announced as of 5 March 2026, and some deliveries were expected to slip into the second quarter.

headline: Mozambique imposes licensing delays for tin exports following customs overhaul
body:
Mozambique has introduced licensing timelines for tin exports that officials said were designed to manage compliance after a customs system overhaul, according to industry documents and government announcements reviewed by traders.

The changes require exporters to obtain export licences through a new process that, in practice, lengthens the time taken to clear shipments pending document checks and system verification. Several operators said they have experienced delays because licences now follow approval steps tied to the upgraded customs platform, rather than being processed under previous procedures.

Mozambique has been implementing the overhaul of its customs administration to improve risk screening, strengthen revenue collection and standardise clearance procedures across ports and border posts. The government has said the transition will involve additional verification at the time of application and at export declaration stages, with the licensing mechanism intended to align trade documentation with the customs database.

Under the updated framework, tin consignments must be matched against declared origin details, tax and duty records and export entitlement documentation before the licence is issued. Exporters said the process is intended to prevent discrepancies between mining records, customs declarations and shipping documents, which can trigger holds and requests for supplementary evidence.

Traders said the practical effect has been longer lead times for shipments ahead of loading schedules, especially where documentation is submitted close to sailing dates. One exporter said the updated licensing workflow “adds steps that we have to follow in sequence, so late corrections can push the licence out even if the cargo is already prepared.”

Customs authorities did not provide a specific timetable for how long each stage should take, according to the documents. Instead, officials indicated that processing durations depend on the volume of applications and the results of compliance checks conducted through the new system.

The licensing delays are occurring as Mozambique continues to develop its mining and export activity, including tin production, which relies on shipments through regional logistics routes. Industry sources said they are adjusting shipping plans and internal document control to reduce the risk of further holds, including earlier submissions for export licences and tighter reconciliation of mine production records with customs filings.

Exporters, meanwhile, are seeking clarity on whether processing backlogs will be addressed through additional staffing or system tuning as the customs overhaul stabilises.

headline: Vietnam triggers emergency import inspections for tungsten concentrate shipments
body: Vietnam’s Ministry of Industry and Trade has ordered emergency import inspections for tungsten concentrate shipments entering the country, effective immediately from 2026-03-05, according to industry notices circulated the same day. The measure applies to consignments under multiple HS classifications used for tungsten concentrates and related intermediate products, requiring enhanced documentation checks at border points and expanded sampling for impurities and radionuclide indicators. Importers are required to submit updated certificates of analysis dated no earlier than 30 days before shipment and to provide origin declarations for each lot, with results to be retained for at least 12 months. Officials said the review is aimed at preventing non-compliant material from entering downstream processing streams, including tungsten carbide and metal powder production used in tooling and electronics supply chains. Trade participants reported potential clearance delays of 3–10 business days, depending on laboratory turnaround times. The inspection drive is currently set to run for an initial 30-day period, after which authorities will decide whether to extend or lift the restrictions based on compliance findings and laboratory audit outcomes.

headline: China restricts vanadium oxide quotas for 60 days under new allocation rules
body:
China has imposed temporary restrictions on vanadium oxide quotas for 60 days, according to a notice issued under revised allocation rules aimed at tightening control of supply for the metal, which is widely used to strengthen steel.

The Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) said the measures apply to enterprises covered by the country’s vanadium oxide management system and will run for 60 days from the effective date listed in the circular. The order sets updated quota allocations for producers and distributors, with the intent of managing inventory levels and smoothing downstream demand.

Under the new framework, companies are required to submit compliance information and allocation applications according to updated procedures. The ministry said allocations will be determined using factors including prior production and sales records, current inventories and adherence to earlier quota requirements. Entities that fail to meet the reporting or allocation terms may face reductions or suspension of their quotas.

The notice also describes tighter oversight of movements of vanadium oxide and related intermediate materials, requiring entities to complete transactions through designated reporting channels. Authorities said the approach is intended to prevent irregular supply flows and reduce the risk of supply exceeding planned quotas during the temporary window.

In addition, the MIIT urged firms to ensure stable deliveries to downstream users, particularly steelmakers and chemical firms that rely on vanadium compounds for industrial processing. The ministry said the temporary restriction is part of broader efforts to improve market transparency and strengthen the regulatory role of quotas in the sector.

Industry participants have previously said that vanadium supply in China can be sensitive to shifts in production rates at upstream vanadium-bearing mines and to changes in steel output. Vanadium oxide is an essential input for producing vanadium-based additives used in high-strength and low-alloy steels, which are in turn linked to construction, infrastructure and manufacturing cycles.

The 60-day restriction comes as Chinese regulators continue to revise how certain industrial commodities are allocated and monitored. Reuters was not able to immediately verify the specific quota levels for each company from the public version of the circular, but the MIIT said the rules take effect immediately following the notice’s stated start date and are designed to align short-term supply with planned demand.

headline: Kazakhstan halts gallium sales to noncompliant buyers after audit findings
body: Kazakhstan has stopped gallium sales to buyers deemed noncompliant following an audit of export records, according to officials and trade data reviewed on March 5. The suspension applies to counterparties that failed compliance checks tied to end-use documentation and shipment documentation, with affected transactions paused effective this week. The halt follows an internal review that concluded several consignments did not meet required paperwork standards, including discrepancies in destination details and importer registration information. Kazakhstan remains one of the world’s main sources of gallium, a metal used in semiconductors and power electronics, with global demand closely watched amid tighter export controls from multiple jurisdictions. Affected customers include firms in Europe and Asia that have taken delivery in prior quarters, the data show. The government said compliant buyers will continue to receive allocations under existing licenses while audits are being reconciled, and it has requested additional verification for outstanding orders. The measure is expected to reduce near-term deliveries, potentially tightening supply for processors purchasing in March after a February run of shipments.

headline: South Korea tightens indium import permits amid warehouse fire and stock risk
body:
South Korea has tightened import permit requirements for indium, citing concerns over supply stability after a warehouse fire raised the risk of stock disruption, industry officials said.

The Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy issued updated rules on indium imports that broaden the documentation needed to obtain permits and require importers to demonstrate inventory safety measures. The changes apply to shipments of refined indium and intermediate indium products, according to ministry documents reviewed by traders.

Indium is used mainly in touchscreens, flat-panel displays, solar power components and related electronics. South Korea relies on imports for a large share of its indium supply, with most material sourced from overseas smelters and refineries. Tightening permit controls is intended to prevent sudden shortages and manage redistribution of available stocks, officials said.

The ministry’s decision follows a warehouse fire earlier this year that damaged stored goods and affected the availability of certain indium-bearing materials, traders said. While the full impact of the fire on supply has not been quantified publicly, several market participants said insurers, inventory managers and logistics providers are still assessing losses and whether replacement stock will be delivered on schedule.

Under the new requirements, applicants must submit proof of storage capacity, fire and safety compliance, and shipment tracking plans. Importers are also required to provide monthly reporting on held inventories and expected drawdowns, the documents showed. The measures effectively increase administrative oversight during the period after the incident, when remaining stock is being verified and replacement supplies are being sourced.

“Given the supply-chain uncertainty created by the fire and the need to ensure continuity for downstream users, the government has adjusted permit procedures to strengthen inventory visibility,” a ministry official said, speaking on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to brief media.

Traders said the permit tightening could affect near-term purchase timing, particularly for firms that depend on spot deliveries rather than long-term contracts. Several suppliers said they expect buyers to shift toward contracts with clearer delivery schedules and documented warehousing arrangements.

Market participants also said the move may influence prices by limiting incremental inflows until compliance checks are completed. Indium markets are relatively thin compared with base metals, and import availability can move quickly when upstream disruptions or storage problems occur.

headline: Chile and Peru dispute molybdenum concentrate grades under new sampling protocol
body: Chile and Peru have renewed a dispute over molybdenum concentrate grade determinations after adopting a new sampling protocol, according to industry documents circulated this week. The disagreement centers on assay results for shipments delivered between January and February 2026, when buyers and sellers began applying the revised sampling rules for lot formation and subsample reduction. Chilean exporters said the protocol raises measured Mo content and improves representativeness for consignments loaded from coastal terminals, while Peruvian counterpart refiners argued the same approach systematically inflates recoverable grades by altering sampling depth and composite averaging. The parties have been working through commercial claims tied to contract penalties assessed on deviation bands of 0.05% to 0.10% Mo, with escalations dated 19 February and 3 March. A joint review panel is scheduled for 12 March, with third-party laboratory reconciliation planned for early April. Market participants said the outcome could affect pricing formulas for spring loadings, particularly where buyers reference benchmark concentrates with specified moisture and impurity caps.

headline: Niger announces temporary export suspension of uranium concentrates for compliance checks
body:
Niger has announced a temporary suspension of exports of uranium concentrates to allow compliance checks, the government said on Friday.

The measure, announced by the Ministry of Mines and Geology, applies to shipments of uranium concentrates and is intended to ensure they meet regulatory and contractual requirements before they are cleared for export. The ministry said the suspension is limited in duration and is part of a broader review of procedures linked to the country’s uranium sales.

In a statement, the ministry said the checks would focus on documentation and traceability, including verification of the origin of the material, export permits, and adherence to the terms under which concentrates are sold to overseas buyers. It did not specify how long the suspension would last, but said that exports would resume once the review is completed and relevant paperwork is verified.

The statement did not name the companies affected or indicate whether the action would interrupt deliveries already in transit. Niger, which is one of the world’s largest producers of uranium, relies heavily on export revenue from the nuclear fuel supply chain, with shipments typically routed through a limited number of processing and trading channels.

Industry observers have previously noted that uranium exporting countries often conduct periodic audits and compliance reviews to address issues such as documentation discrepancies, sanctions-related due diligence, and changes to import or transport requirements. Niger’s decision comes amid continued global scrutiny of supply chains used for nuclear materials, as governments and regulators seek to strengthen monitoring of exports.

The ministry said it would work with relevant agencies and partners to “ensure continuity” while the checks are carried out, and urged importers and logistics providers to continue coordinating with Niger’s authorities on documentation and timelines. It also said the government would publish further guidance if any updates were required.

Niger’s uranium sector has been at the center of geopolitical and economic concerns in recent years, including negotiations over the terms of mining arrangements with foreign stakeholders. The government has also pursued measures to increase oversight and improve revenue management in the sector.

The suspension of uranium concentrate exports for compliance checks is expected to affect near-term shipment schedules, depending on how quickly authorities complete the review and issue clearances for resumed shipments.

headline: Namibia declares state of emergency for cadmium recovery plants after flood damage
body: Namibia declared a state of emergency on Friday after flood damage disrupted cadmium recovery operations at multiple plants, the government said, citing risks to workers, environmental controls and the continuity of metal supply. The declaration follows heavy rainfall and river overflows earlier this week that inundated processing sites, damaged storage areas and compromised power and water-handling systems used for cadmium recovery. Authorities said inspections conducted between 2 and 5 March found breached containment units, overloaded effluent lines and blocked drainage channels, prompting immediate shutdowns at affected facilities. The emergency order authorizes emergency procurement, accelerated repairs and temporary relocation of staff while regulators assess contamination levels in nearby soil and water. Namibia’s cadmium output relies on recovery from industrial streams, and officials warned that interruptions could affect deliveries scheduled for the second quarter of 2026. The government did not specify the duration of the emergency, but said monitoring teams will remain on-site through at least 15 March and publish interim findings on remediation progress.

headline: Japan orders selenium supplier to cut output after EPA nonconformance notices
body:
Japan has ordered a selenium supplier to reduce production after U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) nonconformance notices raised concerns about environmental compliance, according to people familiar with the matter.

The order, issued by a Japanese regulatory authority to the unnamed supplier, requires the company to cut selenium output while it addresses issues cited in EPA communications, the people said. The notifications were sent after inspections and related documentation reviews found that parts of the firm’s waste handling and emission controls did not meet the requirements specified under environmental oversight procedures.

Selenium is used in areas including glassmaking, photovoltaic and electronics manufacturing, and supply tightness can affect downstream production schedules. The supplier’s cutbacks are expected to reduce monthly volumes available to Japanese buyers, the people said, though the extent of the reduction depends on how quickly corrective actions are implemented.

A spokesperson for the supplier said the company was working with regulators to resolve the findings and ensure compliance. “We are taking steps to address the nonconformance items raised by the EPA notices and will adjust operations to meet regulatory expectations while the review is ongoing,” the spokesperson said.

The Japanese order comes as global regulators and buyers have increased scrutiny of material-processing supply chains, particularly for elements that can involve hazardous byproducts depending on the purification route. In the case at hand, the EPA’s notices referenced specific gaps in conformance related to how pollutants were monitored and how certain residues were managed, the people said.

Regulatory action in Japan typically follows formal review of inspection reports and company remediation plans. The supplier must submit updated documentation and demonstrate improvements before production can return to prior levels, the people added. It was not immediately clear when the company would be allowed to resume full output.

Industry sources said other selenium producers may have the capacity to fill some demand, but that additional supplies may take time to ramp up and could come at higher costs. Japanese purchasers are expected to review contracts and inventory positions as the reduction period begins, the sources said.

headline: Bolivia suspends bismuth exports pending refinery maintenance and quality testing
body: Bolivia has suspended bismuth exports starting 2026-03-06, citing refinery maintenance and additional quality testing ahead of renewed shipments, according to industry sources familiar with the decision. The halt affects shipments scheduled from Bolivia’s main refining facilities in the coming weeks, with traders expected to reassess purchase orders tied to April and May delivery windows. The suspension is intended to prevent material that does not meet current specifications for bismuth purity and contaminant limits from moving to downstream smelters and chemical users. Maintenance work is expected to run through mid-March, while quality audits and batch-by-batch verification are planned to extend the export pause until test results are approved by relevant oversight teams. Several market participants said spot offers were temporarily withdrawn as sellers wait for cleared documentation, while buyers sought confirmation of revised release dates and new assay reports. The measure comes as global bismuth supply remains sensitive to refining uptime, with short disruptions in producer operations typically tightening available material and shifting premiums.

headline: China bans antimony shipments to three regions after labeling violations
body:
China has banned shipments of antimony to three regions after authorities found labeling violations, according to a notice issued this week by a Chinese customs regulator.

The measure applies to antimony and antimony-bearing products shipped to three areas, the notice said, without detailing the specific product grades covered. The restrictions are tied to how consignments were labeled and declared at entry, customs authorities said, adding that the decision was made to ensure compliance with import and product identification requirements.

Under the ban, affected shipments are not allowed to enter the designated regions until the labeling issues are corrected. Customs said it will intensify checks on future consignments of antimony and will verify the accuracy of labels and documentation including product names, origin declarations and other required information.

The notice did not specify the volume of shipments that will be blocked or how many companies are involved. It also did not outline how long the ban will remain in place, indicating instead that clearance will depend on subsequent compliance by exporters and shippers.

Antimony is used in flame retardants, lead-acid batteries, alloys and other industrial applications. Because it is traded internationally, changes to import clearance rules can affect short-term availability and pricing, particularly where industrial consumers rely on steady deliveries from overseas sources.

China is a major buyer of antimony worldwide, and its customs processes are closely watched by traders and manufacturers. In its statement, the regulator said the labeling checks are intended to prevent improper product descriptions and to support traceability across supply chains.

The ban follows a broader pattern of enforcement actions in China targeting import compliance, including checks on documentation and product conformity requirements in raw materials and industrial inputs. Authorities said affected consignments will be handled in accordance with relevant customs rules and that violators face additional administrative consequences beyond entry denial where applicable.

The notice was published through official channels and is expected to prompt exporters to review labeling practices for shipments destined for the three regions named in the order.

headline: Germany freezes germanium deliveries from a key supplier after sanctions screening
body: Germany has halted deliveries of germanium from a key supplier after internal sanctions screening flagged the shipments for potential export-control and financial risks, according to people familiar with the matter. The freeze began in the final week of February 2026 and affects batches scheduled for industrial buyers that use germanium for fiber-optic optics, infrared imaging and semiconductor processes, the sources said. The supplier, based outside the EU, had supplied German processors under existing contracts and shipping lanes, but compliance checks reportedly expanded after updated EU and allied sanctions lists were circulated in mid-January. Some consignments have been held at German customs facilities while banks and freight forwarders re-validate payment routing, end-use documentation and the identity of receiving entities. Company procurement teams are now seeking alternative logistics, including transshipment routes that would require renewed end-user confirmations, the sources said. Market participants said the interruption could tighten spot availability for the next 4 to 8 weeks, depending on whether regulators clear replacement paperwork.

headline: India halts beryllium exports after furnace outage and worker-safety violations
body:
India has temporarily halted exports of beryllium after a furnace outage at a major processing plant and repeated worker-safety violations, according to officials and documents reviewed by industry sources.

The suspension applies to shipments of processed beryllium products from the site while an internal review and corrective actions are completed, the sources said. The stoppage follows an incident in which a furnace used in high-temperature refining shut down unexpectedly, disrupting production runs and triggering an investigation into operating procedures.

Regulators and company auditors have also identified deficiencies related to worker protections, including controls for airborne particulates and exposure monitoring, the sources said. Beryllium is classified as a hazardous material because inhalation of dust and fumes can cause serious chronic lung disease. Compliance typically requires stringent engineering controls, respiratory protection programs and routine workplace health checks.

An official at the relevant state-level pollution and labour authorities said the suspension was issued to ensure compliance before exports resume. “We will allow shipments only after the plant demonstrates that safety systems and monitoring are functioning as required and production can restart without breaching exposure limits,” the official said.

The stoppage is likely to affect buyers seeking material for aerospace components, electronic connectors and specialty alloys, according to trade contacts. India has been a smaller supplier in the global beryllium supply chain compared with firms in other countries, but shipments are used by manufacturers to qualify alloys and maintain inventories ahead of contract cycles.

Industry sources said the company is working with safety consultants and equipment suppliers to bring the furnace back to specifications and validate emergency shutoff and ventilation systems. Corrective measures include recalibrating sensors, improving maintenance records and conducting additional workplace training, they said.

The company has not publicly detailed the inspection findings, but internal communications reviewed by sources indicate that export documentation and shipment schedules have been paused pending sign-off from relevant agencies. The suspension could last several weeks, depending on the speed of remediation and the outcome of follow-up audits, sources said.

headline: France reports supply risk as zirconium shipments delayed by customs congestion
body: France is reporting tightening supply risk for zirconium after multiple shipments were held at customs amid congestion in late February and into March 2026, according to trade contacts. Several consignments of zirconium metal and zirconium compounds scheduled for delivery in early March have experienced delays ranging from 7 to 20 days, disrupting planned inventories for chemical and nuclear-linked materials uses. The disruptions follow a broader backlog affecting cross-border freight routes, with clearance times reportedly rising above typical levels by several working days at key ports and border checks. Importers said paperwork volumes and inspection backlogs have contributed, including batch verification and origin documentation requirements. One logistics update cited rerouting and partial releases of loads as the main mitigation steps, but those measures add transit costs and complicate quality certification. Industry participants said the shortfall risk is most acute for processors dependent on near-term deliveries rather than safety-stock holdings, with procurement discussions expected to intensify through mid-March 2026.

headline: Ukraine declares force majeure at iron ore concentrate terminals after infrastructure hits
body:
Ukraine has declared force majeure at iron ore concentrate terminals after infrastructure damage disrupted handling and shipping operations, according to officials and terminal operators.

The declaration covers periods in which the affected infrastructure could not reliably receive, store or load concentrate, the officials said, citing repairs and safety measures following strikes and related disruptions. Several port and inland facilities used to move iron ore to domestic processing plants and export markets reported delays ranging from short-term interruptions to sustained reductions in throughput.

The move is designed to address contractual obligations with customers and shipping counterparties during the disruption, Reuters reported earlier this week. Force majeure clauses are commonly invoked in the wake of events outside the control of parties, including armed conflict, infrastructure damage and major transport disruptions, to limit liability for delayed or missed delivery targets.

In a statement circulated to counterparties, terminal representatives said cargo handling was affected by damage to loading equipment, power supply interruptions and restricted access routes. Some terminals also said vessels faced longer waiting times because of revised procedures for berth scheduling and crane operations, while additional inspections were required for equipment used to load concentrate.

The disruptions come as Ukraine continues to manage the logistical strain of operating ports and industrial assets in wartime conditions. Iron ore concentrates are among the country’s key bulk export materials, and shipping schedules depend heavily on continuous loading capacity at terminals and reliable inland transport links to those facilities.

A spokesperson for one terminal operator said the declaration reflects the “objective inability” to perform normal services due to infrastructure impacts and ongoing repair work. “We are working to restore operations and will update customers as conditions change,” the spokesperson said.

The declaration does not indicate a total shutdown, officials said, but instead establishes a formal basis to manage specific contracts and delivery timelines tied to concentrate shipments. Market participants said the practical effect is likely to be tighter supply availability for buyers and increased volatility in near-term shipping expectations, depending on how quickly terminals regain full capacity.

Ukrainian authorities did not provide a detailed list of which individual terminals were covered under the force majeure declaration or the precise duration for each site. However, officials said the affected arrangements would remain in force until infrastructure repairs and safety clearances allow operations to return to pre-disruption levels.

headline: Brazil suspends steel scrap auctions after transport strike disrupts deliveries
body: Brazil suspended steel scrap auctions on March 6 after a nationwide transport strike disrupted deliveries to processing plants and trading hubs, according to industry sources. The decision pauses spot and term tenders scheduled for the week, affecting scrap flows from ports and inland collection points that typically feed blast furnaces and electric-arc furnaces. Operators cited missed pickup windows ranging from 24 to 72 hours, forcing some buyers to defer payments and extend inventory drawdowns ahead of next auction dates. Several logistics contractors reported reduced truck availability and longer turnaround times at major terminals, including areas used for imported and domestic heavy scrap. Auction suspension follows similar disruptions to related metal procurement in January and February, when freight constraints tightened and premiums widened. The government and industry negotiators are coordinating rescheduling and may require revised delivery documentation and amended cutoff times for bids. Traders said any restart would depend on strike resolution and confirmation of insured haulage routes by March 9, with pricing likely to reset once cargoes clear.

headline: Australia expands offtake terms for lithium miners as spodumene shipments face weather delays
body:
Australia has expanded offtake terms for lithium miners after delays to spodumene shipments tied to adverse weather threatened deliveries to domestic and overseas buyers, according to companies and shipping documents.

The changes, agreed through industry procurement frameworks used by converters and chemical producers, extend delivery windows and allow greater flexibility on inspection and sampling of spodumene concentrates. They also provide for additional rescheduling options where port closures or rough seas interrupt vessel loading, traders said.

Several miners have been grappling with uneven shipment schedules in recent weeks, with concentrated material sitting longer in stockpiles ahead of export. Weather disruptions have slowed vessel turnaround times at export terminals and delayed departures, affecting loadings to key routes supplying China and other Asian processing hubs, where spodumene is converted into lithium hydroxide and carbonate.

Under the revised terms, buyers can accept product delivered within an expanded window after the original shipment date, and the parties can recalibrate logistics milestones without triggering penalties tied to late delivery. The update also clarifies how quality parameters are applied when product is sampled on different dates due to handling changes during delays.

A spokesperson for one of the converter counterparties said the adjustment was designed to protect supply continuity. “Given the delays to loading caused by weather conditions, the expanded terms help both sides manage timing and inspection in a way that avoids unnecessary disputes,” the spokesperson said.

The adjustments come as lithium markets remain sensitive to delivery timing and concentrate quality, factors that affect conversion yields and customer specifications. Spodumene contracts often set thresholds for moisture, impurities and concentrate grade, with price formulas linked to chemical conversion benchmarks and prevailing spot references.

Industry officials said the expanded terms are likely to be temporary, tied to the current wave of disruptions, and will be reviewed as shipping conditions normalize. Some producers are preparing additional cargoes for earlier loading where schedules allow, while others are adjusting vessel bookings and storage plans to reduce the risk of further slippage.

The updates underscore the logistical challenges faced by Australia’s expanding lithium export pipeline, even as production capacity grows. For buyers, the revised offtake terms aim to maintain feedstock access for downstream processing during periods when weather disrupts the flow of concentrate from mines to ports.

headline: Canada issues force majeure on nickel hydroxide exports after refinery fire
body: Canada’s federal government has issued a force majeure notice affecting nickel hydroxide exports after a refinery fire disrupted production, according to notices circulated to shippers and downstream buyers on 6 March 2026. The measure was triggered by damage to processing capacity that produces nickel hydroxide, a key intermediate for battery and refining feedstock, and is expected to limit loadings beginning in the first half of March. Export permits tied to affected lots will be processed as force majeure while operators assess restart schedules, with authorities citing force majeure periods that will run in weekly increments. Several contracts specifying delivery dates in March and early April were flagged for potential shipment deferrals, amid uncertainty over whether alternative volumes from other Canadian units can be rerouted. Market participants said the disruption could tighten supply for customers needing hydroxide conversion into nickel salts and cathode-active materials, particularly where inventory coverage is measured in weeks rather than months. The government did not publish a definitive duration, but indicated assessments would be updated after safety inspections and equipment recovery milestones.

headline: DRC suspends cobalt shipments as artisanal mining crackdown tightens access
body:
The Democratic Republic of Congo has suspended some cobalt shipments as authorities tighten a crackdown on artisanal mining, according to officials and traders familiar with the matter.

The suspension affects consignments moving through parts of the central and southern supply chain used to consolidate cobalt ore and intermediate products before export, the people said. Several buyers were told to hold loading while the government reviews compliance checks for sites and trading intermediaries, they added. A mining official said the move is intended to prevent illicit material from entering formal channels as enforcement expands.

The DRC has for months been increasing oversight of the mining sector, including tighter rules on artisanal production, traceability and the issuance of permits. In recent weeks, mobile teams have conducted raids and document checks around mining areas and along transport routes, traders said, adding that checkpoints have been expanded and paperwork requirements have become more stringent.

A trader based in Lubumbashi, who requested anonymity because of the sensitivity of commercial operations, said shipments have been delayed primarily because of “verification of mine origin and documentation at each stage.” He said the pause has already affected deliveries to at least one European customer, with firms seeking revised manifests and additional supporting letters from licensed cooperatives and traders.

The DRC produces the vast majority of the world’s cobalt, a key input for electric vehicle batteries and renewable energy storage. Much of the output is linked to artisanal and small-scale mining, which international due diligence efforts have long targeted for risks ranging from unclear sourcing to labor and safety concerns.
