The government has said crackdowns are aimed at improving legal compliance and stabilizing revenue flows, while it has also faced criticism from industry and human rights groups that enforcement can disrupt livelihoods.

The mining ministry did not respond immediately to a request for comment, and state authorities overseeing exports were not immediately available. Traders said the duration of the suspension was unclear and that some shipments may resume once officials complete inspections or approve updated documentation.

The latest move comes as global buyers try to ensure continuity of supply while meeting regulatory requirements for responsible mineral sourcing. Analysts have said that disruptions in the DRC can quickly ripple through international pricing and contract deliveries, particularly when shipments are delayed or volumes are reallocated among trading routes.

headline: China bans copper warehousing inflows for a week following audit inconsistencies
body: China has banned copper warehousing inflows for one week after auditors found inconsistencies in related paperwork and inventory records, according to a notice circulated to market participants on March 7. The restriction bars new consignments of copper from entering approved warehousing channels starting March 8 and running through March 14, while existing stocks will be allowed to remain under storage. The regulator said the audit discrepancies involved discrepancies between declared lot sizes, arrival documentation and warehouse tally sheets, which prompted a pause to prevent further inflow activity. Traders indicated the move is likely to tighten near-term availability of deliverable copper in China’s bonded storage system, even though most physical pricing benchmarks are set by spot and exchange delivery mechanisms. Analysts said the temporary ban could also disrupt logistics for refiners and importers planning offloading schedules ahead of the spring demand period. The decision follows heightened scrutiny of metals handling procedures in 2025 and earlier 2026 amid several reported compliance issues across warehousing networks.

headline: United States imposes temporary suspension on tin importers after assay discrepancies
body:
The United States has temporarily suspended some tin imports after discrepancies were found in assay results provided by certain importers, according to trade and customs guidance circulated this week.

The suspension targets shipments involving tin covered under U.S. import monitoring and compliance processes, where the Department of Homeland Security’s Customs and Border Protection (CBP) and related agencies review laboratory measurements and documentation submitted at the border. CBP said the pause is limited in scope and is intended to allow time for additional verification of test results and supplier records before shipments are allowed to proceed.

Under the notice, importers whose cargoes are flagged for “assay discrepancies” may face delayed clearance or additional sampling requirements. The issues relate to differences between assay readings reported by sellers or testing entities and those verified through U.S. procedures, including potential inconsistencies in reported purity or composition. The agency did not provide specifics on the magnitude of the differences or name the affected companies in the guidance shared with importers.

Industry participants said the affected lots could include tin intended for downstream manufacturing, where purity and alloy content are typically critical for processing and customer specifications. A temporary suspension can disrupt logistics schedules and increase the cost of inventory holding, particularly for importers that rely on steady flows of metal for production.

The guidance said importers should be prepared to submit supporting documentation for the disputed assays, including chain-of-custody records, original test reports, calibration information for testing laboratories, and manufacturer or supplier declarations. CBP also indicated it may require additional samples to be tested under U.S. oversight, depending on the circumstances of each shipment.

A spokesperson for a trade compliance consulting firm said the key factor is whether documentation can be reconciled with the measurements reviewed by border authorities. “Importers typically need to demonstrate that the assays were performed consistently with agreed methods and that the results can be supported by verifiable lab records,” the spokesperson said.

CBP characterized the action as a risk-based enforcement step rather than a tariff or broader trade restriction. The temporary suspension is expected to remain in place until the review of flagged discrepancies is completed and affected importers can meet the documentation and verification requirements outlined by U.S. authorities.

headline: Belgium halts palladium deliveries from a distributor after regulatory review
body: Belgium has stopped palladium deliveries from a domestic distributor after a regulatory review found discrepancies in documentation tied to recent shipments, according to authorities and market participants on March 7, 2026. The halt affects deliveries scheduled from at least one distributor to industrial buyers, with affected orders spanning late February through March, a period that follows heightened scrutiny of precious-metal trade controls in the European Union. Regulators said the pause is intended to allow verification of origin paperwork, end-use declarations, and compliance with reporting requirements used to screen controlled commodities. A review window running into early March prompted the suspension, while officials indicated they would reassess distribution authorization once new documentation is submitted and checked. Market sources reported tighter availability for palladium across industrial channels following the stoppage, with spot discussions shifting toward longer lead times and revised delivery terms. No timeline was provided for resumption, and traders said the administrative process could extend beyond mid-March depending on the completeness of the distributor’s records.

headline: Chile introduces export licensing for molybdenum concentrates amid global tightening
body:
Chile has introduced an export licensing requirement for molybdenum concentrates as the government moves to monitor outbound shipments amid tighter global trade conditions, officials said.

The measure, published in the Official Gazette this week, requires companies to obtain authorization before exporting molybdenum concentrates produced in Chile. The licensing system applies to shipments classified under customs codes covering concentrates of the metal, which is used to strengthen steel and in certain chemical and industrial applications.

The government said the new framework is designed to improve traceability and strengthen oversight of export flows. Authorities also indicated that the policy aligns Chile’s procedures with evolving international requirements and administrative practices used in other mineral sectors.

Under the rule, exporters must file an application that includes details on the origin and quantity of concentrates, the destination country, and documentation supporting production and sales. Licenses will be granted by relevant agencies after review, with decisions subject to compliance checks and verification of supporting records.

Officials did not specify any immediate restrictions on volumes, nor did they set a headline timeline for how quickly licenses would be processed. Chile’s mining ministry said the scheme is intended to ensure accurate data collection and to support enforcement against irregularities, without altering Chile’s overall approach to mineral exports.

Molybdenum production is concentrated in northern Chile, where output is linked to copper mining and is generated as a byproduct in some operations. While Chile is a leading global producer of copper, its molybdenum supply has also been watched by industrial users seeking stable feedstock for steelmaking and manufacturing.

The introduction of licensing comes as global market conditions have tightened for certain critical and industrial commodities, driven by shifting logistics, heightened compliance scrutiny, and changing trade policies in multiple regions. Companies exporting minerals have increasingly faced additional administrative requirements, including proof of origin, end-use declarations and documentation connected to sanctions and export controls.

A ministry spokesperson said the government aims “to strengthen the control and transparency of export operations” for molybdenum concentrates and to ensure that shipments are properly documented from production through departure.

Market participants said the new process could slightly affect timing for shipments while companies adjust paperwork and shipping plans to meet the authorization steps, though no major operational changes have been publicly confirmed by producers.

headline: Russia accelerates uranium production but delays exports due to shipping insurance
body: Russia has accelerated uranium output this year, raising production volumes while postponing most export shipments because shippers face tightened insurance and compliance requirements, according to industry sources. Output increased in the first quarter of 2026 as facilities ramped up fuel-cycle operations, with utilities and traders reporting higher deliveries earmarked for foreign buyers. However, exports have been delayed by weeks, with freight schedules disrupted after insurers limited coverage for voyages tied to sanctions risk. Several cargoes that were due to depart between late January and mid-February were rolled to April as importers demanded updated documentation and underwriters recalculated premiums and deductibles. In the global market, contract deliveries typically hinge on timely insurance certificates and port clearances, and the latest disruptions have widened the gap between production and off-take. Analysts said Russia may still meet some calendar-year sales targets, but reduced shipping availability and higher insured transport costs are likely to push more volumes into the second half of 2026. The bottleneck is expected to persist until alternative underwriting arrangements are finalized for non-sanctioned routes.

headline: Peru declares force majeure at zinc-adjacent smelter affecting selenium byproduct
body:
Peru has declared force majeure at a zinc-adjacent smelter, a move industry sources said is expected to disrupt selenium byproduct processing at the facility, according to notices circulated to customers.

The declaration, issued for a period that the company did not publicly detail in its initial communications, cites circumstances affecting the plant’s ability to operate under normal contractual conditions. Force majeure clauses are typically used when disruptions such as equipment failures, supply interruptions, or regulatory constraints make performance impossible or commercially impracticable.

The smelter, which produces zinc and related intermediates, also has output streams tied to selenium recovery, a metalloid used in applications ranging from electronics to glass and photovoltaics. One industry participant said the facility’s selenium byproduct is processed through the same downstream equipment that is being curtailed or placed under alternative operating modes while the plant addresses the underlying issue.

The Peruvian government did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the specific circumstances referenced in the declaration. In the absence of further public detail, market watchers said the strongest immediate impact would likely be on selenium availability rather than on headline zinc volumes, depending on how long the plant remains in a constrained operating state.

A customer notice reviewed by Reuters-style reporting indicated that the supplier is seeking to adjust delivery schedules and pricing terms for shipments covered by existing contracts. “We are working with counterparties to manage allocations and revised timelines while operations are affected,” said a company spokesperson, who declined to comment on the cause of the force majeure beyond stating it was tied to operational conditions at the smelter.

Selenium prices and physical availability have been sensitive to refiners’ output because the material is often produced as a byproduct, with limited standalone capacity in some regions. Producers typically rely on uninterrupted treatment and refining steps to maintain throughput, meaning disruptions at a single plant can quickly propagate into tighter supply for spot and near-term contracts.

Industry participants said the market will likely monitor whether Peru’s force majeure is followed by additional information on the duration of the shutdown or recovery timeline. They also indicated that buyers may seek alternative sourcing in the spot market or adjust purchasing plans ahead of any official update from the smelter operator.

headline: Portugal suspends graphite anode supply to new customers after quality noncompliance
body: Portugal’s government has ordered a suspension of graphite anode deliveries for new customers after quality noncompliance was detected in outgoing shipments, according to industry sources. The measure, effective immediately on 2026-03-08, applies to orders placed after the date and covers anode supply contracts for steelmaking and other high-temperature industrial uses. Compliance checks flagged defects tied to impurity levels and dimensional tolerances, triggering a pause while resampling and retesting are completed for affected production lots. Suppliers said shipments from plants under review may continue only for existing contracted volumes until 2026-03-21, pending approval of revised inspection records. Several buyers received notices that acceptance testing will be tightened, with additional batch documentation required before loading. Traders expect the restriction to affect near-term lead times of 2–6 weeks for new offtakes, depending on alternative sourcing in Europe and Asia. No estimate for the full duration of the suspension was provided, though officials said the initial review window ends at the close of March.

headline: South Korea imposes emergency port closure affecting tungsten shipments on Jul 3
body:
South Korea will impose an emergency closure of several ports on July 3, affecting tungsten shipments, the government said on Sunday, citing safety and operational concerns.

The Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries said the measure applies to designated ports used for bulk and cargo handling, with the closure beginning at midnight local time and running for a 24-hour period. The ministry did not specify which facilities would be affected in its initial statement, but said the shutdown covers vessel entry and cargo handling activities during the closure window.

The emergency action is expected to disrupt the movement of commodities including tungsten, a key input used in the manufacture of hard materials, tools and some defense-related components. South Korea is a major processing hub for industrial metals in East Asia and relies on imported concentrates and refined products, with shipments often scheduled around customs clearance and port operating windows.

A notice from port authorities indicated that cargo already en route may be delayed, and that shippers should coordinate with shipping agents to adjust schedules and prevent congestion when operations resume. The government said exceptions could be made for vessels carrying urgent essential supplies, subject to approval and safety review, though it did not outline any commodity-specific waivers for tungsten.

In related guidance, customs and maritime regulators urged traders to ensure documentation is accurate ahead of the closure, including vessel routing, bill of lading details and expected arrival times. Companies are also expected to review whether any stockpiling or rerouting is needed to meet contractual delivery timelines, particularly for shipments moving through ports designated for the emergency shutdown.

The ministry said the port closure decision follows “immediate risk assessments” and is intended to ensure continuity of maritime safety and traffic management. It added that additional instructions would be issued closer to the date, including guidance on how late arrivals and load transfers would be handled.

No immediate details were provided on the total tonnage expected to be affected, nor on the number of vessels scheduled to arrive on July 3. Industry participants said tungsten flows can be particularly sensitive to short disruptions because concentrate and intermediate product shipments are often booked in advance and may face limited alternate routing options depending on origin and destination.

The announcement comes as South Korea continues to review contingency measures for port operations amid shifting shipping patterns across the region.

headline: China restricts vanadium exports following demand surge and warehouse inspections
body: China has introduced tighter controls on vanadium exports after a surge in global demand and an inspection campaign at domestic warehouses, according to industry sources. The new measures, effective immediately and applying to shipments cleared after 2026-03-01, narrow the list of approved exporters and require additional documentation tied to inventory verification. Authorities have been checking storage volumes and chain-of-custody records across major hubs since late February 2026, with emphasis on stocks held by trading firms rather than integrated producers. The restrictions come as prices for vanadium-containing materials have risen in 2026 amid higher orders for battery-grade products and steel additives used in rebar and high-strength plate. Market participants said the policy could reduce near-term export flows by weeks because customs clearance now hinges on post-inspection reconciliation. Several cargoes scheduled for March were reported to be delayed pending confirmation of available tonnage, while forward contracts for April and May were repriced as buyers weighed potential shortfalls.

headline: Kazakhstan delays gallium production run after critical equipment procurement issues
body:
Kazakhstan has delayed a planned gallium production campaign after encountering problems sourcing critical equipment, according to industry and government-linked sources.

The delay affects the timing of output from a gallium processing run intended to support local supply and downstream customers, several sources said, noting that the setback is tied to procurement rather than feedstock availability. Companies involved in the project said the issue centers on lead times and specifications for key manufacturing components that had not been confirmed in time for the start date.

Gallium is used in electronics, solar power and optoelectronics, and Kazakhstan is among the world’s significant producers through byproduct recovery from zinc and other ores. Demand for the metal has risen as manufacturers seek alternatives to concentrate supply chains and secure refining capacity.

A statement provided to partners involved in the project indicated that production scheduling would be revised once the remaining equipment items are delivered and installed, the sources said. They added that the company and its contractors are working to validate performance parameters for the equipment, including process control systems and high-temperature refining modules, before beginning full-scale operations.

In discussions with suppliers, the sources said Kazakhstan-based procurement teams flagged several constraints, including export compliance checks, transport logistics and late confirmations of delivery dates from component vendors. While some components have arrived, others were reported to be still in transit or awaiting final inspection and documentation.

One official familiar with the procurement process said the revised timeline was intended to avoid “starting an operating run without all critical systems meeting the required specifications.” The official did not provide a new start date.

The gallium delay comes as global industry players try to expand capacity and reduce reliance on single-country supply routes. For Kazakhstan, which has sought to increase value-add activity domestically, refining campaigns are typically closely linked to downstream demand, export contracts and refinery integration work.

The country’s producers are now reassessing the campaign calendar, including whether to adjust batch schedules and interim output targets. Industry sources said any restart could be gradual, with partial processing planned once the first equipment sections are commissioned, before moving to steady-state production.

headline: India orders investigation into indium pricing after sudden supply halt at refinery
body: India’s Ministry of Commerce and Industry has ordered an investigation into indium pricing after a sudden supply halt at an unnamed refinery disrupted trade flows, according to an internal notice circulated on March 8, 2026. Officials said market participants reported abrupt spot shortages beginning in late February, with premiums widening within days and exchange-linked contracts showing unusually sharp price resets. The probe will examine whether the halt was technical or linked to deliberate withholding of inventories, and will also assess compliance with import, distribution, and reporting rules for critical metals. Investigators are expected to review refinery operating logs, shipment manifests, customs filings, and trading records from late January through March 7, focusing on volumes, lead times, and any coordinated bid activity. The ministry said it will also evaluate whether price surges were amplified by speculative positioning ahead of fiscal and procurement schedules in March. Preliminary findings are due by April 15, with potential corrective measures including pricing guidance for state buyers and enforcement actions if violations are found.

headline: Brazil declares state of emergency at iron ore logistics hub after dam breach alert
body:
Brazil declared a state of emergency in the southeastern state of Minas Gerais after authorities issued an alert related to potential impacts from a suspected dam breach at an iron ore logistics hub, the government said on Sunday.

The emergency measures were announced for regions surrounding the infrastructure used to move iron ore, including transport routes and port-related facilities in Minas Gerais, where officials have been monitoring conditions after reports of structural concerns at a nearby tailings dam. The alert followed an incident notification from operators and subsequent inspections by state and federal agencies, according to officials.

Under the emergency declaration, local authorities were instructed to intensify surveillance of watercourses and containment systems, restrict access to affected areas, and prepare contingency plans for communities downstream. Civil defense teams are coordinating with environmental and mining regulators to assess rainfall levels, water flow readings and the stability of structures, while technical reports are being compiled for review.

In a statement, Brazil’s national civil defense agency said it had activated interagency coordination and that the response focused on “risk mitigation and early warning” for residents and workers. The agency said additional monitoring points were being established and that communication channels with municipalities had been reinforced to ensure timely updates.

Officials said train and road operations linked to the logistics hub were not immediately halted, but authorities warned that changes could be implemented depending on the outcome of inspections and the evolution of water conditions. Brazil is one of the world’s biggest iron ore exporters, and disruptions to mining and transport infrastructure can affect shipments through the country’s main corridors to ports.

The state government said it was also working with the operator of the affected site on immediate actions to secure equipment and manage water flows, including targeted sampling and engineering assessments. Environmental agencies are testing water quality parameters and checking whether any release of contaminated material had occurred, while emergency services prepared shelters and medical support if evacuations became necessary.

No injuries were reported in the initial alert, and officials said the situation would be reassessed as monitoring data and technical findings became available.

headline: Chile court suspends permits for Antofagasta copper projects after protests
body: A Chilean court ordered the suspension of permits for copper projects in Antofagasta following protests, according to filings reviewed on March 7. The ruling affects environmental authorizations and related administrative approvals tied to multiple projects in the country’s northern mining belt, where demonstrations have focused on water access, dust impacts, and local consultation. The court said it granted interim relief while it assesses whether the permitting process complied with Chile’s environmental impact framework and procedural requirements for affected communities. The suspension applies immediately and bars start or expansion activities that rely on the challenged approvals. Regulators and project operators now face deadlines to provide additional documentation, with hearings scheduled in the coming weeks. Chile’s mining sector, which produced about 5.7 million metric tons of copper in 2025, has seen growing scrutiny of permitting timelines as community and environmental concerns intensify across the Atacama region.

headline: Indonesia imposes export ban on nickel pig iron amid supply dispute
body:
Indonesia has imposed a ban on the export of nickel pig iron, effective immediately, as the government moves to address a supply dispute that has disrupted shipments to overseas customers, according to officials.

The Ministry of Trade said the restriction applies to shipments of nickel pig iron produced domestically, with exemptions not immediately detailed. The ministry cited the need to ensure the timely availability of raw materials for downstream processing activities in Indonesia, including the production of ferronickel and nickel-based intermediates used in batteries and stainless steel.

The policy follows growing concern among industry players that domestic output and sales allocations have been thrown off by disputes between producers and shipping traders over quotas and delivery schedules. In recent weeks, exporters have reported difficulty obtaining clearance documents for cargoes, while some buyers overseas have said they faced delays in receiving contracted material.

A senior ministry official said the ban was designed to prevent further disruption to Indonesia’s industrial supply chain. “This measure is to secure domestic supply and follow the government’s export management policy while the dispute is resolved,” the official said.

The Indonesian government has in past years tightened export controls on raw minerals and intermediate products to encourage investment in refining and smelting capacity. Under earlier rules, Indonesia limited exports of certain unprocessed ores and imposed requirements tied to downstream processing to keep more value within the country.

Nickel pig iron is a key intermediate product in the production of refined nickel. Indonesia is the world’s largest producer of nickel ore and has been ramping up capacity for later-stage processing, as demand rises for batteries used in electric vehicles. The export ban is therefore likely to affect global market liquidity for the intermediate grade, particularly in trading hubs that rely on Indonesian supplies.

Industry participants said it could take several weeks for buyers to source alternative material or renegotiate contracts, depending on the availability of nickel pig iron from other jurisdictions and whether Indonesia introduces a licensing mechanism for specific shipments. The ministry did not provide a timeline for lifting or reviewing the ban, and said further updates would be issued if the underlying dispute is resolved and export management procedures are adjusted.

headline: DR Congo cobalt buyers face payment delays after audit triggers operational pause
body: Cobalt buyers in the Democratic Republic of the Congo are reporting payment delays and tighter shipment conditions after an internal audit prompted several mining operators to pause output, market sources said on 7 March 2026. The audit, conducted in February across multiple processing and trading units, flagged discrepancies in invoice documentation and concentrate quality records, leading operators to halt receipting of new sales contracts until reconciliations are completed. Buyers said payment terms have shifted from 30–45 days toward extended settlement windows, with some payments stretching beyond 60 days as banks verify beneficiary details and supporting certificates. One cohort of offtake agreements for 2026 cargoes covering several thousand metric tons of cobalt content was partially suspended, with remaining deliveries moved to later loading dates. Traders also reported that penalty clauses tied to assay deviations are being enforced more strictly, affecting net payable pricing per tonne. Industry participants expect operational normalization in mid-April if audit findings are cleared and payment workflows return to prior approval levels.

headline: Zambia copperbelt union strike halts smelter runs for second week
body:
A strike by workers in Zambia’s Copperbelt has halted smelter operations for a second consecutive week, disrupting copper processing and power flows in a region that accounts for most of the country’s output.

The work stoppage began earlier this month and has spread across some mining and processing sites, with union representatives saying members are demanding improved wages and safer working conditions as inflation and energy costs continue to pressure household budgets. Smelters and other processing units have cut or suspended runs because staffing levels needed to keep furnaces running have not been met, according to operators and union officials familiar with the situation.

The Copperbelt is home to major concentrators, solvent-extraction plants and smelting facilities that turn mined ore into copper cathodes. When strikes reduce the movement of concentrate and other feedstocks, smelters typically slow down to prevent stockpile imbalances and avoid idle labor costs, people close to the sector said. Several plants have therefore maintained only minimal activity or shut entirely, with management citing the inability to maintain continuous operations without the full workforce.

Union leaders said the stoppage is continuing despite engagement with employers and government officials. “We are not going back until our demands on wages and allowances are met and there is agreement on safety and staffing,” a union representative said, speaking on condition of anonymity because negotiations are ongoing.

Zambia’s mining industry has faced a string of disruptions in recent months, including logistics bottlenecks and power supply fluctuations. The latest strike adds to operational uncertainty for copper shipments, with some companies relying on interim inventory while others plan for extended production downtime.

Copper is Zambia’s main export and a key source of foreign exchange for the government. Industry analysts said prolonged stoppages can affect near-term copper concentrate availability and concentrate treatment charges, and may also delay refined product deliveries tied to contract schedules.

Companies in the Copperbelt have been attempting to manage the impact by reallocating maintenance staff and adjusting procurement plans. However, industry contacts said restarting smelter lines can take time once operations are fully stopped, as refiring and stabilising processes require trained teams and consistent feedstock flows. The strike’s duration remains unclear, with negotiations expected to continue between union representatives, employers and relevant government agencies.

headline: Canada lithium miner reports force majeure after wildfire closes haul roads
body: A Canada-based lithium miner reported a force majeure on Monday after wildfires closed key haul roads in its operations region, disrupting ore movement to processing facilities. The company said the closure, effective immediately, prevented trucks from accessing designated routes used to transport spodumene and related concentrates, with implications for near-term production and shipments. It cited active fire activity and road restrictions imposed by local authorities, noting that timelines for reopening were uncertain as of March 9. The firm indicated it is working with contractors and emergency management officials to reroute logistics where possible, but said alternative routes may require additional permits and can extend travel distances by more than 50%. The mine operator also warned that procurement schedules and electricity demand for equipment cannot be fully synchronized with revised hauling windows. Investors will be watching whether the company can resume normal haulage within weeks, as the disruption is expected to affect inventory drawdowns and may shift deliveries into April depending on road access.

headline: Peru announces temporary suspension of rail shipments to copper concentrate port
body:
Peru has announced a temporary suspension of rail shipments to its main copper concentrate port as the country carries out operational adjustments, according to a government notice and industry sources.

The suspension, which affects rail services feeding concentrate to the port facility, is set to begin immediately and is scheduled for a limited period, officials said. The measure is intended to address congestion and coordinate with port and rail operators on schedule changes, the notice indicated, without providing a specific end date.

Peru is one of the world’s largest copper producers, and rail is a key logistics link between mining areas in the Andes and export infrastructure on the Pacific coast. Concentrate shipments are routinely moved by train to reduce transport times and concentrate handling costs for exporters.

A spokesperson for the transport authority said the decision was made “to ensure orderly movement of cargo and align rail and port operations during the adjustment period,” adding that authorities would publish updates as the timeframe progressed.

Market participants said the suspension could affect short-term export volumes, particularly shipments already in transit or queued for loading. Several mining companies deliver concentrate to ports under contract terms that depend on loading availability and demurrage rules, making port access and transport schedules a critical factor for cash flow.

The announcement comes amid ongoing efforts by Peruvian logistics providers to manage capacity constraints at export terminals and improve the predictability of shipping windows. Rail schedules can be disrupted when port infrastructure is operating at higher volumes than planned or when maintenance requires changes to rail dispatching and yard operations.

Industry sources said rail operators would continue other freight where feasible, but concentrate trains bound for the affected port would be paused until coordination with terminal management is completed. Exporters and ship agents were advised to monitor revised loading schedules and expected arrival windows for outgoing vessels.

Peru’s copper exports are closely watched by global markets because they help determine available supply and can influence treatment and refining charges. Any disruption to rail-to-port movements, even if temporary, can tighten near-term logistics capacity for buyers awaiting cargoes.

headline: Finland graphite producer halts anode materials loading after inspection failure
body: Finland’s graphite anode materials producer has halted loading operations after an inspection failed at an outbound shipment facility, the company said in an internal notice circulated on March 8. The stoppage affects loading of anode material intended for battery supply contracts, with outbound tonnage suspended pending a re-inspection of batches tied to the failed inspection result. Operations were paused at the plant loading point immediately after the quality team identified a discrepancy during routine checks, according to the notice. No dates were provided for resumption, though the company indicated it will complete corrective actions and document batch compliance before any further movement of product. Industry sources said the incident could delay deliveries scheduled for mid-to-late March, when several customers typically complete monthly receiving cycles. While the inspection failure has not been linked to a production outage, buyers were asked to hold logistics plans that depend on near-term loading slots. The producer did not specify whether the issue related to particle size, coating performance, or moisture content.

headline: China ramps customs checks on vanadium imports after documentation crackdown
body:
China has stepped up customs checks on vanadium imports after a documentation crackdown, according to traders and compliance documents seen by Reuters, as authorities tighten scrutiny of shipments used in steelmaking and battery materials.

The heightened reviews, which began in late February and expanded in March, focus on the accuracy and completeness of import paperwork for vanadium products, including vanadium pentoxide and ferrovanadium. Customs officials have been requesting additional certificates of origin, invoices with more detailed descriptions, and revised declarations that match assay results provided by overseas suppliers.

Several importers said they have faced slower clearance times and, in some cases, requests for amended declarations before goods can be released. One trader said shipments that previously moved through customs in a matter of days were being held longer while officials cross-checked documents against prior consignment records and supplier documentation.

“Customs is asking for more supporting paperwork and consistency checks are taking longer,” the trader said, requesting anonymity because of commercial sensitivity. “The change is documentation-driven rather than a specific ban, but it is affecting timing and costs.”

The move comes as China seeks to improve enforcement against misdeclared and non-compliant commodities, particularly for metals that feed downstream industrial demand. Vanadium is used to strengthen steel and is also increasingly sought for battery applications, making reliable supply and compliance a priority for buyers.

Importers said the new controls have led to more pre-shipment verification by overseas producers and freight intermediaries, including tighter coordination between exporters, customs brokers and Chinese registries. Some companies have also begun rechecking Harmonized System codes used on filings to ensure they align with product specifications described in accompanying documents.

China is the world’s largest consumer and importer of vanadium-related materials. Demand is driven by the expansion of high-strength steel production and government-backed manufacturing initiatives, while supply depends heavily on mining and refining capacity outside China.

Traders said it remains unclear whether the checks will lead to more refusals of shipments or mainly to administrative delays. They added that the updated scrutiny could influence price negotiations for March and April contracts as buyers factor in potential demurrage, inspection-related costs and the risk of additional documentation requests.

Chinese customs authorities did not respond to requests for comment.

headline: South Africa platinum group shutdown ordered after power outage triggers kiln failure
body: South Africa’s power supplier ordered a shutdown of a platinum group metals facility after a grid outage on 2026-03-07 triggered a kiln failure, according to industry and utility communications reviewed on 2026-03-08. The outage caused furnace temperatures to drop rapidly, leading to damage in the kiln lining and a halt to operations at the plant, which processes PGM concentrates for downstream refining. Plant operators were instructed to stop work immediately and keep equipment in a safe state while technicians inspect the refractory and reset commissioning controls. Restart conditions include verification of electrical stability, kiln integrity testing, and the replacement of components impacted by the thermal shock, with compliance checks scheduled for 2026-03-10. The closure is expected to disrupt scheduled deliveries of refined metals in the first half of March, with managers indicating restart could take several weeks depending on spare-part availability. The incident comes as South Africa continues to manage intermittent generation capacity and tighter dispatch rules tied to national grid performance.

headline: Russia palladium shipments delayed by port closure due to ice storm
body:
A disruption at a Baltic port has delayed Russia-linked palladium shipments after an ice storm forced closures, traders and logistics providers said on Monday.

Shipping documents and industry contacts indicated that vessels carrying palladium from Russia were held up when the port suspended operations due to worsening ice conditions. One source with knowledge of the matter said the closure affected planned loadings over the weekend and into Monday morning, pushing delivery schedules back by several days depending on when ice-breaking crews can restore access to berths.

Palladium is widely used in catalytic converters, especially for gasoline and diesel vehicles, and also in industrial applications including electronics and hydrogen technologies. Delays to deliveries can tighten supply in the near term, particularly where buyers have short lead times tied to refinery processing and contract settlement.

The extent of the impact depends on rerouting options. Traders said some shipments may be redirected to alternative terminals once conditions improve, while others face additional delays if the vessels must wait offshore until ice clears or pilots and tug services are reinstated. “The port closure is the main issue right now. Ships are standing by, and the loading window depends on whether the ice-breaking operation can reopen the approaches,” one logistics coordinator said.

Market participants said the slowdown comes at a time when physical palladium flows are already sensitive to regional weather and shipping schedules. While palladium is traded on futures exchanges, many spot transactions rely on timely physical delivery, and postponements can affect settlement for buyers and sellers expecting shipments by a specific date.

Russian exporters typically ship metals through a network of ports servicing regional industrial demand, with handoffs to international freight operators. Traders said they are monitoring whether the disruption is confined to a single facility or spreads to multiple loading points, which would increase the likelihood of broader timing mismatches across the supply chain.

No immediate statement was available from the affected port operator. Officials for ice-breaking and maritime safety services did not respond to requests for comment by press time. Traders said they will reassess shipment schedules later in the day as weather forecasts and port reopening timelines become clearer.

headline: Portugal tungsten supplier faces license suspension pending environmental review
body: Portugal’s main tungsten supplier has been notified that its operating license will be suspended pending an environmental review, according to regulators involved in the case. The action follows concerns raised over waste-water discharge, tailings handling, and associated air-emission controls at the company’s processing site in northern Portugal. The authority said the suspension would take effect immediately after a short compliance period ending 15 March 2026, unless supplemental documentation is provided by 10 March. The review is scheduled to conclude by 30 June, with an interim assessment required every 30 days from the company’s submission date. Inspectors will conduct site sampling for heavy metals and monitoring of runoff pathways, while the firm must provide updated closure and remediation plans covering at least the 2020–2025 operational period. The company supplies tungsten used in steel-alloying and tool manufacturing, and local officials said continuity of deliveries would depend on the outcome of permit amendments.

headline: Vietnam tightens trade licensing for tungsten exports, widening spot supply gap
body:
Vietnam has tightened licensing requirements for tungsten exports, according to industry sources, a move that could widen a spot supply gap as buyers seek material ahead of tighter compliance checks.

Under the updated rules, exporters must submit additional documentation covering origin, processing steps and inventory records before shipments are approved, the sources said. The changes apply to both tungsten concentrate and intermediate products, with authorities requiring more frequent audits for firms that export larger volumes. Several traders said the new process is likely to extend lead times for cleared shipments, even for companies that previously had permits in place.

Tungsten is used in hard metals for cutting tools and industrial machining, and shortages in the spot market can quickly affect purchasing decisions. Traders said the tightening is expected to reduce near-term export availability from Vietnam, one of the more active processing and trading hubs for the metal in Asia. While Vietnam’s output does not dominate global supply, it can influence regional pricing when flows are delayed, especially for cargoes linked to specific customer specifications.

Market participants indicated the immediate impact may depend on how quickly shipments already in the pipeline can be cleared. Some exporters said they are preparing filings to avoid interruptions, while others anticipate that certain lots will be held until authorities complete reviews of traceability documentation. One buyer said the company has begun sourcing from alternative routes and is likely to negotiate more flexible delivery windows to manage timing uncertainty.

“Export licensing procedures have become more stringent, and we have to ensure all paperwork aligns with the origin and processing requirements,” a Vietnamese exporter said, requesting anonymity because they were not authorised to speak publicly. The exporter said it expects approvals to proceed but acknowledged that approvals could take longer than under the previous regime.

The tightening comes as tungsten markets are already sensitive to short-term supply imbalances. Traders said spot buying often accelerates when buyers fear delays, and any step that slows cross-border shipments can widen the gap between available material and demand. Several said they are monitoring how other producing countries respond, including whether inventories held by processors or traders in China and other Asian markets may be redirected to cover potential shortages.

Benchmark prices for tungsten can fluctuate in response to supply expectations, and market participants said they will watch for evidence of whether the licensing change reduces export volumes immediately or primarily affects timing. For now, buyers appear to be shifting to spot purchases and securing contract flexibility while waiting for clearer guidance on the scope and duration of the tightened approvals.

headline: Kazakhstan introduces export quota for molybdenum concentrates starting July
body: Kazakhstan will introduce an export quota for molybdenum concentrates beginning July 1, 2026, according to government measures published in recent days. The policy sets a capped volume for shipments of molybdenum concentrates leaving the country, with allocations administered through a licensing process tied to producer registration and prior export history. The rules apply to concentrates under the relevant customs tariff headings, covering material used in steelmaking and chemical supply chains. Officials said the quota is intended to stabilize domestic availability and support downstream processing as local capacity for refining expands. Kazakhstan, which has long supplied molybdenum products to global markets, has seen pricing swings driven by demand from alloy steel and battery-related applications. The new cap is scheduled to remain in place for at least the remainder of 2026, with a review planned ahead of the end of the year.

headline: US imposes customs action on gallium shipments over valuation and routing issues
body:
The United States has imposed a customs enforcement action affecting shipments of gallium after authorities raised concerns about how the metal was being valued and routed, according to trade and regulatory documents reviewed by industry sources.

The action, issued by U.S. Customs and Border Protection, centers on import declarations for gallium destined for warehouses and processors in the United States. CBP said it is challenging certain entries based on mismatches between reported values and supporting documentation, as well as discrepancies in the stated routing used for the shipments.

Gallium, a key input for semiconductors, LEDs and other electronics, is subject to heightened scrutiny in recent years as governments seek to track flows of strategic materials. While the United States has implemented export controls on gallium-related items, customs reviews can also come into play when shipments cross borders with documentation or commercial details that differ from what importers submit.

In its notices to importers, CBP directed companies to provide additional records supporting declared transaction values, including purchase orders, invoices, contracts and shipping documents. Authorities also requested clarification on the pathway of goods, including where the metal was located and whether intermediate stops or transshipment were reflected correctly in the shipping paperwork. Failure to meet the requests could result in continued holds, changes to how duties are assessed, or potential penalties, trade compliance specialists said.

A compliance consultant familiar with the customs process said the enforcement focus is typical of situations where CBP has identified “inconsistencies that need to be reconciled before an entry can be liquidated.” The consultant added that importers are often required to respond within set timelines and may need to submit corrected documentation.

Industry participants said the action is likely to affect the pace of deliveries and increase costs for firms that rely on gallium inputs. Additional documentation reviews can delay clearance at ports and may trigger re-examination of past entries, depending on how CBP frames the issue and whether further testing or audits are ordered.

CBP did not immediately provide further detail in the materials reviewed about the number of shipments affected or whether the notices apply to specific companies. The enforcement step comes as importers continue to navigate tightened U.S. scrutiny of strategic materials, where valuation disputes and routing-related record gaps have been common triggers for customs intervention.

headline: Japan refinery limits indium processing after upstream supply disruption
body: Japan’s main indium processing facilities have cut run rates after an upstream supply disruption reduced feedstock availability, according to industry sources. Refinery operators in Japan limited processing of the metal used in LCDs and solar products starting in late February 2026, with utilization falling by about 20% to 35% through early March. The constraints follow a disruption to concentrate and intermediate streams from upstream producers, tightening secondary and primary material supplies across Asia. Several refiners revised purchase allocations for March and deferred non-essential batches, contributing to slower throughput and longer lead times for contracted volumes. Spot availability of indium-bearing intermediates reportedly tightened as sellers prioritized immediate contracts rather than extended-term shipments. Traders said the impact is likely to persist into mid-March unless replacement feedstock arrives, with some loads expected to be rescheduled by 2 to 4 weeks. Pricing moved in line with reduced liquidity, as buyers weighed reduced Japanese refinery demand against expectations for stabilized upstream flows later in the quarter.

headline: China graphite anode producer declares force majeure after transformer breakdown
body:
A Chinese producer of graphite anodes for lithium-ion batteries has declared force majeure after a transformer breakdown disrupted operations at one of its facilities, according to a filing released late Monday.

The company said the equipment failure prevented it from supplying stable electricity required for production, leading to interruptions that could affect delivery schedules and customer orders. Under force majeure provisions, the firm is seeking relief from certain contractual obligations during the period of disruption, while it works to restore normal output.

The company did not immediately provide specific timelines for repairs or a duration for the force majeure declaration in the initial notice. It said output and shipments would be subject to the extent of the damage and the progress of restoration work, and that it would issue further updates as the situation develops.

Force majeure declarations are commonly used in China’s industrial sector when unforeseen events prevent performance of contracts, such as natural disasters, major equipment failures or supply disruptions that are outside a company’s control. In battery supply chains, power stability is particularly important because anode manufacturing relies on high-temperature processing steps and consistent operating conditions.

The transformer breakdown comes as demand for graphite anodes remains robust amid continued investment in electric vehicle and energy storage capacity. Graphite anodes are typically produced through processes that include crushing, purification and thermal treatment, with electricity and controlled operating conditions playing a central role.

In its filing, the company said it had initiated contingency measures and repair efforts following the incident. It also warned that the disruption could have an impact on production volumes for the period in question, and that downstream customers may experience adjustments to receiving schedules.

The notice did not specify the facility location or the supplier of the transformer equipment. It also did not quantify financial exposure, though it said outcomes would depend on the duration of the downtime and the rate at which production can return to normal levels.

Companies in the graphite anode segment have increasingly faced operational and logistics challenges in recent years, including tighter energy management requirements and periodic equipment constraints. Traders and downstream manufacturers typically monitor such announcements closely for signs of potential supply shortages or delays in procurement planning.

headline: Namibia uranium operator pauses deliveries following regulator safety findings
body: Namibia’s uranium operator, in a move affecting planned exports, has paused deliveries after the country’s nuclear regulator issued safety findings that it said required corrective actions, according to people familiar with the matter. The suspension, which began this week, applies to shipments tied to recent production runs and is expected to last until regulator reviews of updated procedures are completed, potentially spanning several weeks. The regulator’s assessment, dated 2026-03-06, cited multiple nonconformities in radiation-control measures and documentation for transport packaging, including inconsistencies identified during inspections of storage and loading operations. The operator said it has halted outbound transfers while it retrains staff and implements revised checklists for dose monitoring and waste segregation. Namibia produces uranium for overseas customers under long-term contracts, with deliveries scheduled across 2026 to support projected output at mine and processing sites. Industry sources said delays could trigger force-majeure discussions with counterparties if the pause extends beyond April, while the operator continues underground and milling activities pending clearance.

headline: Australia cadmium smelter production slows after effluent permit renewal delays
body:
Production at an Australian cadmium smelter has slowed after delays in renewing an environmental permit for effluent discharges, according to company statements and regulator documents reviewed by industry sources.

The plant, which processes cadmium for export and domestic industrial use, reduced operating rates following setbacks in the renewal process for its effluent discharge approval, the sources said. The smelter relies on the permit to manage treated water and wastewater from refining operations, and the company said it could not fully ramp back up until authorisation timelines were clarified.

In Australia, effluent permits are issued under state environmental frameworks and are tied to conditions on monitoring, allowable discharge limits and reporting. Industry participants said delays can occur when regulators require additional documentation or when amendments to discharge conditions are under review. In this case, the renewal was not finalised within the timeframe the operator had expected, leading to operational constraints at the site.

The company said it has continued essential maintenance activities and treated effluent management to remain within its existing compliance obligations while the permit is processed. “We are working closely with the regulator to complete the renewal requirements and to ensure discharge controls remain in line with the applicable conditions,” a company spokesperson said in a statement provided to stakeholders. The spokesperson declined to comment on the duration of the production slowdown, citing ongoing discussions with regulators.

A regulator spokesperson confirmed that the renewal request was under assessment and that the review involved both compliance verification and administrative checks. The regulator said it could not provide a specific timetable while the file remained in process.

Cadmium is widely used in rechargeable batteries, pigments and specialty alloys, with refining capacity concentrated in a limited number of facilities. A reduction in output can affect spot availability and downstream costs, particularly for customers that rely on steady contracted supply. Traders said the market impact depends on how quickly the permit is renewed and whether any additional conditions are imposed that would require process adjustments.

The smelter operator said it is managing customer delivery schedules while it operates at reduced rates. It added that once the renewal is granted, production would be adjusted to normal levels subject to commissioning and ramp-up procedures.

headline: South Korea selenium processors cut runs after feedstock deliveries miss schedule
body: South Korean selenium processors reduced operating runs this week after feedstock deliveries missed scheduled arrival windows, according to market participants tracking the specialty metal supply chain. Several processors in the country cut throughput in March, citing logistics delays tied to late shipments and staggered customs clearance for upstream intermediate materials. One producer lowered line utilization for multiple days starting 6 March, while another scaled back deliveries intake after planned lots did not arrive by 8 March. Traders said the schedule slips forced tighter inventory drawdowns, with prompt availability narrowing for near-term contracts covering late March. Prices for selenium products were reported to hold steady despite the reductions, reflecting limited spot liquidity rather than immediate cost relief. Deliveries are expected to normalize later in the month if remaining shipments clear by mid-March, traders added, though they noted lead times could extend by 1–2 weeks depending on port congestion and documentation reviews.

headline: China bismuth processing halted by local safety inspection after blast scare
body:
Chinese bismuth processing at a plant was temporarily halted after local authorities carried out a safety inspection following a reported blast scare, industry sources said on Tuesday.

The suspension affected operations at a facility involved in the processing of bismuth and related materials in a provincial area, the sources said. They added that the interruption was limited to production rather than shipments, but some output was expected to be delayed while inspectors checked equipment, storage conditions and emergency systems.

A local emergency management department and market regulators were among the agencies that conducted on-site checks after the scare, the sources said, citing observations at the site and messages circulated among suppliers. The concern prompted a review of pressurised systems and blasting-related procedures, even though no confirmed explosion was reported, they added.

One industry representative said the inspection focused on compliance with hazardous materials rules and whether safeguards were operating as required. “The plant was asked to stop processing while the inspection team verifies safety measures,” the representative said, requesting anonymity because of commercial sensitivity. The representative did not provide a timeline for when production could resume.

Bismuth, a brittle metal used in alloys, lead-free solders, low-melting-point devices and certain electronics applications, has a supply chain that is concentrated in China, where several smaller processors and refiners operate. Any disruption at a processing site can tighten availability for intermediate feedstock and increase short-term uncertainty for buyers, particularly for customers relying on steady inventories.

Market participants said they were tracking whether the halt would remain limited to a short window or expand to other facilities in the region. Some traders said they expected spot and contract terms to be reassessed if the inspection extended beyond a few days, while others noted that downstream demand for bismuth products is often managed through inventory buffers.

Reuters was unable to immediately reach the plant or local regulators for comment.

headline: Bolivia antimony output reduced after transport blockades disrupt ore flow
body: Bolivia reduced antimony output after transport blockades disrupted the flow of ore from mining regions to processing sites, according to industry sources and importers monitoring deliveries. The disruptions intensified in late February and continued into March, forcing several smelters and concentrating facilities to operate below planned capacity as trucks and rail links were repeatedly halted at checkpoints. One batch of shipments scheduled for early March was delayed by about 10–14 days, according to logistics trackers, while spot treatment charges increased as buyers sought alternative concentrates from neighboring producers. Output curtailments were most pronounced for material graded for high-grade refining, where feedstock shortages compounded slow trucking schedules and reduced stockpiles at plants. The interruptions also affected exports of antimony trioxide intermediates, with some consignments rerouted through longer corridors, extending lead times into mid-March. Traders said the near-term supply outlook remains tight into April if blockades persist, despite seasonal reductions in demand typically seen in the first half of the year.

headline: Norway germanium producer suspends shipments after counterparty breach investigation
body:
Norway’s germanium producer has suspended shipments after it received notice that one of its counterparties breached contractual terms, the company said, adding that it was investigating the issue.

The producer, which supplies germanium used in fibre optics, infrared optics and semiconductor applications, said it has paused deliveries while it reviews the circumstances behind the alleged breach and assesses potential impacts on existing customer orders.

In a statement, the company said it notified customers and counterparties of the suspension and is working to determine whether the breach involved delivery timing, product specifications, payment arrangements or documentation requirements. It did not name the counterparty or disclose the nature of the alleged breach.

The company said it expects the investigation to take time and has told counterparties that shipments will remain on hold until the review is completed or the dispute is resolved. It also indicated it will provide updates as it reaches conclusions about corrective actions, including whether affected shipments will be rescheduled.

Germanium remains a niche commodity and supply disruptions can quickly affect downstream procurement schedules, particularly for manufacturers that rely on assured allocations for optical and electronics production. Market participants typically monitor primary output, converter refiners and long-term supply agreements, where delivery performance and contract compliance are tightly managed.

The company did not comment on whether the suspension is likely to affect overall availability in the near term or whether it will seek alternative logistics and supply options to meet customer demand. It said operational activity is continuing, including production and inventory management, while contract and compliance teams assess the situation.

Contract disputes in the raw materials sector can involve a range of issues, including shipping milestones, quality verification procedures and payment conditions. In this case, the producer said the decision to suspend shipments was intended to protect contractual obligations and ensure that any deliveries made after the pause would meet agreed terms.

The company said it will continue its engagement with customers and counterparties and will release further information if the investigation identifies additional breaches or results in a resolution that allows shipments to resume.

headline: Turkey beryllium producer delays deliveries following licensing review extension
body: Turkey’s leading beryllium producer has delayed deliveries after regulators extended a licensing review, the company said, citing additional time needed to complete compliance checks. The extension, granted this week by the relevant authorities, pushes the expected resolution of the review window beyond mid-March 2026 and affects shipments scheduled for the second half of the month. Deliveries originally planned for March 12–25 are now expected to shift into late March and early April, depending on inspection sign-off and transport clearances. Industry sources indicated the pause could reduce monthly beryllium availability for downstream users that rely on steady inputs for machining and aerospace-related components. No changes were reported to contracted volumes, but lead times are being renegotiated to reflect the new timeline. The company has not provided a date for a final decision, while customers were told that production remains active but dispatches will wait for licensing confirmation.

headline: France zirconium producer halts kiln operation after refractory supply shortfall
body:
A French zirconium producer has temporarily halted kiln operations after a shortfall in refractory supplies needed for its processing lines, the company said on Tuesday.

The shutdown affects kiln use at its zirconium-related production facilities, where high-temperature equipment is lined with refractory materials to withstand heat, thermal cycling and chemical exposure during mineral processing. The producer said the interruption was triggered by delays and reduced availability of refractory products required for planned maintenance and continued operation, rather than by a disruption in feedstock supply.

In a brief statement, the company said it has stopped kiln operations “until critical refractory materials are secured” and installed, to avoid degrading equipment performance and to maintain product quality. It did not provide a precise restart date, but indicated that work is under way to assess replacement timelines with suppliers and to schedule recommissioning once materials arrive.

Zirconium and related intermediates are used in a range of industrial applications, including ceramics, foundry products and, in limited circumstances, materials for specialty chemical and nuclear supply chains. Production interruptions can tighten near-term availability, particularly for customers that rely on consistent kiln capacity for output, although the company did not disclose its inventory position or customer allocation plans.

The refractory shortfall reflects broader supply-chain pressures affecting high-performance materials used in industrial furnaces and kilns. These supplies are typically sourced from a limited number of producers and can be subject to lead times, capacity constraints and logistics delays, especially when equipment changes or maintenance cycles require specific grades. The company said its decision was made after it determined that proceeding without the required refractory supplies could compromise the stability of the kiln lining.

Asked about the expected duration of the stoppage, a spokesperson said: “We are working with our refractory suppliers to resolve the shortfall as quickly as possible, and kiln operations will resume once we have the materials needed to ensure safe and stable production.”

Analysts said that kiln halts, even when temporary, often come with downstream effects such as delayed shipments and adjustments to procurement plans. However, they typically focus on maintaining equipment integrity during the suspension to reduce the risk of longer outages once parts and refractory assemblies are installed. The company said it is monitoring the situation closely and will provide further updates if the restart timeline changes.

headline: Brazil raises royalties for iron ore amid contract renegotiations with miners
body: Brazil’s federal government has raised iron-ore royalty rates as it renegotiates contracts with mining companies, according to a resolution published on 2026-03-09. The change increases the government take tied to output and sales volumes for new and renegotiated concessions, with higher percentages applying across most price brackets for benchmark fines. The updated royalty schedule follows months of talks aimed at reducing revenue losses from earlier contract terms and aligning compensation with current market conditions. The government said the adjustments are effective for payments due from April 2026, covering extraction during the first quarter and recalculating portions of earlier royalty bills where terms have been restated. Companies operating in the Carajás and other major provinces are expected to face increased costs, particularly where 2025 production levels exceeded prior assumptions. Authorities framed the move as part of a broader compliance effort that includes tighter reporting requirements and new risk-sharing mechanisms for mine performance and environmental liabilities.

headline: Ukraine steelmaker declares force majeure on iron ore intake amid rail disruptions
body:
A Ukrainian steelmaker has declared force majeure on its iron ore intake, citing disrupted rail shipments that have affected deliveries from suppliers, the company said.

The move follows ongoing problems in rail transport that have slowed the arrival of raw materials at steelworks, according to the firm and industry contacts familiar with supply flows. The company did not specify the duration of the disruption or the volume of ore affected.

Force majeure is a contractual term used to excuse or suspend performance of obligations when extraordinary events outside a party’s control prevent fulfillment. In this case, the company said it is responding to interruptions in scheduled rail deliveries that have altered delivery windows and reduced the reliability of incoming shipments.

The declaration comes as Ukraine continues to face constraints in moving goods across its rail network, with capacity changes and damage linked to the war disrupting logistics. Rail remains a key mode for transporting bulk commodities such as iron ore and coal, and even short-term delays can affect blast furnace operations and inventory levels.

A representative for the steelmaker said the company is working to secure alternative logistics routes and adjust production planning while it monitors the situation. “We are taking all steps within our control to mitigate the impact of the rail disruptions on ore intake,” the representative said.

Analysts and traders said the practical effect is likely to shift the timing of deliveries and complicate contracts with suppliers and customers. When force majeure is invoked, counterparties often seek negotiated reschedules or alternative sourcing, depending on contract clauses and documentation requirements.

The rail disruptions also raise questions about the availability and pricing of iron ore in the near term, particularly for producers that rely on consistent inbound supply. Spot purchases and rerouted shipments can involve additional transport costs and lead times, while long-term contracts may be affected by revised delivery schedules.

The company said it will provide updates as conditions change, including when rail services stabilize and iron ore deliveries return to agreed terms. In the meantime, it is continuing operations using available inventories and pursuing other supply options to limit disruption to its production timetable.

headline: Iron ore prices jump as China ports restrict loading after safety inspections
body: Iron ore prices rose on 10 March after Chinese ports began restricting vessel loading following safety inspections, tightening near-term supply. Traders reported additional delays at major receiving locations along the northern coast and in the Bohai region, with some consignments held for inspection verification of unloading and vessel documentation. Freight availability narrowed over the last 48 hours, and spot offers for 62% Fe were marked up as buyers sought remaining cargo slots before further curbs. Several market participants said inspection-related slowdowns were concentrated around bulk-handling facilities, where throughput limits reduced daily arrivals for the first full week of March. The move comes after a volatile February in which steel output and import demand swings were already shifting balances. Analysts noted that even short interruptions can lift benchmark premiums in Asia, where inventories at ports are sensitive to scheduling changes and where rescheduling can take 3–7 days to clear.

headline: India begins probe into steel scrap imports as supply tightens for iron producers
body:
India has begun a probe into steel scrap imports after concerns that tightening global supply and shifting trade flows could affect the availability and pricing of feedstock used by iron and steel producers, according to government and industry sources.

The investigation, initiated by the Directorate General of Trade Remedies (DGTR), will examine whether imported steel scrap is being sold in India at unfairly low prices and whether any such imports have caused or are likely to cause material injury to domestic producers. The probe comes as iron ore processing and downstream steelmakers increasingly rely on scrap to balance raw material costs and maintain production schedules.

Officials said the inquiry will review import data, price trends and the impact on Indian mills, including those operating electric arc furnaces and other scrap-based routes. DGTR will also assess whether any dumping margins exist, and the role of market conditions, including freight and currency movements, in influencing import pricing. The investigation will run through a defined timeline set by the trade remedy framework, with evidence submissions from stakeholders.

India’s steel industry has faced pressure from a combination of tighter global scrap availability and higher logistics costs in recent months, sources familiar with the matter said. Some traders have also reported changes in procurement patterns by overseas suppliers, which has coincided with fluctuations in demand from major steelmaking hubs. Industry participants said domestic buyers have been attempting to secure volumes while maintaining feedstock quality and compliance requirements.

In written submissions reviewed by investigators, some domestic producers argued that imports have undercut local offers and pressured margins, while importers and users of scrap said the market is functioning normally and that domestic supply constraints should not be treated as injury. A trade body representing scrap processors said the probe should distinguish between temporary shortages and any deliberate pricing behavior by exporters.

DGTR is expected to issue questionnaires to companies and seek supporting documentation, including purchase and sales records, freight details and contracts for the period under review. The authorities may also conduct verification of information submitted by participants.

The investigation is likely to influence how Indian importers source scrap in the coming months, depending on whether DGTR finds evidence of dumping and injury and recommends compensatory measures. Any provisional actions, if considered, would be subject to further procedural steps and public interest assessments under India’s trade remedy rules.

headline: South Africa nickel producer curtails output after strike hits refinery maintenance
body: South Africa’s nickel producer reduced output after a strike disrupted refinery maintenance, according to people familiar with operations. The curtailment began on 9 March and affected planned work at the company’s main refining unit, where maintenance schedules were aligned with a March shutdown window. Production rates were cut by an undisclosed percentage over the week ending 15 March, with crews unable to complete critical tasks on units used for feed processing and residue handling. The stoppage followed labor walkouts that started at the refinery site and spread to contractor teams responsible for turnaround logistics. Inventory buffers are expected to narrow by mid-month, though shipments remain possible on existing contracts, subject to revised loading dates and quality checks. The disruption comes as regional nickel prices have been volatile, with traders closely watching physical availability through the first half of April.
