The company said repairs are being reprioritized and maintenance will resume once staffing coverage returns, without giving a timeline for full normalization.

headline: Ghana cobalt recycler halts production after expired permits delay hazardous handling
body:
A cobalt recycling firm in Ghana has halted production after delays in renewing expired permits that cover the handling of hazardous materials, according to company documents and local officials.

The company, which operates a recycling line for cobalt-bearing batteries in the country’s industrial belt, suspended operations this month after regulators flagged that several licences had lapsed. The permits govern storage, transportation and treatment of chemical waste produced during processing, including solutions containing metals and other regulated by-products.

Local authorities said the company submitted renewal applications but did not receive confirmation within the required timeframe. “We instructed the facility to stop processing because the authorisation for hazardous handling was no longer valid,” a municipal environmental officer said, adding that the firm could resume once it completes the remaining compliance steps and receives written approval.

Industry sources familiar with the matter said the suspension is expected to affect the supply of recycled cobalt that is used by refiners and battery material processors. Recycling operations typically rely on continuous input from scrap batteries and residues from upstream collection networks, meaning a halt can slow purchase schedules and deliveries for downstream customers.

The company said it has continued limited activities aimed at maintaining environmental controls, including monitoring waste storage areas and keeping treated residues within permitted conditions, according to a statement provided to officials. It also said staff were reallocated to maintenance work while it addresses permit requirements and submits additional paperwork tied to waste management procedures.

Regulators in Ghana have been tightening oversight of hazardous substances in recent years as global demand for battery minerals has expanded. Officials said the affected permits relate to the facility’s capacity to handle corrosive and toxic materials, as well as reporting obligations for waste quantities and disposal routes.

A timeline for resuming full production was not immediately available. Officials said the renewal review is ongoing and that the facility’s restart would depend on inspection results and receipt of final authorisations, including updates to documentation submitted during the pause.

headline: Philippines copper smelter forced to cut rates due to water shortage
body: A water shortage has forced a Philippines copper smelter to cut production rates, according to industry sources, as utilities tightened supply to industrial users in recent weeks. The plant, which processes copper concentrate into refined output, reduced operating schedules after local reservoir levels fell below contracted thresholds, affecting process water and cooling demand. The curtailment began in early March 2026 and accelerated over the past 10 days as authorities prioritized municipal supply. Output schedules were cut by an estimated double-digit percentage range, with smelting runs reportedly shortened and stockpiles used to bridge gaps in throughput. Power use remained steady, but operators adjusted water-intensive steps, including slag cooling and solution circulation, to limit drawdown. The disruption is expected to affect concentrate treatment rates and could ripple into refined copper deliveries over the next 2 to 4 weeks, depending on whether water restrictions are eased.

headline: China palladium refiner halts processing during labor dispute with contractors
body:
A Chinese palladium refiner has stopped processing metal at one of its facilities amid a labor dispute with contractors, according to documents and people familiar with the matter, underscoring continued disruption in the country’s precious-metals supply chain.

The shutdown began after workers and contractor groups stopped operations at the plant and refused to resume tasks related to refining and downstream handling, the people said. The company has not issued a public statement describing the situation, but internal notices circulating among staff cited a disagreement over payments and working arrangements, they added.

Palladium is used in catalysts for vehicle exhaust systems and in industrial chemical and electronics applications. China is one of the largest processors of the metal, helping convert imported or recovered materials into refined output that is then sold to downstream buyers. The halt is expected to reduce near-term supply and could affect product availability for refiners and traders that rely on regular production schedules, the people said.

One supplier that typically receives semi-processed material said the company informed customers that delivery timelines could shift while the dispute is resolved. “The processing line is currently offline, and we are waiting for an update on when normal operations will restart,” the supplier said, speaking on condition of anonymity because it was not authorized to comment publicly.

Disruptions in refining are typically difficult to absorb quickly because processing steps require continuity, including segregation of inputs, chemical treatment, and quality checks before output can be released. If the stoppage extends, some inventory may be carried over to subsequent batches, but contractors involved in specific tasks may demand revised terms before resuming work, people familiar with contractor operations said.

The dispute also comes as companies in metals and materials have faced heightened scrutiny over labor practices and contractor management in recent years, with authorities in some regions increasing inspections. Additional complications can arise when contractor groups seek mediation through local labor bureaus, which can prolong downtime.

The people familiar with the matter said negotiations between the refiner and contractors were ongoing and that officials from local authorities had been asked to assist. They did not provide a timetable for resolution. The refinery’s output for the current month is expected to be significantly lower than planned if the stoppage continues.

headline: Kazakhstan tungsten mine suspends operations after equipment failure and repair delay
body: A tungsten mine in Kazakhstan suspended operations after a piece of processing equipment failed and repairs were delayed, according to company statements filed this week. The site, located in the country’s central mining region, halted production late on March 7, citing the need to replace or refurbish a key unit used for ore processing and concentration. The company said technicians identified the malfunction during a routine run and that spare parts and maintenance work were expected to extend the restart window beyond previously planned timelines. Operations remained paused through March 10 while contractors completed diagnostics and ordered components. The suspension comes as global tungsten demand remains sensitive to supply disruptions, with pricing and contract deliveries typically tied to month-end production schedules. No estimate was provided for lost annual output, but the company indicated the delay could affect March shipments and subsequent loading plans depending on test results after restart.

headline: Mexico sets temporary cap on molybdenum exports to secure domestic supply
body:
Mexico has imposed a temporary cap on exports of molybdenum to ensure adequate supplies for domestic users, according to a government notice published this week.

The measure limits shipments of the metal outside the country while authorities assess near-term availability, the notice said. It applies to molybdenum and certain molybdenum-containing products covered by specific customs classifications, and it sets export quotas for a defined period rather than a permanent ban.

Molybdenum is used in steelmaking to improve strength and corrosion resistance and is also used in chemicals and industrial components. The government action follows ongoing efforts to manage supply conditions for critical industrial inputs, particularly where production and processing capacity can affect downstream manufacturers.

The export cap is intended to “secure domestic supply” and to reduce the risk of shortages for local producers and processors, the notice said. It directs relevant authorities to monitor inventory levels and export flows during the temporary restriction and to adjust controls if supply conditions change.

Under the notice, exporters are required to comply with quota allocations and provide documentation of destination and shipment details. The government said it will coordinate with customs and industry stakeholders to verify compliance and prevent circumvention through transshipment or misclassification of goods.

The temporary framework is designed to maintain continuity for domestic demand while the government reviews supply outlooks. Officials did not provide an immediate statement on the size of the quotas or the exact duration of the restriction in the notice summary released with the regulation.

Industry participants typically watch export controls closely because molybdenum prices and availability can shift quickly in global markets, where demand is closely tied to steel production cycles. The Mexican measure is expected to affect export volumes from the country during the quota period, depending on how quickly producers can reallocate material to domestic contracts.

The regulation takes effect in accordance with the publication schedule stated in the notice. Further guidance on implementation and quota administration may be issued by the responsible ministry, the document said.

headline: China imposes export controls on graphite to address strategic stockpile needs
body: China has imposed new export controls on graphite, starting 2026-04-01, as part of efforts to secure supply for strategic stockpiles and downstream manufacturing needs, according to measures published by Chinese authorities. The rules require exporters to apply for permits for specific forms and grades of natural and synthetic graphite, including materials used in high-temperature furnaces and for battery anodes, with initial review timelines of 7 to 15 working days. Thresholds set in the notice focus on exports exceeding defined tonnage bands per destination, and shipments without approved quotas are subject to seizure and fines under existing customs regulations. The government said the move targets “stabilization of domestic reserves,” citing ongoing demand linked to industrial heating, nuclear-related applications, and electric-vehicle value chains. Market participants expect tighter availability for non-Chinese buyers during the April–June quarter, with potential contract renegotiations where delivery schedules extend beyond 2026-06-30. The controls follow broader Chinese export-management steps implemented in 2024 and 2025 across several critical mineral categories.

headline: Portugal antimony producer pauses shipments after customs hold over documentation
body:
Portugal’s only antimony producer has paused shipments after Portuguese customs placed a hold on outgoing consignments over documentation issues, the company said on Wednesday, adding that it is working with authorities to clear the paperwork.

The producer, whose operations are centred in the Iberian country, said it has stopped exports of antimony concentrate while customs checks are under way. A company representative said the hold relates to “missing or non-conforming documentation” for a set of shipments, without specifying which forms were affected or naming the recipient countries.

Antimony is a strategic metal used in flame retardants, batteries and ammunition, and it is also important for defence and industrial supply chains. Portugal has attracted attention in recent years as European efforts seek alternatives to concentrated supply from a small number of countries.

Customs authorities initiated the hold at the point of exit, according to the producer. The company said it is compiling the requested documents and providing additional information to support the shipments’ classification and movement under customs rules. It did not provide a timeline for when shipments would resume, saying the next steps depend on customs’ review and approval.

A person familiar with the process said customs holds can delay freight for days or weeks, depending on whether paperwork can be corrected promptly and whether inspectors require additional checks. The same person noted that documentation-related delays are common in commodity logistics, especially when shipments involve cross-border transit or updated regulatory requirements.

In the meantime, the pause may affect near-term delivery schedules and could shift volumes to later cargo windows if exports are cleared. The producer said it continues to operate domestically, but concentrates intended for export are being held at storage facilities pending customs release.

Antimony prices have been volatile globally in recent months, reflecting disruptions in upstream output and shifting demand from industrial and defence-linked procurement. Any interruption in shipments from Portugal, even for a short period, could contribute to tightness in available material for buyers seeking contracted volumes.

The company said it is in contact with customs and shipping partners to minimise further delays and that, once documentation is accepted, it will seek to restore shipment schedules as quickly as possible.

headline: Chile declares force majeure at Escondida copper mine after flooding
body: Chile has declared force majeure at the Escondida copper mine after flooding disrupted operations in recent days, the government said on Wednesday. The declaration covers activities at the world’s largest copper producer and is intended to address delays tied to damage and safety risks from water ingress, officials said. Escondida, operated by BHP with minority partners, reported that rainfall and runoff led to flooding at processing and related facilities, forcing temporary suspension of parts of the production line. While the company has been conducting inspections and repairs, output has been constrained as pumps and drainage systems were brought online and damaged equipment assessed. The notice, dated March 11, sets a framework for contract relief across supply obligations during the interruption period and for any downstream effects on shipments. Chile’s mining authorities said they will monitor recovery progress and provide updates as dewatering stabilizes site conditions and production resumes.

headline: Indonesia halts nickel ore exports for 30 days amid port congestion
body:
Indonesia has halted exports of nickel ore for 30 days starting this week, citing congestion at loading ports and delays in shipping capacity, according to a government notice seen by industry sources.

The export ban, covering shipments of nickel ore and related materials, follows a recent buildup of vessels waiting to load in a number of export terminals, particularly in Sulawesi and parts of the Maluku islands. The measure is intended to reduce port queues and allow operators to clear stockpiles ahead of the next export cycle, officials said in the notice issued to relevant agencies and export stakeholders.

Under the temporary restriction, exporters are required to suspend outbound shipments until the end of the 30-day period, after which authorities will review port conditions and logistical readiness. The government said it would coordinate with port authorities and mining companies to manage inventory movements and loading schedules, while continuing monitoring of compliance.

Several companies have reported tighter shipping turnarounds in recent weeks as demand for bulk vessels increased and loading rates slowed, partly due to weather disruptions and staffing constraints. Industry players said the congestion has led to longer dwell times for ore cargoes at terminals and higher demurrage costs for shipowners and charterers.

“This is to address congestion at ports and ensure smoother export operations,” one official from the coordinating ministry said, without giving further details on the number of affected cargoes or vessel queues.

Indonesia is the world’s largest producer of nickel ore and a key supplier to the stainless steel and battery supply chains. It has frequently adjusted export rules as it pushes the expansion of domestic nickel processing, including the production of ferronickel and nickel pig iron, while seeking to manage environmental and regulatory concerns.

The 30-day pause is likely to affect near-term market flows and may shift some cargoes to alternative routes or delay offtake deliveries for customers, traders said. Any longer disruption could also influence prices for nickel ore benchmarks, though market impacts depend on whether stock coverage and inventories elsewhere can absorb the shortfall during the suspension.

The government did not immediately specify exemptions or categories of cargo that would continue to move during the ban period, but said it would publish further guidance to clarify implementation.

headline: Congo sends cobalt shipments to customs after a suspension of barge permits
body: The Democratic Republic of Congo notified customs authorities it has resumed cobalt shipments after a suspension of barge permits, according to documents seen by industry contacts on March 11. The move follows a brief halt to inland transport that reduced outbound deliveries along the country’s river routes in the first half of the week, when permit controls were tightened. Receipts filed with customs show multiple lots cleared for onward transport, with shipments dispatched from central loading points to regional transfer hubs before final export documentation is completed. Traders said the process is designed to preserve storage and demurrage windows while verifying quantities and grades for each consignment. Several operators reported that barge schedules had been suspended for 24 to 48 hours before authorities instructed compliance checks and permit renewals. Market participants expect the cleared lots to re-enter the physical market over the next 1 to 2 weeks, depending on port availability and paperwork backlogs. The incident is the latest disruption to Congolese cobalt logistics as regulators continue to tighten transport documentation for bulk minerals.

headline: Zambia declares state of emergency at copper processing hub after power cuts
body:
Zambia has declared a state of emergency in its main copper processing and industrial area after prolonged power cuts disrupted operations, the government said on Wednesday.

The declaration covers parts of the Copperbelt Province, where large smelters, refineries and industrial power users operate, and follows what authorities described as “sustained load shedding” that affected production and supply of essential services. The government said the measure is aimed at restoring stable electricity distribution and ensuring continuity of operations at facilities that depend on uninterrupted power.

In a statement, the Ministry of Information said the emergency will allow authorities to coordinate faster deployment of power-related resources, expedite repairs to infrastructure and strengthen monitoring of electricity demand and supply. It did not provide details on how long the state of emergency would last, nor did it specify which companies were affected most directly.

Power cuts have become a recurring concern for Zambia’s copper sector, which relies on electricity for refining, smelting and associated processing. Several industrial firms have previously reported that interruptions can lead to equipment shutdowns, higher costs and delays in producing copper cathodes and other refined products. Zambia produces a significant share of the world’s copper, and many of its processing facilities are concentrated in the Copperbelt.

The energy ministry said it was working with the power utility to restore reliability while also reviewing system performance across the grid. Officials cited increased demand and aging infrastructure, along with generation shortfalls during peak periods, as contributing factors to the outages.

“This is to bring sanity and stability to the power system in the Copperbelt so that critical industries are not unduly affected,” an official from the Office of the Vice-President was quoted as saying. The official added that teams were being deployed to identify faults and prevent further disruptions, and that authorities would continue assessing supply conditions over the coming days.

Zambia has faced intermittent electricity shortages linked to variability in hydropower generation and constraints in other parts of its energy supply chain. The government has also been pursuing new generation projects and transmission upgrades to reduce reliance on hydropower, while regulators have sometimes implemented demand management measures during periods of low supply.

Residents in affected areas have reported reduced industrial activity during outages, with businesses scaling back shifts and transport and service operations encountering disruptions. The state of emergency is expected to support tighter coordination between provincial authorities, utilities and industrial operators as repairs and monitoring efforts proceed.

headline: Tungsten prices jump on Vietnam licensing row for key concentrates
body: Tungsten prices rose sharply in early Asia trading after a licensing dispute involving Vietnam disrupted supplies of key tungsten concentrates. Market participants said the escalation centers on permits for concentrates processed or stored in Vietnam, where some shipments require new documentation tied to import and downstream processing approvals. The price impact was most visible in benchmark market quotations, which climbed between 3% and 5% on March 10–11, following days of thin liquidity for high-grade material. Several traders attributed the move to expectations that delayed clearances could push delivery windows by 2–6 weeks, particularly for concentrates used in ammonium paratungstate conversion. Spot premiums for concentrates were also reported to widen as buyers sought alternatives from China and Central Asia. Analysts noted that the timing coincides with the first quarter production schedule, when term-contract inventory tends to tighten, leaving few immediate substitutes. The dispute remains under review, but the immediate effect is volatility in concentrate availability rather than changes to finished tungsten metal demand.

headline: South Africa shuts platinum line briefly after safety regulator stop-work order
body:
South Africa briefly halted operations on a platinum line after the country’s safety regulator issued a stop-work order, according to mine operators and industry sources.

The shutdown affected processing activities on a platinum production line, where personnel were required to stand down while the regulator assessed safety compliance, the sources said. Work was stopped for a short period before operations were allowed to resume, they added, following immediate remedial actions at the site.

The Department of Employment and Labour, which oversees workplace safety, issued the stop-work order after inspectors identified alleged breaches of health and safety requirements, according to the regulator’s notice. The order required management to halt affected operations and to demonstrate that risk controls were restored to the required standard before work could continue.

A company representative said the pause was limited and that the business complied with the order. “We took immediate steps to address the concerns raised by the inspectorate and provided the required assurances to resume work. No serious injuries were reported,” the representative said.

Stop-work orders are issued under South Africa’s Occupational Health and Safety framework and are meant to prevent workers from being exposed to hazards while corrective measures are undertaken. Inspectors typically cite specific failures, ranging from ventilation and risk assessments to equipment guarding and operating procedures, depending on what they find at each facility.

Platinum group metals producers operate in a heavily regulated environment, with regulators and unions frequently scrutinizing workplace safety, contractor management and maintenance practices. Industry observers have said enforcement has increased in recent years, including more frequent inspections tied to incidents in mining and refining operations.

The brief interruption comes as South Africa remains one of the world’s most important suppliers of platinum. Any stoppages, even short ones, can disrupt scheduled production runs and concentrate logistics planning around compliance reviews and equipment inspections.

Authorities said they would continue monitoring the site until inspectors were satisfied that the required controls were implemented and sustained. The company did not provide further details on the specific issues cited by the regulator, but said it would cooperate with follow-up inspections.

headline: Russia delays palladium exports as rail strikes disrupt deliveries to ports
body: Russia has delayed some palladium exports after rail strikes disrupted deliveries from inland suppliers to key Black Sea and Baltic ports, according to market participants citing shipping and booking data. Work stoppages starting in late February and extending into early March reduced rail capacity and lengthened yard dwell times, leaving producers unable to move metal for loading schedules in the first half of March. One effect was postponement of deliveries tied to April contracts, with rerouted cargoes expected to shift by 2 to 4 weeks depending on port congestion and wagon availability. Exporters reportedly sought alternative haulage windows, but rolling stock shortages and increased inspection backlogs at terminals slowed dispatches to regular weekly sailings. The disruption comes as global inventories tighten around spot flows, where prompt premiums have been sensitive to day-to-day availability of Russian material. Traders said the immediate impact is concentrated in parcels staged for rail-to-port transfer rather than furnace output, but the timing of shipments is expected to remain uncertain through mid-March.

headline: Peru suspends tin loading at Callao following customs document backlog
body:
Peru has suspended tin loading operations at the main port of Callao after accumulating customs documents, according to shipping and trade sources.

The measure, which took effect over the past day, halted shipments of tin from loading berths while Peruvian customs processes catch up, the sources said. They cited a backlog of export and clearance paperwork that delayed release of goods for inspection and final approval, according to two parties familiar with port operations.

Local industry participants said the suspension affects scheduled cargo movements rather than a ban on tin exports. “The issue is administrative processing at customs, not the product,” one trade representative said, requesting anonymity because negotiations with port and customs officials were ongoing. The representative added that carriers were being advised to hold bookings or reroute within a limited window while documentation is verified.

Callao is Peru’s primary gateway for minerals and other commodities, and tin shipments are typically handled through bulk and container arrangements depending on the seller and buyer. Peru has been one of the world’s notable tin producers, and trading activity can be sensitive to port disruptions that delay delivery to smelters and traders.

Customs officials did not immediately respond to requests for comment. However, the sources said the backlog has built up across multiple product lines, prompting customs to prioritize verifications and issue delayed clearances in batches. As a result, loadings tied to tin cargoes were paused to prevent further congestion at the port and to ensure that paperwork matches manifests and inspection outcomes.

The suspension could affect near-term supply for buyers awaiting deliveries, though the extent depends on whether shipments are held at the port or rerouted to nearby ports or alternative logistics routes. Shipping agents said discussions were under way with customs and port authorities to resume loading once the remaining documents are cleared and cargos receive release orders.

Traders will be watching for updates on whether the measure is limited to specific lots currently awaiting documentation or expands to additional tin consignments at Callao. If the backlog is cleared quickly, normal loading could resume within days, the sources said, while longer delays would likely cascade into rescheduling of vessel calls and contract delivery timelines.

headline: China imposes temporary anti-dumping probe on graphite anode imports
body: China has launched a temporary anti-dumping investigation on graphite anode imports, targeting shipments used in lithium-ion battery production, according to a notice issued in March 2026. The probe covers imports from unspecified countries and is set to run while authorities assess whether dumped pricing is harming domestic producers. The anti-dumping measures are described as temporary, with the investigation intended to reach a final determination after further cost and sales data reviews. Companies involved in the supply chain are required to submit detailed information within set deadlines that start from the publication date of the notice. China’s finance ministry and commerce ministry conduct the reviews using transaction records, normal-value calculations and injury assessments tied to product categories of graphite anodes. The action follows increased scrutiny of battery materials supply and pricing dynamics in global trading, as Chinese demand for anodes continues to expand for both grid storage and electric vehicles.

headline: Norway halts zirconium deliveries after quality recall at refining plant
body:
Norway has halted zirconium deliveries after a quality recall at a refining plant, according to industry sources and company statements reviewed by traders.

The stoppage affects shipments of zirconium produced at the facility following issues identified during internal checks and subsequent recall procedures, the sources said. Several customers have been notified to suspend scheduled receipts while the material is re-evaluated and retested to confirm it meets agreed specifications, they added.

A recall at the plant was triggered after irregularities were detected in product sampling and inspection results, prompting a broader review of production lots. Company representatives said the matter was limited to certain batches and that affected material is being quarantined pending verification, according to a notice sent to customers.

Zirconium is used widely in nuclear fuel applications, chemical processing and coatings, and is traded under tight quality regimes. Any disruption to supply can quickly affect contract timing, especially for processors and utilities that rely on steady feedstock availability for procurement cycles.

Traders said the delivery halt is likely to run until the plant completes retesting and releases clearance for the affected inventory. “The recall is being handled through lot-by-lot checks, so timelines depend on how quickly the results come back,” one procurement manager at a European materials supplier said. The manager was not authorized to speak publicly and asked not to be identified.

Norway’s industry and trade bodies did not immediately provide detailed figures on the volume tied to the stoppage. However, market participants said the country remains an important source of refined zirconium components in parts of Europe, and the interruption could shift buying toward alternative suppliers or existing stockpiles.

Companies seeking to rebook deliveries may also face administrative delays as documentation is updated and certificates are reissued for cleared lots. Several customers are reportedly working with their contractors to determine whether any substitution of material is acceptable under current quality agreements.

Regulators in the supply chain are being kept informed as required, sources said, though the scope of any wider compliance review had not been confirmed by the plant as of Thursday. Additional updates are expected once the company completes its recall testing and restarts shipments from the cleared inventory.

headline: Kazakhstan issues export quota for vanadium ferroalloy citing tight supply
body: Kazakhstan has introduced an export quota regime for vanadium ferroalloy, according to a government notice issued March 11, 2026, citing tight upstream supply and the need to stabilize domestic availability. The quota applies to exports processed in Kazakhstan and covers deliveries during the April 1 to September 30 window, with specific shipment limits set by license requirements administered through the export control system. Authorities said production constraints from primary vanadium inputs have tightened market availability, raising lead times for major grades used in steelmaking and specialty alloys. The measure sets a reduced allowable tonnage for the first half of the period, with additional allocations contingent on reported inventory levels at smelters and traders. Exporters are required to submit monthly production and stock statements, and noncompliance may result in license suspension or reallocation of unused quota. The policy is expected to affect contracts signed for April-June shipments and may shift spot pricing as buyers seek alternative supply routes.

headline: Canada declares force majeure at molybdenum mine after tailings incident
body:
Canada has declared force majeure at the Mont-Wright molybdenum mine following a tailings incident that disrupted operations, according to notices issued by the site’s operator and energy regulators.

The declaration, effective immediately, signals that the company may be unable to meet contractual obligations because of circumstances beyond its control, including remediation work and uncertainty over how quickly affected systems can return to normal. The mine, in Quebec’s Nord-du-Québec region, produces molybdenum concentrates used in steelmaking and other industrial applications.

The operator said the declaration followed an incident involving the mine’s tailings management system. Officials did not provide detailed technical findings in the public notices, but they said the company has implemented containment measures and is working to stabilize the facility. The mine’s output has been constrained while assessments are underway, with personnel focused on water management, monitoring and repairs.

Quebec’s environment ministry and other regulators have been notified, and the company said it is cooperating with inspections related to tailings integrity, water quality and equipment performance. A review is expected to determine the cause of the incident and whether additional modifications are required to prevent recurrence. The operator said it is also preparing updated risk controls and revised operating procedures.

“The force majeure declaration reflects the inability to reliably continue normal operations and supply schedules while we address the incident and complete required monitoring,” a company spokesperson said in an email statement.

Force majeure declarations are often used in mining and infrastructure when disruptions prevent delivery of commodities, supplies or services. Under typical contracts, the clause can excuse penalties or delays, though counterparties may seek mitigation plans, updated timelines and evidence that the disruption remains outside the company’s control.

The incident comes as companies in the sector face heightened scrutiny of tailings facilities worldwide, following stricter safety expectations and the growing use of independent reviews. The operator did not specify how long the declaration could remain in effect, but said the timetable would depend on regulator feedback and the results of ongoing assessments at the site.

Market participants have been watching Canadian molybdenum output for supply signals, though the size and duration of the disruption will depend on how quickly the mine can ramp back up after stabilization work. Further updates are expected as regulators publish findings and the operator submits a remediation plan.

headline: DRC mining union calls strike affecting cobalt and nickel concentrate trucking
body: A mining union in the Democratic Republic of Congo has called for a strike affecting trucking of cobalt and nickel concentrate from southern and central processing sites, disrupting shipments scheduled from 13 March. The work stoppage, announced for the next 48 to 72 hours, is expected to constrain daily haulage volumes used to feed copper-cobalt refineries and concentrate export corridors. Logistics operators said departures from loading zones toward river ports and dry-cargo depots will be reduced to spot availability, with some convoys delayed by 1 to 3 days as drivers and security personnel are pulled from routes. Concentrate movements for cobalt are most at risk, followed by nickel-bearing shipments that rely on the same trucking networks and weigh-in requirements. Several exporters have reported halted loading windows tied to limited truck bookings and compliance checks, potentially affecting March allocations already set for delivery into global trading hubs. While no estimate of lost output was immediately published, market participants said the disruption could tighten near-term supply of cobalt and nickel concentrates during the week of 16 March.

headline: Argentina restricts lithium brine procurement permits, tightening feed for processing
body:
Argentina has tightened requirements for permits used to procure lithium brine, restricting access for companies that supply domestic processing plants, according to government documents and industry participants.

The change limits how firms obtain procurement authorizations for brine from the country’s salt flats, a key input for carbonate and hydroxide producers. Under the updated rules, applications are subject to additional checks on project scope, contracted volumes and compliance plans, according to a copy of the permit guidance reviewed by traders and submitted to operators in the lithium provinces.

The measures are expected to reduce near-term availability of brine for processing, particularly for operators that rely on spot or newly contracted deliveries rather than existing long-term supply arrangements. Companies that already hold valid permits will generally be able to continue operations, participants said, though they may face revised reporting obligations and tighter timelines for bringing additional extraction volumes online.

Authorities have also increased scrutiny of logistics and water management plans tied to extraction, reflecting broader compliance concerns across Argentina’s Andean lithium districts. The procurement authorization process now requires more detailed documentation of how companies will manage brine volumes during extraction and transport, as well as proof of downstream processing capacity or contracted offtake, sources said.

One industry source said the updated permit framework was aimed at ensuring that brine procurement better matches processing demand and environmental requirements. “The adjustment is narrowing the flow of permits and, in practice, it tightens the feed for who can bring brine forward for processing,” the source said, speaking on condition of anonymity because the person was not authorized to discuss internal administrative changes.

Argentina’s lithium production has been central to global supply chains used in batteries for electric vehicles and energy storage. Most of the country’s commercially significant projects are located in the provinces of Jujuy, Salta and Catamarca, where brine extraction is typically linked to chemical processing facilities that convert raw brine into battery-grade products.

The new restrictions may influence procurement strategies among firms competing for supply and could affect contract negotiations ahead of expected expansion phases. Industry participants said they are monitoring how the government will implement the rules, including the duration of review periods and whether authorities will grant limited interim authorizations for contracted deliveries while applications are reviewed.

headline: Japan puts uranium fuel shipments on hold pending regulator review
body: Japan has paused uranium fuel shipments from overseas suppliers pending a fresh review by its nuclear regulators, according to officials familiar with the matter. The hold, initiated in early March 2026, applies to deliveries tied to reactor fuel fabrication and associated transport, with affected lots scheduled for ports between March 15 and April 30. The review is intended to check compliance with current technical and licensing requirements, including documentation tied to enrichment history, chain-of-custody records, and transport safeguards. Several consignments that were expected to arrive under existing contracts have been placed in temporary storage, extending lead times by weeks rather than days. Japan’s Nuclear Regulation Authority is conducting the assessment alongside relevant export-control and safety agencies, with a decision expected in late April. Industry sources said the pause could tighten spot availability for utilities already managing burnup schedules for 2026 refuelling campaigns.

headline: Namibia stops selenium shipments after mislabeled batches trigger inspection
body:
Namibia has suspended shipments of selenium after mislabeled batches prompted an industry inspection, the government said, as regulators worked to identify affected consignments and prevent further distribution.

The Ministry of Industrialisation and Trade said the decision followed findings from a routine verification and subsequent checks at points of export. Inspectors detected discrepancies in labeling documentation for certain selenium lots, triggering a broader review of batch identification, origin statements and handling records supplied by exporters.

In a statement, the ministry said the suspension applies to “affected selenium shipments” and will remain in place until corrective actions are completed and shipments can be re-screened for compliance. It did not specify the number of batches involved or the total volume of selenium suspended, nor did it name the companies under review.

Selenium, a byproduct associated with copper, nickel and other industrial metals, is used in glassmaking, electronics and photovoltaic applications. Namibia’s mining sector contributes to global supplies of several strategic materials, and exports typically follow strict documentation requirements for quality and traceability, particularly when shipments are destined for manufacturing and chemical processing.

The ministry said it is coordinating with relevant agencies and trading partners to reconcile shipment records and assess whether mislabeled batches could have implications for quality assurance, customs clearance or end-use regulatory requirements in importing jurisdictions. It added that inspectors are reviewing chain-of-custody information, including storage conditions and batch tracking documentation from the production and packing stages.

An official said the immediate focus is on traceability and verification before any cargo is released. “We are ensuring that only selenium that has been properly identified and cleared through inspection can proceed,” the ministry said.

Regulators also instructed exporters to pause dispatches tied to the flagged lots and to provide supporting batch records for auditing. Authorities said enforcement could continue beyond the initial suspension if further irregularities are discovered during follow-up checks.

Namibia did not provide a timeline for the resumption of shipments, saying the pace of reinstatement depends on the outcome of laboratory and document verification processes. The incident comes as governments in several exporting countries have tightened controls on industrial inputs and raw materials used in sensitive manufacturing supply chains.

headline: Bolivia cancels antimony export licenses for failing compliance checks
body: Bolivia has canceled antimony export licenses after officials found multiple compliance failures in shipments reviewed during March, according to a government notice dated 2026-03-12. The measures affect licenses issued for deliveries scheduled from March through June 2026, with authorities citing irregular documentation and gaps in traceability related to origin and processing. The review followed earlier audits conducted in January and February that flagged discrepancies between declared output and customs records at participating logistics and trading entities. Exporters whose licenses were revoked are required to resubmit documentation, including updated production registers, chain-of-custody records, and inspection certificates, or face continued suspension. The government said the enforcement aims to strengthen alignment with international trade requirements and reduce risks tied to underreported volumes and inconsistent assay results. Industry contacts estimated the canceled permissions cover several thousand metric tons of antimony-equivalent material, though officials did not immediately publish an aggregate figure.

headline: Germany requires additional permits for germanium shipments after new controls
body:
Germany has introduced additional permitting requirements for shipments of germanium after new government controls took effect, according to officials and trade documentation seen by industry participants.

The Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action said the updated rules are linked to tightened screening of strategic raw materials and will apply to cross-border transfers of germanium, a critical input used in optics, fiber communications and semiconductor applications, as well as in some defense-related technologies. The ministry said the changes are aimed at improving compliance with existing export control frameworks.

Under the new regime, companies shipping germanium into or out of Germany may need additional approvals depending on the destination, end use and end user. Authorities will assess whether shipments could be diverted to restricted applications or parties, according to the permit conditions described in internal guidance referenced by market sources. Existing authorisations may not automatically cover new shipments if they fall under newly defined control lists or reporting triggers.

Industry players said the new paperwork could slow logistics for some consignments, particularly for firms that rely on just-in-time deliveries of germanium intermediates and compounds. Several exporters and traders also said they were reviewing their documentation to determine whether previous classifications remain valid under the revised measures, including how quantities and product descriptions are recorded.

A spokesperson for the economics ministry said the rules strengthen transparency and ensure that “shipments of strategically relevant raw materials can be assessed comprehensively before they leave or enter the country.” The ministry did not provide figures on the number of permits expected or the scope of the new control list.

German controls follow similar tightening elsewhere in Europe and growing attention to supply chains for rare and strategic materials. Germanium is considered important for maintaining capabilities in telecommunications and high-performance optical systems, and for producing components where alternative materials may not fully substitute.

Companies affected by the measures are expected to apply for the relevant permits through the federal export control authorities, with review timelines varying by case. Traders said they were in contact with customers to clarify end-use statements and contractual details that could be required for approvals.

headline: China halts gallium exports for 2 weeks due to customs system outage
body: China has halted gallium exports for about 2 weeks after a customs system outage disrupted clearance procedures, according to market participants monitoring trade flows on 2026-03-13. The stoppage began late March 12, with shipments requiring export declarations placed on hold while customs IT services were restored. The interruption is expected to last roughly 10 to 14 days, spanning the first half of the week for processors and electronics makers relying on deliveries for both supply chain planning and contract settlement. Gallium is used in semiconductors, LEDs, and high-frequency components, and tighter availability can affect spot pricing and offtake negotiations with immediate effect. Traders said the pause may also delay processing of existing paperwork for cargoes already at ports or in bonded zones, increasing the risk of rescheduling for buyers in Europe and Asia. Market liquidity for gallium derivatives and primary material was reported thin as sellers sought confirmation of renewed clearance times.

headline: Kazakhstan issues temporary ban on beryllium scrap exports amid audits
body:
Kazakhstan has imposed a temporary ban on exports of beryllium scrap while it conducts audits of companies involved in the metal’s supply chain, the government said on Thursday.

The measure covers beryllium scrap leaving the country for foreign destinations and is intended to ensure compliance with export rules and licensing requirements, officials said. The ban is temporary and comes as authorities review records tied to the collection, processing and sale of the material.

Kazakhstan is one of the world’s notable producers of beryllium compounds used in aerospace components, electronics and other industrial applications, though the country’s scrap flows have been closely monitored because of the metal’s regulatory status and the risks associated with improper handling. The government said the audits are focused on verifying the origin of shipments, documentation and the eligibility of exporters.

A statement from the competent authority said the suspension applies immediately and is expected to remain in place until checks are completed and companies demonstrate that their operations meet the required standards. Export permits issued before the ban may be reviewed, the statement added, without specifying whether existing contracts could proceed.

Companies affected by the ban are required to provide documentation, including records of production volumes, waste and scrap collection procedures, and details of downstream buyers, officials said. The audits also aim to identify discrepancies in declared quantities and to confirm that shipments are not routed through intermediary traders that do not hold the proper authorisations.

The ban reflects heightened scrutiny of critical materials as governments seek to tighten oversight of export channels and improve traceability. Kazakhstan has previously said it wants to prevent irregularities in the export of industrial and strategic raw materials and to ensure that trade complies with national regulations.

The authorities did not provide a specific end date for the temporary restrictions. Further updates will be issued after the results of the audits are reviewed, the statement said.

headline: India suspends indium recovery at zinc smelter after furnace failure
body: India has suspended indium recovery at a zinc smelter in the wake of a furnace failure, according to people familiar with operations, halting a process that typically captures small quantities of the metal from zinc residues. The outage began on 2026-03-11 after the furnace malfunctioned, and recovery activities were paused the following day while the unit undergoes inspection and repairs. Indium output from the site is expected to be delayed by at least several weeks, with restart contingent on furnace performance tests and stable feed throughput. The smelter has been working at reduced rates since early March, but the failure is projected to cut near-term recovery volumes by an amount not yet specified. The company is also reviewing procedures for feed preparation and heat treatment to prevent recurrence. Market participants said the suspension could tighten availability of indium in coming weeks, even as downstream demand for ITO and electronics-linked applications remains steady.

headline: United States imposes tariffs review affecting cadmium imports from South Korea
body:
The United States has initiated a review of tariffs covering cadmium imports from South Korea, according to notices posted by U.S. agencies and trade filings seen by industry participants.

The action is tied to a tariff review process that examines whether certain imported metals are being sold at unfair prices and whether imports are harming or threatening to harm domestic producers, people familiar with the matter said. The review is expected to determine whether duties should be adjusted or extended for cadmium entering the U.S. from South Korea.

Cadmium is used in a range of industrial applications, including rechargeable batteries, pigments, and semiconductor-related processes. U.S. demand is closely monitored by manufacturers and suppliers because cadmium procurement can be constrained by environmental regulations governing handling and disposal, as well as by global output patterns.

The U.S. review follows a broader period of heightened scrutiny on metal and battery supply chains, where officials have sought to assess trade impacts and the competitiveness of U.S. production. While cadmium is not a rare earth element, the metal is frequently discussed alongside other strategic materials because of its role in electronics and energy storage.

Under the tariff process, importers and exporters can submit documentation and respond to questions from the reviewing authorities, which typically include U.S. Commerce Department and related trade agencies. A determination would follow after the collection of data on pricing, volumes, and injury factors. Additional administrative steps may affect how quickly any new duties take effect, depending on the timeline set by the agencies.

South Korean exporters, trading houses, and downstream buyers are expected to monitor the outcome closely, particularly where contracts and purchase orders reference existing duty levels. In the meantime, shipments may be subject to revised assessments if the review results in updated duty rates or special instructions for cash deposits.

A U.S. industry representative said the review’s scope will likely focus on “the pricing practices and the extent of any injury to domestic operations,” adding that companies are preparing compliance submissions and supporting data to reflect their sourcing and sales conditions.

headline: Turkey blocks bismuth exports temporarily as traders face licensing suspension
body: Turkey has temporarily blocked bismuth exports after suspending trading licenses tied to shipments, according to market participants. The measure, effective immediately after regulators issued the suspension guidance on 2026-03-12, applies to bismuth metal and selected bismuth compounds routed through export customs declarations dated from 2026-03-13 onward. Several traders said licenses covering existing contracts are being reviewed and that consignments scheduled for departure this week may be delayed by 2–4 weeks pending approval. The restriction has tightened supply in spot markets ahead of the mid-March purchasing cycle, with buyers reporting slower access to confirmed lots and shorter validity windows for new offers. Prices moved in line with reduced availability as freight bookings and bank documentation were put on hold pending license clearance. Authorities indicated the action is intended to ensure compliance with export controls and shipment documentation, without providing an end date, but one broker said the temporary ban could be lifted once affected permits are reissued.

headline: Iranware sanctions disrupt molybdenum sales; buyers seek alternative supply
body:
Sanctions tied to Iranware have disrupted the sale of molybdenum, prompting buyers to seek alternative supplies for the metal used in steelmaking and industrial alloys, according to traders and buyers.

The impact has been felt most acutely in shipments linked to Iranian intermediaries and trading houses, several market participants said, as banks and logistics providers tighten compliance checks. As a result, contracts have faced payment delays, documentation backlogs and, in some cases, cancellations, they said.

Molybdenum is traded internationally in concentrates and refined forms, and demand is closely watched by producers of high-strength steel, including for infrastructure and energy applications. With routes affected, buyers are turning to other origins such as China, Europe and North America, depending on grade and delivery schedules, traders said.

“Sales are still possible, but the timelines are stretching and counterparties are being asked for additional documentation,” one buyer at a steel input procurement firm said, adding that sourcing teams were “actively rebalancing” volumes to maintain mill feedstock. The buyer declined to be named because of commercial sensitivities.

Market sources said the disruption is likely to be uneven across supply types. High-purity refined material and specific chemical grades can be harder to replace on short notice, while standard formats and lower-purity product may be easier to substitute by drawing from existing inventories or re-scheduled deliveries. Traders also cited a rise in requests for alternative certificates of origin and end-use assurances, particularly for shipments that previously cleared with less scrutiny.

In the spot and near-term market, participants reported tighter availability for certain lots and more frequent price adjustments in response to delivery uncertainty. Some sellers have offered shorter or more conditional terms to address payment risk, while buyers have widened the set of approved suppliers and increased due diligence checks.

Industry analysts said the episode could shift trading patterns without necessarily changing underlying global demand for the metal, at least in the near term. However, if compliance measures remain restrictive for several months, procurement costs and lead times could rise, they warned, affecting both distributors and steel producers that rely on predictable input flows.

headline: China declares force majeure at a major graphite mine after landslide
body: China has declared force majeure at a major graphite mine after a landslide disrupted operations, according to a filing by the local operator and related notices circulated to downstream buyers. The incident occurred on March 8 in the mining area, where a landslide following heavy rain damaged road access and partially buried parts of the tailings and processing zone, according to internal assessments referenced in the notices. The force majeure declaration applies to supply deliveries scheduled from March 10 through April 30, affecting both bulk concentrate shipments and planned offtake volumes under existing quarterly contracts. Industry contacts said product output has been curtailed as crews work on slope stabilization and equipment recovery, with initial restoration estimates extending into mid-April. The mine is considered a key source of natural graphite feedstock used in battery and industrial applications, and the curtailment is expected to tighten spot availability ahead of the second-quarter procurement cycle. Traders noted that force majeure typically shifts allocation terms, including delivery windows and penalty clauses, while buyers seek alternative supply from other provinces and import routes.

headline: Brazil lifts steel export restrictions after port labor dispute resolved
body:
Brazil has lifted steel export restrictions after resolving a port labor dispute that had disrupted shipments, government sources said, allowing exporters to resume deliveries from key terminals.

The measures, imposed earlier in the year to manage congestion and stabilize domestic availability, had limited certain outbound steel flows from ports used for shipments to South America, the Middle East and parts of Africa. With operations restored, the government confirmed that the restrictions would be withdrawn immediately, according to industry and logistics officials familiar with the terms.

The dispute centered on labor issues at ports handling steel and related commodities, leading to delays in loading, container backlogs and higher demurrage costs. During the disruption, importers and distributors said the bottlenecks tightened supply windows and raised the risk of missed contracts, particularly for exporters reliant on scheduled vessel sailings.

Brazil’s Ministry of Development and Industry said the decision followed the restoration of normal port operations and the resumption of expected throughput levels. In a brief statement, the ministry said it would continue monitoring freight and inventory conditions to prevent a repeat of the disruption-driven shortage scenario that had prompted the earlier controls.

Steel producers and traders welcomed the change, with one shipping and trade executive saying the lifting of the curbs reduces pressure on logistics contracts. “The resolution at the port level clears the immediate operational block, and shipments that were held back can move,” the executive said, speaking on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to discuss policy details.

While the export limits are removed, companies indicated they will work to unwind accumulated shipment schedules. Some exporters had rerouted orders or shifted delivery dates, and buyers in multiple regions reported that confirmation of revised availability could take several days as exporters rebook space and update documentation.

The episode highlighted the sensitivity of Brazil’s steel trade to port labor stability, especially in months when demand and shipping capacity can be constrained. With restrictions lifted, industry participants said the next test will be whether throughput remains steady and whether vessel schedules normalize without renewed labor action.

The government did not specify how quickly shipments will return to prior volumes, but officials said monitoring would focus on port performance, domestic inventory indicators and the continued ability to meet contractual obligations for both export and local markets.

headline: Ukraine force majeure shields iron ore deliveries after rail infrastructure strike
body: Ukraine has invoked force majeure for several iron ore shipments after a rail infrastructure strike disrupted freight services, according to shippers and logistics intermediaries. The move, dated 13 March 2026, is being used to excuse delayed delivery windows for cargoes moving from Kryvyi Rih and other rail-linked hubs to domestic yards and export terminals. Operators said locomotive shortages and track-access limits followed the strike, which began on 12 March and caused cancellations across multiple lines, with turnaround times rising from typical 6–18 hours to 24–72 hours for affected trains. Under contract terms, force majeure coverage is expected to apply for deliveries with contractual dates between 12 and 30 March, depending on loading confirmations recorded at weighbridges. Companies indicated that vessels waiting at Black Sea loading points may still be subject to demurrage if documentation is not corrected within agreed notification periods. Market participants said the disruption could temporarily tighten near-term spot availability, while longer-term supply plans depend on rail restoration and the resumption of priority dispatch slots.

headline: Australia delays tungsten concentrate shipments as insurers tighten coverage
body:
Australia has delayed shipments of tungsten concentrate after insurers tightened coverage terms for the export of the metal, according to industry sources, as buyers sought alternative arrangements for logistics and risk.

The delays affect concentrate cargoes from multiple mines and processors, sources said, with some shipments pushed back by several weeks while carriers and trading firms renegotiated insurance documentation and voyage terms. Tungsten concentrate is used in hard metals and cutting tools, and supply contracts often depend on timely delivery to smelters and downstream manufacturers.

Several traders said insurers are requiring more detailed vessel-level information, including tighter reporting on route screening, port handling and ownership structures. Others pointed to higher premiums and reduced coverage for certain transit corridors, which can make it uneconomic for sellers to ship under existing contract conditions. Under typical arrangements, freight and insurance costs are managed through letters of credit or specified “incoterms,” but tightened insurance can trigger renegotiations and amendments.

One Australian exporter said delays were being managed commercially and that shipments were being re-scheduled to maintain customer supply, rather than withdrawn from the market. “We have been working with counterparties and insurers to align documentation and coverage so cargoes can move,” the exporter said.

Tungsten has seen increased attention in recent years as manufacturers look to diversify sources of critical materials. Australia is among the world’s key producers of tungsten concentrates, and most sales are tied to conversion into tungsten products in Asia and Europe, where smelters assess incoming feedstock quality and delivery timing. Industry participants said the shipment delays are likely to affect short-term inventory levels at processing sites, depending on the duration of the insurance restrictions.

Marine insurers’ recent tightening comes amid broader scrutiny of trade flows and sanctions compliance, sources said, which can affect coverage for shipments passing through high-risk jurisdictions or involving complex beneficial ownership structures. While no single incident was cited, traders said the change in underwriting standards is prompting a more cautious approach to risk.

Market participants said the impact on pricing is expected to be limited in the near term because concentrates are often covered by multi-month supply agreements. However, any extended interruption could increase spot premiums for immediate delivery, particularly if alternative supply routes are not secured quickly.

headline: Botswana suspends zirconium shipments after audit flags traceability lapses
body: Botswana has suspended zirconium shipments after an internal audit identified traceability lapses affecting shipments leaving mining and processing sites, according to officials familiar with the matter. The suspension, imposed on 2026-03-10, applies to all outbound consignments during the audit period and covers both bulk material and semi-finished zircon products slated for delivery in March and April 2026. The review, conducted by compliance teams in coordination with trade documentation staff, flagged missing or inconsistent lot identifiers across tracking records and discrepancies between weighbridge readings and shipping invoices. Auditors also cited gaps in chain-of-custody logs for samples used in quality checks, including delays in recording custody transfers over multiple transport legs between the extraction area and the port staging facility. Botswana’s ministry of trade directed affected operators to submit corrected documentation within 5 business days and to provide a revised traceability protocol before resuming exports. The halt is expected to disrupt near-term contracts tied to European and Asian buyers, though no immediate estimate was provided for the volume affected.

headline: Philippines halts copper cathode deliveries after typhoon damages handling
body:
The Philippines has halted copper cathode deliveries to a major domestic handling facility after typhoon damage disrupted receiving and storage operations, industry sources said, tightening supply for regional buyers.

The shipping suspension follows damage to port handling equipment and temporary flooding at a key site used to receive imported copper cathodes, according to traders monitoring freight and cargo schedules. Several consignments scheduled for unloading were held in port queues while the operator assessed repairs and checked for water exposure, the sources said.

Copper cathodes are typically imported by Philippines refiners and traders, then processed or distributed to industrial customers. Any interruption in the flow can affect short-term inventory levels and premiums, though market participants said the impact would depend on how quickly facilities resume full loading and unloading.

A spokesperson for the facility operator did not immediately respond to a request for comment. In the meantime, logistics providers said they were coordinating with shipping lines and customs officials to reschedule shipments and validate cargo conditions before discharge. One trader said paperwork and inspection requirements were being applied more strictly after reports that some areas had experienced water ingress during the storm.

Typhoon-related disruptions have become a recurring risk for commodity supply chains in Southeast Asia, with storms sometimes damaging cranes, warehouses and yard surfaces used for metals logistics. In the Philippines, authorities often issue advisories after severe weather that can also affect truck movement and inland transport corridors, further complicating deliveries even when ports remain partially operational.

Traders said the halt was focused on specific deliveries into the affected facility rather than a nationwide ban on imports. Alternative routing to other warehouses or refiners may be possible, but would require additional trucking capacity and updated receiving schedules, they added. Buyers are now seeking confirmation of revised demurrage terms and revised delivery dates.

The copper market has been sensitive to logistics disruptions because cathodes are time-bound for industrial procurement and downstream sales. Market participants said they were watching whether the facility could return to normal operations within days or whether repairs would extend the suspension into the next delivery cycle. For now, rerouting and rescheduling are expected to determine the extent of any supply shortfall in the near term.

headline: China cancels vanadium tender due to delivery delays from transport curbs
body: China has cancelled a scheduled vanadium purchase tender after delays tied to transport curbs, according to market participants. The tender, issued for delivery dates in late March 2026, was voided as carriers reported reduced haulage capacity and longer lead times at checkpoints along major freight corridors. Sellers said vehicles were unable to meet loading and handover windows agreed under the contract terms, with some consignments slipping by 10 to 20 days. The cancellation affects volumes expected to support benchmark pricing linked to steel demand for the first quarter, a period when firms typically aim to replenish inventories ahead of peak production schedules. Traders estimated the withdrawn procurement could remove several hundred tonnes of buying interest from the spot market, tightening near-term availability for buyers that rely on periodic state-aligned purchases. The next tender date was not immediately confirmed, but participants indicated that rescheduled sales may be issued once freight conditions stabilize and inspection throughput returns to normal levels.

headline: South Africa halts platinum group metal production after power-supply outage
body:
South Africa halted production of platinum group metals after a power-supply outage disrupted operations at mines and smelters, industry sources said on Saturday.

The interruptions affected processing and production schedules at several sites that rely on stable electricity to run crushing, concentrators and refining equipment, sources familiar with the situation said. In some areas, operations were brought to a standstill to protect machinery and maintain safe operating conditions while power was restored.

Company representatives did not immediately respond to requests for comment. One industry source, speaking on condition of anonymity because they were not authorised to talk to media, said the outage led to temporary shutdowns across parts of the value chain, from ore processing through to the stages used to produce refined metals.

Platinum group metals, which include platinum, palladium, rhodium, ruthenium, iridium and osmium, are central to South Africa’s mining exports and industrial supply chains. The sector has faced repeated disruptions in recent years from operational incidents, infrastructure constraints and broader energy shortages that have increased reliance on backup systems and tighter maintenance windows.

Electricity problems have affected portions of South Africa’s grid, and mining companies have adapted by investing in generation capacity and grid resilience. Still, when outages occur, the ability to restart equipment can vary depending on how long power is lost and whether it triggers automatic safety shutdowns.

The precise duration of the halt and the scale of output impacted were not immediately clear. Sources said companies would reassess production plans once power stabilised, with some operations expected to resume in stages while sites work through start-up and recovery procedures.

South Africa accounts for a large share of global platinum and palladium supply, and disruptions in the country can influence international pricing and inventories. Traders typically watch for signals on production downtime and refurbishment schedules to assess near-term availability and downstream refining capacity.

Authorities and power utility Eskom have previously warned that constraints on the grid can cause load-shedding and frequency fluctuations. While the utility’s latest outlook was not immediately available, mining sources said the outage would likely delay deliveries for the current production window and could increase working pressure for affected plants if the interruption extended beyond a single shift.

headline: Sweden orders temporary suspension of copper smelter operations for emissions
body: Sweden has ordered a temporary suspension of copper smelter operations in response to repeated emissions breaches, according to regulators and industry sources. The order, issued on 2026-03-13 by the relevant environmental authority, applies to selected smelting lines at a site in southern Sweden and requires output to halt until corrective measures are verified. The measure follows monitoring results showing exceedances of permitted limits for SO2 and particulate matter over multiple weeks, with compliance checks recorded on 2026-02-10, 2026-02-24, and 2026-03-06. Operators are required to submit within 14 days a revised emissions control plan covering scrubber performance, filtration upgrades, and restart procedures. A restart approval is contingent on independent testing and on meeting threshold levels during a full operating cycle. The stoppage is expected to reduce national copper processing capacity by several thousand tonnes in the coming weeks, with potential knock-on impacts for contract deliveries in Europe’s industrial supply chain.

headline: DRC cooperates on cobalt mine safety probe; output temporarily constrained
body:
The Democratic Republic of Congo said it has cooperated with a safety probe into a cobalt mine where operations have been temporarily constrained, according to a statement released after the investigation began.

The probe, initiated following concerns raised by industry and local stakeholders about working conditions and potential safety risks, is focused on compliance with operating procedures and the integrity of mine infrastructure, officials said. The company involved, which mines cobalt used in batteries, has continued to work with authorities while responding to the findings of inspectors.

DRC’s mining regulator said it engaged with investigators and shared information related to the site’s safety management systems, including recent maintenance records and procedures for handling hazardous materials. The regulator did not name the mine in the statement, but said the cooperation includes access for inspectors and technical documentation relevant to the assessment.

In the meantime, output at the site has been temporarily constrained as inspectors review whether operational practices meet regulatory requirements. Industry sources cited by the regulator’s statement said production levels are being held below normal while corrective actions are evaluated and verified, with the objective of ensuring continued compliance before full-scale restart.

A spokesperson for the regulator said the government’s approach is to balance ongoing oversight with continuity of operations once safety conditions are satisfied. “We are working with the mining operator and the inspection team to establish what needs to be corrected, and we will allow activity to resume at a pace consistent with confirmed compliance,” the spokesperson said.

The probe comes amid broader scrutiny of cobalt supply chains, particularly in the DRC where most of the world’s cobalt is produced. Regulators and international buyers have increasingly emphasized workplace safety, environmental protections and traceability measures. The DRC has in recent years implemented reforms aimed at tightening mine safety enforcement and improving monitoring of artisanal and industrial production sites.

The regulator said it will publish the inspection outcomes once the review is complete and that further measures could follow depending on findings. Those measures may include additional audits, mandated upgrades and, in cases of serious violations, suspension or restrictions on operations.

headline: United Kingdom tightens review of palladium imports amid documentation delays
body: The United Kingdom tightened its review process for palladium imports after delays in supplier documentation, according to industry filings cited by traders on March 14, 2026. Under the updated scrutiny, shipments arriving with incomplete certificates of origin or inconsistent declarations will face additional checks before customs clearance, extending average processing times by 1 to 3 business days. The measure applies to palladium in unwrought and semi-finished forms, and covers consignments scheduled to land from March 15 through at least April 30 while paperwork systems are assessed.
