Firms reported that some invoices and assay statements were received out of sequence or with mismatched lot numbers during the past 3 to 4 weeks, prompting the regulator to pause routine routing. Importers said they were asked to resubmit documents within 48 hours of notification, failing which goods may be held at port pending verification. Traders expect the tighter controls to reduce near-term availability by shifting deliveries into April and to increase basis premiums for short-dated supply until documentation backlogs clear.

headline: India declares force majeure at molybdenum processing plant after power instability
body:
India has declared force majeure at a molybdenum processing plant after unstable power supply disrupted operations, according to an official notification seen by Reuters.

The declaration, issued by the plant operator under contract terms, covers production and supply obligations during periods affected by “power instability” that led to operational interruptions, the document said. Force majeure is a contractual concept used to suspend performance when events beyond a company’s control prevent fulfillment of obligations.

The notification cites fluctuations in electricity availability and voltage levels that caused the plant’s equipment to slow down or shut down for safety. It also references additional impacts on downstream processing activities that depend on continuous power for furnace, cooling and filtration systems.

An official at the company said the firm had restored partial operations and was working to normalize output as supply stabilized. “We have taken steps to mitigate the effect of power instability, including switching to controlled operational schedules, but certain processing steps require consistent power,” the official said.

Molybdenum, a metal used in steelmaking and high-temperature industrial applications, relies on energy-intensive processing to convert ore into intermediate products. Industry participants said interruptions can delay batch processing and affect inventory turnover, particularly where plants run on tightly sequenced production schedules.

The force majeure notice does not indicate a specific end date, the document said, adding that updates would be provided as conditions change. It applies to customer deliveries and logistics tied to the impacted time window, subject to further assessment of operational readiness.

Power instability has been a recurring challenge for industrial sites across parts of India, particularly where grid reliability or local distribution constraints affect energy-intensive operations. While companies sometimes invoke force majeure during prolonged disruptions, the practical effect typically depends on the extent of downtime and the ability to reroute production to other lines or suppliers.

The company did not immediately respond to questions about the duration of the disruption or whether any inventories could offset delivery delays. No immediate comment was available from relevant government agencies.

headline: China restricts tin concentrate exports after smelting capacity checks expand
body: China has restricted exports of tin concentrate after authorities expanded checks on smelting capacity, according to industry contacts and trade data, in a move that could tighten global supplies into the second quarter. Starting in mid-March, customs and port operators began applying additional documentation requirements for shipments of tin concentrate, including updated processing-capacity filings and compliance confirmations tied to licensed smelters. The expanded review follows earlier audits in January and February that targeted throughput claims and environmental limits at major producing hubs in Yunnan, Guangdong and Guangxi. Export permits for concentrate are now being issued with narrower windows, and some traders reported delays of 7 to 21 days for cargoes already booked for overseas buyers. One effect is expected to redirect more of the concentrate stream into domestic refining, where operating rates were re-checked during the March inspection cycle. Market participants said the policy could raise regional concentrate premiums while holding refined tin output steady if smelters pass capacity verification.

headline: China pauses graphite anode shipments after environmental regulator inspection
body:
China has paused some graphite anode shipments after an environmental regulator inspection, according to industry sources and shipment documents reviewed by traders, a move that could tighten supply for companies making lithium-ion batteries.

The suspension applies to certain lots of spherical and other processed graphite anodes shipped from facilities in multiple provinces, the sources said. One trader said dispatches were halted while regulators reviewed compliance with wastewater treatment and air-pollution controls, adding that paperwork for rescheduling deliveries was being processed. The pause is expected to last at least several days, with affected volumes varying by site.

China is the world’s largest producer of graphite anode materials used in lithium-ion batteries for electric vehicles, energy storage and consumer electronics. Sourcing disruptions can quickly affect downstream makers because many battery supply chains rely on fast-turnaround deliveries and short inventory cycles.

The inspection was conducted by local environmental authorities and focused on emissions and waste-handling practices at processing lines used to purify and coat graphite, according to two sources familiar with the review. Companies involved in the shipments said they were cooperating with inspectors and taking corrective measures where problems were identified. One source said the inspection did not amount to a full shutdown, but it triggered a stop in shipment clearance until documentation was verified.

A logistics provider handling some of the paused shipments said container dispatches were delayed and that some consignments were being held at terminals pending confirmation from compliance officials. “The pause is linked to inspection findings and the re-issuance of transport clearance,” the provider said. The provider added that some customers were being offered alternative dates or rerouting to other suppliers, depending on product specifications and certification requirements.

Battery material buyers often track shipment schedules closely because anode production typically operates continuously to meet customer demand. Any interruption may increase freight costs and prompt spot purchases if inventories run down, particularly for higher-purity grades used in certain cathode chemistries.

China’s graphite industry has faced periodic scrutiny in recent years as regulators push for tighter controls on pollution and waste discharge, including in industrial clusters tied to mining, processing and chemical purification. Traders said the latest pause is likely to remain limited to specific facilities until inspections conclude and paperwork is restored for exports and domestic deliveries.

headline: Chile extends force majeure at Escondida copper mine after storm damage (Apr 18)
body: Chile has extended force majeure for output at the Escondida copper mine after storm damage, keeping disruption in place beyond its initial deadline set for mid-April. The state-backed legal mechanism covers parts of the mining and concentrator operations affected by flooding and storm-related damage, according to authorities and mine communications. The extension runs through late April, with the exact end date tied to inspections of key power, water-management, and haulage systems. Escondida, operated by BHP and partners, said remediation and repairs are continuing while safety checks are completed and equipment is brought back into service in phases. The decision follows earlier force majeure declarations issued in the weeks after the storm hit the Antofagasta region and damaged access roads and drainage infrastructure. Industry supply schedules may remain constrained through the extended period, although the mine indicated some activity could resume on sections not directly affected by the damage. The extension comes as copper prices and logistics planning continue to be sensitive to short-notice production changes.

headline: Indonesia halts nickel ore exports for 30 days citing processing capacity (Apr 12)
body:
Indonesia has halted nickel ore exports for 30 days, citing a need to ensure ore is processed domestically amid limited processing capacity, the government said on Sunday.

The Ministry of Trade announced the suspension of exports of nickel ore and related products from April 12 for a period of one month, according to a notice published late Saturday. The ministry said the measure is intended to align shipments with the availability of processing facilities and to support the development of Indonesia’s downstream nickel industry.

Indonesian authorities have previously implemented export restrictions as the country seeks to increase the volume of nickel processed into intermediate and higher-value products such as nickel matte and ferronickel. The policy has been designed to raise earnings from the mineral and to attract investment in smelters and refineries.

Under the new rules, the trade ministry said shipments covered by the restriction will be subject to compliance checks at ports. It did not specify exceptions in the notice, but it said the export pause applies during the 30-day window while the government reviews supply and capacity conditions.

The government’s action follows ongoing scrutiny of whether Indonesia’s smelters can take in enough ore to justify export limits. The authorities have argued that rushing exports could undermine investment plans and disrupt production at domestic plants, while companies have said delays in commissioning additional processing lines can constrain throughput.

The suspension comes as global prices for nickel and related commodities have remained sensitive to changes in supply flows from major producing countries. Indonesia is the world’s leading supplier of nickel ore and a key source of the metal used in stainless steel and, increasingly, in batteries.

The trade ministry said it will evaluate processing capacity and the progress of downstream projects during the 30-day period, and that further adjustments would be made depending on conditions in the domestic supply chain. It added that it would continue coordinating with other agencies involved in mineral policy, port administration and industrial regulation.

A spokesperson for the ministry said in the notice that the pause reflects “the need to maintain steady ore supply for domestic smelters during the review of processing capacity.” The government did not provide a timeline beyond the initial 30-day suspension.

headline: DRC cobalt smelter strike disrupts deliveries to global buyers (May 3)
body: A strike at a cobalt smelter in the Democratic Republic of Congo has disrupted deliveries to global buyers ahead of the May 3 delivery window, according to two logistics and trading sources familiar with shipments. The stoppage began in late April, after workers protested pay arrears and changes to shift schedules, and it has since idled smelting capacity that typically supplies refined cobalt and cobalt intermediates to customers in Asia, Europe, and North America. One shipment scheduled for early May was delayed by at least 10 days, while two additional lots tied to May 3 terms are now expected to miss the loading deadline, the sources said. Traders are seeking alternative intake from other DRC facilities and adjusting offtake timelines, with some buyers reportedly raising banked inventory to cover near-term shortfalls. Benchmark prices for refined cobalt were not immediately reflected in spot spreads in the first days of the disruption, but freight and demurrage costs have increased for road and port-linked movements. The smelter operator did not respond to requests for comment as of March 14.

headline: Zambia declares state of emergency at copper processing hub over power outages (Mar 27)
body:
Zambia has declared a state of emergency at its copper processing hub after prolonged power outages disrupted operations and threatened supply, the government said on Friday.

The measure was announced as electricity shortfalls continued to affect mines and smelters clustered around the country’s Copperbelt province, where copper ore is refined into concentrates and cathodes for export. The government said the emergency status will enable faster deployment of equipment and fuel, support temporary power solutions, and accelerate maintenance of critical grid infrastructure feeding the industrial zone.

In a statement, the Ministry of Energy said the decision followed repeated failures to stabilise power delivery to key processing sites despite interventions planned under existing emergency and load-shedding protocols. It said outages had escalated in frequency and duration over recent weeks, forcing some plants to cut production and creating risks to safety, product quality and contractual deliveries.

The state of emergency allows authorities to coordinate directly with utilities, regulators and operators and to streamline approvals for contingency measures, including procurement and deployment of generation capacity on a short-term basis. It also provides a legal framework for targeted inspections and enforcement around energy demand management at facilities designated as priority sites.

Zambia’s power system, dominated by hydropower, has been under strain from variable water flows and rising demand. The government has previously relied on load shedding and emergency procurement to manage supply, while long-term solutions include additional generation capacity and transmission upgrades.

Copperbelt processing is central to Zambia’s economy, accounting for the bulk of national copper output and export revenues. Production interruptions can reverberate across the supply chain, affecting downstream operations such as acid recovery, electro-winning and refining stages that depend on continuous power and stable operating conditions.

Companies operating in the region said they have been working to mitigate the impact, including by adjusting schedules and operating parameters. One industry executive said the outages were “serious enough to disrupt normal production planning,” adding that “stable power is essential for smelters and refineries to meet specifications.”

The government said it will publish updates on the situation and the steps taken to restore power reliability, and it called on stakeholders to implement measures to reduce load during peak periods where required.

headline: Tungsten rises after Vietnam delays import licenses for new shipments (Feb 9)
body: Tungsten prices firmed after Vietnam delayed approval of import licenses for new shipments, tightening near-term supply expectations, according to market participants on Feb 9. Fastmarkets data indicated the rise followed official processing backlogs that slowed issuance for multiple lots destined for industrial users in the second half of February. Traders said some contracts scheduled for early-March delivery were pushed back, increasing demand for spot material and lifting offer prices across standard grades. The delay also affected secondary market flows, with converters seeking inventory ahead of potential licensing caps on additional tonnages. Several buyers reported longer lead times for shipments passing customs, while freight schedules were adjusted to align with revised clearance dates. Market sentiment turned cautious but constructive as attention shifted to the next batch of approvals expected later in February, with dealers watching whether Vietnam would reopen full licensing throughput or extend restrictions into March.

headline: China tightens graphite export quotas ahead of EV battery demand ramp (Jan 24)
body:
China has tightened graphite export quotas ahead of a projected ramp in demand for electric-vehicle battery materials, according to industry and official notices seen this week.

The Ministry of Commerce and customs authorities notified exporters that the annual volume of graphite shipments covered by existing quota management will be reduced for the first half of the year, with tighter issuance for licences covering both natural graphite and certain processed grades used in battery anodes. The changes follow recent adjustments to China’s broader controls on critical mineral exports and are intended to better align outbound supply with domestic processing capacity, sources said.

Graphite is a key input for anodes in lithium-ion batteries, particularly for EVs and other applications including energy storage systems. China dominates downstream processing of battery materials, while supply constraints and logistics have contributed to volatility in global graphite availability and pricing. Market participants have been tracking export policy as automakers step up EV production targets and battery makers expand procurement ahead of the middle of the decade.

Exporters said the new quota framework applies to shipments already contracted for specific destinations, but that licence approval for additional volumes may take longer under the revised caps. Companies have been instructed to submit updated shipment plans and to provide documentation that tracks material origin and processing steps. Where quotas are exhausted, additional shipments could be limited until new allocations are issued, they said.

A spokesperson for the China Battery Materials Association said the country’s graphite supply policy is aimed at “ensuring stable domestic availability for high-quality anode production,” adding that upstream and downstream firms should coordinate to avoid mismatches between raw material supply and customer demand.

The policy comes as battery producers look to secure graphite feedstock for upcoming manufacturing expansions. Demand for anode materials is expected to rise alongside EV sales, though industry forecasts vary depending on vehicle production cycles, battery chemistries and inventory levels at cell makers and automakers.

Global customers have increasingly diversified sourcing of graphite and sought alternative suppliers, but analysts say China’s processing capacity and the concentration of skilled purification and coating steps still make it a pivotal supplier for the anode supply chain. Any change to export quota allocations can quickly affect contract negotiations and lead times for buyers outside China.

headline: Peru suspends tin mine operations after landslide forces evacuation (Mar 2)
body: Peru suspended operations at its tin mine in the country’s southern region after a landslide forced the evacuation of workers on March 2, according to company and local reports. The stoppage affects mining activities at a site that had been producing concentrates used in international smelter contracts, and the company said it would keep production halted while engineers assess slope stability, access roads, and tailings drainage. Emergency teams evacuated staff and contractors the same day as the slide, with officials citing unstable ground and blocked haul routes. Authorities reported no immediate fatalities, but said at least several workers were housed temporarily in government shelters pending safety verification. The evacuation and suspension come as Peru continues to manage heightened risks from seasonal rains, with regional meteorological agencies forecasting further precipitation over the following days. Operations were expected to remain suspended through at least March 7, subject to clearance from local civil protection authorities and updated geotechnical reports.

headline: Russia applies temporary curbs on palladium supplies amid logistics constraints (Apr 7)
body:
Russia has introduced temporary curbs on palladium supplies, citing logistics constraints, according to industry sources, in a move that could tighten availability for buyers across industrial and automotive markets.

The measures, effective from early April, limit the volume of palladium shipments that can be released by certain suppliers while capacity is redirected to higher-priority deliveries, the sources said. Several traders described a staggered approach, with allocations set to be reviewed as transport routes and warehousing capacity come back online.

Russian exports of palladium are a key part of global supply, particularly for converters used in automotive emission control systems and for chemical and electronics applications. Any disruption to scheduled deliveries can force refiners, manufacturers and dealers to adjust procurement plans or draw down inventories to meet near-term demand.

One palladium buyer said the restrictions were being communicated through individual allocation arrangements rather than a broad, public directive. “We were notified that some shipments will be delayed and volumes will be reduced for a limited period,” the buyer said, adding that the company is seeking alternative logistics routes and timing to secure material.

The curbs come at a time when shipping and customs processes have remained uneven for many commodities moving between Russia and downstream markets. Traders said the latest step reflects continued constraints including freight availability, documentation lead times and bottlenecks at transshipment points. They added that the impact is likely to be most acute for customers relying on frequent, short-cycle deliveries rather than long-term contractual volumes.

Russia has previously implemented supply management actions affecting precious metals, though the specific structure and duration of the current restrictions were not immediately clear. Market participants said prices could react to the expected reduction in available tonnage during the adjustment period, depending on the extent to which other producing regions or inventory holders can offset the gap.

Industry sources said they expect the curbs to be eased gradually if logistics conditions improve, with allocations potentially increasing after follow-up assessments. Dealers meanwhile indicated they are closely monitoring shipment notices and allocation confirmations from Russian suppliers for the coming weeks.

headline: Canadian plant outage hits primary nickel output after acid plant failure (Feb 15)
body: A plant outage in Canada has disrupted operations at a primary nickel facility, cutting output after an acid plant failure on Feb. 15, according to industry sources. The incident stopped production at the acid unit, which feeds downstream processing used to produce nickel material, and caused a broader process slowdown across the site. Operators began corrective maintenance immediately following the failure, with initial ramp-up attempts starting later the same week. The reduction in throughput is expected to affect near-term volumes from the primary nickel stream, tightening supply during the first half of March. Traders said the impact could be amplified because nickel refining schedules are sensitive to acid availability and can require several days to stabilize once repairs are completed. While the company has not published detailed downtime figures, market participants indicated that restart timing will determine whether the shortfall is limited to days or extends into subsequent weeks. Further updates are expected as the facility completes verification runs and returns to steady-state production.

headline: South Africa halts platinum conveyor line due to safety incident at Rustenburg (Jan 30)
body:
South Africa has temporarily halted a platinum conveyor line at Lonmin’s Rustenburg operations after a safety incident, the company said, suspending material handling while investigations are carried out.

The stoppage, announced on Monday, affects a conveyor system used to move ore and concentrate within the mine’s processing area. Company officials said the line was brought to a standstill as part of standard safety procedures following the incident, which did not immediately lead to any confirmed release of hazardous substances in available information.

Lonmin said it has initiated an internal review and is working with mine safety regulators on the findings. Personnel were evacuated from the immediate area and production teams switched to alternative handling routes where possible, while maintenance staff inspected the conveyor, associated supports and mechanical components.

A spokesperson said the incident is being assessed to identify the cause and determine whether any equipment damage occurred. “Our priority is to establish what happened, ensure the conveyor is safe to restart, and prevent a recurrence,” the spokesperson said in a statement. The company did not provide additional details on the nature of the incident or specify whether any injuries were sustained, indicating that information would be released once the investigation is complete.

Platinum operations in Rustenburg have faced intermittent disruptions over recent years as companies balance output targets with stricter safety measures and infrastructure maintenance. Conveyor systems are critical to moving material between stages of crushing, grinding and concentration, and a prolonged outage can affect throughput and shipment schedules depending on the capacity of stockpiles and bypass systems.

In its statement, Lonmin said it would provide updates on the timing of the line’s return to service once checks have been completed and the system has been cleared by engineering teams and safety officers. The company added that work to rectify the issue and revalidate operating parameters could take several days, depending on the extent of any required repairs.

South Africa is the world’s largest producer of platinum group metals, with Rustenburg forming one of the country’s key production regions. Any operational interruptions can influence near-term supply availability and logistics planning for refiners and industrial buyers, particularly during periods of tight demand for platinum and palladium.

headline: Morocco issues export ban on some phosphoric-grade uranium intermediates (Apr 21)
body: Morocco has issued an export ban covering selected phosphoric-grade uranium intermediate products, effective immediately after approval by national regulators on Apr. 21, according to documents circulated to industry participants. The measure targets intermediates used in the chemical chain from uranium-bearing inputs to phosphoric-grade materials, while leaving out finished uranium products and most non-uranium phosphates. Traders said the restriction applies to shipments departing from Moroccan ports and free zones, and requires new end-use declarations for any remaining permissible phosphorus-related exports. Compliance checks are expected to start within 48 hours, with customs holds for products identified by specific HS classifications and internal product codes. The ban comes as Morocco’s state-linked phosphate operations expand capacity and seek additional scrutiny from partners tied to nuclear fuel-cycle safeguards. No timeline for lifting the restrictions was provided, and officials did not specify whether exemptions would be granted for contracts signed before Apr. 21.

headline: China imposes quality checks on molybdenum concentrate from overseas suppliers (May 6)
body:
China has begun imposing quality checks on molybdenum concentrate shipped from overseas suppliers, according to measures circulated by industry regulators and buyers, following earlier concerns about variability in grades and contaminants.

The checks, which took effect this week, require importers to provide documentation on concentrate composition before shipments clear customs, and they mandate laboratory testing on selected consignments at Chinese ports. Concentrate lots that fail to meet specified thresholds for key impurities may face hold orders, rerouting to secondary processing, or rejection, buyers said.

Molybdenum concentrate is a key feedstock for producing molybdenum metal and various steelmaking chemicals used to harden and strengthen alloys. China dominates downstream processing capacity, making its import rules a potential swing factor for offshore suppliers, particularly those exporting from regions where concentrate specifications can vary due to ore source and processing methods.

Several market participants said the new requirements focus on verification of molybdenum content as well as limits on elements that can affect refining yields. The checks also address moisture and particle characteristics that can influence smelting performance, according to one trading company that handles imports and freight for multiple overseas mines.

“The main change is that buyers and importers now face tighter verification at the port stage, and more lots are being sampled for lab confirmation,” a China-based trader said. “This increases compliance steps, but it also reduces surprises on specification after delivery.”

The measures arrive as China continues to refine how it monitors imported industrial inputs, including through expanded testing regimes and strengthened documentation rules across bulk commodities. For molybdenum concentrate, regulators have sought to ensure that importers can demonstrate conformity to agreed specification ranges prior to and after arrival.

For suppliers, the impact is likely to depend on how consistently they can document assay results and how quickly they can respond to requests for additional samples or clarifications, traders said. Overseas producers exporting to China may need to tighten quality control at their own processing facilities and align their analytical methods with Chinese buyers’ sampling protocols.

Market participants will be watching whether the checks translate into revised purchasing terms, including payment linked to final lab results, and whether they slow arrivals ahead of spring procurement cycles. Several buyers said they will continue to evaluate the frequency and strictness of rejections over the coming weeks as they gain more data from the new inspection approach.

headline: Kazakhstan declares force majeure at beryllium producer following rail derailment (Feb 20)
body: Kazakhstan has declared force majeure at its main beryllium producer after a rail derailment disrupted shipments, according to a notice dated Feb 20. The incident occurred on a rail corridor used for deliveries of beryllium concentrate and related material to processing and export terminals, and the disruption is expected to affect loadings through at least late February. The government action allows the producer to suspend or renegotiate contractual delivery timelines and take steps to manage claims tied to late performance. Market participants said rail recoveries and wagon availability will likely determine whether normal dispatches resume before March 1. Traders have already started adjusting planned orders for March-loading supply, with freight rerouting adding several days to transit times. The force majeure applies to specific volumes under affected contracts and is being monitored for extension if track restoration and inspection delays persist.

headline: Angola restricts manganese-byproduct shipments affecting copper blister contracts (Mar 11)
body:
Angola has restricted shipments of manganese produced as a byproduct of copper operations, a move that traders said has begun to disrupt supply timelines for copper blister contracts and related offtake arrangements.

The measures, implemented in the wake of tighter controls on exports from the country’s mining sector, limit the volume and timing of manganese shipments leaving Angola, traders said, adding that several counterparties have been forced to renegotiate delivery schedules. In practice, the restrictions have constrained the availability of manganese material that is sold alongside or used to balance inventory linked to copper production.

Angola is one of several producers that generate manganese as a secondary output at certain copper processing sites. While the manganese has its own market, the shipments are often handled through the same logistics channels and contractual structures as copper-related flows, traders said. As a result, delays or reductions in manganese exports can ripple into contractual performance for copper blister, the intermediate product sold to refiners for conversion into refined copper.

A trading source said the curbs have been communicated to buyers through notice letters and revised shipment instructions, with some consignments held back while documentation and export approvals are reprocessed. “The restrictions are not just about manganese volumes, but also about the timing of departures. That has made it harder to match blister deliveries to contract windows,” the source said.

Industry participants said the impact is most visible on spot and short-term contracts, where delivery flexibility is limited. Longer-term arrangements may be able to absorb the changes through buffer inventory and alternative sourcing, but traders noted that offtake terms tied to byproduct streams can still require adjustments, including revisions to quality parameters, shipment dates and demurrage terms.

The restrictions arrive as global metal markets continue to price risks around supply chain reliability, with buyers increasingly focused on export compliance, customs clearance and transport continuity. For copper blisters, most contracts specify delivery windows and penalties for delays, heightening the effect of any upstream bottlenecks in associated product flows.

Manganese demand is used in steelmaking and battery material applications, and traders said the tighter supply from Angola could shift buying toward other origins in the near term. Exporters and buyers were attempting to verify how long the restrictions would last and whether additional exemptions or phased increases in volumes were planned, according to traders.

headline: Zimbabwe introduces licensing for graphite exports amid traceability checks (Jan 16)
body: Zimbabwe has introduced a licensing requirement for graphite exports starting on Jan. 16, according to officials, as authorities step up traceability checks for shipments leaving the country. The measure covers producers and traders exporting natural graphite and processed graphite products, with permits tied to documentation on origin, mining sites and volumes. Companies are required to submit shipment data ahead of departure, including mine-of-origin declarations, transport manifests and buyer registration details, to allow verification by the relevant minerals regulator. The government said the licensing system is intended to reduce diversion and improve compliance with tax, customs and export-control rules, after past concerns about under-invoicing and undocumented flows. Trading companies will also face periodic audits, with penalties for incomplete records and consignments that fail origin verification. The rollout follows a broader push to strengthen mineral supply-chain oversight and comes as international buyers increasingly request full chain-of-custody information for battery and industrial graphite inputs.

headline: Japan refinery says copper cathode shipments delayed by typhoon-related port closures (Mar 19)
body:
Japan’s Sumitomo Metal Mining (SMM) said copper cathode shipments were delayed after typhoon-related closures disrupted port operations, affecting export schedules for the company’s refined copper product.

In a statement dated March 19, SMM said port closures in Japan and delays in vessel handling led to postponed loading of cathodes at its export facilities. The company did not provide specific dates for when shipping would resume, but indicated that shipments would be processed once ports reopened and berthing schedules normalized.

The company said it was coordinating with logistics providers and shipping lines to reroute vessels where possible and to reschedule departures for affected cargoes. SMM added that the disruptions could shift delivery timing for customers, while production continued to run with adjustments to shipment planning.

Copper cathodes are used as a benchmark refined copper product in global trading and are shipped in standard packaging to destinations including Asia, Europe and North America. Disruptions to port operations can therefore affect near-term availability for buyers, particularly when multiple shipment windows are missed during weather-driven closures.

Typhoons and seasonal storms have periodically disrupted logistics across Japan, including grain, fuel and metals transport, as well as container handling at major ports. In this instance, the company linked the delays specifically to the impact of the closures on loading operations and vessel departures rather than to any manufacturing interruption.

SMM said it would provide further updates if the disruption persisted beyond the immediate window. It also urged customers and counterparties to monitor shipment timing, citing normal variability in ocean freight schedules following port disruptions.

Industry participants typically watch for confirmation of loading delays and revised shipping plans as a signal for whether inventories will tighten in receiving markets. For now, the stated impact appears limited to export timing, with SMM attributing the delay to operational disruptions at ports tied to the typhoon conditions.

headline: Brazil authorizes emergency power procurement for iron ore pellet plant (Feb 28)
body: Brazil has authorized emergency power procurement to keep an iron ore pellet plant operating, according to a government decision dated Feb. 28. The measure, issued under emergency supply rules, allows the plant operator to contract electricity outside standard procurement timelines to mitigate risks from generation shortages and transmission constraints during peak demand. The authorization covers short-term purchases intended to maintain continuous production while long-term supply arrangements are finalized. The order specifies that contracts must be executed within defined deadlines and that volumes and pricing should follow emergency procurement ceilings set by regulators. Financial terms are expected to be published after contracting, with costs tracked against an emergency energy budget and subject to audit for compliance. The authorization comes as pellet demand remains supported by steelmaker restocking ahead of second-quarter production schedules, while iron ore processing plants face high power intensity and exposure to grid interruptions.

headline: Australia halts zirconium supply after unexpected permitting delay with regulator (Apr 4)
body:
Australia has halted zirconium supply after an unexpected delay in approvals from the federal regulator, according to industry sources, as producers wait for a revised permitting schedule to clear shipments.

The interruption, which began earlier this month, affects deliveries from at least one processing site, with operators saying they are holding product pending confirmation that new or amended permissions meet regulator requirements. One source said companies are prioritising inventory processing and customer communications while awaiting formal notice on the next steps for compliance.

The delay comes as zirconium demand remains steady from sectors including nuclear fuel cycle activities, chemicals and specialty manufacturing, where supply continuity is closely tracked. Zirconium is used in applications ranging from corrosion-resistant components to ceramics and as a key material in nuclear reactors, making permit timing a potential operational risk for producers.

A spokesperson for the regulator did not immediately comment on the specific case, citing that regulatory actions and timelines depend on the details of each facility. In a statement to one of the companies affected, the regulator indicated the approvals were taking longer than expected due to additional checks and documentation review, according to a person familiar with the matter.

Companies involved in the supply chain said the permitting hold has forced adjustments to logistics and sales plans. Some shipments have been deferred to later loading windows, while others have been paused to avoid breaching contractual delivery terms or violating conditions tied to the relevant approvals.

One industry representative said the goal is to resume exports promptly once the regulator confirms the permits are effective, adding that the current pause is limited to actions that require formal authorisation. “We’re working through the additional requirements as quickly as possible so we can restart shipments under the approved framework,” the representative said.

Regulatory permitting delays are common in sectors where environmental, safety and transport conditions must be met before output can be shipped. In zirconium’s case, approvals typically intersect with how facilities manage waste streams and handling procedures, as well as how material is transported and stored.

Market participants said they will monitor whether the halt is short-lived or expands to additional suppliers. Any prolonged disruption could tighten availability and shift buying patterns in Asia and Europe, where zirconium buyers often rely on Australian sources to balance inventory ahead of contract windows.

headline: Namibia suspends selenium production at coastal facility for maintenance (Jan 9)
body: Namibia suspended selenium production at its coastal facility on Jan. 9 for scheduled maintenance, according to an internal notice cited by industry sources. The stoppage affects operations at the plant that processes selenium-bearing inputs for metal and chemical markets, with restart work planned after equipment checks and preventive replacements. The company did not provide an output estimate for the duration of the outage, but maintenance typically runs 1–3 weeks depending on component availability and safety inspections. Coastal logistics were also adjusted to pause shipments tied to the production line, while stock levels are expected to be used to cover near-term demand. The suspension comes as global selenium supply remains sensitive to operational disruptions at a limited number of producing sites. Market participants said the January halt could tighten spot availability and influence pricing in the weeks after restart, particularly for industrial buyers that rely on steady treatment yields.

headline: Bolivia orders temporary shutdown of tin smelter after emissions permit breach (Feb 12)
body:
Bolivia has ordered a temporary shutdown of a tin smelter after the plant exceeded conditions in its emissions permit, the government said, as regulators moved to reduce air pollution risks in the mining region.

The decision, issued by the environmental authority, requires the smelter to suspend operations for a defined period while it brings its emissions back into compliance with its authorization, according to a statement from the regulator. The order follows a review of monitoring results and inspections that found the facility breached limits set for particulate emissions and other pollutants.

Under the temporary suspension, the plant must halt smelting activities and adjust its operating procedures. The regulator said it will verify corrective measures, including maintenance of filtration equipment and changes to the process parameters used to control emissions. The smelter will be allowed to resume only after it demonstrates compliance through approved monitoring data and inspection checks.

The regulator also said it would open an administrative process related to the permit breach, which can include fines or further restrictions depending on findings. The statement did not immediately specify the size of the penalty or whether the smelter had previously been cited for similar violations.

Local authorities have previously reported that smelting operations can affect nearby communities through dust and fumes, particularly during periods of high production when control equipment is under strain. The latest action comes as governments in mining countries have tightened enforcement of environmental rules and increased reliance on real-time emissions monitoring.

Tin production is a significant part of Bolivia’s extractive economy, and smelting capacity plays a central role in turning concentrates into marketable metal. Any interruption in processing can affect output schedules and shipments, depending on how quickly the facility can complete corrective actions and satisfy regulators.

The government said it expects the smelter to submit a plan for emissions remediation and to provide evidence of compliance within the timeframe set in the shutdown order. “We will not allow operations to continue under permit conditions that do not protect air quality,” an official said in the statement, adding that compliance verification would determine whether production restarts.

headline: Belgium strike disrupts copper wire production impacting copper rod deliveries (Mar 30)
body: A strike at a Belgian facility has disrupted copper wire production, tightening supplies of copper rod shipments to downstream wire and cable makers, according to market contacts. The stoppage began in late March, with disruption expected to intensify through Mar 30 as plants throttle output and reallocate ingots to remaining lines. Several producers reported delayed rod deliveries for the week of Mar 30, with lead times extending from standard 1–2 weeks to as long as 3–4 weeks in specific grades. Traders said the impact is concentrated in red-wire and low-tolerance rod used for telecom and industrial cable applications, where output reductions directly affect diameter availability and surface specifications. Spot premiums for rod and related intermediates were described as firming in response to spot scarcity, while some buyers deferred orders into early April to secure allocation. The full duration and restart timeline remain uncertain as negotiations continue, with any partial restart before the end of March likely to stabilize deliveries for late-April schedules.

headline: China caps germanium exports to priority semiconductor buyers from May 1 (Apr 25)
body:
China will cap exports of germanium to selected “priority” semiconductor buyers starting May 1, according to a notice published by China’s commerce ministry on April 25, as Beijing tightens controls on strategic materials used in advanced electronics.

The measure applies to transactions for germanium, a minor but critical metal used in fiber optics, infrared imaging and certain semiconductor and detector applications. Under the policy, exporters will be required to comply with export quotas for buyers designated as priorities, which the notice links to China’s broader support for domestic technology supply chains.

China has used licensing and quota frameworks for a range of critical minerals in recent years, citing national economic security and the need to manage supplies. The new step narrows access by subjecting exports to additional conditions for specific end users, though the notice did not immediately spell out a timeline beyond the May 1 start date.

Details of the quota allocation system were laid out in the ministry notice, including administrative requirements for firms seeking to ship germanium. Companies are expected to apply for export permissions through the relevant licensing process and demonstrate that shipments fall within the quota set for the approved buyers. Exporters that fail to meet the stipulated conditions would be subject to enforcement under existing trade and export control rules.

The notice also clarifies that the priority buyer list and the corresponding quotas will be managed through China’s trade administration system, with adjustments made as needed. It does not provide specific figures for how much germanium will be available under the capped export regime, nor does it indicate whether the restrictions will be expanded to other customer categories later.

Germanuim supply constraints can affect production schedules for manufacturers that rely on it for optical components and specialized semiconductor uses. Changes in Chinese export availability have previously been closely watched by global industry players, particularly as demand for high-performance chips and related technologies continues to grow.

The commerce ministry statement was issued in the context of ongoing export licensing governance for strategic materials and follows earlier steps that have aimed to regulate the flow of key inputs to overseas customers.

headline: India extends antimony import safeguard measures after stockpiling surge (Jan 8)
body: India extended safeguard measures on antimony imports after a surge in stockpiling intensified supply risks for domestic buyers, according to a government notification dated Jan. 8. The extension keeps temporary import restrictions in place for the relevant products, covering antimony and mixtures used in flame retardants, alloys, and other industrial inputs. The move follows a rapid build-up by intermediaries ahead of anticipated rule changes, which pushed inventories higher during late 2025 and early 2026. Authorities cited concerns that the resulting import volumes could depress local prices and strain domestic production capacity, even as global antimony availability remains uneven. The safeguard action, originally introduced to address sudden import pressure, was reviewed after import data and market indicators showed continued volatility through the first quarter. Trade officials said the updated period will allow monitoring of landed-price trends, duty impacts, and compliance by importers listed under the measure.

headline: DRC signaled cobalt shipment audit plan after customs documentation changes (May 1)
body:
The Democratic Republic of Congo signaled it would audit cobalt shipments after making changes to customs documentation, according to a statement shared with traders ahead of the May 1 deadline.

The DRC’s copper and cobalt state mining agency, Cominière, and officials involved in export clearance indicated that documentation updates would be paired with additional checks on shipments of the battery metal. The review is intended to verify the information provided at the point of export, in particular volumes, origin and permit details submitted through customs systems, traders said.

The move comes as industry participants have sought greater clarity on the procedures used to declare shipments leaving Congolese territory. Several exporters have reported that documentary requirements have shifted in recent weeks, including adjustments to the way supporting forms are completed and presented to customs authorities.

Under the planned audit approach, authorities will compare declarations lodged for outgoing cargoes with underlying consignment data held by companies and logistics providers, traders said. The process is expected to include spot checks as well as a review of selected consignments for compliance with export rules and documentation standards.

A source familiar with the process said the administration’s aim was to “ensure consistency between customs filings and shipment particulars” following the changes to paperwork. The source added that companies would be expected to retain records that demonstrate how shipment figures were determined, including weights, grades and certificate information.

Separately, cobalt producers and trading firms have been monitoring broader regulatory and enforcement actions in the DRC, where concerns have periodically surfaced regarding fraud risks in documentation and the tracing of mineral origin. International buyers often rely on due diligence and chain-of-custody checks to meet supply requirements under procurement and compliance frameworks.

Traders said the audit plan is likely to affect near-term shipment handling as exporters adjust internal processes to ensure their declared figures match supporting documentation. Several firms also indicated they are preparing for potentially longer clearance timelines if audits are expanded beyond initial spot checks.

The DRC is a key source of globally mined cobalt, with output heavily linked to the country’s copper belt. Any tightening or clarification of export documentation procedures can influence logistics planning and contractual terms for shipments into refining and trading hubs.

headline: Russia begins force majeure at palladium refinery due to flooding (Mar 5)
body: Russia has begun a force majeure at its palladium refinery after flooding disrupted operations, according to industry sources citing notifications issued around Mar 5. The declaration covers shipments tied to impacted processing lines and is expected to affect delivery schedules for several weeks, sources said. The disruption follows heavy rainfall and water ingress at the refinery site, which operators reported required emergency shutdowns and pumping, with some equipment remaining offline into early March. Traders said the move raises near-term supply risk for palladium concentrates and refined metal, particularly where contracts reference refinery transfer windows. The force majeure also complicates invoicing and inspection timing for deliveries scheduled between Mar 10 and Mar 31, with some counterparties seeking extensions or alternative sourcing. Market participants expect the immediate impact to be concentrated in spot and short-cycle offtake, while longer-term contract negotiations are likely to reflect any prolonged downtime.

headline: Portugal delays tungsten shipment clearances after documentation mismatch (Apr 14)
body:
Portugal delayed customs clearance of a tungsten shipment in mid-April after officials identified a documentation mismatch, according to information from trade and shipping records seen by Reuters.

The shipment, described in export and transit documents as containing tungsten metal products, was held at a Portuguese port on April 14 pending clarification of details on the consignment, including quantities and product specifications, according to the records. Clearance was delayed as the documents submitted by the exporter did not match data recorded in the shipment manifest and supporting paperwork presented for import.

Customs officials requested amended documentation to reconcile differences between the invoice description and the technical references attached to the shipment, the records show. A mismatch was also flagged between the declared unit measures and the figures used for valuation and classification in the import paperwork, according to the same documentation. The shipment remained under hold status while the importer and broker worked with the exporter to provide corrected forms.

Portugal’s customs authority did not immediately respond to a request for comment. A representative for the logistics operator handling the transport declined to comment on the specific case but said companies typically resolve discrepancies by resubmitting corrected documents and responding to customs queries within set deadlines.

Tungsten is widely used in manufacturing, including in hard metal tooling and industrial components. Shipments of the metal are often subject to detailed checks because of tight controls on certain forms of tungsten and related industrial inputs, particularly when goods are routed through multiple jurisdictions.

Trade compliance delays can add days to transit times, especially when paperwork issues require reclassification or when technical specifications need to be verified. In this case, the mismatch concerned how the product was described across documents rather than indicating any immediate safety or regulatory breach, according to the records.

Customs clearance eventually proceeded after the parties submitted revised paperwork, the records indicate, though no information was provided on whether any penalties were imposed. The incident highlights how administrative discrepancies—such as differences in product naming, measurement units or classification codes—can disrupt international movements of industrial commodities, even when shipments are otherwise compliant.

headline: Poland halts iron ore loading at Baltic terminal after inspection findings (Feb 23)
body: Poland has paused iron ore loading at a Baltic Sea terminal after an inspection conducted on Feb. 23, according to authorities overseeing port operations. The halt affects shipments handled through the terminal under current loading procedures, with operators ordered to address findings identified during the inspection and submit corrective documentation before resuming activity. Port officials said the suspension was intended to ensure compliance with safety and handling requirements tied to bulk commodity operations, without providing an immediate end date. Inspection results circulated internally in the days following Feb. 23, and the loading pause took effect after verification of the issues on site. During the stop, rail and truck movements linked to scheduled ore batches are expected to slow, potentially shifting deliveries by several days into late February and early March. The decision comes as Baltic iron ore flows face steady logistical pressure from winter scheduling and vessel turnaround times.

headline: Turkey bans certain cadmium export lots pending environmental review (Mar 21)
body:
Turkey has banned the export of certain lots containing cadmium until an environmental review is completed, according to a notice published in the country’s official gazette on March 17.

The restriction applies to specific export shipments of cadmium-bearing material, with the ban intended to prevent further movement of products deemed subject to pending assessments of environmental impacts. The measure halts outbound trade for the designated lots rather than imposing a blanket prohibition on all cadmium exports, the notice said, indicating that compliance will be determined shipment-by-shipment.

Under the requirements set out in the notice, exporters must await the outcome of an environmental review process before resubmitting the affected lots for clearance. The review is expected to evaluate factors including the handling, storage and disposal risks associated with cadmium, a heavy metal widely used in some industrial applications but regulated in many jurisdictions due to its toxicity.

Turkey is also expanding the scope of regulatory scrutiny over hazardous substances as part of broader efforts to align with environmental controls and international chemical management standards. Cd-containing products are covered under hazardous materials frameworks, with authorities assessing both domestic risk management and the implications of cross-border transport and end use.

Authorities did not specify in the notice when the environmental review would conclude, but exporters affected by the ban are required to keep shipments from leaving Turkey. The measure also applies to any related documentation used for export declarations for the listed lots, according to the notice.

A copy of the official gazette announcement indicates the ban takes immediate effect for the designated export lots. Exporters are expected to cooperate with regulators to provide information needed for the review, including product composition and other details relevant to environmental risk assessments.

The restrictions come as regulators across Europe and elsewhere tighten rules for heavy metals and hazardous chemicals. Cadmium is subject to strict limits in several sectors, including battery production and industrial coatings, and exporters are increasingly required to demonstrate compliance with chemical and waste regulations.

Turkey’s ban is aimed at ensuring that any decisions on the future export of the affected cadmium lots follow completed environmental evaluations.

headline: Spain restricts bismuth sales quotas for domestic refining runs (Jan 27)
body: Spain has imposed tighter controls on bismuth sales by setting new quarterly quotas for domestic refining runs, effective for shipments scheduled from 1 April 2026, according to a ministry notice circulated on 27 January. The measure caps bismuth output eligible for sale under approved refining schedules, with the first allocation covering 1 April to 30 June and the second covering 1 July to 30 September. Refiners must submit monthly tonnage forecasts by the 5th of each month and provide batch-level assays before lifting any delivery restriction, with nonconforming material excluded from the quota. The rules apply to both primary bismuth and refined by-products from lead- and copper-stream smelting operations, and they require segregated inventory accounting for plants receiving spot deliveries. The government said the change aims to smooth domestic supply and reduce exposure to sudden spot-market pricing swings, while maintaining compliance with EU trade and stock-monitoring requirements. Industry participants said the quotas may delay contract shipments by 2 to 6 weeks if assay documentation volumes increase in March and early April.

headline: China holds gallium production at two plants after equipment failures and review (Feb 6)
body:
China has kept gallium output steady at two facilities after equipment failures prompted a production review, according to industry sources and company statements, according to information dated Feb. 6.

Gallium producers in the country have been under scrutiny because the metal is widely used in semiconductors and other high-tech products. Several firms have faced operational disruptions as demand fluctuates and supply chains remain sensitive to export controls and industrial capacity changes.

One of the affected sites is operated by a company in Guangdong province, where officials ordered an internal review after maintenance problems affected processing equipment. Production at the plant was held at its existing level while technicians replaced components and verified performance parameters, people familiar with operations said. The review focused on stabilizing yields and ensuring the recovery rate of gallium from upstream materials.

A second facility, located in the southwestern region of Guizhou, also maintained output after reported mechanical failures at parts of its refining line. Sources said the downtime did not fully shut production, but it reduced reliability for certain steps and triggered a temporary halt to upgrades until the issue was resolved. Operators said they tightened inspection schedules for pumps, filters and furnace controls and adjusted operating parameters to keep product quality within specifications.

In both cases, companies said they did not plan to announce changes in official capacity figures, instead describing the moves as operational contingencies. “We are conducting a technical review to restore equipment stability and ensure continuous compliance with product standards,” a spokesperson for one of the firms said in a statement provided to employees.

Market participants said the decision to keep output unchanged rather than suspend production entirely may limit near-term volatility in supply, though any prolonged repairs could still affect delivery schedules for buyers. Gallium is commonly sold under contracts tied to semiconductor demand, and even small shifts in production planning can influence spot availability.

The latest updates come as China remains the world’s main source of gallium supply. Companies have increasingly focused on maintenance and process optimization to reduce disruptions in an industry where refining runs depend on consistent feedstock quality and stable operating conditions. Further announcements are expected once the equipment reviews are completed and performance tests confirm normal operating conditions.

headline: Norway stops molybdenum shipments under contract pending new shipping rules (Apr 10)
body: Norway has stopped molybdenum shipments covered by existing supply contracts pending publication of revised shipping rules, industry sources said on April 10. The halt applies to deliveries scheduled to leave Norwegian ports under contracts that previously allowed shipments under the former licensing and transport requirements. Companies with cargoes already booked are seeking clarification on new compliance steps expected to take effect after the rule update, according to the sources, who cited administrative lead times for certificates and customs pre-notification. The change comes as spot and term pricing for molybdenum-linked products remain highly sensitive to timing of loadings, with some contracts tied to delivery windows measured in days rather than weeks. Shipments held back include consignments scheduled for mid-April and early May, reducing near-term export volumes from Norway while businesses rework documentation. Authorities have not indicated a ban beyond the contract-specific pause, but shippers said they will resume only after officials confirm that revised rule compliance will be accepted for cargoes under review.

headline: Canada pauses vanadium processing feed due to outage at crushing circuit (Mar 8)
body:
Canada has paused its vanadium processing feed after an outage at a crushing circuit at its plant, according to the company and industry sources, a disruption that is expected to limit ore throughput for the duration of repairs.

The pause was triggered when the crushing system, used to break down feed material before it enters downstream processing steps, stopped operating due to an equipment failure. As a result, the facility switched to a maintenance and troubleshooting mode, with the vanadium processing feed held back to prevent downstream units from running without sufficient feed, sources said.

A company spokesperson said in a brief statement that the site is working to restore the crushing circuit and assess the cause of the outage. “We are pausing the vanadium processing feed while we address the crushing circuit failure and complete safety checks,” the spokesperson said. The spokesperson did not provide a timeline for when the feed would resume.

Vanadium is typically recovered from mined ore through a series of crushing, leaching and separation processes, depending on the source of the feed and the plant’s flowsheet. The crushing step is generally a critical path activity because it determines how quickly feed can be prepared for chemical processing. When the circuit is unavailable, operators often reduce or halt production to avoid stockpiling issues and unbalanced inventory across processing stages.

The operational interruption comes as companies in the battery supply chain and steelmaking markets closely monitor vanadium production schedules, given ongoing demand tied to energy storage and high-strength steel grades. Any reduction in output can tighten availability in the near term, particularly when outages occur during periods of already constrained supply.

Industry sources said the immediate impact is likely limited to curtailed feed rather than a full plant shutdown, although the duration will depend on the extent of damage to components in the crushing circuit and the time required to clear and restart the equipment safely. Operators are expected to publish a revised operating plan once the repair work is complete and performance tests confirm the circuit is running at designed capacity.

headline: Kazakhstan issues temporary export limit on refined tungsten products (Feb 16)
body: Kazakhstan has imposed a temporary export limit on refined tungsten products effective Feb. 16, according to local authorities, in a move aimed at managing supply for domestic processing and stabilizing availability for downstream users. The measure covers refined tungsten metal and select tungsten compounds shipped for export, with volumes capped under a licensing regime rather than a blanket ban. Exporters must apply for permits through a designated government platform, with approvals tied to reported production runs and inventory levels as of late January 2026. The restriction runs for a limited period pending a reassessment of market conditions, with authorities indicating adjustments could be made based on demand forecasts and import needs from partner countries. Trade flows are expected to tighten immediately, particularly for buyers seeking spot deliveries of refined grades used in hardmetals, aerospace and electronics supply chains. The government said the policy is intended to reduce volatility in refined product availability while maintaining uninterrupted domestic supply, and it comes as global tungsten pricing remains sensitive to China-led refining dynamics.

headline: South Korea refinery reports copper anode delays after refinery fire (Apr 2)
body:
A South Korean copper refinery reported delays in supplying copper anodes after a fire at one of its facilities earlier this week, according to the company’s latest update, with operations disrupted as repairs and safety checks continue.

The refinery, operated by a major domestic metals producer, said the blaze affected parts of its anode production line, delaying output and deliveries to customer smelters and traders. The firm did not specify the volume of output lost, but it said it was prioritizing the return of affected equipment to normal operating conditions.

In a statement accompanying its report, the company attributed the disruption to damage from the fire and follow-up inspections required to confirm the affected systems were safe to restart. It said production scheduling would be adjusted to reflect the downtime, and that it was coordinating with customers on revised delivery timelines.

The fire prompted emergency response crews to contain the incident and assess damage across the plant, according to industry sources familiar with the matter. The refinery subsequently implemented precautionary measures, including halting related processing steps to prevent secondary incidents while repairs were underway.

Copper anodes are a key intermediate product used in electrolytic refining, and delays can tighten supply for downstream cathode makers that rely on steady input. Traders typically monitor disruptions at smelters and refineries because even short production interruptions can affect near-term availability and pricing expectations in physical markets, especially when multiple facilities are already operating under maintenance schedules.

The company said it would provide further updates as inspections progress and as equipment is brought back online in stages. It also indicated that it is working to stabilize production rates to limit the duration of the anode shortfall.

South Korea is a major hub for copper refining and fabrication in Asia, with domestic capacity feeding regional demand for copper cathodes used in power and electronics. Industry observers said it will become clearer later whether the refinery’s delay is limited to a single line or whether additional areas of the facility were impacted.

headline: Chile announces force majeure at Collahuasi copper operations amid water shortage (May 8)
body: Chile’s government has declared force majeure affecting operations at Compañía Minera Doña Inés de Collahuasi after the company reported constraints from an ongoing water shortage, according to an announcement dated May 8. The measure applies to copper output and related activities at the Collahuasi facilities in the Tarapacá region, where water availability has been reduced amid drought conditions. Collahuasi has previously signaled that it is prioritizing production schedules around limited water access, which has affected ore processing and ancillary services. The force majeure designation, effective immediately upon notification under local rules, is intended to cover delays or interruptions that are beyond the operator’s control. Industry sources said the action could contribute to near-term variability in concentrate and cathode delivery timelines through the second quarter, depending on water allocations and commissioning of alternative supply measures. Chilean regulators indicated that compliance checks and revised operational plans will be reviewed on a rolling basis as conditions evolve.

headline: Zimbabwe declares ban on unprocessed graphite exports to boost domestic refining (Mar 14)
body:
Zimbabwe has declared a ban on exports of unprocessed graphite, seeking to force miners to sell ore for further processing at home, the government said, in a move aimed at expanding domestic refining capacity.

The restriction, announced on March 14, applies to graphite “in its raw form” and is intended to ensure that value is added within Zimbabwe rather than overseas, according to a statement released by the Ministry of Mines and Mining Development. Authorities said the policy is part of broader efforts to develop local downstream industries for critical minerals used in batteries and industrial applications.

Graphite exporters will be required to supply product that has been processed domestically, with compliance tied to conditions set by the ministry. The government said it is working with industry stakeholders to establish timelines and licensing arrangements for existing exporters, while new approvals will be contingent on processing commitments and permitted output levels.

Officials did not provide the immediate effect on production volumes, but the ministry said the ban is designed to increase investment in refining, improve product quality and strengthen Zimbabwe’s position in global graphite supply chains. The policy is also expected to support the creation of jobs in processing and related services, the statement said.

Zimbabwe is seeking to capitalize on its graphite resources as demand grows for battery materials used in electric vehicles and energy storage. The country has several graphite projects at different stages of development, and companies have in recent years sought offtake agreements with international buyers for both raw and processed material.

Industry players have argued that local processing is constrained by access to equipment, power and permitting, and by the costs of meeting specifications required by customers. In the ministry statement, Zimbabwe said it will continue engaging with companies to address implementation issues and ensure that supply contracts can be adjusted to meet the new requirements.

The ban comes as Zimbabwe continues to revise mineral marketing and export rules across multiple commodities, aiming to increase local beneficiation. It is expected to be phased in through regulatory measures and enforcement actions, with penalties for non-compliance to be set out in subsequent guidance, the ministry said.

headline: U.S. tariffs slow imports of tin and antimony; supply tightens (Feb 1)
body: U.S. tariffs that took effect around Feb. 1 have slowed imports of tin and antimony, tightening availability for metal buyers and processors, according to market trackers. Import activity into the United States weakened after the tariff measures, with spot deliveries of both refined tin and antimony ore and metal reported as delayed at terminals and in freight schedules. Traders cited reduced landed volumes over the first two weeks of February, with sellers shifting toward alternative routes and currencies to preserve margins. In the background, global supply remains constrained by limited secondary material availability for antimony and by slower output ramp-ups from several smelters supplying tin. Benchmark pricing moved unevenly as contracts rolled, and some exchanges widened bid-ask spreads on lower physical offer depth. One-week lead times for certain lots reportedly stretched to 3–5 weeks by late February, affecting alloy makers and chemical producers that rely on steady inputs.

headline: South Africa unions extend nickel strike affecting Nkomati and related logistics (Jan 11)
body:
South Africa’s unions have extended a strike affecting nickel operations at the Nkomati complex and related logistics, prolonging disruptions to production and shipments, the parties involved said on Tuesday.

The strike, which began earlier this month at Nkomati, has been extended to continue negotiations over wages and working conditions, union representatives said. Workers at the site, operated under a joint arrangement, have been refusing to return to work, according to statements from labour organisations monitoring the stoppage.

The walkout has also spilled into transport and handling functions linked to the plant, with trucking and warehouse operations operating at reduced capacity, logistics providers and union officials said. Movement of materials used in refining and maintenance work has been constrained, while export scheduling has been adjusted to account for the reduced flow of product from the facility, they said.

Nkomati is one of South Africa’s important nickel-producing assets, and the strike has implications beyond the mine itself due to the way nickel shipments are managed through regional ports and specialized freight. Industry sources said shipping windows depend on stock availability and that delays can compound if production remains suspended for extended periods.

Unions said they have continued talks with management but that progress has been insufficient to end the strike. “We have communicated our demands clearly and we are waiting for a substantive response that addresses workers’ concerns,” a union spokesperson said, adding that members would not resume work until negotiations reach an agreed outcome.

The company operating Nkomati and subcontractors involved in logistics did not immediately provide a detailed timeline for when operations could normalize. Disruptions are also affecting contractors that rely on consistent site access for scheduled services, such as safety inspections, maintenance support and fleet dispatch, union representatives said.

The extension increases uncertainty for customers and counterparties who use nickel from the region in industrial supply chains. It also raises the risk of further adjustments to freight bookings and inventory levels among smelters and traders depending on product availability, particularly if the strike lasts into subsequent shipping cycles.

headline: Peru cancels zirconium shipment permits after failing assay compliance (Apr 18)
body: Peru has canceled zirconium shipment permits after failing assay compliance checks, according to authorities, in a decision dated Apr. 18. The action affects permits tied to overseas deliveries scheduled in the second quarter, with the affected material categorized under zirconium-related exports subject to assay verification before loading. Officials said inspections found discrepancies between reported assay results and laboratory measurements, triggering the permit cancellations and halting further transport for the implicated consignments. The compliance review followed routine port screening and document audits conducted over several days in early April, with final lab results delivered after the initial submissions were flagged. Industry participants said the cancellations could delay shipments by weeks, depending on whether importers pursue re-testing or new documentation. No information was released on total volume, shipment names, or recipient countries in the initial notification.
