Authorities said the case will proceed under Peru’s export compliance procedures, which allow re-application only after corrected assay evidence is provided.

headline: Indonesia extends nickel export suspension for second month (Apr 29)
body:
Indonesia has extended its suspension of nickel ore exports for a second straight month, according to a government notice, as the country keeps in place restrictions meant to push investment in domestic refining.

The extension, effective for the next month, maintains the ban on shipments of nickel ore and certain intermediate products that require downstream processing under Indonesia’s export rules, the notice showed. The measures are designed to support the growth of smelters and to increase the supply of refined nickel for batteries used in electric vehicles and other applications.

Indonesia previously suspended nickel ore exports in early 2024 and has periodically extended and adjusted the restrictions. The latest step comes as Jakarta has sought to ensure companies complete planned capacity expansions before resuming exports of raw or semi-processed material. Under the framework, exporters can only ship products that comply with processing requirements, with the government monitoring whether firms meet the conditions.

The decision affects producers that have been preparing to restart shipments but have to comply with the suspension timeline. It also shapes expectations for global nickel supply, although Indonesia’s nickel sector has continued to produce refined material for export, supported by existing smelting operations.

Officials have linked the temporary bans to enforcement of the “downstreaming” policy. By tightening access to export markets for ore, the government aims to increase the share of nickel processed domestically, while also encouraging companies to build or upgrade processing facilities that can handle ore at higher purity levels.

Industry players have said the suspension has been used as leverage to accelerate construction and ramp-up schedules, while also raising short-term uncertainty for trade flows. Companies typically adjust by shifting output toward products that remain eligible for export or by accelerating downstream processing.

The government said the extension is part of ongoing implementation of nickel export regulations, without providing details on any change to the policy design. It reiterated that permits and shipments must follow the relevant requirements in force during the suspension period.

Further guidance on compliance and any revisions to export eligibility were expected through the regular administrative process, with companies watching for signals on when ore shipments may resume after the current extension ends.

headline: Ecuador reports selenium output curbs after corrective maintenance at refinery (Mar 7)
body: Ecuador reported reduced selenium output after completing corrective maintenance at its primary refinery, with production curbs starting on Mar 7 and affecting processing through the week of Mar 10, according to industry contacts. Output declines were linked to downtime tied to scheduled and unplanned repairs in at least one unit used for selenium recovery, following operator adjustments aimed at stabilising extraction yields. The stoppage and commissioning reportedly tightened available feedstock for the refinery’s selenium stream, lowering daily volumes versus pre-maintenance runs that had been scheduled through early March. Volumes were expected to ramp only gradually, with full throughput targeted after equipment checks and performance verification completed by mid-March. Market participants said the tighter supply window could influence spot availability for selenium products in regional markets over the next 1–2 weeks, depending on how quickly recovery targets are met. The maintenance timeline and the magnitude of the reduction were not formally quantified in public statements as of Mar 17.

headline: China restricts uranium exports for enrichment-related compliance checks (May 12)
body:
China has tightened controls on uranium exports by requiring additional compliance checks tied to enrichment-related end use, according to a government notice issued this week.

The directive, published on Wednesday by China’s Ministry of Commerce, updates the procedures exporters must follow when shipping uranium and certain uranium-related materials. It introduces a review step intended to verify whether shipments are linked to enrichment activities or other fuel-cycle processes, and it applies to transactions that fall under China’s licensing and sanctions-screening framework.

Under the updated requirements, exporters must submit documentation showing the intended end use, the identity of the end user, and details of the receiving facility before export approval is granted. The notice also calls for checks against licensing conditions and related trade restrictions, including export control rules and list-based counterparty screening. Where enrichment-related use is suspected, authorities may require supplementary information or additional verification before clearing the shipment.

A separate section of the notice describes how compliance reviews will be conducted, including coordination between commercial authorities and relevant agencies responsible for nuclear export supervision. The process is designed to ensure that exported materials are not diverted to activities prohibited under Chinese regulations and international obligations, the document said.

The rules come as global nuclear commerce remains under heightened scrutiny due to proliferation concerns and the monitoring of uranium supply chains. China, which plays a significant role in uranium processing and nuclear fuel services, has previously modified its export-control practices in response to regulatory changes and changes in end-use monitoring standards, according to industry compliance procedures.

Regulators did not specify in the notice how often enriched-related verifications would be triggered or how long reviews would take. Exporters said in recent filings that they expect the new steps to increase administrative time for certain shipments, particularly where documentation for enrichment-linked end use is incomplete.

The Ministry of Commerce statement did not name specific destinations or companies affected by the update. It said the notice is aimed at improving “compliance checks” for uranium exports and standardising information requirements for licensing, with enforcement carried out through the existing export permit system.

headline: Indonesia imposes export ban on nickel ore after government review April 2026
body: Indonesia has imposed a ban on the export of nickel ore after a government review concluded in March 2026, according to officials and trade documents reviewed on 18 March. The measure halts shipments of unprocessed nickel ore from Indonesia’s main producing regions from 1 April 2026, affecting contracts for both domestic and overseas buyers that rely on raw material deliveries. The decision follows a revision of Indonesia’s nickel downstream policy, which sets processing requirements tied to investment plans and the development of smelters and refining capacity through 2026. Industry sources said the ban targets grades commonly traded as nickel ore concentrates, while exports of certain processed intermediate products may continue under permit conditions. The review also referenced the country’s progress on meeting output milestones for ferronickel and nickel matte, with inspection schedules starting 10 April 2026 for shipments scheduled before the cutoff. The export restriction is expected to tighten global supply for raw nickel feedstock even as Indonesian producers ramp up higher-grade processing output.

headline: DRC regulators suspend cobalt shipments amid mine safety probe and port congestion
body:
Democratic Republic of Congo regulators have suspended cobalt shipments from parts of the industry as authorities probe mine safety incidents and contend with congestion at export ports, according to officials and industry sources.

The measures, announced by regulators over the past several days, affect producers and exporters operating in cobalt supply chains that rely on trucking to ports or bulk handling at coastal terminals. The suspension does not cover all shipments nationwide, sources said, but it targets specific routes and facilities while investigators review compliance with safety and transport rules.

A government statement circulated to companies said the suspension is linked to “ongoing verifications” at industrial sites following reports of accidents and safety lapses. It also cited the need to ensure documentation and transport arrangements are consistent with export requirements. Regulators have asked companies to halt loading of cobalt until they receive clearance.

At the same time, port congestion has built up at key points for exports, according to several freight and trading participants. They said delays in container handling and customs clearance have created backlogs, prompting authorities to tighten controls to prevent further accumulation of shipments. Some exporters have faced restrictions on dispatch schedules as clearance processes are reviewed.

One exporter said the suspension has disrupted planned deliveries and that shipments already on the route have faced uncertainty about final loading. “We are complying with the instructions from the regulator and waiting for confirmation that the facilities and documentation meet the required checks,” the exporter said, speaking on condition of anonymity because of ongoing compliance negotiations.

The cobalt sector in the DRC, the world’s largest producer, has been repeatedly scrutinized after deadly incidents involving informal mining and lapses in safety at industrial and semi-industrial operations. Regulators have previously targeted illegal sites and unsafe working conditions, while also pressing companies to improve traceability for buyers.

Industry sources said the suspension is likely to affect near-term volumes and could shift delivery timelines into subsequent weeks if inspections are extended. Companies have been instructed to keep stockpiles segregated and maintain audit trails for shipments, while regulators conduct field visits and review loading records.

A number of firms are expected to re-enter the export channel only after inspectors confirm that safety protocols are in place and that port and customs procedures can accommodate shipments without triggering further backlogs, the sources said.

headline: Chile declares temporary force majeure at Escondida copper operations due to water shortage
body: Chile’s government declared temporary force majeure for water-related disruptions at Minera Escondida’s copper operations, citing sustained water shortages across the Antofagasta region. The measure, issued on 2026-03-18, allows the operator to temporarily suspend or adjust contractual obligations tied to uninterrupted water supply and related industrial processes while alternative sourcing is arranged. Escondida, the world’s largest copper mine, has faced tightening availability of surface and groundwater after prolonged drought conditions and stricter restrictions on water withdrawals. The declaration applies to affected facilities and activities at the site, which produces millions of tonnes of copper annually, though the government did not immediately specify an output target during the period covered by the waiver. Companies and service providers are expected to review force majeure clauses with counterparties for force majeure-driven delays spanning loading, processing, and maintenance schedules. Water authorities have continued monitoring industrial drawdowns, with further operational guidance anticipated as the shortage evolves.

headline: Peru copper union strike halts shipments at major coastal terminal on May 14
body:
A copper union strike in Peru halted shipments at a major coastal export terminal on May 14, according to dock and company sources, as workers protested labor and contract issues affecting mining operations.

The stoppage centered on the Port of Callao area, where ships scheduled to load copper concentrates and related products were delayed or stood at anchor after union members stopped work at loading facilities, the sources said. Several vessels scheduled between early morning and midday could not berth, while terminal operators limited access to equipment and loading crews, they added.

The union action was initially scheduled for the day but expanded into a broader slowdown at the terminal after workers refused to handle cargo, according to one port official who requested anonymity because they were not authorized to speak to media. “The strike is impacting loading operations and dispatch schedules,” the official said, adding that the work stoppage would continue while negotiations remain unresolved.

Companies with shipments tied to the terminal said they were adjusting logistics to comply with port restrictions. Industry sources said inventories of concentrates at mines could be drawn down temporarily, but continued delays risked further disruptions to export timetables and could affect customer deliveries abroad.

Peru is one of the world’s largest copper producers, and labor actions in mining and ports can quickly ripple into global supply flows given tight shipping schedules. The May 14 stoppage comes amid ongoing scrutiny of labor arrangements across the sector, with unions seeking assurances on employment terms, job security and workplace conditions.

Negotiations between union representatives and employers were ongoing as of late May 14, according to the port official. Terminal personnel said they were monitoring developments and would resume normal loading only after workers returned to their posts and clearance processes were completed.

The latest disruption underscores the sensitivity of Peru’s export chain to industrial disputes, particularly when strikes coincide with scheduled vessel movements at key coastal terminals.

headline: Zambia orders emergency inspections at copper smelters after power instability April 2026
body: Zambia has ordered emergency inspections of copper smelters after electricity instability in April 2026 disrupted operations and raised safety concerns, according to regulators. The directive, issued in mid-March 2026, requires smelter operators to inspect furnaces, gas handling systems, and cooling circuits within 72 hours and report findings by March 25. Power fluctuations, which began in early April and continued intermittently over several weeks, forced temporary slowdowns at multiple facilities and increased the risk of equipment overheating and uncontrolled emissions. The Mines Safety Department said violations of permit limits for sulfur dioxide and particulate matter could trigger suspension of processing lines. Smelter operators are also being asked to review backup power arrangements, including uninterruptible systems and diesel generation capacity, and to verify alarm and shutoff mechanisms. Zambia is a major copper producer, and officials said continuity of refining is critical as concentrate supply remains tight, while grid upgrades planned for late 2026 may reduce future outages.

headline: Vietnam tightens tungsten import licensing as customs backlog delays consignments May 2026
body:
Vietnam has tightened rules for importing tungsten, a move aimed at controlling supply as customs processing delays have held up consignments since late May 2026, according to industry sources and government documents.

The Ministry of Industry and Trade issued updated guidance requiring importers of tungsten products and intermediate materials to complete additional licensing steps before shipments enter the country. Under the revised process, firms must submit batch-level documentation covering end use, product specifications and declared origin, customs officials said, adding that approvals may be issued in narrower time windows than under prior rules.

Traders said the changes come after a backlog at Vietnam’s customs offices caused longer clearance times for rare and specialty metals, including tungsten concentrates, tungsten carbide scrap and related feedstock. Several consignments reportedly arrived at ports but could not be cleared for distribution due to requests for supplementary paperwork and verification of product classifications under Vietnam’s tariff schedule.

Customs delays have been most acute at major entry points handling bulk metals and industrial components, according to logistics providers. They said the backlog has extended demurrage periods and increased the cost of warehousing while importers waited for release. Some shipments were also held pending clarification of whether certain tungsten-containing products fall under separate import categories, affecting both licensing and duties.

A representative for one trading firm, who asked not to be identified because of commercial sensitivity, said the new licensing requirements are intended to reduce repeated documentation checks at the border. “The additional submissions we provide up front are helping customs reconcile product descriptions earlier, which should shorten clearance once shipments land,” the person said.

Vietnam’s policy tightening follows broader government efforts to manage strategic industrial materials and ensure that imports align with domestic demand for manufacturing inputs. Tungsten is used in cutting tools, hard metal components and mining equipment, and Vietnam’s electronics and industrial sectors rely on steady supplies from international markets.

Industry participants said they expect the revised licensing rules to affect lead times for new orders and may shift buying toward suppliers able to provide tighter specifications and supporting certificates. Companies importing through established channels said they are preparing for longer pre-shipment processing, even as they continue to track customs release timelines in real time to avoid further accumulation of delayed stock.

headline: China auctions additional graphite export quotas after refinery maintenance reduces output
body: China has launched another round of graphite export quota auctions after refinery maintenance reduced output, according to market notices issued on 2026-03-18. The additional quota allocation targets shipments from state-linked refiners whose production rates fell during maintenance completed over late February and early March, with inventories tightened into mid-March. Auction terms specify loading periods beginning in April 2026, with buyers required to submit end-use and destination information before award notices. Export rights were made available in multiple lots covering both high-purity and battery-grade material, reflecting separate demand streams from anode supply chains and industrial friction and casting uses. The new sale follows earlier auctions in January and February that were partially oversubscribed, contributing to tighter physical availability. Traders said the replenishment should stabilize spot premiums over the second quarter, though final pricing is likely to remain sensitive to whether additional maintenance cycles extend beyond the current April start.

headline: South Africa power outage cuts platinum concentrate deliveries for 10 days in March 2026
body:
A power outage in South Africa has cut platinum concentrate deliveries for 10 days in March 2026, disrupting logistics for producers and traders, according to industry participants.

Several mining and processing operations were affected when electricity supply was interrupted and industrial power loads were curtailed during the period, the participants said. The outage forced some facilities to reduce throughput and delayed shipments from concentrators to rail and port routes, they said, with the shortfall concentrated in March rather than spread evenly across the quarter.

Platinum concentrate is commonly produced in South Africa’s primary mining basins and then transported to refineries for further processing. Output interruptions can quickly translate into fewer consignments, as concentrate is typically moved under tight scheduling requirements that align with refinery intake and shipping windows.

“One consequence was that concentrate couldn’t be moved or accumulated at the same rate, so offtake shipments were delayed for about a week to 10 days,” a metals trader said, declining to be named because of ongoing commercial discussions. The trader said the disruption affected delivery volumes but that the impact was expected to be worked through as schedules normalized.

The participants said the timing of the outage, which coincided with routine maintenance planning at some sites, compounded the effect on deliveries. While inventories at certain facilities helped cushion the immediate loss of production, the ability to replace shipments depended on how quickly rail movements and loading operations resumed.

South African electricity constraints have periodically affected mining operations in recent years, leading to efforts such as investing in backup generation, adjusting production schedules and improving energy management. Industry participants said no single mine or company was responsible for the full interruption, characterising it instead as a broader supply disruption that ran across parts of the sector.

For the market, shorter concentrate deliveries can influence near-term inventory levels at refineries and traders’ warehouses, potentially affecting spot premiums and contract scheduling, participants said. They added that deliveries were already catching up after the outage ended, with follow-on shipments being rescheduled into the second half of March and early April.

headline: Russia raises fees for palladium product deliveries, citing logistics disruptions Feb 2026
body: Russia’s metals exporters have raised fees for palladium product deliveries, according to notices issued in mid-February 2026 and circulated to counterparties in March, citing ongoing logistics disruptions. The increases apply to spot and contract shipments scheduled from late February through May, with supplemental charges added to base transport and handling rates for deliveries routed via rail and bonded warehousing. The notices link the higher costs to reduced freight capacity, border processing delays, and tighter documentation checks along key corridors, including routes through western transit points and export hubs. Fees also vary by destination and delivery window, with the steepest adjustments tied to near-term transport slots and longer layover times at origin. Traders said the change effectively shifts additional working-cost risk to buyers, particularly for industrial users reliant on continuous palladium supply. The developments come as market participants monitor Russia’s role in global palladium flows and reassess pricing assumptions for refined and alloy-grade product deliveries during the first quarter of 2026.

headline: Oman’s force majeure delays uranium concentrate loading at Sohar port in January 2026
body:
Oman has delayed the loading of uranium concentrate at the Sohar port in January 2026 because of force majeure circumstances, according to shipping and logistics documents reviewed by industry sources.

The delay affected shipments that were scheduled to load uranium concentrate during the period, pushing loading to later dates, the sources said. The paperwork indicates the interruption was tied to circumstances beyond the control of the contracted parties, a common clause used in shipping contracts when events disrupt port operations or cargo handling.

Sohar Port, on Oman’s northern coast, is a key hub for bulk and industrial commodities, including materials shipped for energy and metals supply chains. Uranium concentrate shipments are typically transported in covered bulk systems or containers depending on the customer and the approved handling procedures, with documentation closely coordinated between shippers, freight forwarders and port operators.

One of the documents cited by a source described the force majeure notice as being issued after operational issues emerged that prevented work to proceed as planned. The notice referenced the interruption to loading operations but did not specify in the reviewed material the precise cause, such as mechanical problems, weather disruptions, labor constraints or disruptions to receiving logistics.

The delay did not change the underlying contracts, the sources said, but it altered delivery schedules and associated demurrage or laytime calculations. Parties often seek amendments to loading windows when port activity slows, to account for repositioning of vessels, rescheduling of inland transport and the availability of storage facilities for bulk materials.

Industry participants said the January disruption could have had downstream timing impacts for utilities and traders that rely on monthly or quarterly shipment windows. Uranium concentrate supply chains are generally managed through contracted delivery milestones, with rescheduling sometimes handled through alternative transport arrangements or by shifting quantities to subsequent loading periods.

Neither the port operator nor the companies involved in the shipments immediately provided a detailed explanation for the January force majeure notice in the reviewed documents. The matter highlights how even short-term disruptions in port handling can ripple through regulated and tightly scheduled raw material movements, particularly for nuclear fuel inputs.

headline: Namibia halts beryllium ore processing lines after reagent supply disruption May 2026
body: Namibia has halted processing at its beryllium ore plants after a disruption in reagent supplies, according to industry sources cited by operators. The stoppage affects processing lines scheduled to run through May 2026 and comes as the facilities rely on consistent deliveries of cleaning and leaching reagents typically sourced under multi-month logistics windows. Company schedules indicated inventory cover of roughly 2 to 3 weeks as of mid-March, but the latest shipment was delayed beyond its expected arrival window in early March. Production has been paused across the affected circuits, with staff reassigned to maintenance and water-treatment checks rather than active recovery. The disruption is expected to affect output volumes for the April–May period, with ripple effects on downstream sales into specialty metal and aerospace-grade segments. No firm restart date was provided, though operators said equipment readiness checks could take 5 to 10 days once reagent supply resumes.

headline: Kazakhstan declares state of emergency at zinc-adjacent refinery handling germanium sludges
body:
Kazakhstan has declared a state of emergency at a refinery that processes zinc-related materials, following concerns about handling and storage of germanium-bearing sludges, according to officials and emergency services.

The emergency was announced on Wednesday in the industrial district of the facility, which is part of a wider complex involved in non-ferrous metal production. Authorities cited the need to ensure safe operations while additional inspections and stabilization measures are carried out for waste streams containing germanium compounds.

The government said the declaration allows for an accelerated response by regional authorities, including the temporary tightening of rules governing transport, unloading and treatment of slurry and sludge materials. It also enables the rapid deployment of specialized personnel and equipment to monitor air quality, worker safety conditions and the integrity of storage infrastructure.

Local emergency services said they have established an expanded safety perimeter around sludge handling and treatment areas and increased monitoring for potential releases. Testing of environmental samples, including soil and water near the plant boundary, is planned over the coming days, officials said, adding that contingency measures are in place to redirect material to designated holding units if irregularities are detected.

A statement from the emergency management agency said the situation is being assessed as a precautionary measure and that the goal is to prevent any escalation while technical experts evaluate the cause of the concern that prompted the emergency declaration. “Work is continuing under enhanced control procedures, and monitoring is being stepped up to verify the absence of harmful emissions,” the agency said.

The refinery’s operations are linked to germanium recovery, a process that often involves complex filtration and chemical treatment steps. Germanium, a critical material used in fiber optics and semiconductor applications, is typically obtained from byproducts associated with zinc and other metal refining. Sludges from such treatment can contain concentrated compounds requiring careful containment and disposal.

Kazakhstan is one of the world’s significant producers of germanium, and changes to production and waste handling can affect both supply planning and downstream customers. Authorities did not provide figures on output disruption, but they said plant operations would continue under an updated safety regime during the emergency period. The term and conditions for lifting the state of emergency will be determined after the inspection results are reviewed, officials added.

headline: China imposes temporary anti-dumping duties on indium intermediates following import surge
body: China’s finance ministry has imposed temporary anti-dumping duties on indium intermediates after a rapid import surge, according to an official notice dated March 18, 2026. The measures apply to specified indium compounds used in downstream refining and electronics supply chains, following an investigation triggered by evidence of dumping practices and a material increase in imports during the review period. The duties run for an initial period of up to several months while a final determination is pending, with rates set by exporter and product category. Authorities said the actions are intended to prevent further price undercutting and protect domestic producers from injury linked to the increased volumes. The ministry did not provide an immediate estimate of the duty impact on global indium prices, but it cited continued volatility in the metal’s supply-demand balance. Industry sources said the decision could affect procurement planning for smelters and wafer and thin-film manufacturers that rely on intermediates.

headline: Japan signs long-term molybdenum offtake as supplier delays due to strike in April
body:
Japan has signed a long-term offtake agreement for molybdenum after a supplier delay linked to an April strike, according to industry sources familiar with the deal.

The Japanese buyer agreed to take deliveries of molybdenum concentrate or refined molybdenum over multiple years under terms intended to secure supply stability, the sources said. The contract follows earlier schedule slips caused by work stoppages at a mine or processing facility in the supplier’s chain in April, when output and logistics were affected, they added.

Molybdenum is used in steelmaking and industrial equipment, including pressure vessels and machinery, and it is also incorporated into some alloys for high-temperature applications. Japan relies on imports to meet most of its needs, making long-term offtake arrangements a key tool for managing procurement risk.

Under the new arrangement, shipments are expected to resume and run on a more predictable timeline, with pricing tied to market benchmarks and applicable adjustments for product quality and delivery timing, the sources said. They declined to name the companies involved, citing confidentiality provisions in the contract.

The supply disruption in April had delayed initial deliveries and forced temporary re-sourcing, which elevated lead times for downstream processors. Several buyers in Japan had sought alternative volumes while working through extended logistics and inventory drawdowns, according to the sources.

One molybdenum market participant said the offtake deal is aimed at reducing the likelihood of repeat disruptions and aligning quantities with planned furnace and alloy production schedules. “The long-term term supports planning for both volumes and logistics after the earlier disturbance,” the participant said.

The agreement comes as global molybdenum markets navigate fluctuating production and demand patterns tied to steel output and infrastructure spending. While the April strike was described as a temporary disruption, the contract’s length suggests buyers are placing greater emphasis on contractual security rather than solely short-term spot procurement.

Regulatory filings and company statements were not immediately available. The parties involved did not respond to requests for comment.

headline: Canada’s nickel producer issues force majeure on concentrate sales after wildfire damages roads
body: Canada’s nickel producer issued force majeure affecting concentrate sales after wildfire damage disrupted road transport in its operating region, the company said on Thursday. The notice covers shipments scheduled for March 18–31 from a processing site to third-party buyers, citing blocked or unsafe routes caused by fire activity and related evacuation measures. Company officials said access to key haul roads was reduced after the blaze damaged bridges and culverts, with authorities issuing temporary restrictions for certain stretches of highway and industrial access lanes. The disruption is expected to constrain trucking capacity and delay weigh-ins at contracted receiving terminals, which may extend into April depending on debris removal and inspections. The producer did not specify the volume impacted but said product availability will depend on alternative logistics, including rerouting to rail where feasible. Buyers are being advised to treat orders under existing offtake terms as subject to force majeure relief for delivery shortfalls resulting from road closures, according to the notice.

headline: Mongolia suspends tungsten mine operations following regulator revocation of environmental permits
body:
Mongolia has suspended operations at a major tungsten mine after the government regulator revoked the company’s environmental permits, according to a statement from the operator.

The suspension affects the mine’s current operations and related activities, the company said, adding that it would halt production while it seeks clarification and works to address the regulatory decision. Mongolian authorities have cited environmental compliance requirements tied to the permits, which cover aspects of mining-related impacts and mitigation measures.

The regulator’s action follows a review of the environmental documentation and compliance record associated with the mine, the company said in its statement. It did not specify whether the revocation was linked to a particular inspection finding, nor did it provide details on the scope of the permit withdrawal. The suspension does not immediately extend to other assets owned by the operator, the company said, though it indicated that restart timelines would depend on the outcome of discussions with regulators.

Tungsten production is strategically important for Mongolia, which is one of the world’s significant suppliers of the metal used in hard materials, industrial cutting tools and defense-related applications. Industry analysts often track Mongolia’s output closely because the country’s mining sector is sensitive to permitting and environmental enforcement, which can affect production continuity and supply schedules.

Local environmental and oversight rules in Mongolia require companies to hold valid environmental authorizations before beginning or continuing operations that can affect land, water and air quality. When permits are revoked, operators are typically required to suspend affected activities and may face conditions for reinstatement, including remediation plans, additional monitoring and updated impact assessments.

A representative for the company said the decision was “a significant regulatory development,” adding that the firm was engaging with authorities to understand the basis of the permit revocation and the steps needed to resume operations. The operator said it remained committed to meeting environmental requirements and to providing requested information to the regulator.

Mongolian officials have not publicly detailed the mine-specific reasons for the revocation in the information available to Reuters. The regulator’s decision, however, signals tighter scrutiny of environmental compliance within the mining sector, as Mongolia seeks to balance resource development with environmental protection obligations.

headline: Morocco halts vanadium pentoxide exports for repairs to rail link starting June 1
body: Morocco will halt exports of vanadium pentoxide from its main production hubs for scheduled maintenance on a rail link, beginning June 1, according to industry sources. The suspension is planned to allow repairs and upgrades to rail infrastructure used for moving concentrate and refinery feed to ports, where shipments are typically loaded within the first half of each month. The measure is expected to run through the maintenance window, with timing subject to track and inspection outcomes over the following weeks. Traders said the interruption could tighten prompt availability for high-grade vanadium pentoxide used in steel additives and in the production of vanadium-based energy storage materials. Morocco supplies a meaningful share of global volumes, and logistics disruptions can shift buying to alternative origins or prompt stock draws. If the restart on the rail line slips beyond early June, spot prices for intermediate vanadium compounds could face additional upward pressure across Asian and European trading desks.

headline: India restricts gallium shipments temporarily as domestic semiconductor buyers negotiate quotas
body:
India has temporarily restricted shipments of gallium as domestic buyers negotiate supply quotas for the metal used in semiconductors and other high-technology products, according to government and industry sources.

The restriction, issued in the form of temporary controls on exports and shipments, aims to ensure adequate availability of gallium for local processing and manufacturing while buyers agree on the quantities they will receive over the coming months, the sources said. The measures follow tighter global flows for gallium in recent years as countries adjust controls on critical materials and governments seek to secure strategic inputs for their industries.

Gallium is used in compound semiconductors, including gallium arsenide and gallium nitride, which are employed in power electronics, wireless communications and light-emitting diodes. The metal is also used in the production of integrated circuits and optics. With limited primary production and concentrated refining capacity globally, disruptions in trade routes have periodically led to price swings and shortages for downstream users.

Industry participants said the negotiations are focused on allocating shipments to meet demand from India’s semiconductor supply chain, particularly firms working on wafer-related components and substrate processing. One source familiar with the talks said the government is coordinating with domestic processors and traders to prevent sudden shortages while importers and suppliers settle quota arrangements.

“Temporary restrictions are being applied to manage the transition to quota-based procurement for gallium,” a person involved in the discussions said. The source added that discussions were expected to conclude in the near term, after which normal shipment patterns could resume.

The restriction comes amid ongoing efforts across multiple jurisdictions to regulate access to critical materials. While India has not previously used long-term, outright bans on gallium shipments, analysts say short, time-bound controls can be used to align imports and domestic distribution with industrial policy goals.

Traders said they are seeking clarification on documentation requirements and delivery timelines under the new rules, including whether shipments already in transit are exempt and how quotas will be applied across different categories of gallium products. Some companies have also turned to alternative supply channels and contract renegotiations while awaiting the outcome of quota negotiations.

The government did not immediately respond to requests for comment. However, the sources said the measure is intended to be temporary and limited in scope, with the next updates expected once domestic allocation terms are finalized.

headline: Belgium lifts palladium stockpiling rules after warehouse strike resolution July 2025
body: Belgium has lifted palladium stockpiling restrictions after resolving a warehouse labour dispute that had disrupted deliveries and limited government purchases, the finance ministry said on 19 March 2026. The temporary measures, introduced in late July 2025 following strike-related delays, required refiners and trading houses to hold palladium inventory in designated bonded facilities and capped monthly transfers outside Belgium at pre-set quotas. Under the updated rules effective 20 March, the caps were removed and firms may resume shipments to existing private warehouses, while remaining subject to standard reporting of holdings and movements. Government stockpiling purchases are expected to restart at full cadence, with officials targeting a return to procurement of 8–10 tonnes per month by April 2026. The resolution also reduced backlogs in inland logistics, with operators reporting normalization of rail and truck turnaround times within 2–3 weeks of the settlement. Belgium remains aligned with EU-level guidance on strategic metals, but the ministry said day-to-day compliance will shift from strike-era controls to routine audits.

headline: Australia cobalt smelter shut after regulator finds violations in effluent discharge August 2026
body:
Australia’s only cobalt smelter in the country has been shut down after an environmental regulator found violations related to the discharge of wastewater effluent, according to authorities and company statements.

The closure follows an inspection by the state environmental regulator in August 2026, when officials identified breaches of permit conditions governing the volume and quality of liquid effluent released from the processing site, the regulator said in a written notice. The regulator required the facility to halt operations and undertake corrective actions before resuming.

The company operating the smelter said it received the directive and has stopped production while it reviews the findings and upgrades its wastewater treatment and monitoring systems. In a brief statement, the firm said it would cooperate with regulators and submit documentation demonstrating compliance with discharge limits and reporting requirements.

Regulators said the violations were identified through routine monitoring records and subsequent sampling during the inspection period. They cited exceedances tied to parameters including chemical characteristics of the effluent and operational controls intended to prevent contaminants from entering nearby waterways. The regulator also noted that effluent discharge records were not maintained in full compliance with reporting rules.

The shutdown is expected to disrupt supplies of cobalt-bearing intermediate products produced at the site, though the company did not provide an estimate of how long the closure would last. The smelter processes cobalt concentrates into refined outputs used by battery and industrial supply chains.

The regulator said it will verify that corrective works have been completed before issuing permission to restart operations. Requirements include demonstrating compliance with discharge permits, implementing additional treatment safeguards, and providing updated monitoring plans and data for independent review, the agency said.

Australian mining and processing firms have faced increased scrutiny over water quality and tailings management in recent years, with regulators tightening reporting and enforcement mechanisms for chemical and waste handling at industrial sites. The cobalt sector, which has expanded to support electric vehicle and battery production, has also drawn attention to the environmental controls needed for refining operations.

headline: Brazil declares force majeure at copper mine after tailings seepage incident May 2026
body: Brazil’s government declared force majeure for a copper mine in Minas Gerais after seepage from tailings spilled into nearby areas, according to an official notice issued on March 18, 2026. The declaration covers the period beginning May 2026 and allows the operator to suspend certain contractual obligations while authorities assess contamination risks and remediation costs. Regulators said the incident involved tailings seepage that reached drainage pathways and triggered monitoring upgrades at watercourses downstream, with sampling scheduled through at least late June 2026. The company said it has deployed containment barriers, increased pump-and-treat operations, and rerouted some processing flows to reduce further seepage. State environmental agencies have ordered a revised risk plan and additional reporting every 15 days during the force majeure window. While officials did not specify financial exposure, the mine produced copper concentrates valued at tens of millions of reais annually before the disruption, and the government noted potential impacts on shipping schedules and employment levels pending inspections.

headline: Ghana orders temporary suspension of zirconium concentrate exports pending assay audit
body:
Ghana has ordered a temporary suspension of zirconium concentrate exports pending a full assay audit to verify product quality and compliance with export requirements, officials said on Thursday.

The order, issued by the Ministry of Lands and Natural Resources and circulated to exporters and port operators, directs that consignments of zirconium concentrate covered by existing export permits be withheld from shipment until inspectors complete laboratory testing and review the results. The suspension is intended to “ensure accurate assay reporting” and to determine whether recent batch measurements match contractual specifications, according to a statement seen by industry participants.

Zirconium concentrate is produced primarily from mineral sands in Ghana and shipped to processors that use the material to make zirconium compounds and zirconium-based products. Ghana’s export documentation typically includes assay results submitted by operators, which are then used to determine grading, pricing and compliance with buyer requirements.

A government spokesperson said the audit will involve independent sampling and laboratory verification at designated facilities. “Until the assay audit is concluded and the findings are reviewed, exports will remain paused to prevent further consignments from leaving the country without verified test outcomes,” the spokesperson said, speaking on condition that the ministry’s written instruction be treated as the formal notice.

The suspension follows an audit trigger raised by discrepancies identified in assay documentation for recent shipments, the spokesperson said. The ministry did not provide details on the suspected variance or the duration of the pause. Exporters were instructed to keep records of all samples taken from stockpiles and to make production lots available for inspection when scheduled.

Port clearance for affected shipments has been suspended, according to trade sources, while customs and inspection teams review which consignments fall under the zirconium concentrate category. Exporters that have already shipped cargoes are expected to continue under separate compliance checks, the sources said.

Industry participants said the decision could disrupt near-term sales and logistics, given that testing and paperwork are often tied to shipping schedules. The ministry indicated that it would communicate the outcome of the audit and any remedial steps required for resuming exports, including possible adjustments to sampling procedures and reporting formats.

headline: Finland union action delays nickel cathode shipments to EU customers in February 2026
body: Finland-based union action in February 2026 disrupted nickel cathode output and delayed shipments to EU customers, according to logistics updates and trade contacts. Production interruptions began during the second week of February and extended through late month, reducing available cathode volumes for the period’s EU delivery slots. Several EU-bound loads scheduled for mid- to late-February arrival were pushed back by 1–3 weeks, with rerouting and port-handling delays reported as major contributors. One shipment batch intended for customers in Germany, France, and the Netherlands was split into multiple smaller consignments after loading windows closed, raising demurrage risk and transport costs. Cathode loading resumed toward the end of February, but vessel departures and customs clearance timelines constrained catch-up deliveries into March 2026. Market participants said the disruptions could tighten short-term supplies for contract positions referencing February delivery, while also influencing March pricing expectations as replacement inventory works its way into EU warehouses.

headline: Portugal copper trader granted emergency license after port authorities halt loading May 2026
body:
A Portuguese copper trader was granted an emergency licence after port authorities halted loading at a key export terminal, according to regulatory filings seen by Reuters.

The decision, issued by Portugal’s maritime and transport regulator on March 19, allows the company to resume limited cargo operations despite unresolved administrative issues related to vessel documentation and clearance procedures at the port, the documents showed. The emergency authorisation was granted for a short, time-bound window running through mid-April, with loading volumes capped pending further checks.

The halt was triggered earlier in the week when port authorities stopped loading operations for a copper shipment scheduled to depart in May 2026. A spokesperson for the port authority said the suspension was implemented “as a precaution” while officials verified compliance with port clearance and safety requirements. The company said the cargo had been ready for loading and that it was working with authorities to address the points raised.

In a statement accompanying the licence request, the trader said it was seeking emergency measures to prevent additional demurrage costs and to avoid disruption to downstream contracts. The company did not provide details of the specific documents under review, but said its submission included revised manifests and confirmation of product specifications.

Under the terms of the emergency licence, the trader is required to ensure that any resumed loading is supervised by port officials and that all safety and operational protocols are met. The licence also stipulates that no additional vessels can be added to the schedule without further approval, and that the terminal must complete its ongoing review before full clearance is granted for the broader export plan.

Portuguese regulators said the emergency authorisation is intended to address an immediate operational risk while maintaining oversight. “This temporary authorisation permits controlled activity under strict conditions until the competent services conclude their assessment,” a regulator said in the written decision.

The case comes as European metals trade remains sensitive to port bottlenecks and paperwork requirements, particularly for bulk and containerised commodities moving under tight timelines. The trader’s next steps depend on the outcome of the port authority’s review, which is expected to determine whether the May-loading schedule can proceed without further interruptions.

headline: South Korea cancels tin import tenders citing contamination concerns at origin March 2026
body: South Korea’s Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy has cancelled scheduled tin import tenders after contamination concerns at the source, according to industry notifications dated 19 March 2026. The action affects tenders covering multiple cargo lots expected to arrive in April, with procurement previously planned under standard spot-buy procedures tied to metal supply contracts. Officials cited risk indicators from origin inspection reports, including elevated impurity levels linked to processing sites outside South Korea, without naming specific suppliers. Market participants said the cancellation could tighten near-term availability for tin feedstock and may shift demand toward alternative origins that can provide updated test results. Several bidders were asked to re-submit documents or wait for a revised tender schedule, which the ministry said would be released after additional verification. Traders estimated that delays of 2 to 6 weeks could move premiums versus benchmark tin and increase compliance costs for importers.

headline: China increases graphite production tax credits after kiln outages reduce output in April 2026
body:
China has raised graphite production tax credits after kiln outages cut output earlier this year, according to a notice issued by authorities and industry sources, with the adjustment aimed at supporting supply in the weeks following the disruptions.

The policy change, effective for April 2026 output, increases the credit level for eligible graphite producers that operate specified kiln lines and maintain production continuity, officials said in the circular. The move follows what the notice described as “temporary operating disruptions” that reduced output during April, when several facilities reported lower firing capacity due to unexpected maintenance and equipment failures.

Graphite is a key input for lithium-ion battery anodes and other industrial uses, and China is the dominant supplier of natural and synthetic graphite components. Any interruption to kiln operations can ripple through downstream processing, where the timing of refining and purification steps often depends on uninterrupted feedstock from upstream manufacturers.

Under the revised rules, producers that can demonstrate reduced production attributable to the outages may apply for the higher credits by submitting documentation covering kiln downtime, output records and maintenance logs, the notice said. The credits are intended to partially offset lost revenue and fixed costs incurred during the affected period, and they will be reflected in enterprise tax filings for the relevant quarter.

Industry participants said several plants suffered staggered kiln interruptions in early April, resulting in lower shipments and a temporary slowdown in conversion to higher-grade material. While some lines resumed after repairs, others remained constrained by furnace replacement parts and shortened production windows, sources said. The adjustments to the tax framework are expected to encourage facilities to restore full operating schedules and keep materials flowing to anode and specialty-grade markets.

A representative for one graphite producer said the increased credits “help cover the gap between scheduled commissioning and actual production while we complete kiln repairs and stabilise output.” The company added that it had submitted the required outage records to support eligibility for the April credits.

The authorities did not publish facility-level details of the outages or the credit amounts in the summary notice circulated to market participants. However, the higher credits are understood to be tied to production targets and verified downtime, with eligibility limited to firms meeting compliance and reporting requirements.

headline: United States imposes sanctions-linked compliance checks on molybdenum imports Feb 2026
body: The United States has introduced sanctions-linked compliance checks for molybdenum imports starting in February 2026, according to an industry notice circulated this week. The measures require importers to submit enhanced documentation and screening records for shipments entering U.S. ports, with a focus on whether the material is connected to sanctioned entities or restricted end uses. Coverage extends to refined molybdenum and molybdenum intermediates classified under multiple customs categories, with transactions processed through standard customs entry systems subject to additional review. Companies are required to verify supplier identity, trace final destination information, and maintain records for audit purposes for up to 5 years. Enforcement is set to begin with heightened scrutiny during February and March 2026, with full operational alignment expected by late April. Industry participants said the checks may slow clearance times by several days and could increase compliance costs for both domestic importers and overseas producers.

headline: United Arab Emirates declares force majeure for steelmaking iron ore deliveries in May 2026
body:
The United Arab Emirates has declared force majeure for deliveries of iron ore used in steelmaking scheduled for May 2026, according to a notice circulated to market participants on Thursday.

The declaration, issued by UAE counterparties involved in receiving the cargoes, cites disruptions affecting the supply and logistics chain for the benchmark raw material. Force majeure is a contractual clause that can excuse parties from performance when events beyond their control prevent delivery or acceptance of goods as agreed.

Participants said the notice affects shipments tied to UAE steel production arrangements that rely on imported iron ore. Several supply contracts in the region include clauses allowing buyers or sellers to seek suspension of obligations when force majeure is invoked, potentially extending delivery windows or triggering renegotiations over quantities and pricing.

Under such provisions, the impact can vary depending on how counterparties apply the clause. Market sources typically expect that acceptance of cargoes may be delayed, that fresh loading or discharge schedules may be proposed, and that settlement terms could be revisited once operational conditions improve.

The UAE is one of the Gulf markets that imports large volumes of iron ore and processes it through domestic steel mills. Shipments are usually arranged through a mix of long-term supply agreements and spot purchases, with timing dependent on port capacity, shipping availability and clearance procedures.

In the force majeure notice, the parties referenced circumstances that have hindered delivery timelines, without specifying a single cause such as congestion at a particular port or a specific shipping route. One industry participant familiar with the contract language said the declaration is intended to provide legal protection for missed or postponed deliveries while discussions continue with suppliers.

Force majeure declarations have become a recurring feature of commodity trade in periods of shipping disruption and operational constraints. Similar notices issued in other markets can affect near-term order flow, as buyers weigh whether to wait for rescheduled cargoes or replace supplies through alternate origins and freight arrangements.

Traders said the UAE’s move could influence freight demand and the near-term availability of prompt iron ore cargoes for the Gulf, particularly for contracts tied to May 2026. Prices for iron ore, which are sensitive to physical delivery tightness and expectations for future arrivals, may face additional uncertainty as market participants assess how widely the disruption will propagate across related contracts.

headline: Ukraine missile damage disrupts iron ore rail flows, prompting rerouting and higher premiums
body: Missile damage from attacks in Ukraine has disrupted iron ore rail flows through the Dnipro and Kryvyi Rih corridors since mid-March, forcing multiple operators to reroute shipments and causing freight premiums to rise across regional routes. Shippers reported delays of 3–6 days as rail cars were diverted to western trunk lines and loading schedules were reworked after damaged track segments and power outages reduced throughput by an estimated 20–30% during peak windows. Several contracts rolled over in the week ending March 19 were repriced to reflect longer transit times and higher demurrage exposure, according to market participants monitoring logistics. Premiums for movement into EU-linked hubs increased by mid-single digits in euro terms, while spot rates for short-haul legs rose more sharply amid constrained capacity. Traders said the knock-on effect is narrowing availability of spot 62% Fe material and tightening prompt supply into April loadings, with additional congestion expected through at least March 31. The disruption comes as steelmakers already face seasonal demand shifts and competing iron ore sourcing from alternative origins.

headline: Brazil halts bauxite-related shipments; downstream iron ore buyers brace for delays March 2026
body:
Brazil has halted some bauxite-related shipments, a move that traders said is beginning to ripple into the iron ore supply chain and could delay deliveries for downstream buyers, according to market participants and shipping data reviewed this week.

The stoppage affects exports tied to bauxite processing and associated logistics routes used to supply alumina refiners and other industrial users, people familiar with trading said. While Brazilian producers have not published a unified notice covering all cargoes, several chartering and freight desks reported that sailings have been paused or rescheduled for vessels loading at Atlantic ports.

Downstream buyers of iron ore and other bulk commodities are bracing for delays as freight capacity and port slots tighten, they said. Bauxite cargoes typically move in similar vessel classes to other dry bulk shipments from Brazil, potentially affecting booking windows and rerouting choices. “Freight availability is the first problem,” one logistics broker said, adding that schedule disruptions can cascade when vessels are diverted to alternate loading and discharge points.

Commodity analysts said the impact on iron ore volumes is unlikely to be immediate in terms of tonnage, but shipment timing could shift across trading windows. Several buyers hedge against late arrivals by pulling forward inventories, adjusting offtake nominations, and seeking alternative suppliers from other origins if lead times extend.

Market participants also pointed to the operational complexity of Brazilian bulk exports, which can be influenced by inspection procedures, weather conditions, and customs clearance—factors that can be magnified when multiple cargo streams are disrupted at once. Shipping agents said they are receiving more frequent requests to update estimated time of arrival and discharge windows as carriers reassess itineraries.

Separately, some alumina and refining-linked buyers may face pressure if bauxite availability tightens, although the extent depends on whether existing stockpiles and contracted tonnages can cover near-term needs. Traders said they are monitoring port-level developments and additional guidance from authorities and exporters over the next two weeks.

Brazil remains a major supplier of bauxite for global alumina production, and any interruption can affect downstream chemical and industrial supply chains. For iron ore buyers, the current focus is on confirming vessel schedules and ensuring that contract delivery dates can be met amid potential congestion and reallocation of maritime capacity.

headline: China pauses indium oxide exports amid compliance investigation of smelter records April 2026
body: China has paused exports of indium oxide starting in April 2026, according to industry sources, as authorities investigate a compliance case involving smelter record-keeping. The suspension follows a review of production and shipment documentation tied to multiple operators, with the inquiry focusing on whether batch, assay, and export declarations match inventory and furnace-run logs. Companies affected said the pause covers shipments leaving Chinese ports and applies to both standard-grade and higher-purity material, pending remediation of documentation gaps. Traders expect at least 2 to 6 weeks of market disruption, depending on when customs clearances resume and whether additional audits are triggered. Indium oxide prices have been sensitive to export availability since early 2026, with buyers securing spot coverage after reduced lead times earlier in the quarter. Further updates are expected after regulators complete their record verification and issue guidance for compliant exporters.

headline: Peru copper mine dewatering failure triggers output cut and shipment suspension April 2026
body:
A dewatering failure at a Peruvian copper mine has forced an output cut and led to a suspension of shipments, the company and local authorities said, as operations work to restore water control in the pit.

The incident occurred at the mine in the southern Andes on April 2026, according to statements shared with regional officials. The failure disrupted the pumping system used to drain groundwater and storm runoff from mining areas, raising water levels and prompting a halt in certain phases of production.

In response, the operator reduced production rates while crews repair and replace damaged components in the dewatering circuit, the company said. It also suspended shipments of concentrate as a precautionary step to prevent excess inventory from accumulating at the loading facilities while plant performance remains constrained.

“We had a dewatering incident that required immediate intervention to stabilize the system,” a company spokesperson said in an email. “We are prioritizing restoration of pumping capacity and will resume shipments once product flow and water control return to stable operating parameters.”

Authorities in the region said they were monitoring the situation, including checks of water levels and drainage channels to ensure there is no overflow risk to surrounding areas. The government did not immediately provide an estimate for the duration of the suspension or the volume of output potentially affected, citing the need for technical assessment.

Copper output in Peru is frequently shaped by water management, particularly during periods of seasonal rainfall and rapid groundwater fluctuations. The dewatering failure, however, comes at a time when several mines and concentrators across the country have been operating under tighter maintenance schedules.

The mine operator said it is continuing work to bring pumping back to specifications and to test the system under normal flow conditions before restarting shipments. It did not specify whether the issue was caused by mechanical failure, power disruptions, or operational factors, saying investigations are ongoing.

Financial impacts are expected to depend on how quickly the mine can restore stable recovery rates at the concentrator and restart logistics. Traders typically react to shipment suspensions and production cuts in line with expected supply disruptions, particularly for refined concentrate deliveries and contracted offtake schedules.

headline: Portugal suspends tungsten sales after customs seizes consignments for documentation gaps
body: Portugal has suspended tungsten sales after customs officials seized consignments over documentation gaps, according to industry participants familiar with the matter. The seizures occurred in recent weeks at entry points handling imports of tungsten concentrates and intermediary products, where authorities flagged missing or incomplete shipping records and licensing paperwork required for cross-border trade. The suspension, effective immediately as of 2026-03-21, applies to shipments tied to the affected consignments while regulators review compliance documentation. Several traders said the action could delay deliveries scheduled for late March and early April, with downstream users also expected to halt purchases until clearance procedures are completed. One distributor indicated that affected lots represented a limited portion of monthly volumes, but the stoppage may tighten availability for specialty grades used in hard materials and defense supply chains. Authorities have not disclosed the number of seizures or the total estimated value, but customs described the measure as a risk-based enforcement step aimed at preventing non-compliant imports.

headline: Russia sets new palladium supply schedule after plant outage and planned maintenance in June
body:
Russia has set a revised palladium supply schedule for buyers after an unplanned plant outage and planned maintenance activities in June, according to industry sources familiar with the matter.

The updated timetable, circulated to counterparties under existing offtake arrangements, reflects reduced output during the outage period and a production catch-up following scheduled work at refining facilities, the sources said. The change is designed to realign deliveries across the second and third quarters, with some volumes rescheduled to later delivery windows.

Under the new plan, supply shipments are expected to be front-loaded in early summer once maintenance work is completed, while volumes during the maintenance month are adjusted downward. The sources said the company responsible for the schedule has not changed overall expected annual availability, but has reshuffled the cadence to account for slower processing capacity in June.

Palladium supply has remained sensitive to operational disruptions at major Russian processing sites, where outages can quickly alter monthly shipment levels. Buyers often rely on Russia’s contractual deliveries to support sales of palladium-containing catalysts used in automotive exhaust systems and industrial chemical processes.

One trader said the revised schedule “tracks the operational reality on the ground” and provides more clarity on delivery timing for counterparties. The trader added that while there may be tighter availability during June, subsequent deliveries should compensate for missed volumes.

Market participants said the scheduling revision could influence near-term contract and spot liquidity, particularly in Europe and Asia where traders and refiners use Russian material as a benchmark for pricing and inventory planning. They noted that even small changes to monthly availability can affect storage and financing decisions for intermediaries.

The sources did not provide specific tonnage figures for the revised schedule, nor the dates of the plant outage and maintenance. However, they said the communications to buyers indicate a structured recovery plan and incremental deliveries in the months following June.

headline: China cobalt refining slowdown hits cathode material availability following regulator review May
body: China’s cobalt refining slowdown accelerated after a regulator review in May reduced feedstock throughput to cathode producers, industry sources said on Friday. Several refineries in China’s major processing hubs cut runs for weeks following the review, lowering production of cobalt intermediates and pushing spot availability of cathode-grade material out of sync with downstream demand. Cathode manufacturers reported intermittent shortages from late May through September, with procurement lead times rising to 4-8 weeks versus 2-3 weeks earlier in the year. Market participants said inventories at some NMC and NCA plants were drawn down as imports of refined product were not fully able to offset the reduced local supply. Pricing pressures emerged as buyers competed for limited cargoes, while treatment and refining charges were also pressured by tighter refinery utilization. Analysts said the impact is likely to persist into the first half of 2026 unless refinery operating rates normalize and regulator clearance schedules remain on track.

headline: Morocco cadmium supplier declares force majeure after chemical shipment blockage at border
body:
A Moroccan cadmium supplier has declared force majeure after a chemical shipment was blocked at a border, disrupting deliveries of cadmium used in pigments and industrial applications, according to a statement issued by the company.

The supplier said the blockage prevented it from moving a consignment of cadmium chemicals and related materials as planned, citing delays arising from border inspection procedures and access restrictions affecting freight operations. It did not immediately specify which border crossing was involved or name the destination.

Under the force majeure notice, the company said it would be excused from performance obligations to customers for the duration of the disruption and would reassess shipment schedules once clearance is restored. The statement indicated it is working with shipping agents and relevant authorities to secure release of the goods and to re-route future loads where possible.

Force majeure clauses are commonly used in trade contracts when circumstances outside a seller’s control prevent delivery, such as severe disruptions in transport or regulatory actions. The supplier said the incident is not due to manufacturing or internal operational issues and that any timing adjustments would depend on how quickly authorities complete inspections and release the cargo.

Cadmium is a regulated heavy metal, with trade and handling governed by environmental and health rules. It is used in rechargeable batteries, stabilisers for plastics, and cadmium pigments, and supplies are often tightly monitored by buyers for compliance documentation and chain-of-custody requirements. Any delay in shipments can affect customer inventories, particularly for industrial users that run just-in-time production.

A company spokesperson, in the statement, said: “We have declared force majeure because the shipment cannot be released through the border within the agreed timeframe despite our efforts, and we are coordinating with all parties to resume deliveries as soon as clearance is granted.”

The supplier did not provide an estimate of the duration of the blockage or the value and quantity of the affected consignment. It said it will provide updates to customers and trading partners as further information becomes available, including any impacts on contract delivery dates and logistics arrangements.

headline: Japan secures selenium supply as upstream producer halts operations for maintenance in March
body: Japan has secured additional selenium supply after an upstream producer in March halted operations for scheduled maintenance, according to trade and logistics sources. The interruption, slated to begin in early March and last into mid-March, is expected to tighten global spot availability and raise freight-linked costs for refiners. Japanese buyers reportedly arranged replacement volumes through short-term contracts and warehouse allocations ahead of the downtime, aiming to prevent interruptions to glass, electronics and photovoltaic-grade output. Selenium prices in Asia have been sensitive to supply timing, and a production gap can affect shipments that typically require 2–4 weeks for port clearance and inland distribution. The maintenance comes as several refining runs are already scheduled for seasonal slowdowns, limiting the flexibility of spot purchases. One risk remains that lead times for alternative material could extend into late March if upstream restart schedules slip. Market participants said Japan’s procurement actions should stabilize domestic inventories through the end of March, but near-term pricing may stay volatile given thin spot liquidity.

headline: Canada bismuth producer cut output after labor strike closes processing facility February 2026
body:
A Canadian bismuth producer cut output after a labor strike forced the closure of its processing facility in February 2026, according to company and industry sources.

The disruption affected a plant responsible for refining bismuth feed into saleable metal, leaving the producer with limited access to processing capacity during the strike period. Several buyers in North America were informed of reduced shipments and extended delivery timelines, the sources said.

The company said the shutdown was tied to a labor dispute that began in early February and resulted in the temporary closure of the site for multiple weeks. While production at upstream steps was maintained where possible, the lack of access to full processing operations meant finished output could not be scaled to normal levels, according to a person familiar with the matter.

Bismuth is used in alloys, electronics and materials for radiation shielding, with demand closely linked to industrial manufacturing and specialty chemical production. The metal is also a byproduct in some mining and refining supply chains, which can make downstream processing a critical bottleneck when facilities are interrupted.

One source said the producer restarted operations shortly after the work stoppage ended, but initial output volumes were below historical rates as equipment returned to routine operating parameters and stockpiles were worked through. The company did not provide detailed figures for total bismuth output for February, but said it was prioritizing customer orders and working to restore production volumes.

Trade data and procurement schedules suggest bismuth supplies have remained relatively tight in recent quarters, with buyers maintaining inventories at varying levels depending on contract terms. With the February interruption centered on processing capacity rather than mining, the impact was felt primarily in the availability of refined metal.

Industry participants said the company is aiming to stabilize output through the spring, although the pace of recovery could depend on staffing levels, maintenance requirements and demand patterns from alloy and electronics customers. They also said customer allocations could remain in place until shipment schedules normalize.

The incident follows a broader period of labor disruptions across industrial sectors, but the bismuth supply chain is particularly sensitive to interruptions at a small number of refining facilities, sources said.

headline: South Africa vanadium producer faces transport halt after rail strike disrupts concentrate flows
body: A vanadium producer in South Africa said a national rail strike has forced a temporary halt in shipments of vanadium concentrate, disrupting concentrate flows to processing and export terminals. The company reported that trains carrying product from ore-processing sites were stopped starting 2026-03-19, with volumes affected during the subsequent 48 to 72 hours as existing loading windows closed.
