Inventory at rail-connected stockyards is expected to cover only limited drawdown through 2026-03-22 to 2026-03-23, based on current dispatch rates and average daily concentrate tonnage. The disruption comes as concentrate demand remains concentrated among processors converting material into ferrovanadium, with logistics also constrained by port queue variability and road-haul capacity limits. Market participants noted that even short interruptions can tighten regional availability for spec product grades, particularly where concentrate is needed to meet furnace feed schedules. The producer is seeking alternative haulage arrangements and rerouting options, but said any restart is dependent on rail service restoration and inspection clearances, with no confirmed date for a full return to normal throughput.

headline: Brazil iron ore company declares force majeure on exports following flooding at port access
body:
A Brazilian iron ore exporter has declared force majeure on shipments after flooding disrupted access to the port used to move its products, the company said.

The declaration was issued following heavy rainfall and flooding that affected roads and logistics routes leading to the port facility, according to company communications. As a result, the firm said it expects delays in loading and shipping schedules for customers in the coming days, while assessing damage and reviewing alternative logistics options.

Force majeure typically allows suppliers to suspend contractual obligations when extraordinary events outside their control prevent performance. The company said it would notify counterparties of developments as conditions evolve, including when normal operations resume or partial shipments become possible.

Port access has been a recurring bottleneck for freight operations in Brazil during periods of extreme weather, when land transport links are interrupted or when port authorities restrict movements for safety reasons. In this case, the exporter’s statement tied the disruption directly to flooding affecting the approach routes to the port, rather than to any issue at the mine or processing sites.

The company did not specify the volume of cargo likely to be affected, but it said that current export plans would be reviewed in line with port availability and emergency repairs. It also indicated that shipments already contracted could be rescheduled, subject to agreement with buyers and shipping agents.

Local authorities have been responding to the flooding with emergency services and civil defense measures, including road closures in affected areas. The company said it is working with port operators and logistics partners to monitor water levels and restore access.

Market participants typically watch such declarations closely because disruptions can affect near-term iron ore supply and freight flows, particularly when they coincide with broader weather disruptions across shipping corridors. The exporter said it would continue regular updates through the period covered by the force majeure notice.

The company’s statement added that it is coordinating with customers and counterparties to reduce the impact of the stoppage, including by exploring alternate staging routes and adjusting delivery windows where possible.

headline: Chile issues force majeure at Escondida copper mine after water restrictions
body: Chile’s government has issued a force majeure order affecting the Escondida copper mine run by BHP and partners after expanded water restrictions in the northern Antofagasta region, according to a notice circulated on March 21. The measure allows the operating unit to suspend or adjust certain contractual obligations tied to production and logistics, citing increased limits on water extraction and the risk of supply shortfalls for processing needs. Escondida, the world’s largest copper producer, has faced tighter sourcing constraints following government efforts to address chronic drought across the Atacama Desert basin. The order follows additional restrictions announced in late February and enforcement that began earlier in March, when authorities tightened monitoring of water wells used for industrial operations. Industry sources said the mine’s output plans for the second quarter could be reshaped, although no immediate reduction targets were specified in the notice. The ministry indicated the force majeure would remain in effect while the water limits persist, with reviews scheduled on a rolling basis.

headline: Indonesia suspends nickel ore export permits for 30 days following factory protests
body:
Indonesia has suspended nickel ore export permits for 30 days after protests by workers at processing plants, according to the country’s trade ministry and industry officials.

The suspension applies to permits issued for the export of nickel ore, amid complaints from factory staff that shipment approval delays had disrupted operations and pay tied to production schedules. Indonesia is a major supplier of nickel used in batteries for electric vehicles, and its export rules are closely watched by traders and manufacturers globally.

The trade ministry said the decision was taken to allow inspections and to review compliance by companies holding export authorisations. It directed relevant agencies to verify production and stock levels at facilities and to ensure companies meet the conditions attached to export permits under Indonesia’s mineral export policy.

In several locations, workers gathered outside industrial sites and union offices over the past week, according to local reporting and social media posts. Protesters said they had been waiting for shipments while management postponed plant activity, increasing pressure on wages and overtime arrangements. Police were deployed at some sites to keep order, and no immediate injuries were reported.

A ministry official, speaking on condition of anonymity because they were not authorised to discuss the matter publicly, said the suspension was aimed at “bringing permit issuance back in line with factory operations and regulatory requirements.” The official added that companies that can demonstrate readiness to process material locally would be prioritised once reviews are complete.

Indonesia’s nickel policy has increasingly required that ore be processed domestically before export, with enforcement strengthened over the last several years to support local smelters. The 30-day permit suspension is likely to affect near-term cargo planning for traders who rely on Indonesian supplies, particularly for shipments moving under short-term approvals.

Market participants said the interruption could tighten availability in the coming weeks, though the full impact will depend on how quickly the suspension is resolved and whether alternative export routes are used for other grades of material. Any renewed permit approvals, they said, will depend on inspections, documentation and compliance findings for each holding company and facility.

headline: DRC mining regulator orders cobalt production caps at two sites after safety violations
body: The Democratic Republic of the Congo’s mining regulator has ordered cobalt production caps at two mining sites following safety violations, according to a directive dated March 20, 2026. The measure applies to operations at the permitted facilities in Haut-Katanga that were flagged after inspections conducted in late February and early March, when regulators reported repeated breaches of worker-safety procedures and noncompliance with waste-handling controls. Under the order, output at the two sites will be limited starting April 1 to levels capped at a combined monthly volume of X tonnes, pending corrective actions and follow-up audits. The regulator said violations included inadequate protective equipment enforcement, incomplete ventilation checks at processing areas, and failures to document incident reporting within statutory timeframes. Operators have been given 30 days to submit remediation plans detailing staffing of safety officers, updated training schedules, and revised tailings and effluent monitoring. The caps are expected to affect near-term deliveries, particularly for battery-grade cobalt supply contracts tied to April and May shipments.

headline: Peru introduces rail curbs affecting copper concentrate shipments for 10 weeks
body:
Peru has introduced temporary rail curbs that will affect shipments of copper concentrate for 10 weeks, according to industry sources and documents seen by Reuters, as authorities seek to reduce congestion on key routes used to move ore to ports.

The measures, which take effect immediately, restrict rail capacity and impose scheduling limits for trains carrying copper concentrate from mining regions toward coastal loading points. The curbs are designed to prioritize other freight categories during peak operating periods, but they are likely to force exporters to adjust logistics plans, sources said.

Under the new rules, rail operators will cap the number of trains per day on the affected segments and require shipments to be slotted within narrower time windows. Several shippers were also told to consolidate consignments to meet new train-length and turnaround requirements, according to one freight provider familiar with the new timetable.

“The intent is to manage the flow on the corridor,” said one logistics executive at a Peruvian rail services company, speaking on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to discuss the operational details publicly. “For concentrates, it means more planning and less flexibility on dispatch dates.”

Copper concentrate is a major export product for Peru, which is among the world’s top producers of the metal. Many shipments are moved by rail to reach Pacific ports where concentrate is loaded for overseas smelters. A delay of multiple weeks can translate into higher demurrage and storage costs at terminals, and it can also affect the timing of sales contracts that reference loading windows.

Exporters have been preparing for tighter logistics during periods of maintenance and weather disruptions in previous years, but the 10-week duration of the restrictions is unusual, several market participants said. Some companies are expected to increase use of alternative routes or rely more on trucking for short transfers, though road capacity is limited and is often constrained by traffic and road conditions.

Authorities have not publicly detailed the specific rail lines affected, but sources indicated the curbs focus on segments used by concentrate trains to reach the main port corridors. The government has said previously that it coordinates transport schedules to manage infrastructure wear and align freight flows with port handling capacity.

Reuters was not able to obtain an official response from Peru’s transport authorities by press time. The affected period ends in late May unless extended.

headline: Zambia announces emergency inspection regime at copper smelters amid power shortages
body: Zambia has announced an emergency inspection regime for copper smelters as the country faces recurring power shortages, according to a government notice issued late on March 20. The inspection campaign, ordered under emergency powers, will begin immediately and cover all operating smelting and refining units connected to national grid supply arrangements. Regulators said the checks will focus on compliance with energy-use limits, maintenance records for critical transformers and furnaces, and safeguards intended to prevent emissions during periods of load shedding. Power constraints have intensified in recent weeks as utilities struggled to maintain generation capacity, prompting rolling outages that have disrupted industrial operations across Lusaka and the Copperbelt. Officials said smelters that fail to demonstrate continuity plans, fuel-switch capabilities where available, or corrective maintenance schedules could face temporary suspension of specific lines within 14 days. The government did not specify a duration for the inspections, but stated weekly reporting to the energy and environment authorities will be required from March 27 onward.

headline: Philippines suspends graphite mining permits after tailings breach investigation
body:
The Philippines has suspended graphite mining permits in response to an investigation into a tailings breach at a mine site, the government said on Saturday, as officials reviewed potential risks to communities and the environment.

The suspension covers operations under existing permits connected to the incident, according to a statement from the Department of Environment and Natural Resources (DENR). The department said it ordered the halt as investigators examine the cause of the breach and whether required safeguards and monitoring systems were in place. The DENR said affected companies were instructed to submit documents and comply with remediation requirements before any restart is considered.

The tailings breach, which involved material stored as part of ore processing, prompted temporary concern over possible contamination of nearby waterways and land. Local authorities and the mining regulator have been collecting samples and conducting inspections to assess impacts on water quality and surrounding areas, the DENR said.

In its update, the department said its investigators are focusing on tailings storage design and construction, operating procedures, maintenance of containment systems and emergency response protocols. It also said it is reviewing whether the mining operator followed permit conditions and environmental compliance commitments.

DENR Secretary Maria Antonia Yulo Loyzaga said the suspension reflects the government’s “zero tolerance for failures that could threaten public health and the environment,” adding that the investigation will determine accountability and the scope of corrective actions needed. She did not specify when the review would be completed.

Graphite mining has attracted interest in the Philippines in recent years due to global demand for the mineral, which is used in batteries and industrial applications. However, the sector has also faced scrutiny over environmental permitting and the management of waste from beneficiation and processing.

The DENR said further actions may include permit revocation or additional enforcement measures depending on the findings of the investigation and subsequent monitoring results. It urged communities near mining sites to remain vigilant and to report any unusual discharges, while inspectors continue to verify compliance with existing environmental controls.

headline: China announces temporary controls on gallium scrap exports to stabilize domestic supply
body: China has announced temporary controls on exports of gallium scrap to stabilize domestic supply for semiconductors and solar materials, according to a notice issued March 21, 2026. The measures apply to gallium scrap collected from industrial processing and manufacturing offcuts, and are designed to tighten outflows while domestic refiners and oxide producers rebuild inventories after recent demand spikes. The government set an initial control window beginning immediately and running through mid-year, with specific shipment approvals required for cross-border consignments during the period. Authorities said compliance will be monitored through customs declarations that distinguish scrap grades, with penalties for misclassification and missing documentation. The controls follow efforts earlier this year to manage gallium and related feedstock availability amid tighter supplies of high-purity metal and the feedstock used to produce gallium oxide. Market participants said the rules could reduce short-term exports and shift spot pricing in Asia, while longer-term effects will depend on how quickly domestic scrap collection expands and how much alternative material is diverted to refining.

headline: Norway force majeure disrupts platinum shipments after port closure from storms
body:
Norway declared force majeure after storms closed a key port, disrupting platinum shipments and raising short-term uncertainty for traders and industrial users, according to market participants and logistics providers.

The disruption follows the shutdown of port operations used for exports of refined metals and concentrates from inland processing sites to international buyers. Storm-related damage to infrastructure and ongoing safety inspections halted cargo movements, forcing shipping schedules to be revised and increasing demurrage risk for some contracts, several sources said.

A number of platinum lots scheduled for loading in the coming days were delayed while carriers wait for authorities to reopen facilities and verify that cranes and storage areas can resume normal handling. The timing of any restart remained dependent on weather conditions and repair work, sources close to the matter said, adding that alternative routing could take several weeks depending on vessel availability and port congestion elsewhere.

The force majeure declaration was triggered by the inability to perform contractual obligations “due to circumstances beyond the control of the parties,” industry documentation circulated among counterparties indicated. Under typical terms, the clause can allow sellers and logistics operators to postpone delivery or adjust obligations without breaching contract, depending on the duration of the interruption.

Platinum is used in autocatalysts, fuel cells and chemical processes, and supply flows from Europe are closely watched by buyers because inventories can be sensitive to short delays. While the immediate impact is largely on near-term deliveries, traders said the broader effect will depend on whether the closure extends beyond current estimates and whether insurance and documentation requirements can be met for rerouted cargo.

One freight forwarder said it was working with clients to reroute shipments to other ports where operations were continuing, but that rerouting is constrained by scheduling windows and the location of stockpiles. “We can often move the paperwork quickly, but the physical move and the vessel plan are what determine how fast we can recover,” the forwarder said.

Market activity was cautious after the news, with some buyers delaying confirmations while suppliers assessed the likelihood of restarting cargo flows within contract timeframes. Analysts said the situation should be monitored closely for updates from Norwegian authorities and for any further declarations affecting other metals exported through the same logistics network.

headline: South Africa stops palladium deliveries for 72 hours due to rail strike escalation
body: South Africa has suspended palladium deliveries for 72 hours starting Monday, according to logistics providers, as an escalated rail strike disrupted shipments from key processing and distribution nodes. The stoppage is expected to affect volumes moving on freight corridors used for refined metal transfers and on-time contracts scheduled through late March. Rail operators said service has been constrained across multiple routes since escalating work stoppages, limiting container and bulk handling capacity and forcing rerouting to alternative ports and depots. Traders estimated that the temporary interruption could delay deliveries of palladium intended for buyers in Europe and Asia, with knock-on effects likely into the first week of April. The suspension begins after operators implemented rolling delays earlier this month, culminating in the full 3-day halt announced for Mar. 23–25. Market participants said inventories at receiving warehouses may absorb near-term disruptions, but longer delays could tighten prompt availability and widen spot premiums relative to benchmark contracts. The strike action, now in its second week, remains a risk to scheduled freight cycles for other platinum group metals.

headline: Vietnam halts tungsten mine loading after licensing dispute with local authorities
body:
Vietnam has halted loading at a tungsten mine in the northern province of Thai Nguyen after a licensing dispute with local authorities, according to company and official sources.

The stoppage affects operations at the mine’s product-loading stage, where concentrates are typically prepared for shipment, and comes as Vietnamese agencies review permits covering site operations and environmental conditions, the sources said. The company involved told local stakeholders that it would suspend loading activities while the dispute is resolved.

The mine, which is among the country’s tungsten-producing projects, has been operating under a set of approvals that include provincial registrations and environmental assessments. According to the sources, the dispute arose after local authorities raised questions about the validity or scope of one or more documents used to authorize specific steps of the workflow, including stockpiling and loading arrangements.

A representative for the operator said in a statement shared with traders that “loading will be paused until the relevant licensing matters are clarified and the authorities confirm compliance.” The statement added that the company would continue routine maintenance and site safety work during the suspension.

Vietnam’s mining sector is regulated through a chain of national and local licensing requirements, and companies sometimes face delays when permits are renegotiated or when regulators seek additional documentation. Officials in Thai Nguyen did not provide immediate details on the specific paperwork at issue but said relevant departments are coordinating to review the project’s compliance status.

The halt is expected to tighten near-term availability for tungsten products sourced from Vietnam, traders said. Tungsten is used in hard metals for tooling and industrial applications, and buyers typically rely on predictable delivery schedules tied to mine loading cycles.

The operator said it is engaging with the provincial authorities to address the concerns and expects to resume loading once approvals are confirmed. Further guidance from the licensing review is expected to determine the timeline for restarting shipments.

headline: Mongolia declares state emergency at vanadium projects after winter infrastructure failures
body: Mongolia declared a nationwide state of emergency at its vanadium projects on 21 March 2026 after winter infrastructure failures disrupted power, transport, and processing operations, according to government and operator notices. The emergency covers mine sites and associated processing facilities in the country’s main vanadium areas and will remain in force through at least mid-April while repairs to power lines, heating systems, and fuel storage are completed. Officials said extreme cold and wind damaged above-ground utilities and access roads, forcing temporary suspensions of ore hauling and limiting concentrate output during February and early March. Operators reported reduced throughput in several processing lines, with stockpiles drawn down to maintain customer shipments of vanadium products. The emergency authorization allows expedited procurement and contractor mobilization for replacement transformers, insulated piping, and snow-clearing equipment. Mongolia’s vanadium supply chains rely on winter road corridors and grid stability, and disruptions have raised concerns over delivery schedules and working capital tied to inventory rebuilding.

headline: Brazil delays uranium fuel shipments after insurer withdraws coverage citing compliance risk
body:
Brazil has delayed shipments of uranium fuel to a nuclear processing facility after an insurer withdrew coverage, raising compliance concerns, according to officials and industry documents reviewed by Reuters.

The delays affect deliveries scheduled under long-term arrangements for nuclear fuel assemblies used in power generation, the sources said. One of the shipments was due to leave Brazil later this month, but the transporter and Brazilian counterpart renegotiated insurance terms, they said, after the insurer cited regulatory and compliance risk.

The insurer’s withdrawal was linked to additional documentation requirements tied to international transport rules and verification of end-use and handling procedures, two sources familiar with the matter said. The insurance coverage was intended to cover the full shipment route, including port operations and transit periods, they said.

A Brazilian government official, speaking on condition of anonymity because they were not authorised to discuss the matter publicly, said the country was working to “restore the conditions necessary for safe and compliant shipment” while ensuring there was no interruption to nuclear fuel supply. The official said the delay was administrative and contract-related rather than a change in nuclear policy.

Brazil relies on uranium fuel supplied and processed through international channels for reactor operations at home and uses a combination of domestic and imported services under safeguards agreements with the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). Any disruption to fuel logistics can increase the risk of scheduling pressures at power stations, though operators maintain working inventory levels designed to absorb short delays, the sources said.

Insurance is often a key gating factor for hazardous-material transport. When coverage is withdrawn, shippers typically must secure replacement terms that meet both insurer requirements and the standards demanded by carriers and customs authorities, particularly for shipments involving radioactive materials. In the current case, the insurer sought additional assurance that specific compliance controls were in place, including documentation audits and reporting timelines, the sources said.

The Brazilian nuclear supply chain has faced periodic delays in the past due to insurance, customs and shipping constraints, industry representatives said. The renewed delay is expected to be temporary while Brazil and contractors identify an alternative insurer or revise contract terms, they said.

Brazil’s nuclear regulator and the ministry responsible for energy oversight were contacted for comment. The operator for the fuel supply arrangements did not respond to requests for information by the time of publication.

headline: Russia restricts molybdenum concentrate exports ahead of seasonal maintenance shutdowns
body: Russia has tightened controls on molybdenum concentrate exports ahead of seasonal maintenance shutdowns planned for the spring, according to traders and trade data reviewed by industry participants. The restrictions, effective this month, limit shipments through key customs channels and require additional documentation on origin and assay results, with the stated aim of aligning loading schedules with refinery and roasting line downtime. Several buyers reported reduced availability for March 25–April 10 loadings, a period when producers typically halt processing equipment for repairs and stabilization runs. Exportable volumes are also being adjusted to reflect higher internal inventory targets, traders said, as domestic smelting plants switch to alternative feedstock. Pricing impact is expected to show up in forward contracts settled for April delivery, when freight and inspection delays are likely to add to landed costs. The measures come as global molybdenum demand remains tied to steelmaking and energy-related construction, where buyers rely on steady concentrate inflows to manage furnace chemistry during maintenance seasons.

headline: Kazakhstan orders cadmium output reductions at a refinery after odor complaint crackdown
body:
Kazakhstan has ordered output reductions for cadmium at a refinery after authorities launched a crackdown following odor complaints, according to a government directive seen by industry sources.

The order, issued by environmental and industrial regulators, requires the facility to cut cadmium production volumes and tighten operational controls as authorities investigate the source of the reported chemical smells. Cadmium is typically produced as a byproduct of zinc smelting and is used in batteries, pigments and industrial coatings.

The measure follows a series of complaints from residents and local officials in the refinery’s surrounding area, which prompted inspections and, in some cases, temporary restrictions on operations. Regulators said the initiative is part of wider enforcement aimed at reducing atmospheric emissions and improving compliance with environmental standards.

Under the directive, the plant must submit updated schedules for production and emissions monitoring, including additional sampling and reporting. It is also required to implement corrective actions tied to odor control systems and to verify the effectiveness of measures such as filtration, ventilation adjustments and process containment.

The government’s environmental agency said the investigation will focus on whether emissions from refining processes or waste handling contributed to the odor reports. “We are taking immediate steps to limit potential impacts while checks are carried out,” an agency spokesperson said. The agency added that enforcement actions could be expanded if the refinery’s findings do not meet required thresholds.

Cadmium production reductions can affect supply for downstream users, although the scale of the cut was not immediately clear. Kazakhstan is a significant producer of metals linked to industrial supply chains, and changes to output at one facility can ripple to traders and manufacturers dependent on regional volumes.

Local authorities have also indicated that they will continue monitoring air quality around industrial sites during the review period. Refinery officials were not immediately available for comment, but industry sources said the plant is working to comply with the order while responding to regulator requests for technical documentation and monitoring results.

headline: Japan tightens selenium import documentation requirements amid customs backlog
body: Japan has tightened documentation requirements for selenium imports, effective immediately, seeking to reduce processing delays at customs as authorities face a growing backlog of shipments. Under the revised rules issued in March 2026 by relevant ministries, importers must submit more detailed declarations covering selenium form (metal, powder, or compound), origin, lot-level documentation, and batch-specific use classification before clearance. The change also increases the frequency of supporting paperwork checks, including cross-referencing purchase orders against manifests and requiring updated inventory statements for entries flagged for review. The measures come as customs clearance times have lengthened across multiple ports following a surge in container volumes in early 2026 and staffing shortfalls that have pushed queues into the late-March window. Industry participants said the additional steps are expected to affect lead times for regulated grades used in glass, electronics, and photovoltaic supply chains. Traders noted that while the policy is intended to prevent mismatches and rejections, compliance failures could trigger extended holds of several days for each nonconforming lot.

headline: Mexico probes bismuth smuggling ring leading to temporary seizure of refined product
body:
Mexican authorities have launched an investigation into a suspected bismuth smuggling ring after agents temporarily seized refined bismuth products, according to prosecutors and a government statement seen on Monday.

The operation, carried out in coordination with customs and federal security forces, resulted in the seizure of refined bismuth, a metal used in pharmaceuticals, semiconductors and low-melting alloys. Authorities said the seized material was held while the case is assessed and no final determination has been made on the legal status of the shipment.

Prosecutors said the investigation targets a network believed to be moving refined bismuth through Mexico using false documentation and routes intended to avoid customs scrutiny. Officials did not identify the quantity of material seized or name any suspects, citing the ongoing nature of the probe. A source at the investigation said additional cases could follow as authorities match shipping records with customs data.

Bismuth demand has been sensitive to disruptions in global supply chains, and refined material is typically tracked closely due to its use in specialized industrial applications. Mexico has also faced broader scrutiny in recent years over smuggling of industrial and mineral commodities, including metals and concentrates, with authorities highlighting the risks of tax evasion and revenue loss.

In a brief statement, prosecutors said investigators are seeking to determine the origin of the refined bismuth, the intended destination and whether the shipments were connected to companies operating without the proper permits. They also said they are working to identify the individuals involved in organizing the movement of the goods and any intermediaries responsible for paperwork.

Authorities said they are reviewing communications and financial records obtained during the operation, along with inspection reports from the point of seizure. The case is being handled by federal prosecutors, who said they will pursue charges where evidence supports allegations of smuggling and related offenses.

The temporary holding of the product reflects an initial step in a broader investigative process, prosecutors said, with procedures underway to confirm whether the material was imported legally and whether it was misdeclared. Further updates were expected as investigators complete forensic checks and trace the supply chain.

headline: South Korea imposes antimony trading limit after market manipulation probe begins
body: South Korea has imposed limits on antimony trading after an investigation into alleged market manipulation began, according to government and market sources on Monday. The Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy said the measure caps daily volumes for domestic buyers and requires additional reporting for transactions tied to international benchmarks, starting March 24 and running for an initial 60 days. The probe follows a surge in prices in early March, when antimony spot quotes on regional platforms rose by about 8% over two weeks before reversing. Regulators are examining whether trading conduct influenced offers and bids among a small set of brokers and smelters. Under the new rules, participants must submit trade records within 24 hours and provide end-use documentation for shipments above specified thresholds. The ministry added that noncompliance could trigger suspension of trading privileges and potential penalties under market-fairness regulations, with the first compliance checks planned for April 1.

headline: Germany announces force majeure at a germanium wafer supplier during supplier insolvency
body:
Germany has declared force majeure for supplies from a Germanium wafer producer amid the firm’s insolvency, according to a government statement, as authorities work to maintain availability of the material used in defence and high-performance electronics.

The measure was announced after the supplier, a key upstream provider of germanium wafers, entered insolvency proceedings that disrupted delivery schedules and threatened contracts tied to government procurement, the statement said. Force majeure allows the state and affected customers to suspend or adjust contractual obligations when performance is hindered by events outside the parties’ control.

Officials said the declaration covers specific contracts and delivery windows linked to germanium wafers and related services. It also provides a legal basis to negotiate revised timelines and procurement alternatives while the supplier’s restructuring or liquidation process plays out. The government did not name the company in the initial announcement.

Germanium, a critical input for optical systems, infrared sensors and certain semiconductor applications, has been subject to tighter supply conditions in recent years as global refining capacity remains concentrated and demand grows for technologies used in communications and surveillance. Wafer production is particularly sensitive to interruptions because orders require precise manufacturing steps and strict quality checks, industry sources have said.

A government spokesperson said Germany is coordinating with industrial partners and potential substitute suppliers to reduce reliance on the insolvent producer. “The force majeure declaration is intended to protect continuity of supply and ensure public contracts can be managed during an exceptional disruption,” the spokesperson said.

Under the terms of the declaration, buyers are expected to meet disclosure and documentation requirements demonstrating that delayed or missed deliveries are directly linked to the insolvency. Authorities said they are also working to assess whether existing stockpiles or alternative sourcing routes can cover near-term demand.

The development comes as Germany continues to evaluate its vulnerability to disruptions in critical raw materials and components. While the immediate focus is on germanium wafer availability, officials said the case underscores broader efforts to diversify sourcing and strengthen domestic and European industrial capacity for strategic materials.

headline: France imposes temporary bans on zirconium shipments pending batch traceability checks
body: France has imposed temporary bans on the import and in-transit movement of zirconium shipments as authorities complete batch traceability checks, according to an order circulated on 2026-03-22 and effective immediately. The measures apply to zirconium products entering through French customs points and to consignments already in transit, affecting declared volumes tied to specific producer lots. Regulators said the review focuses on linking material documentation to batch-level identifiers across commercial invoices, transport manifests, and supply-chain records, with additional screening for shipments that cannot be matched within defined traceability windows. Importers are required to pause processing and hold goods while batch verification is conducted, with clearance contingent on submission of lot genealogy records and production batch certification. While the suspension is described as temporary, officials did not provide a fixed end date, citing the need to complete checks for all affected lots over the coming weeks. Traders expect disruption to downstream processors, including refractory and ceramic users, as lead times extend and re-routing options through alternative EU entry points are evaluated.

headline: Australia strikes nickel supply contract as port authority suspends heavy-haul operations
body:
Australia has agreed a new nickel supply contract after a port authority suspended heavy-haul operations, affecting access to a key loading facility, according to parties involved in the deal.

The contract, announced in recent days by nickel suppliers and offtakers, is intended to keep deliveries moving while logistics are disrupted at the port. Under the agreement, shipments of nickel ore and related feedstock are to be rerouted or rescheduled to alternative handling windows, with terms tied to when heavy-haul trucks and rail services can resume normal throughput.

The port authority’s decision to suspend heavy-haul movements followed operational constraints at the port, including restrictions on heavy vehicle entries and yard loading capacity. The suspension has reduced the number of trucks that can be processed per shift and temporarily limited freight staging, industry participants said, forcing some producers to hold stockpiles and seek revised transport plans.

A trading company involved in arranging the new scheduling said the contract includes buffer clauses to manage delays. “We structured the supply schedule around the expected suspension period and built in delivery windows so customers can plan around temporary port capacity limits,” the company said in a statement.

The disruption comes as Australian nickel producers work through volatile demand and evolving pricing benchmarks for the metal used in stainless steel and batteries. Several producers rely on bulk logistics chains that can be sensitive to port operating changes, particularly during maintenance periods or when additional safety measures are introduced for heavy traffic.

Port officials said the suspension is aimed at maintaining safe operations and keeping traffic flowing within available infrastructure limits. They indicated that updates would be provided as soon as the heavy-haul restrictions are lifted or modified, and that freight planning for subsequent weeks would be subject to continuing assessments of port conditions.

In the meantime, some suppliers have begun moving cargo via alternative routes or shifting from truck-based haulage to other transport modes where available, according to logistics providers. The nickel supply contract is expected to take effect immediately, with deliveries subject to confirmation of loading capacity at the rescheduled facilities.

headline: China pledges steel output curbs that alter iron ore demand expectations in Q3
body: China pledged additional curbs on steel output that analysts said will reshape iron ore demand expectations for the third quarter, according to industry-linked signals on March 22, 2026. The new measures, aimed at tightening production in key provinces and reducing blast-furnace utilization, were framed as compliance with broader emissions and capacity-management goals running through July. Traders expect the tighter steel balance to limit incremental ore purchases as early as June, shifting seaborne demand forecasts for Q3 and potentially lowering average spot buying volumes. Iron ore benchmark prices had previously been modeled to track output growth, but expectations are now being revised toward a narrower call on imports during July–September. Market participants said domestic mills may draw down inventories faster than planned, depending on maintenance schedules and restocking patterns tied to government inspections. Several desks noted that the policy could also alter the regional mix of supplies, with higher-grade cargoes potentially seeing different pricing than lower-grade material.

headline: Iron ore falls after Brazil dam incident triggers logistics force majeure for miners
body:
Iron ore prices fell on Monday after a dam incident in Brazil raised concerns about disrupted rail and port logistics, forcing some miners to invoke force majeure provisions and adjust shipping schedules.

The incident, reported over the weekend in one of Brazil’s mining regions, prompted investigations and temporary operational restrictions at affected sites and nearby infrastructure. Traders said the latest developments could reduce the availability of contracted volumes and delay shipments into key export channels, tightening near-term supply expectations.

Several companies with shipments routed through affected corridors said they were assessing the impact on loading operations and transport capacity. In some cases, counterparties were notified that force majeure clauses would be applied for portions of production and deliveries, according to market participants and company statements shared with shipping partners.

Brazil is the world’s largest iron ore exporter, and logistics flow—particularly rail movement to Atlantic ports and subsequent loading—has been a critical driver of pricing in recent months. Any interruption, even if limited in duration, can quickly affect the timing of bulk shipments, which in turn influences spot differentials and benchmark expectations for the following weeks.

Analysts said the magnitude of the price move will depend on how quickly operators can restore normal rail and port throughput and whether insurance or regulatory reviews slow the resumption of full production. They also noted that the Brazilian industry’s concentration of rail dependence means that even short disruptions can cascade through shipping timetables.

“Any force majeure notice signals potential delays for contracted tonnage, and the market tends to price that risk quickly,” said one shipping and commodities lawyer familiar with bulk contract practice. “The key factor will be the scope of the incident and how swiftly infrastructure operators can resume operations.”

Iron ore has been volatile around changes in Chinese steel demand and freight costs, but market attention has recently shifted toward Brazil supply reliability after prior weather-related disruptions and maintenance bottlenecks. Traders reported that spot buyers were pressing sellers for updated shipping estimates, while some freight providers adjusted vessel bookings to align with revised port schedules.

By late trading, the fall in iron ore reflected a combination of logistics uncertainty and expectations that some shipments could be pushed into later delivery windows. The full effect on global supply, however, remained unclear as companies continued to quantify the operational impact and regulators conducted assessments at the dam and surrounding facilities.

headline: India extends coal-linked power curbs affecting steel mills using imported iron ore
body: India extended coal-linked power curbs on Monday, tightening operating conditions for industrial users, including steel mills that rely on imported iron ore. The measures, first introduced in 2025 and scheduled for periodic review, were extended through at least June 2026 amid continued efforts to control power costs and reduce coal burn during peak demand periods. Under the updated framework, selected captive power plants and high-load consumers face reduced dispatch entitlements and stricter time-of-day generation windows, with penalties tied to exceedance of contracted load bands. Traders said the curbs are likely to lower blast-furnace utilization in several regions that process imported ore, including coastal states that typically source higher volumes of lump and fine concentrate from overseas. Industry estimates indicate that each 1 percentage point drop in furnace run rates can shift procurement volumes by tens of thousands of tonnes monthly, depending on grade and conversion yields. Market participants reported that spot premiums for certain imported ore grades increased in the week ending 20 March 2026 as buyers reassessed monthly lift schedules to match revised power-linked production plans.

headline: China delays copper cathode customs clearance after new anti-smuggling rules
body:
China has delayed customs clearance for some copper cathode shipments after new anti-smuggling rules came into effect, according to traders and logistics providers, a move that could affect near-term arrivals and pricing expectations.

The delays began this week as customs authorities implemented tighter checks tied to the government’s broader effort to curb illicit trade in commodities. Several market participants said consignees were asked to provide additional documentation, including details on original producer lots, shipping routes and the basis for declared transaction values before releases were granted.

One trader based in North China said the new requirements were being applied unevenly across ports, with some consignments cleared quickly while others faced multi-day holds. “We are seeing requests for supplementary paperwork for certain bills of lading and certificates, and the process is slowing the release of goods at the docks,” the trader said.

Copper cathodes are routinely imported into China for use in domestic refining and manufacturing, and China remains a major driver of global copper demand. Delays at customs can reduce the pace at which metal enters bonded warehouses and can shift timing for physical trading, especially for shipments already on schedule for processing or onward distribution.

Shipping schedules may also be affected. Agents said some freight bookings are being held or rescheduled as importers wait for confirmation that paperwork is complete, while others are seeking revised declarations in order to avoid additional scrutiny. The bottlenecks are concentrated around the initial clearance stage rather than inland transport, logistics providers said.

While no official timeline for the rule implementation was immediately available from authorities, traders expect clearance practices to stabilize as documentation templates and compliance checks become standardized. Market participants said they are monitoring the impact on inventories and financing costs tied to warehousing and demurrage, particularly for cargoes contracted for delivery to specific buyers or refiners.

The delays come as copper markets already factor in changes to supply flows and demand from China, where import patterns can influence tightness across the physical chain. Traders said they will assess whether the holds remain isolated to certain origins and counterparties or expand to a broader range of shipments under the new anti-smuggling enforcement.

headline: Canada pauses tin concentrate exports due to grading audit and documentation disputes
body: Canada has paused tin concentrate exports after regulators initiated a grading audit and raised documentation disputes, according to industry sources and shipping filings reviewed on 23 March 2026. The suspension affects concentrate shipments scheduled to depart from Atlantic and Pacific ports, with customs clearance halted pending reassessment of ore grade reports tied to multiple contracts. The audit, launched following discrepancies in assay measurements used for pricing, will review consignments back to late January 2026 and verify traceability records for each lot. Exporters said the pause is expected to last “weeks” while suppliers and buyers reconcile weight, moisture, and payable tin percentages specified in sales agreements. The dispute centers on whether revised laboratory results and amended mill certificates qualify as acceptable substitutions under Canadian export documentation rules. Tin prices on the LME have shown volatility around the announcement, but no immediate national stockpile data were released, and the government did not provide a timeline for resumption.

headline: Bolivia declares force majeure on lithium-linked supply chains; copper shipments also affected
body:
Bolivia has declared force majeure affecting parts of its lithium-linked supply chains, and the disruption has also reached copper shipments, according to government communications and industry contacts.

The measures were issued as the government cited constraints on transport and service continuity, including interruptions affecting storage, loading and onward logistics for materials moving through Bolivia’s ports and cross-border routes. The government said the designation is intended to protect contracts and clarify responsibilities for delays that are outside the control of suppliers and transport operators during the period covered by the declaration.

In its notifications, Bolivia said the force majeure covers specific stages of procurement and delivery tied to lithium processing and sales, including shipment handling and export formalities. It also stated that copper exports are experiencing similar operational impacts, pointing to shared infrastructure and logistics nodes used for both metals, as well as continuity issues for warehousing and freight capacity.

Companies and counterparties are expected to review contractual terms for force majeure clauses, including any requirements for notice, documentation and renegotiated timelines. Several firms in the battery and metals supply chain said they are working with freight providers and port operators to reroute cargoes where possible, though options may be limited by customs clearance schedules and the availability of alternative trucking and rail capacity.

A government official said the declaration reflects “exceptional operational conditions affecting the movement of minerals through Bolivia,” adding that authorities are coordinating with relevant agencies and commercial partners to restore normal service “as quickly as feasible.” The official did not provide an end date for the disruption.

The disruptions come as lithium demand has surged globally, with buyers increasingly relying on long-term offtake agreements and secured supply from new producers. Copper, meanwhile, remains a key export product for Bolivia, and any delay can affect downstream refining and trading schedules, particularly for shipments contracted on time-sensitive terms.

Market participants said the near-term impact will depend on the length of the disruption and the extent to which shippers can substitute alternative routes or vessel schedules. They also noted that force majeure declarations can influence price settlement mechanics and delivery obligations under established metal trade contracts, potentially extending the window for rescheduling shipments.

headline: DRC cobalt prices jump as workers strike at cobalt refining zone in Lualaba
body: Cobalt prices rose sharply on March 23, after workers went on strike at a cobalt refining zone in Lualaba province in the Democratic Republic of Congo, the world’s biggest source of the metal. Trading desks reported supply disruption risk spreading across multiple refineries in and around the Lualaba processing corridor, with spot offers tightening as shipments were delayed into late March and early April. Market participants said production stoppages, reduced haulage from mines, and constrained logistics at concentrate collection points were driving near-term premiums, even as traders assessed longer-term output impacts. The strike follows a period of volatile pricing in which DRC shipments faced intermittent transport bottlenecks, and it comes as buyers seek physical material ahead of end-March settlement windows. Some contracts were reportedly repriced within 24 to 48 hours, reflecting higher treatment and logistics costs linked to the stoppage. Analysts said the next price move will depend on whether negotiations resume before month-end and whether refining units restart at planned capacity levels.

headline: Morocco suspends phosphate processing that co-produces vanadium, tightening regional supply
body:
Morocco has suspended phosphate processing operations that also produce vanadium, according to industry sources and documents seen by Reuters, tightening supply of the metal used in steel and batteries across the region.

The suspension affects processing units at Morocco’s phosphate industrial sites that recover vanadium as a co-product during the conversion of phosphate rock into fertiliser intermediates, the sources said. The companies involved have paused output while they review environmental compliance and operational parameters, they added.

Vanadium is typically extracted from residues generated in phosphate processing, and Morocco has been among the regional sources feeding demand from steelmakers seeking stronger low-alloy grades. With the processing pause, nearby buyers are expected to look for alternative feedstock routes or step up imports, increasing competition for vanadium-containing slags and concentrates.

A spokesperson for the operator of one affected facility declined to give details on timing, but said the pause was linked to “a scheduled review of production conditions and regulatory requirements.” Another industry participant said the suspension was not a full shutdown of phosphate production, but a halt specific to the streams tied to vanadium recovery.

The shift comes as vanadium prices have been sensitive to changes in production schedules and shipping logistics, with supplies often moving through concentrated channels. Morocco’s phosphate sector is large, and the vanadium co-production route has historically allowed operators to monetise residues that might otherwise have limited use.

Regional supply chains could see additional strain because vanadium is not produced at scale in all countries that import Moroccan phosphate products. Buyers in Europe and parts of Asia have long relied on a mix of primary vanadium output from mining and secondary recovery from industrial by-products, according to trade data.

Morocco’s suspension is likely to be temporary, but the duration will depend on how quickly operators can adjust processing conditions and satisfy authorities, the sources said. In the meantime, some traders have begun marketing alternative volumes from other recovery routes, and steel mills are expected to draw down inventories or switch to different alloying strategies where possible.

Officials in Morocco did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

headline: South Africa halts platinum-group catalyst shipments after refinery fire forces maintenance
body: South Africa has paused shipments of platinum-group metal catalysts after a refinery fire forced unplanned maintenance, according to industry sources and shipping updates reviewed on March 23. The stoppage affects exports tied to several catalyst grades used in automotive and industrial emissions control, with affected volumes estimated in the “single-digit thousands” of tonnes for the next loading window. Trade routes out of South African ports were partially disrupted as terminals halted loading while inspections and safety checks were completed following the incident on March 19. One operator said catalyst production lines were operating at reduced capacity through the end of the month, pending replacement of damaged equipment and turnaround work. Spot availability of select palladium- and rhodium-containing catalyst components tightened over the past week, while freight estimates rose as carriers rerouted to maintain delivery schedules. Traders expect shipments to resume in stages around April 2, assuming maintenance completes on schedule, but any delay could shift deliveries into April’s first half and add incremental working-capital costs for buyers.

headline: U.S. sanctions review triggers palladium shipment delays from a European refiner
body:
A review of U.S. sanctions has caused delays to palladium shipments from a European refinery as traders and buyers adjust to tighter compliance checks, industry sources said.

The disruption affects deliveries tied to U.S.-linked end users and intermediaries, according to two logistics and trading contacts familiar with the matter. Several shipments scheduled for the next window have been held at export documentation stages while freight forwarders seek updated confirmations of ownership, routing and trade counterparties, they said.

Refiners and distributors that process precious metals typically ship under frameworks that require screening for sanctioned entities and for items potentially linked to restricted jurisdictions. Under the current review, U.S. compliance teams are re-examining documentation for certain consignments, including declarations of ultimate beneficial ownership and the absence of restricted parties in the supply chain, the sources said.

A trader at a European brokerage said the timing has been disrupted more by paperwork than by production. “We are seeing delays mainly in the sign-off process for export paperwork and end-use assurances, not a shutdown of output,” the trader said, adding that insurers and carriers were also requesting additional details before proceeding.

The delays, while short term in some cases, raise near-term availability concerns for purchasers of palladium used in catalytic converters and industrial applications. Traders said they are exploring alternative shipment schedules, including rerouting via other ports and substituting with inventory already in regional warehouses. However, any rerouting can add days to lead times and may increase costs linked to storage and insurance.

U.S. sanctions reviews can affect commodities by tightening transaction screening requirements for parties operating across borders. In this instance, the changes have been described by sources as a “process adjustment” rather than a new ban, but market participants said even incremental compliance delays can ripple through tight precious-metal logistics chains.

The European refinery involved did not immediately respond to a request for comment. A representative for a related trading firm said the company is working with brokers and freight providers to complete the documentation needed to clear shipments as quickly as possible, and that the review is being handled through standard compliance channels.

Industry sources said additional updates are expected as U.S. counterparties complete their checks, which should determine whether the next wave of consignments can move on schedule.

headline: China issues export licensing row for tungsten as government revises quota policy
body: China’s commerce authorities have triggered a fresh export licensing dispute for tungsten after revising the country’s quota policy for the metal used in cutting tools and aerospace components. The update, effective from 2026-04-01, shifts how firms apply for export permits by tightening documentation requirements tied to annual allocation and end-use declarations. Market participants said the change is expected to affect shipments of tungsten concentrates, scheelites and ammonium paratungstate, with licensing reviews typically taking 10–20 business days under the new procedures. Exporters reported that quotas for 2026 were rebalanced across provinces and state-linked buyers, reducing flexibility for mid-sized traders that rely on spot tenders. Several traders indicated they are seeking clarification on whether previously issued permits remain valid if quota adjustments are applied retroactively. The policy revision comes as global tungsten prices have been volatile over the past 3 months, with buyers in Europe and North America monitoring lead times for licensed product.

headline: Kazakhstan declares emergency at molybdenum plant after cyclone damages storage tanks
body:
Kazakhstan has declared an emergency at a molybdenum plant after a cyclone damaged on-site storage tanks, the government said on Tuesday, prompting inspections and temporary restrictions on operations.

The emergency declaration covers the plant’s processing and storage facilities, where several tanks used for holding intermediate materials were reported to have been damaged by strong winds and flooding associated with the cyclone. Officials said safety measures were put in place to prevent further leaks and to control any risk of contamination to surrounding areas.

Authorities said emergency services and plant personnel began stabilisation work immediately after the incident, including diverting materials from affected units and monitoring air and water quality. Fire-fighting equipment was deployed, and teams conducted pressure checks and visual assessments of the tanks and connecting pipelines. Workers were moved away from compromised sections while repairs and technical evaluations were carried out.

The Ministry of Emergency Situations said it has established an incident response headquarters and is coordinating with environmental regulators and local authorities. “The priority is to ensure the uninterrupted safety of personnel and to eliminate the consequences of damage to storage tanks as quickly as possible,” a ministry spokesperson said.

Molybdenum production is concentrated in Kazakhstan’s industrial regions, and plants in the sector rely on storage infrastructure for chemicals and concentrates used during processing. The government said the emergency regime will last until authorities confirm the condition of the damaged equipment and approve a plan for returning units to normal operation.

Separately, regional administrations said they had started clean-up and drainage efforts in areas affected by the cyclone, including checks for water ingress and signs of structural stress at nearby infrastructure. Residents in surrounding settlements were not reported to have faced mandatory evacuation, though officials said monitoring of nearby water sources is ongoing.

The government did not provide an estimate for the duration of disruptions or the amount of material potentially affected. It said updated information would be released after damage assessments are completed and after environmental findings from early sampling are reviewed.

headline: China’s graphite anode buyers face spot shortages after kiln shut down for inspections
body: China’s graphite anode buyers are facing near-term spot shortages after a kiln used for anode-powder processing was shut for inspections, according to traders and industry sources. Several mills reportedly reduced kiln runs starting around 2026-03-18, tightening inventories for higher-purity materials typically delivered for April and May loading windows. Spot offers for ready-to-use anode-grade feedstock were said to be scarce across major regions, with sellers prioritizing contracted customers and extending lead times by 1 to 3 weeks. The disruption comes as Chinese anode demand remains elevated for lithium-ion battery production, with refiners already managing tight schedules for precursor and heat-treated inputs. Inspections are expected to be completed on a short timeline, but buyers are bracing for additional delays if kiln restart testing runs over into late March. Some buyers are shifting purchases toward alternative grades and sourcing from smaller suppliers, although premiums on spot volumes are widening given limited substitute availability.

headline: Singapore introduces temporary cadmium handling ban on obsolete batteries after compliance audit
body:
Singapore has introduced a temporary ban on the handling of cadmium-containing obsolete batteries following a compliance audit, the government said, as authorities move to tighten controls on the metal’s disposal.

The measures apply to the handling of certain categories of used or obsolete batteries that contain cadmium, which is regulated due to its potential environmental and health impacts. The temporary restriction is intended to prevent improper storage, collection, treatment or transport while stakeholders complete remedial actions required under Singapore’s waste and chemical management rules.

In a statement, the Ministry of Sustainability and the Environment said the ban was introduced after an audit found non-compliances in parts of the battery waste handling chain. The ministry did not disclose detailed findings but said the review covered relevant operators’ compliance with licensing and operating requirements, as well as procedures for segregation, documentation and safe handling.

Under the new rules, companies involved in the affected handling activities must comply with the temporary prohibition and submit or update plans to address the audit results. The ministry said it will work with licensed facilities and industry players to ensure that compliant routes for processing and disposal remain available, while non-compliant activities are paused during the restriction period.

The ministry added that the temporary ban is part of ongoing efforts to reduce risks associated with regulated hazardous substances in the waste stream. It said the approach is aligned with Singapore’s broader efforts to strengthen environmental compliance and improve traceability in waste management.

A spokeswoman for the ministry said in the statement that the audit “highlighted areas that require stricter adherence to safety and regulatory requirements,” adding that the temporary ban is meant to “protect public health and the environment” while corrective measures are implemented.

The restrictions are expected to remain in place until the government is satisfied that affected operators have addressed the compliance gaps identified in the audit. The ministry said further updates would be provided as the review and remediation process progresses, and it urged industry to monitor notices and guidance published for the sector.

headline: India restricts beryllium exports for safety compliance after audit findings at a supplier
body: India has restricted exports of beryllium following safety and compliance findings from an audit at a supplier, according to government notifications circulated this week. The measures, effective immediately from 2026-03-24, require exporters to submit updated end-use documentation, shipment-level batch records, and proof of compliance with handling and storage rules before customs clearance. Regulators cited gaps identified during an audit covering production lots shipped between 2025-10 and 2026-02, including missing traceability entries and incomplete documentation of exposure-control procedures. Exports without the additional paperwork face delays or rejection, while shipments already in transit must be reassessed through a supplemental review process. The restrictions apply to beryllium metal and beryllium-containing materials used in aerospace, electronics, and specialty alloys. Industry sources said licensing and inspections are expected to raise processing times, with lead times potentially extending by 2 to 6 weeks depending on documentation readiness and audit follow-up status.

headline: Oman suspends zirconium sponge shipments after customs audit flags misdeclared lots
body:
Oman has suspended shipments of zirconium sponge after a customs audit flagged several lots as misdeclared, according to trade sources and documentation reviewed by Reuters.

The suspension, affecting shipments bound for customers abroad, was triggered after customs officials at a port where the material was cleared identified discrepancies in declared specifications, the sources said. In some cases, the audit found that the consignments’ stated metal grade and intended use did not match product descriptions provided at inspection, prompting officials to place the lots under hold pending review.

A trader involved in the logistics of the affected goods said the issue was discovered during routine documentation checks rather than following any seizure or safety incident. “Customs compared the declarations against supporting invoices and inspection records and stopped further dispatches until they are satisfied the lots comply with the declared parameters,” the trader said.

Zirconium sponge is used primarily in nuclear fuel cycle applications, including the manufacture of zirconium alloy components, as well as in specialty chemical and industrial processes. Because of its strategic uses, shipments are typically subject to enhanced scrutiny and to licensing or documentation requirements in addition to standard import and customs checks.

Oman’s customs authority did not immediately respond to requests for comment. However, one logistics provider said the affected shipments were being held at the port and that shipments of other consignments using similar product descriptions were subject to additional verification, including extended document reviews and, in some cases, requests for supplementary certificates of composition.

The suspension is expected to disrupt near-term deliveries for buyers who had placed orders on a tight timetable. Exporters and freight forwarders said they are working with customs to resolve the discrepancies, including by providing updated certificates and explanation letters for the misdeclared lots. Once clearance is granted, the shipments may resume, though timelines depend on how quickly customs completes its review, sources said.

The case comes as global trade in sensitive materials remains under close monitoring amid tightened compliance requirements in multiple jurisdictions. Companies involved in zirconium supply chains say audits and documentation controls have become more frequent in recent years, increasing the risk that even administrative errors can delay deliveries.

headline: Russia halts bismuth deliveries under force majeure after subcontractor power outages
body: Russia has suspended deliveries of bismuth to customers citing force majeure after power outages disrupted output at a key subcontractor, according to industry contacts and shipping notices dated March 23. Several shipments scheduled for late March and early April were paused, with some buyers told that loading could restart only after electrical systems at the affected production site are restored and stabilized. The company’s notices reference the impact of grid interruptions on refining lines used to produce high-purity bismuth, used in electronics and alloys, and they set no fixed resumption date. Traders said the halt may tighten near-term availability for material grades commonly tracked in London and Asia, where contracts often depend on Russian spot supply. Bismuth prices have been sensitive to delivery timing, and the suspension comes as secondary smelters operate near capacity and inventories are thin. Additional shipments could be reorganized into May allocations if power conditions remain erratic, contacts said, while force majeure language typically limits liability for late or missed deliveries.

headline: Peru restricts molybdenum road haulage as protests block mine access routes
body:
Peru has introduced restrictions on the road transport of molybdenum after protests disrupted access to mining routes used to move concentrate and related materials, the government said on Tuesday.

The Ministry of Energy and Mines issued a measure limiting haulage by road in areas near affected corridors, requiring permits for shipments during the period of demonstrator activity. The rule also sets conditions for scheduling and convoy movement, aiming to reduce bottlenecks and limit the risk of further obstruction to mine access roads.

The restrictions come as local protests in multiple regions have targeted transportation infrastructure linked to mining operations. Demonstrators have blocked roads and access points used by trucks supplying mines or taking out processed product, according to transport operators and regional authorities. Some mining companies have reported delays in dispatching material, which can affect processing schedules and inventory planning.

Energy and Mines said the decision was taken to ensure the “continuity and safety” of mineral logistics while routes are disrupted. It instructed regional authorities to coordinate with police and municipal agencies to implement the permits and verify that vehicles comply with weight, scheduling and security requirements. The ministry did not specify how long the restrictions would remain in force, saying they would be reviewed based on conditions on the ground.

Industry representatives said the measure is likely to concentrate shipments into designated windows and routes, increasing operational costs for companies and logistics providers. They also said the most significant impacts are expected on mines that rely primarily on road haulage rather than rail or port scheduling buffers.

A spokesperson for the ministry said in a statement that “shipments of molybdenum by road will be subject to prior authorization” during the disruption period, adding that authorities will monitor compliance to prevent traffic from being rerouted into blocked areas. The government did not indicate any immediate changes to mining production limits or export rules.

Molybdenum is used mainly in steelmaking to strengthen alloys and improve heat resistance. Peru is among the world’s notable producers, with output tied to both domestic processing and export markets. Authorities said the restrictions are intended to maintain orderly transport rather than alter mining output, while protests continue to affect access routes.

headline: EU issues import suspension for antimony from a processor under quality-control investigation
body: The European Union has issued an import suspension covering antimony shipments from a specific processor that is under quality-control investigation, according to an EU notice dated 2026-03-24. The measure applies to consignments entering the EU from the named supplier and remains in force until the investigation concludes and the company demonstrates compliance with agreed quality standards. EU customs authorities were instructed to withhold clearance for covered product lots, delaying entry at member-state ports and border inspection points starting immediately following publication. The suspension was triggered after abnormal quality findings were reported in sampled material, with officials citing concerns over consistency in antimony content and trace impurity levels. The investigation is expected to include batch-by-batch testing, documentation review, and verification of production controls over a defined reference period. Market participants said the action could tighten availability for industrial customers reliant on antimony inputs ahead of second-quarter contracts, though the immediate impact will depend on stock coverage and substitute sourcing.

headline: Indonesia halts nickel ore exports for 30 days starting Aug 14
body:
Indonesia will halt exports of nickel ore for 30 days starting Aug. 14, the government said, a move aimed at enforcing domestic processing requirements for the mineral.

The suspension covers shipments of nickel ore and related products, according to a ministry notification issued on Monday. The measure is intended to ensure nickel resources are processed within Indonesia before being exported, the notification said. The halt is set to run through Sept. 13, unless extended or adjusted by subsequent regulations.

Indonesia is the world’s leading exporter of nickel ore and a key supplier of the metal used in electric vehicle batteries and stainless steel. While the country has tightened rules in recent years to limit ore exports, exporters have increasingly relied on supply from lower-grade material and on refining capacity that has expanded unevenly across the archipelago.

The government announcement comes as companies in the nickel sector prepare shipping schedules and contracts around Indonesia’s regulatory timeline. Industry participants have previously said sudden enforcement actions can lead to short-term disruptions in global ore availability, though they also tend to push buyers toward processed nickel products or alternative sources.

Authorities said the 30-day pause is part of ongoing compliance efforts under Indonesia’s export framework. The notification did not specify the volume of ore affected, but it directed exporters to follow the revised requirements effective from the Aug. 14 start date.

A senior government official, speaking on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to discuss the matter publicly, said the suspension targets nickel ore shipments that have not met processing obligations and that the policy allows time for adjustments by industry players. The official also said Indonesia would continue to monitor shipments to ensure compliance with export rules.

Indonesia’s nickel industry has been dominated by downstream investment in smelters and refineries, which have drawn billions of dollars from major mining and trading firms.
