Capacity additions have gradually increased the supply of nickel products used for battery materials, but producers still depend on ore flows and on consistent permitting for exports.

The 30-day export halt is expected to be closely watched by traders and battery supply chains, which rely on predictable procurement schedules for raw materials during periods of demand growth.

headline: Zambia suspends copper loading at two ports amid rail labor strike
body: Zambia has suspended copper loading at two ports after a rail labor strike disrupted deliveries from mines in the Copperbelt, according to officials and industry sources. The stoppage begins immediately at the ports of export at the affected facilities and covers shipments scheduled for the next several days, with some cargos held for rescheduling rather than redirected to alternative routes. The work stoppage began late in the week, tightening logistics for refiners and trading houses that rely on rail for bulk concentrate and cathode movements to the coast. Zambia’s rail network, which moves a majority of national copper exports, has faced delays since 2026-03-21, according to tracking data. The suspension comes as buyers seek tighter delivery windows ahead of April payments, while smelters reported rising inventories at inland yards. Market participants said the measure could reduce near-term shipments and increase demurrage risk, depending on whether freight operations restart within 48 to 72 hours.

headline: DRC approves cobalt force majeure claims at two processing plants
body:
The Democratic Republic of Congo has approved force majeure claims for cobalt at two processing plants after production disruptions tied to power shortages and supply constraints, according to a government decision seen by industry sources.

The approval, issued by the DRC’s relevant authorities, covers operations at two sites involved in cobalt processing and follows notifications submitted by the operators describing circumstances beyond their control. Force majeure is typically used in contracts to suspend obligations such as delivery or payment when specified events prevent performance.

The decision allows the companies to invoke the clause for the affected period, industry participants said, reducing the risk of penalties linked to shortfalls in output or shipment schedules. The plants were among those affected by irregular electricity availability and logistics bottlenecks that have weighed on concentrate processing and refined output.

Cobalt remains a key export commodity for the DRC and a central input for global battery supply chains. Under the DRC’s contractual framework with commercial offtakers and traders, force majeure can be triggered when disruptions prevent meeting delivery commitments, subject to notification and approval requirements.

A government official familiar with the matter said the approvals were issued after reviewing the evidence provided by the companies and verifying that the disruptions met the force majeure criteria outlined in the relevant agreements. “The authority examined the causes cited by the operators and confirmed that they were outside their control,” the official said.

The approvals come as the DRC seeks to maintain investor confidence while balancing expectations from buyers that are monitoring supplies of cobalt and other critical minerals. Companies operating in the country have increasingly cited infrastructure and energy constraints as well as transportation delays affecting shipments to regional hubs and international markets.

Operators at the two plants are expected to continue remediation steps while they work to restore normal throughput. In the meantime, their ability to defer contractual obligations related to processing volumes is expected to be aligned with the approved force majeure period, the sources said.

Market participants will be watching whether the approvals translate into changes in delivery schedules for cobalt from the affected sites and whether additional requests from other processors are submitted. The DRC, which accounts for a large share of global cobalt output, is also expected to continue publishing updates on material disruptions as the year progresses.

headline: China issues provisional ban on graphite export quotas for July 2026
body: China has issued a provisional ban on graphite export quotas for July 2026, according to a notice circulated by relevant authorities on March 25, 2026. The measure targets shipments covered by quota allocations for graphite used in battery anodes and high-temperature industrial applications. The provisional restriction is scheduled to apply from July 1, 2026, and runs through the quota period for that month, with approvals for covered shipments suspended pending further review. Trading sources said affected consignments may face delays at customs if export licenses tied to the July 2026 quota have not been issued by the end of June. The policy comes as China continues to tighten controls on strategic raw materials and as downstream demand for spherical and flake graphite remains sensitive to supply availability. Market participants were asked to monitor updated quota and licensing guidance that could follow within the second quarter of 2026.

headline: Peru grants new tungsten export licenses after permit review delays
body:
Peru has granted new export licenses for tungsten shipments after reviewing permits that were delayed, the government said, as the Andean country works to restart flows to overseas buyers.

The Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM) said it issued additional licenses following “a review and adjustment” of tungsten export authorizations whose processing had been slowed. The measure is intended to ensure that producers holding valid permits can ship contracted volumes while the underlying administrative procedures are finalized, MINEM said.

Tungsten is mainly used in steel hardening, mining tools and other industrial applications, and Peru is among the world’s notable producers. Delays in export documentation can affect logistics schedules and downstream contracts for buyers who rely on timely deliveries, particularly for industrial and energy-related supply chains.

MINEM said the new licenses were approved for specific exporters and shipments and that the review focused on compliance with existing requirements, including documentation tied to production and shipment eligibility. The ministry did not provide a figure for the quantity of tungsten covered by the newly issued authorizations, or the number of licenses granted.

Several Peruvian companies involved in mineral processing have previously reported that permitting and export paperwork can be affected by changes in administrative timelines and the need for additional checks. In some cases, permits issued by regional authorities or required for customs clearance can be subject to follow-up reviews, extending the time between production and shipment.

Industry participants said the government’s action should help stabilize export schedules while regulators complete their permit review framework. MINEM added that exporters will remain subject to oversight and that the licenses are aligned with the country’s mining and export rules, including requirements relating to traceability and customs procedures.

A government statement said MINEM would continue to work through the remaining cases in the permit review queue. It also said it aims to reduce future delays by streamlining parts of the authorization process, including coordination between administrative offices involved in export documentation.

headline: South Africa halts platinum shipments as rail operator work stoppage spreads
body: South Africa has halted platinum shipments from key mines after a rail-operator work stoppage spread through logistics corridors used for exports, according to industry sources. The stoppage began late last week and by 24 March had disrupted loading at several hubs in Gauteng and the North West provinces, with freight trains left idle at marshalling yards. Export volumes were cut sharply as rail capacity to the Port of Durban and the Port of Richards Bay was reduced to a fraction of normal schedules, forcing some shippers to pause deliveries for spot contracts. Platinum flows were additionally affected by congestion at terminal gates, where container dwell times reportedly rose to 48–72 hours. Traders said the interruption could last several days depending on how quickly dispatch operations resume and whether unions agree to incremental service levels for inbound coal and outbound metals. Market participants expect immediate knock-on effects across autocatalyst supply chains, with nearby refiners bracing for tighter availability and delayed inventory builds into April.

headline: Russia temporarily suspends palladium processing after power grid fault
body:
Russia has temporarily halted palladium processing at a key industrial facility after a fault disrupted power supply, according to industry sources and company statements, in a move that could affect near-term output of the precious metal.

The suspension, which began following a disturbance on the regional power grid, has forced operators to pause processing activities while electricity stabilises and safety checks are completed. Processing is expected to resume only after equipment is restarted and power conditions meet operating requirements, the sources said.

Palladium is a critical input for catalysts used in automotive exhaust systems and industrial chemical processes. Russia is one of the world’s major producers, and any interruption in refining and processing can influence availability for buyers, particularly for contracts tied to monthly production schedules.

One of the plants involved is part of Russia’s integrated precious-metals production chain, where mined material is converted into refined products through a sequence of processing stages, including chemical treatment and recovery. When power supply is disrupted, operators typically scale back or stop steps that require stable electricity for heating, pumping and filtration systems, according to engineering practices cited by sources familiar with operations.

Operators said the fault did not involve any damage to core processing units, but that restarting would require a phased restart procedure. “The interruption was caused by the external power supply fault. The facility is conducting the restart programme and checks, and processing will continue once stable electricity is confirmed,” a plant representative said.

Grid faults have become an increasing operational risk for energy-intensive metallurgy, especially where utilities rely on ageing transformers and constrained transmission capacity. Russia has previously experienced short-term disruptions across industrial regions tied to power instability, though the duration and knock-on effects for metal output can vary depending on how quickly supply is restored and whether inventories can offset lost runs.

Market participants will be watching whether the stoppage remains limited to a temporary halt or extends into multiple processing cycles. Any extended pause could tighten domestic supply for refiners and downstream users, and potentially feed into pricing dynamics for palladium used in industrial manufacturing.

headline: China introduces emergency tariffs affecting vanadium ferroalloy imports
body: China has introduced emergency tariffs that affect imports of vanadium ferroalloys, according to trade notices circulated this week. The measures, effective immediately after publication on 25 March 2026, set provisional duties on shipments classified under vanadium ferroalloy product codes used by customs brokers for steelmaking inputs. The tariff rates apply to imports arriving by sea and rail from multiple overseas origins, with enforcement tied to the declared port of entry and the documented composition of the alloy. Traders said the emergency action targets a period of supply volatility ahead of the second-quarter procurement cycle, when major buyers typically rebalance inventories. Importers are required to file revised declarations and may face delayed clearance for cargo held for tariff-rate verification. Analysts expect the higher landed costs to filter into domestic pricing of vanadium-bearing steels within 2 to 6 weeks, depending on downstream offtake. The Ministry did not specify a fixed end date for the emergency schedule, but indicated ongoing reviews through mid-June 2026.

headline: Kazakhstan declares supply disruption for molybdenum concentrates after mine fire
body:
Kazakhstan has declared a supply disruption for molybdenum concentrates after a fire at a mine, government officials said on Wednesday, raising concerns about short-term availability of the key steelmaking and chemical input.

The disruption notice, issued by Kazakhstan’s authorities, said production and shipment schedules at the affected mining operation have been impacted since the blaze, although it did not immediately specify the size of the outage or the expected duration. Molybdenum concentrates are typically refined into molybdenum metal and chemicals used in steel alloys, catalysts and lubricants.

The incident occurred at the mine producing the concentrates shipped for further processing both domestically and for export, according to the notice. Fire damage and safety measures have interrupted normal operations, the authorities said, adding that affected stockpiles and logistics routes are being reassessed while inspections continue.

Traders and manufacturers rely on uninterrupted shipments because molybdenum concentrates are often booked on contracts tied to monthly or quarterly delivery windows. Any disruption can shift flows to alternative suppliers or increase near-term premiums for spot material, though the overall impact depends on the volume of output that has been taken offline and whether replacement shipments can be arranged from other producers.

Industry sources said Kazakhstan is one of the larger global suppliers of molybdenum concentrates. The country’s output is used by processors to produce ferro-molybdenum, which is incorporated into high-strength steels, including those used in the oil and gas sector, power generation and construction. Concentrates can also be converted into molybdenum trioxide and other chemical grades.

A representative for the mine operator, speaking in a statement referenced by Kazakhstan’s notice, said efforts are focused on restoring production and ensuring worker safety. “We are working to stabilize operations and evaluate the full extent of damage so we can resume shipments as soon as conditions permit,” the statement said.

Kazakhstan’s disruption declaration is intended to flag the event to buyers and counterparties and to support adjustments in contract and logistics planning. The authorities said further updates would be issued as repair timelines and shipment recovery progress become clearer.

headline: Canada pauses graphite offtake deliveries due to trucking force majeure
body: Canada has temporarily paused graphite offtake deliveries after a trucking force majeure was invoked, according to industry sources, disrupting planned shipments during the last two weeks of March 2026. The pause affects offtake volumes scheduled for transport from producing regions to downstream customers, with logistics operators citing route congestion and vehicle availability constraints that prevent timely pickups. Several contracts tied to monthly offtake schedules have been re-phased, delaying deliveries originally targeted for mid-to-late March and early April. Traders said the interruption is expected to be short if haulage conditions normalize, but the timing of resumption remains under review under force majeure clauses. The disruption comes as buyers seek to maintain inventory cover for battery-grade feedstock amid continuing uncertainty in North American supply chains. One logistics provider indicated that alternative trucking capacity is being arranged and that partial shipments may resume within days, but full catch-up volumes are not yet guaranteed.

headline: Rwanda starts arbitration over cobalt supply shortfalls from halted shafts
body:
Rwanda has begun arbitration proceedings related to cobalt supply shortfalls linked to mining shafts that were halted, according to documentation reviewed by industry participants.

The dispute centers on contractual deliveries of cobalt from mining operations in the country where production was disrupted following the suspension of certain shafts, raising questions about whether suppliers could meet volumes and timing agreed under supply arrangements. Rwanda, acting through relevant state entities, filed the arbitration to seek remedies for late or reduced deliveries and any associated costs, sources familiar with the matter said.

The arbitration is being handled under procedures set out in the parties’ contracts and the governing rules chosen at the time of signing, participants said. Several contracts include provisions on force majeure and adjustment mechanisms, but those clauses have become central to the case as Rwanda argues that the shortages were not fully covered by the events that prompted the shutdowns.

Industry groups and traders have been watching the case because the country’s output is frequently tied into regional and global supply chains used in battery materials. The loss of cobalt volumes—particularly where mining disruptions coincide with heightened demand—can force buyers to substitute from alternative sources, often at different prices and with different quality specifications, sources said.

Rwanda’s government has previously said it is tightening oversight around industrial activity and ensuring that mining operations comply with environmental and safety requirements. In recent years, several shafts in the region have been paused or closed, and the timing of those stoppages has varied by site, according to reporting and public statements from companies and regulators.

A representative for one of the contract parties declined to comment on the merits of the arbitration but said the process is being handled through the agreed legal framework. “We will address the claims through the arbitration process and provide the relevant documentation,” the representative said.

The arbitration follows wider efforts to stabilize critical mineral supplies as countries and automakers seek longer-term visibility over battery inputs. Traders said the outcome could affect future contracting terms, including how suppliers and buyers allocate responsibility for production disruptions and delivery shortfalls.

No hearing schedule was immediately available, and it was not clear which specific contracts are in dispute or the value of the claims.

headline: Norway suspends bismuth refining permits following environmental inspection findings
body: Norway has suspended bismuth refining permits after an environmental inspection found multiple compliance failures at two refining sites, according to a decision issued late on 2026-03-25. The suspension covers permits held for processing and recovery operations, with immediate effect from 2026-03-26, and requires operators to submit corrective action plans within 30 days. Inspectors cited exceedances of discharge limits for heavy-metal compounds, inadequate management of contaminated residues, and missing documentation for waste handling and storage across several production runs in 2025 and early 2026. Regulators said the findings indicate risks to surrounding soil and water, prompting the halt until monitoring data demonstrate compliance. The affected facilities were previously audited in 2024 and had received conditional approvals tied to upgrades in filtration and effluent treatment. Norway is a minor but strategic supplier of bismuth refining capacity for industrial alloys and semiconductors, and the suspension is expected to tighten regional availability over the second quarter of 2026.

headline: India restricts cadmium imports under revised safety rule effective May 1
body:
India has restricted imports of cadmium under a revised safety rule that takes effect on May 1, according to an official notification seen by industry participants.

The regulation tightens controls on the import of cadmium and cadmium compounds by requiring compliance with prescribed safety and handling standards, including limits on certain forms and conditions for authorisation. The rule applies to imports covered under the notification’s scope and replaces earlier provisions, the participants said.

Under the update, importers are expected to ensure that consignments meet documentation and traceability requirements, including information on the chemical form, purity and packaging specifications. The notification also calls for adherence to storage, transport and workplace safety measures to reduce exposure risks associated with the toxic metal, they said.

Cadmium is primarily used in rechargeable batteries, pigments and coatings, and is widely regulated globally due to its health and environmental hazards. In India, the metal is used in industrial applications and in supply chains that depend on imports of refined material and certain intermediate compounds, market participants said.

The Ministry’s notice was issued as part of an effort to align import controls with updated safety norms, according to a government source familiar with the matter, who said the revised rule is intended to “strengthen checks on hazardous substances at the point of import.” The source did not provide further details on enforcement timelines or penalties.

Industry representatives said the new effective date could affect procurement schedules for buyers who place orders ahead of shipping windows. Several importers are expected to seek clarity on paperwork requirements and classification of specific cadmium products to ensure shipments are accepted under the revised regime.

The rule is likely to be implemented through customs processes and pre-import compliance checks, participants said, adding that compliance documentation and product specifications will be key for clearance. If documentation does not match the updated requirements, shipments may face delays or rejection, they said.

Cadmium market dynamics in India could be influenced by the country’s import availability and global supply, especially for battery-grade material, though the extent of any price impact will depend on how quickly suppliers and buyers adjust to the new conditions, participants said.

headline: Germany seizes germanium lots at port over missing export documentation
body: German customs authorities seized multiple lots of germanium at a port facility after finding incomplete export documentation, according to officials familiar with the case. The action was taken as shipments were being processed for outbound transport, with inspectors citing missing or inconsistent certificates required for controlled technology-related materials. The seized consignment involved batches of germanium metal and intermediary product forms intended for export, with quantities reported by the kilogram range pending further verification. Officials said the review focused on whether the paperwork matched declared end users, destinations, and license conditions dated on or around March 2026. Under German and EU rules governing strategic raw materials, exporters must provide traceable documentation covering origin and intended use. The shipments were held pending submission of corrected documents and an assessment of compliance with export-control requirements. Authorities did not disclose the identities of the shipping parties or final destination, but said the investigation could determine whether administrative penalties or further enforcement steps are warranted.

headline: Japan tightens gallium shipments after customs backlog triggers delays
body:
Japan has tightened controls on gallium shipments after a customs backlog disrupted deliveries, industry sources said, prompting new screening and tighter routing requirements for the metal used in semiconductors and other electronics.

The Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI) informed importers of the measures in guidance issued this month, according to a document seen by industry participants. The steps include additional pre-notification of cargo details, more frequent checks of declared end-use information, and limits on shipment consolidation that can complicate customs clearance.

The disruption stemmed from delays at Japanese customs facilities as officials worked through an accumulation of declarations, the sources said. Several companies reported longer turnaround times for gallium lots arriving by air and sea, with some orders carried over into the next billing cycle. While gallium is not subject to the same licensing framework as some strategic technologies, importers said they faced renewed scrutiny under existing compliance rules.

A second issue raised by traders was that gallium is commonly shipped in forms that can be difficult to classify under standard product codes, particularly when mixed with other materials or processed for purification. That classification can determine which inspection procedures are triggered, sources said. Under the new guidance, importers are required to submit supporting documents earlier, including batch records and technical specifications, to reduce the likelihood of re-inspection after arrival.

“Customs processing capacity was strained, and the resulting holds affected delivery schedules,” one logistics executive at a freight forwarder said. “METI’s changes aim to smooth declarations upfront so shipments can move more predictably through clearance.”

METI did not respond to a request for comment.

Gallium demand has been a focus for governments seeking to secure supply chains for semiconductors, particularly as global output shifts toward fewer refining and production hubs. Japan is among the countries that import gallium for use in manufacturing and research, drawing from a limited pool of suppliers and refining capacity.

Industry participants said the new measures are likely to increase compliance costs and could slightly lengthen lead times for first-time importers, but they expected the rules to prevent larger disruptions. Importers have also been asked to coordinate delivery windows more closely with customs brokers and to avoid bundling multiple lots under one declaration where possible, according to the guidance.

headline: Australia bans temporary nickel concentrate stockpile transfers after audit
body: Australia has banned temporary transfers of nickel concentrate stockpiles following an audit by federal and state regulators, tightening controls on shipments at inland hubs and export terminals. The restriction, effective immediately as of 25 March 2026, applies to the movement of concentrate volumes staged for short periods pending sampling, dewatering, or re-bagging, and it does not cover fully documented final exports. Regulators cited nonconformities identified during audits of weighbridge records, chain-of-custody paperwork, and tracer-detention logs used to match incoming lots to outgoing consignments. The review focused on transfers carried out since 1 January 2025 across multiple jurisdictions, where the concentrate was reported under overlapping product codes and inconsistent moisture specifications. Companies must now submit revised transfer plans at least 14 business days before any staged movement and provide independent sampling results for each lot. Penalties for noncompliance range from administrative directions to suspension of export-related permits, with enforcement expected to begin after 30 days of transition.

headline: Tanzania halts iron ore rail shipments as union strike begins Nov 9
body:
Tanzania has halted iron ore rail shipments as a union strike scheduled to begin on Nov. 9 starts to disrupt transport of the mineral from mines to ports, according to industry officials.

The stoppage began after the rail network servicing iron ore operations suspended services on affected routes, following the start of industrial action by members of the workers’ union, who are demanding wage and working-condition changes. Several trains carrying concentrate and related cargo were held at loading points and en route sections, slowing deliveries to onward buyers and shipping terminals.

In communications with freight operators, rail and logistics providers cited operational constraints linked to the strike, including reduced staffing for signaling, locomotive maintenance and yard operations. Companies involved in the movement of iron ore said they were reassessing schedules and expected that delays would extend beyond the initial days of the walkout as crews and technical teams were affected.

The disruption is expected to impact export flows from northern and central Tanzania, where iron ore production relies on rail links to move bulk material. While some shipments can be rerouted to road transport for limited distances, carriers said trucking capacity is insufficient for the volumes handled by rail, particularly for concentrate grades that require tight handling and timing.

A union representative said the action was intended to force negotiations with employers ahead of an end-of-year bargaining window. “We are stopping work because talks have not produced agreements on pay and safety,” the representative said in a statement, adding that members would remain on strike until proposals meet their demands.

Authorities in Tanzania’s transport and labor sectors said they were monitoring the situation and called on parties to maintain essential services. The government has previously urged mediation during industrial disputes involving freight corridors, citing the knock-on effects that prolonged stoppages can have on revenue, inventory and downstream processors.

Iron ore remains one of the country’s key minerals for export earnings, and rail disruptions can compound existing logistics challenges such as seasonal weather and rolling stock availability. Companies said they would publish revised shipment plans once staff availability and dispatch schedules stabilize.

headline: Brazil declares emergency at steelworks after power rationing cuts output
body: Brazil has declared an emergency at its main steelworks after electricity rationing reduced power availability and cut production, according to officials on March 25, 2026. The government said rolling outages and reduced grid supply forced furnace curtailments that lowered output by more than 20% in the first week of rationing, with some lines operating at under half capacity. The emergency order covers staff redeployment, priority energy procurement, and accelerated maintenance to prevent equipment damage during intermittent supply. Local utilities reported that industrial allocations were tightened following a rise in system demand in late March, though the exact start date of the rationing program was not specified. The company warned that blast-furnace downtime could extend lead times for shipments scheduled for April. Emergency measures are scheduled to be reviewed every 72 hours, with targets tied to restored power deliveries and stabilized operating rates across the plant’s rolling and casting sections.

headline: Namibia suspends uranium concentrate exports pending regulatory review
body:
Namibia has suspended exports of uranium concentrate to allow for an ongoing regulatory review, the Ministry of Mines and Energy said on Thursday.

The decision affects the export of uranium concentrate produced in Namibia, which is typically processed further abroad for use in nuclear fuel and other industrial applications. The ministry said the suspension is intended to ensure that current permits, compliance steps and shipment conditions align with Namibian regulations and international requirements.

In a statement, the ministry said the pause is temporary and that the timeframe for the review would be announced once regulators complete their assessment. Officials said the suspension applies to exports that would otherwise proceed under existing procedures, while authorities continue to evaluate documentation related to mining, processing and export handling.

The ministry did not specify the exact number of shipments affected or the volume of concentrate involved. Namibia is one of several countries that produce uranium, and its exports are closely monitored because of the material’s sensitive nature and the need for oversight across the supply chain.

Regulatory scrutiny in uranium transactions typically covers licensing, safety standards, chain-of-custody documentation and safeguards measures intended to prevent diversion. Namibia participates in nuclear non-proliferation frameworks and requires exporters to meet conditions set by national regulators and relevant international bodies.

A senior ministry official said the review is focused on “confirming full regulatory compliance across licensing and export documentation before shipments resume,” without providing further details on which requirements may have been under review. The official added that companies holding authorisations are expected to cooperate with regulators during the assessment period.

The suspension comes as global interest in uranium remains high due to efforts by utilities to secure long-term fuel supplies. Namibia’s mining sector has also faced broader scrutiny in recent years over permitting, environmental controls and the handling of radioactive materials, areas that regulators typically prioritise during compliance checks.

No information was released on whether any specific producer or export route prompted the action. The ministry said exporters may submit additional materials as part of the review process and that updates will be issued once a determination is made on whether shipments can resume under existing terms or new conditions.

headline: Uzbekistan imposes shipment curbs on selenium due to quality compliance issues
body: Uzbekistan has imposed new shipment curbs on selenium imports after detecting recurring quality non-compliance by suppliers, according to market participants tracking the rare metals trade. The measures, effective immediately from 2026-03-26, restrict the acceptance of selenium consignments unless documentation and test results meet updated specifications for purity and impurity limits. Under the curbs, shipments arriving without batch-by-batch certificates of analysis may be delayed for re-testing, while lots failing compliance checks face rejection or return, traders said. The change applies to bulk and packaged selenium deliveries destined for domestic industrial users and warehousing facilities, with customs processing expected to slow during the first days of enforcement. Several companies have been asked to resubmit laboratory reports using agreed sampling methods and reporting formats, raising the probability of revised lead times of 3 to 10 business days. Market participants are also monitoring whether the restrictions could tighten available volumes in the near term and lift spot premiums for higher-grade material.

headline: China issues seizure notices for zirconium shipments after traceability audit
body:
China has issued seizure notices for certain zirconium shipments after a traceability audit found gaps in documentation, according to a notice circulated to importers and customs brokers.

The notices, issued by Chinese authorities in recent days, target consignments of zirconium and zirconium-related products where customs paperwork and shipment records did not match internal traceability requirements, the document showed. Importers were instructed to halt unloading or processing of affected cargoes and to present additional materials for review, including bills of lading, supplier declarations and batch traceability records, before further clearance.

Zirconium is widely used in nuclear fuel components, chemical processing equipment, ceramics and aerospace applications. In global trade, the material is often handled through multiple intermediaries, making end-to-end documentation a key focus for regulators seeking to verify provenance and compliance.

The audit that led to the notices was described in the circular as a review of “product traceability and transaction consistency” across shipments entering China. It flagged discrepancies such as missing or incomplete batch information, differences between declared shipment quantities and lot numbers, and cases where supplier documentation could not be verified against earlier filings. The audit also noted that some consignments lacked supporting records for “key identifiers,” a term used in the notice to describe batch or production-specific details.

One importer representative who received the notice said authorities had asked for additional verification but did not provide a timetable for release of the cargoes. “We are working with suppliers to reconcile lot numbers and provide the missing batch-level documentation,” the person said, speaking on condition of anonymity because the company was still in discussions with officials.

Customs brokers said seizure actions are typically part of a wider compliance process that can include inspections, reclassification of goods, or requests for documentary evidence before shipment release. In prior enforcement campaigns affecting other metals and specialty materials, authorities have relied on traceability audits to identify shipments with incomplete records and to delay clearance while verification is conducted.

For now, the notices apply to specific shipments identified in customs systems, rather than a blanket ban on zirconium imports, brokers said. Companies affected were urged to respond promptly to avoid escalation, including potential confiscation or penalties if authorities conclude documentation cannot be substantiated.

headline: Finland halts indium supply under customer dispute amid rising demand
body: Finland has halted deliveries of indium, a critical metal used in touchscreens and thin-film solar panels, after a customer dispute disrupted scheduled shipments, according to trade contacts. The suspension begins immediately, affecting volumes tied to contracts that were set to run through March and April 2026, with resumption dependent on settlement of billing and delivery terms. The stoppage is arriving as global indium demand rebounds from a two-year lull, with analysts pointing to steady project pipelines for LCD and photovoltaic components in Asia and Europe. Market participants said the halt tightens near-term availability despite existing stockpiles at processors in Germany, Japan and South Korea. The supply disruption also comes amid broader semiconductor-materials stress, where delivery lead times for indium-bearing intermediates have already stretched to several weeks. Traders expect price volatility as buyers seek alternative sources from China and Canada, while industrial end users plan inventory rebalancing to cover potential delays into Q2.

headline: Portugal restricts antimony exports citing stockpile management measures
body:
Portugal has moved to restrict exports of antimony, citing steps to manage national stockpiles of the strategic mineral, according to a government notice published this week.

The measure, issued under Portuguese authorities responsible for trade and industrial resources, places additional controls on shipments of antimony outside the European Union. It requires exporters to obtain authorisation and comply with conditions linked to domestic availability and stockpile levels. The restrictions apply to commercial flows covered by the relevant customs classifications, with the scope and licensing requirements set out in the official document.

Antimony is used in flame-retardant materials, batteries and alloys, and is also involved in certain industrial and defence supply chains. Global supply is concentrated, and European authorities have increasingly treated antimony as a critical raw material that may be vulnerable to market disruptions. In Portugal’s notice, officials pointed to the need to ensure continuity of supply for domestic industry and to align export management with stockpile policy.

The government said the export authorisations are intended to prevent sudden depletion of inventories and to support procurement planning. It also indicated that the licensing regime would allow authorities to monitor volumes leaving the country and to adjust measures if stock levels or market conditions change. Companies would be required to provide information about destination, quantities and intended use as part of the authorisation process.

An official from Portugal’s ministry overseeing the measure said the decision was aimed at “strengthening control over strategic inventories while maintaining a predictable framework for legitimate trade.” The ministry did not specify whether the restriction is temporary or permanent, but said it would be reviewed in line with stockpile management and policy objectives.

The notice also references existing European and national frameworks for critical raw materials, including efforts to diversify supply and reduce dependence on external sources. Portugal’s move comes as businesses and policymakers across Europe continue to assess supply-chain risks related to minerals such as antimony, which are not widely substituted in certain end uses.

Exporters affected by the change are expected to seek authorisations for shipments planned for the coming months, according to trade compliance practices typically required under export-control licensing schemes. No details were provided about specific companies or the immediate size of the impact on outgoing volumes.

headline: Poland flags cadmium supply risk after refinery maintenance extends to June
body: Poland has flagged a potential shortfall in cadmium supply after planned maintenance at a key refinery was extended, pushing scheduled restart out to June. Industry sources said the outage, originally timed for earlier in the quarter, will keep output curtailed through at least late May, with full operating rates expected only in June depending on commissioning checks and yield ramp-up. The risk is concentrated in contracts tied to spot and near-term delivery windows, where buyers typically require cadmium content specs for plating and stabilizer use. Traders noted that inventory levels in regional distribution channels are thin ahead of seasonal demand, with lead times already stretching by several weeks. Polish procurement officials are reportedly assessing alternative sourcing from other European processors and weighing conditional tenders linked to restart progress. The development comes as market participants monitor cadmium price sensitivity to treatment charges and refining throughput, with any further delays likely to tighten availability for April and May shipments.

headline: US sanctions delay tungsten shipments from a key supplier, traders report
body:
US sanctions have delayed tungsten shipments from a key supplier, traders said, as companies adjusted sourcing and logistics amid stepped-up scrutiny of restricted materials.

Several market participants reported that deliveries of tungsten ore and related concentrates were held up over the past two weeks, citing documentation checks and additional compliance reviews required by U.S. authorities. One trader said shipments that were expected to clear customs on a tight timetable were diverted to alternative inspection routes, extending transit times and pushing some orders into April.

The delays appear to be linked to the timing of U.S. enforcement actions that expanded restrictions on certain entities and material flows. Traders said the supplier’s contracts required revised end-use declarations and updated due-diligence statements before goods could be released. In some cases, freight forwarders reportedly sought confirmations from downstream customers on the final destination and processing plans to satisfy screening requirements.

Tungsten is widely used in steelmaking and industrial applications, including hard metals, cutting tools and aerospace components. Because tungsten supply chains rely on a limited number of producers and processing facilities, even short disruptions can affect inventories at traders’ warehouses and at manufacturers that hedge delivery schedules months ahead.

Market players said the impact so far has been uneven across regions. Some buyers reportedly secured partial shipments from stockpiles already en route to processing hubs, while others were forced to renegotiate delivery windows or switch to alternative feedstock. Several traders also said prices for spot tungsten concentrates and certain intermediate products moved modestly as buyers weighed the risk of further delays.

“There’s been extra paperwork at the border and the compliance checks are taking longer than usual,” one trader said, adding that carriers and brokers were coordinating with banks and shipping lines to prevent further holds.

Sanctions-related disruptions have also encouraged buyers to request more granular documentation, including chain-of-custody records and supplier certifications. Traders said that in the near term, order flow could depend on how quickly the supplier and its customers can provide the required assurances, as well as on U.S. agencies’ processing times for screening and licensing decisions.

While companies have not publicly detailed the specific shipments affected, traders expect negotiations between counterparties and compliance teams to continue. They said the next few weeks should clarify whether the delays are temporary or broaden into a longer procurement gap for the tungsten supply market.

headline: South Korea halts molybdenum production line after maintenance outage expanded
body: South Korea has halted operations at a molybdenum production line after a maintenance outage expanded beyond its original scope, according to industry sources tracking plant schedules. The shutdown, which began during routine maintenance on March 24, affected upstream processing equipment and forced operators to stop both concentrate handling and downstream roasting, reducing throughput across the site. Plant operators initially expected restoration within 12 to 18 hours, but recovery delays extended into March 26 as troubleshooting uncovered additional electrical and cooling-system faults. Inventory coverage at the facility is limited, with several days of working stock tied to daily drawdowns used for contract shipments. The interruption is expected to disrupt procurement of intermediate molybdenum materials from domestic processors and shift some deliveries into early April. Industry participants said the event could tighten South Korea’s near-term availability for molybdenum products, potentially influencing regional pricing benchmarks for at least the March 27–April 3 window. Further updates depend on equipment diagnostics and restart testing, which will determine whether the line returns in phases or remains offline until repairs are complete.

headline: Turkey suspends beryllium export licenses amid export-control review
body:
Turkey suspended some export licences for beryllium following a review of its export controls, according to a notice issued by the country’s customs authority this week.

The measure affects licences related to beryllium products and reflects an administrative reassessment of how Turkey applies restrictions under international export-control regimes, the notice said. It did not specify which shipments are covered, but indicated that the suspension would remain in place while the review is concluded and new authorisations are issued or existing ones are reconsidered.

Beryllium is used in specialised sectors including aerospace components, industrial electronics and defence-related systems due to its strength-to-weight ratio and thermal properties. Because of its potential end-use in high-performance technologies, shipments are typically monitored through licensing requirements and end-user checks.

Turkey’s export-control framework is designed to align with restrictions imposed by export-control partners and multilateral non-proliferation understandings. The customs authority said the suspension was part of routine enforcement and compliance work and referenced the need to ensure that exports meet the conditions required under applicable regulations and licence terms.

Companies involved in the trade said they are seeking clarification on how the suspension will affect outstanding applications and deliveries. A source familiar with the licensing process said exporters are expected to provide updated end-use documentation and information on final destinations to complete the review. “The suspension is meant to pause processing until the authorities confirm compliance with the revised review requirements,” the source said.

No immediate details were provided about timelines for lifting the suspension or the scope of any future approvals. The notice also did not comment on whether Turkey would introduce new restrictions or simply apply existing ones more strictly.

The development comes as governments worldwide examine export licensing for sensitive materials used in military and advanced industrial applications. In addition to administrative reviews, such checks often focus on changes in end users, routing, and whether shipments could be linked to prohibited or high-risk programmes.

For now, beryllium exporters that rely on licence issuance from Turkish authorities said they are adjusting compliance procedures to meet the review’s requirements. The customs authority did not indicate whether enforcement actions would extend beyond the suspended licences or whether other mineral-related exports are under similar scrutiny.

headline: Vietnam halts graphite sales from a mine after licensing review gridlock
body: Vietnam has stopped graphite sales from a mine in a move tied to a licensing review that has stalled, according to industry participants. The halt, effective immediately this week, applies to shipments sourced from a domestic operation pending clearance of export permits and related environmental documentation, with authorities citing administrative bottlenecks rather than mine performance. Buyers were informed that contracts may be paused through at least late April 2026, depending on when the review panel clears the files. The decision follows several months of scrutiny that began after licensing paperwork was submitted in late 2025, with revisions requested through February 2026 and subsequent inter-agency signoff delays. Market observers said the stoppage could tighten supply for anode-grade and flake graphite used in batteries, where lead times are typically 6–10 weeks. Traders expect pricing volatility for spot lots as alternatives are sourced from China and Africa ahead of the new quarter.

headline: Oman declares force majeure for copper cathode exports after port damage
body:
Oman has declared force majeure for copper cathode exports after damage at a key port facility disrupted shipments, according to a notice issued by an industry authority on Thursday.

The declaration applies to contracts covering copper cathode shipments leaving Oman during the period in which the disruption continues, the notice said. It cites operational impacts at the port stemming from recent damage, which has affected loading schedules and the availability of berths for export cargoes.

Market participants said the declaration is expected to allow exporters and buyers to suspend performance under affected contracts without triggering penalties, depending on contract terms and the duration of the disruption. Copper cathode shipments are typically scheduled well in advance, and delays can shift delivery windows, affecting settlement dates for traders and downstream processors.

A representative for the Oman metals and minerals sector authority said the force majeure was issued to ensure fair treatment for counterparties while repair and recovery work proceeds. “The declaration is intended to reflect circumstances beyond the parties’ control and to provide a framework for contract management during the interruption,” the representative said.

The notice follows reports that port operations were disrupted after damage to infrastructure used for export handling. Authorities have indicated that repairs are underway, and shipping schedules may remain uneven until inspections are completed and normal loading procedures resume.

Oman is a producer and exporter of copper cathodes used in refining and industrial applications, with exports typically moving by sea to regional and international buyers. In recent years, Oman’s output has fed both spot and longer-term supply arrangements, making port reliability a key factor for consistent deliveries.

Traders said shipments affected by the force majeure may be redirected once the port returns to full capacity, or buyers may seek alternative supply from other producers. Any further extension of the declaration would depend on updates to port status and the pace of restoration work, they added.

headline: Qatar imposes 60-day restriction on vanadium deliveries after inspection dispute
body: Qatar has imposed a 60-day restriction on vanadium deliveries following an inspection dispute, according to trade and shipping sources. The measure, effective immediately and running until mid-May 2026, applies to consignments arriving at Qatari ports under existing vanadium contracts and requires additional documentation tied to quality checks. Several shipments scheduled for late March and April were held at anchor or diverted to waiting areas as inspectors verified traceability records, the sources said. Market participants reported that the decision could tighten spot availability for vanadium products used in steelmaking, with premiums expected to widen as distributors seek alternative supply routes. The dispute centers on whether incoming material meets agreed inspection protocols and sampling intervals, prompting Qatar’s customs and industrial oversight authorities to pause acceptance pending review. Traders said they are monitoring whether the restriction will be extended or replaced by a revised inspection framework after the 60-day window.

headline: China requires new paperwork for tungsten shipments, raising logistics delays
body:
China has introduced new paperwork requirements for tungsten shipments, a change that industry participants said is slowing logistics and increasing compliance burdens for exporters and traders, according to documents shared with trade intermediaries and freight operators.

The updated administrative steps apply to outbound shipments of tungsten and related products, requiring additional declarations and supporting documentation before goods clear customs, the sources said. Several intermediaries described the measures as an expansion of existing customs controls rather than a ban, but noted that the additional forms can extend processing times at ports and border points.

Shipping timelines are already tight for tungsten used in hard metals, tooling and aerospace components, and the new documentation adds further coordination between manufacturers, trading houses and freight forwarders, sources said. In practical terms, companies must align commercial invoices, product codes and end-use information with the new paperwork, and provide supporting materials that customs authorities can request for review.

One logistics provider said the change has created temporary uncertainty over which form sets are required for specific tungsten product categories and whether certain declarations can be submitted in advance of arrival. “We are asking customers to confirm the exact classification and end-use details earlier than usual to avoid holds,” the provider said, speaking on condition of anonymity because it was not authorized to discuss operational matters.

Customs brokers in China said the procedures are designed to tighten traceability and ensure that shipments are consistent with declared specifications. They also said the rules may affect freight scheduling, especially when documentation is incomplete or inconsistent across a shipment’s paperwork. Traders typically rely on standardized product descriptions and bill-of-lading information, and the new requirements have prompted renewed checks by compliance teams, brokers said.

The change comes as tungsten markets and supply chains remain sensitive to regulatory and trade frictions. China is a major supplier of tungsten materials and concentrates, and changes to administrative processes can ripple through contracts and inventory planning abroad, particularly where buyers are operating on just-in-time procurement.

Industry participants said they expect the impact to be most pronounced in the next few weeks while companies adjust systems and templates used for declarations. Several said they are working with customs brokers and forwarders to streamline submissions, but acknowledged that cargo delays could persist until documentation practices stabilize across ports.

headline: Ghana suspends cobalt exports after cyclone damages access roads
body: Ghana has suspended cobalt exports after Cyclone Idai-Two struck in late March and damaged access roads to mining areas, disrupting shipments and storage at loading points, according to trade and logistics officials. The suspension, effective 26 March 2026, covers all outbound cobalt concentrate and intermediate products leaving via the country’s southern corridors, where multiple bridges and feeder roads were washed out or left impassable. In the Western and Central regions, authorities reported repairs needed to reopen at least 14 key road sections used for hauling ore from processing sites to ports. Port operators said last-week trucking volumes fell to less than 30% of normal levels, with congestion extending three to five days due to rerouting and safety inspections. Customs and the mining commission issued temporary instructions to pause new export declarations while companies conduct roadworthiness checks and restock weighbridge equipment at remaining routes. The suspension period will be reviewed after road access is restored and test shipments confirm product integrity and moisture controls.

headline: Botswana declares state of emergency for copper tailings dam repairs
body:
Botswana has declared a state of emergency to support urgent repairs to a copper tailings dam after authorities identified risks related to its structural safety, the government said.

The emergency status, announced late on Friday, will allow relevant ministries and agencies to move more quickly on engineering works, procurement and emergency response planning at the dam site. Officials said the declaration is aimed at protecting surrounding communities and maintaining continuity of operations while remediation activities are underway.

A statement from the President’s office said the decision followed assessments by technical teams that indicated the need for immediate corrective measures. It said the measures include reinforcement works, monitoring upgrades and the implementation of additional safety controls to reduce the likelihood of dam instability and potential environmental impacts.

Under the emergency framework, the government said it will coordinate with mining operators and regulators to fast-track repair timelines and ensure compliance with safety requirements. The declaration also authorises temporary measures to manage access to the area, strengthen surveillance, and allocate resources for contingency planning, including readiness for further escalation if conditions change.

Local authorities in the affected region were instructed to review evacuation and communications protocols, the statement added. It said residents near the facility should follow guidance issued by emergency services and district officials, without specifying the scope of any relocation or restrictions on movement.

Botswana’s Ministry of Minerals and Energy said in a separate statement that tailings facilities require constant monitoring and that remediation plans are being executed in line with international standards. “The state of emergency will ensure that the necessary technical and logistical support is mobilised without delay,” the ministry said, adding that inspections and reporting will continue during the repair period.

The government did not disclose the name of the dam or the operator in its initial remarks. It said details on the repair schedule, costs and interim safety measures would be provided as work progresses, and that oversight arrangements have been put in place to track milestones and verify compliance.

The declaration comes as regulators across the region have intensified scrutiny of tailings storage safety following incidents elsewhere involving dam failures and releases of mining waste.

headline: China closes major rare-earth-like “magnet” route to graphite suppliers
body: China has tightened access to a key supply route for graphite used in battery and magnet manufacturing, according to industry tracking cited by traders. The closures, announced around 2026-03-24 and taking effect through late March, affect shipments from several graphite processing and logistics hubs that also handle rare-earth magnet inputs. Market participants said the restriction targets “magnet” material routing rather than total graphite output, but it disrupts conversion schedules and raises lead times by an estimated 2 to 6 weeks. Prices for spot coarse spherical graphite and related anode feedstocks were reported volatile after the news, with some deals rerouted to alternative provinces or to inventories held by importers. The move arrives as China consolidates control over downstream steps that include high-purity carbon products and magnet-grade powders, areas where foreign manufacturers have been seeking more secure sourcing. For 2026, traders forecast the route tightening could cut available spot volumes by low double digits compared with early-quarter levels, while supporting producer margins in the near term.

headline: Chile sets new nickel concentrate quotas after review of export data
body:
Chile has set new quota levels for exports of nickel concentrate following a review of recent shipment data, the government said, adjusting the framework used to measure trade and compliance for the country’s copper-adjacent refining sector.

The updated quotas, published by Chile’s relevant mining and trade authorities, follow an internal review of export records covering recent quarters. Officials said the reassessment used declared volumes, port shipping confirmations and customs filings to refine the figures that determine how much concentrate can be exported within the quota system.

Chile is one of the world’s leading producers of copper and a major exporter of industrial metals in the region, with nickel concentrate exports tied to operations that produce and ship material for further processing abroad. The quota mechanism is designed to align reported exports with contracted supply, monitoring requirements and the timing of shipments, according to government documentation accompanying the decision.

Under the revised scheme, quota allocations were recalculated for reporting periods that begin after the publication date of the measure. Companies subject to the rules are required to apply within specified deadlines, using shipment-level documentation to demonstrate expected exports and to ensure quantities remain within the updated ceilings.

The authorities did not immediately provide a breakdown of the total national quota or the specific changes by company, but said the adjustment reflects “updated export data and verification results” from the review. The review also considered cases where discrepancies between declared product descriptions and customs classifications were later clarified, officials said.

In a statement, a spokesperson for the government agency said the revision aims to “increase the accuracy of the quota allocations and ensure consistency between customs records and export reporting.” The spokesperson added that the measure supports continuity for exporters while the monitoring process is periodically refined.

The decision comes as Chile’s mining sector continues to operate amid shifts in global demand and supply chains for refined and intermediate metal products. Export quotas for concentrates can influence logistics planning and sales schedules, particularly when shipments are bundled across multiple ports and processing destinations.

headline: Indonesia extends nickel export ban beyond 30 days due to policy review
body: Indonesia will extend a ban on nickel exports beyond the initial 30-day period, officials said on 27 March 2026, citing an ongoing policy review tied to downstream investment requirements. The original restriction, announced earlier in March and set to expire after 30 days, is expected to remain in force as the government evaluates export permits and compliance timelines for smelters across Sulawesi and Maluku provinces. The review follows revisions to domestic processing rules that require higher utilization of nickel ore and refined products before any outbound shipments. Industry participants said the extension could affect both direct ore exports and intermediates, with shipment schedules disrupted through at least late April 2026 while authorities finalize guidance for license issuance. The government has also been assessing whether producers meet updated benchmarks for refining capacity expansion, including staged commissioning targets. Market watchers noted that the extended pause is likely to tighten physical availability of Indonesian nickel products in the near term and increase contract renegotiations among trading houses ahead of the ban’s next review window.

headline: South Africa arrests illegal platinum ore shipments, disrupting spot flows
body:
South African authorities have arrested people linked to illegal shipments of platinum-group metals, disrupting physical spot flows and tightening availability for buyers, according to officials and market participants.

The arrests followed inspections at logistics hubs and along routes used to move ore and concentrate to ports, where investigators found shipments that did not match customs declarations and lacked required export permits. Several individuals were detained and a number of containers and documents were seized as part of a criminal probe into alleged misreporting and smuggling, the authorities said.

Police and other agencies involved in the operation said the targeted material was described as platinum ore but was not accompanied by verifiable chain-of-custody records. Investigators also seized cash, communications devices and commercial invoices used to facilitate the movement of the cargo, according to a statement from the lead agency. Further arrests were possible as checks continued, officials added.

The disruption was felt quickly in the spot market, where buyers rely on short-notice supplies to cover refinery and catalyst demand. Traders said the detained cargo represented planned near-term deliveries and that some counterparties have paused spot transactions while compliance teams verify whether replacement volumes are available. One dealer described the situation as a “temporary tightening” of spot supply, saying that pricing and delivery schedules were likely to adjust until shipments could be cleared or replaced.

Market participants said the crackdown could affect flows of concentrates and ore that feed domestic processing and export-oriented trading desks. While South Africa is a major source of platinum and related metals globally, the quality and documentation of feedstock can materially influence pricing, scheduling and blending decisions at processing sites. Several buyers indicated they were seeking reassurances that any substituted cargo would meet regulatory requirements and laboratory specifications.

The government has faced repeated concerns over the diversion of platinum-group metals into illicit channels, including through under-invoicing, false labeling and theft. Authorities have said enforcement efforts are intended to protect legal producers and to ensure taxes and royalties are collected. The case is expected to move through the courts, with officials indicating they will pursue charges relating to fraud, smuggling and contravention of mineral export rules.

headline: Russia suspends nickel deliveries under contract after sanctions compliance delay
body: Russia has suspended nickel deliveries under existing supply contracts after a delay in compliance steps related to Western sanctions, according to trade sources cited by market participants. The interruption affects shipments scheduled to start in late March and run through April for buyers in Europe and Asia, the sources said, though quantities and contract volumes were not immediately confirmed. The stoppage follows internal checks by Russian exporters on documentation tied to end-use, routing and financial settlement controls, with some consignments flagged for additional verification. Several counterparties were informed over the past week that loading windows would be paused until sanctions screening is completed, with revised delivery dates expected within days rather than weeks. Nickel prices on the LME have shown sensitivity to supply expectations, and traders said the suspension could tighten nearby physical availability if it persists beyond early April. The measures come amid heightened scrutiny of metals flows and payment channels since new enforcement actions in late 2025.

headline: China declares force majeure for copper wire rod shipments during smelter outage
body:
China’s industrial ministry has declared “force majeure” covering copper wire rod shipments tied to smelter outages, according to a notice circulated among traders, as supply disruptions ripple through the domestic refining and semi-fabricated copper markets.

The declaration applies to shipments originating from smelters affected by operational disruptions and is intended to exempt exporters from default clauses in contracts during the outage period, traders said. Affected companies typically have to document downtime and provide supporting statements to counterparties to invoke the clause.

The notice comes after multiple smelters reduced output or suspended processing due to equipment and maintenance issues, leading to tighter availability of wire rod, a key intermediate used for copper wire and cable production. Wire rod is produced by casting and rolling refined copper into standardized product formats, meaning outages at refining and casting facilities can quickly constrain downstream deliveries.

Market participants said the force majeure declaration is expected to shift delivery schedules and renegotiate terms for some cargoes, rather than immediately expanding supply. While some buyers may seek alternative sources, the ability to switch suppliers can be limited by product specifications, logistics and lead times for casting batches, traders added.

One trade participant familiar with the documentation process said the notice would likely be applied “case by case,” depending on proof of disruption and the specific shipment timeline. “Contracts will have to be supported with outage records and product identifiers, otherwise force majeure may not be accepted,” the participant said.

China is the world’s largest copper consumer and also a major hub for refining and semi-manufacturing. Any sustained interruption to smelting and wire rod output can affect industrial procurement, particularly for cablemakers and electrical component producers that rely on consistent intermediate supply.

Traders said they are monitoring whether the outage is confined to specific plants or whether broader production curtailments will follow. They also indicated that compliance processes could take time, potentially prolonging uncertainty in near-term pricing and settlement as contracts are reviewed and shipments are rescheduled.

headline: Mexico announces steel-buying limits as iron ore supply tightens
body: Mexico has announced limits on steel purchases as iron ore supply tightens amid higher freight costs and slower shipments, according to officials in the Ministry of Economy. The new measures, effective 1 April 2026, cap the volume of imported steel by category for domestic buyers and require additional licensing for contracts above established thresholds. The caps are tied to monthly procurement quotas that start at 90% of each firm’s 2025 average intake and step down to as low as 75% through September. Authorities said the policy is intended to preserve furnace feedstock availability for domestic mills, which have faced sporadic ore shortages since February. Global iron ore seaborne prices have been supported by constrained supply from major producers, while buyers in North America report lead times extending from 15-20 days to 30-45 days. The government also set a compliance deadline of 15 May for existing import orders, with penalties including delayed customs clearance for noncompliant shipments.

headline: India delays gallium deliveries after furnace breakdown at primary producer
body:
India has delayed some gallium deliveries after a furnace breakdown at the country’s primary producer, according to industry sources, as buyers sought alternative supply to avoid shipment gaps.

The disruption, which occurred at a furnace used to process gallium feedstock, has temporarily constrained output, leading at least one Indian supplier to adjust delivery schedules for customers, sources said. No change in demand was immediately reported, but buyers were asked to accept revised shipment dates as the producer worked to restore operations.

Gallium is used mainly in semiconductors, LED lighting and power electronics, and supply is concentrated among a small number of producers globally. Within India, gallium output is closely watched because domestic material can partially offset import dependence for some downstream users.

One trader said the delays were limited to certain contract lots rather than a complete production halt. “The furnace incident affected throughput, so deliveries were pushed back while they repair and stabilize the process,” the trader said, adding that alternative supply was being considered for urgent orders.

Sources said the producer was sourcing critical inputs needed for restarting the furnace and conducting checks before resuming full processing. They did not provide a timeline for full recovery, but indicated that shipments were being managed through partial dispatches where possible.

For international markets, the event is expected to have a near-term impact on availability and pricing sentiment, though Reuters reported earlier that gallium prices have been sensitive to changes in contracted flows. In recent months, buyers have increasingly relied on spot purchases and inventory buffers amid tight supply conditions in key refining stages.

Industry participants said the delay could extend into the next few weeks depending on how quickly the furnace returns to stable operating parameters and whether any additional defects are discovered during troubleshooting. If output remains constrained, customers may further shift to inventories or diversify sourcing across other producers.

The producer declined to comment when contacted. Gallium market participants will likely monitor follow-up updates on restart progress and whether the delayed deliveries are fully rescheduled or re-negotiated under existing contracts.

headline: Kazakhstan halts molybdenum shipping after rail derailment near Aktobe
body: Kazakhstan has halted exports of molybdenum concentrate after a rail derailment near Aktobe on March 26, disrupting shipments to processing and port routes, according to industry and rail sources. The accident derailed a freight consist carrying ore and concentrate toward the national network, leaving locomotives and covered wagons off track while investigators assessed track damage and possible spillage. Authorities closed the affected section temporarily and rerouted traffic, but the backlog extended into the March 27–28 window when several scheduled loads were due to move. Affected volumes were linked to typical spring export flows that run in multi-rail batch consignments of 20–30 wagons per train, with shipments commonly moving within 24–48 hours after dispatch. Molybdenum producers said the stoppage may delay deliveries by several days depending on repairs, inspections, and the ability to restore normal train speeds. The halt follows heightened rail scrutiny after prior derailments in 2025, when regulators tightened wheel and track monitoring standards.

headline: Chile imposes force majeure at Escondida copper mine after flooding
body:
Chile’s mining regulator has declared force majeure for the Escondida copper operation after heavy flooding disrupted work at the country’s largest copper mine, according to company and government sources.

Escondida, jointly owned by BHP Group and Rio Tinto, was affected by flooding in the region late last week, leading to interruptions in key processes and challenges for logistics and power supply at the site. The declaration allows the parties involved in scheduled deliveries and contractual obligations tied to the mine’s operations to invoke the legal provision for events beyond their control.

The force majeure notice was issued following an assessment by Chile’s authorities and reflects the extent of damage and operational impact from the flooding, which affected parts of the mine’s infrastructure and surrounding areas. The measure is intended to provide regulatory and contractual clarity during the recovery period, while repairs and safety inspections are carried out.

In a statement, BHP said the company is prioritising the safety of workers and restoring normal operations, adding that teams are working to repair affected areas and re-establish stable production conditions. The company said it is evaluating the duration of the disruption and will provide updates to customers and stakeholders as more information becomes available.

Rio Tinto separately said it expects production to be affected until water control measures and rehabilitation work are completed, while maintenance and recovery activities continue on-site. Both companies indicated that power restoration and transport routes are being monitored closely, with contingency plans activated to reduce further interruptions.

Escondida is typically a bellwether for Chile’s output and one of the world’s most important copper producers. Any prolonged disruption could tighten near-term supply expectations for physical copper, particularly given the mine’s large share of national production.

Chile has used force majeure declarations in previous incidents when extreme weather or infrastructure failures prevent operations from meeting contractual or regulatory requirements. The current notice is based on the flooding impact and will remain in force for as long as authorities determine that conditions prevent normal operations.
