headline: Indonesia halts nickel ore exports for 30 days amid new shipping rules
body: Indonesia has halted exports of nickel ore for 30 days starting on 28 March 2026, citing revised shipping requirements that are set to take effect for exporters, according to trade and government officials involved in the process. The move suspends shipments while port and vessel compliance checks are conducted under the new rules, which include updated documentation standards, tighter routing and scheduling controls, and verification procedures for cargo weight and origin. Exporters are expected to resume loading only after approval from designated authorities, with the first window for restart set for mid-April 2026, subject to compliance. The government said the suspension is intended to prevent irregular shipments and to align outbound logistics with domestic industrial policies. Market participants estimated the 30-day stoppage could affect near-term supply into China and other Asian buyers, where monthly nickel ore demand is typically concentrated in the first half of each quarter. The policy follows earlier Indonesia export curbs and comes as global nickel prices have remained volatile amid weak stainless demand and shifting battery supply expectations.

headline: Canada miner cuts cobalt production guidance after processing plant outage
body:
A Canadian miner has cut its cobalt production guidance after a processing plant outage disrupted ore treatment, the company said on Friday.

The Toronto-listed firm said the outage affected throughput at its primary processing facilities, limiting the amount of cobalt-bearing material that could be processed during the period. As a result, it revised its expected cobalt output for the year downward from its prior forecast, while maintaining its overall production targets for other major product streams subject to continued operational recovery.

In a statement, the company said maintenance teams worked to restore operations and stabilise the plant’s processing circuit. It did not provide a precise duration for the downtime, but indicated that ramp-up activity was underway and that operational performance would be monitored closely over coming weeks. The company said the revision reflects the direct impact of lower volumes treated during the outage rather than a change in mine plans or ore grade.

The miner added that it expects the plant to return to a more normal operating rate as corrective work is completed. It also said it would continue to prioritise the restart of relevant equipment and quality controls to ensure concentrate and intermediate product specifications are met. The company’s guidance cut is linked to the timing of when material can be processed, with some inventory flows affected by the disruption, management said.

Cobalt prices have remained sensitive to supply disruptions and refining constraints as demand is tied to batteries and electrification. Companies producing the metal have faced operational challenges in recent years, including equipment failures and maintenance outages that can reduce quarterly output and alter production schedules.

Market participants will be watching for additional updates on recovery progress and any knock-on effects to downstream shipments. The company said it will publish further details about production results when it reports its quarterly figures and will update guidance again if further events affect plant performance.

The revision marks another near-term adjustment to supply expectations from a major producer as the sector navigates operational downtime and efforts to maintain consistent throughput.

headline: DR Congo suspends cobalt shipments as security forces close two loading sites
body: The Democratic Republic of the Congo suspended cobalt shipments after security forces moved to close two loading sites in the southeastern copper belt, according to industry participants and local officials. The suspension, effective this week and expected to last at least several days, halts exports from facilities used to route cobalt-bearing ore and concentrate to smelters and traders. Security personnel closed one site in the Haut-Katanga corridor and another near Lubumbashi logistics areas after reports of armed groups operating around perimeter roads and warehouses. Several trucking queues were stopped on March 27 and March 28, leaving containers staged for loading without clearance documents. The disruption comes as DRC remains the world’s largest source of cobalt, with exports typically running in year-round cycles tied to refinery and contract schedules. Traders said the stoppage could delay consignments of refined cobalt material and raise demurrage risk for cargo already in transit, while miners reported slower cashflow from withheld payments. The government did not immediately specify whether shipments will resume after inspections, renewed site security measures, or additional administrative approvals.

headline: Zambia declares emergency at copper smelter after power supply disruptions
body:
Zambia has declared an emergency at a copper smelter after power supply disruptions, the government said, as operators worked to stabilize production amid intermittent electricity outages.

The measure, announced by the Ministry of Energy, applies to operations at the smelting facility and authorizes emergency procedures to manage demand, protect equipment and ensure continued safe handling of concentrates. The government said the disruption to the national power grid had affected load levels required for continuous smelting processes.

Power cuts and voltage fluctuations have become a recurring challenge for Zambia’s mining sector, which relies heavily on electricity for smelting, refining and auxiliary systems. When power supply is interrupted, smelters can face slower throughput, equipment shutdowns and increased restart costs, companies have previously said.

In a statement, the ministry said it had instructed the smelter operator to implement contingency measures, including prioritizing critical control systems and coordinating with the national utility on load-shedding schedules. It also said inspectors would monitor compliance with safety protocols during the emergency period.

A spokesperson for the ministry said the emergency declaration was intended to minimize risks to workers and the environment while efforts to restore stable power were under way. “The priority is safe operations and maintaining control of the smelting process while the electricity supply is being stabilized,” the spokesperson said.

The government did not provide the duration of the emergency or specific figures on production impacts. However, it indicated that communication between the smelter and electricity providers had been stepped up, and that alternative operational planning would be used where feasible.

Zambia is the world’s top copper producer after the Democratic Republic of Congo and depends on copper exports for a large share of public revenue. The mining industry has frequently cited electricity reliability and costs as constraints on output and expansion, particularly as domestic generation and transmission infrastructure face growing demand.

Emergency declarations in key industrial facilities have historically been used to streamline approvals for temporary measures and to reinforce safety compliance during disruptions, officials said.

headline: Philippines lifts suspension on nickel shipments after port strike ends
body: The Philippines has lifted a suspension on nickel ore shipments after a port strike ended, restoring schedules at key loading facilities, according to market participants on March 28. The halt, imposed earlier in March following disruptions at major ports, had affected exports of nickel laterite and related products used for stainless steel feedstocks and intermediate processing. Under the suspension, outbound bookings were paused from March 12 and gradually extended through the strike period, leaving buyers to cover shortages via alternative routes from Indonesia and the broader ASEAN region. Shipping agents said vessels are resuming loading and that customs clearance processes are being fast-tracked for consignments ready for departure. Traders estimated that the rebound could recover part of the previously deferred tonnage within 2–4 weeks, depending on berth availability and vessel turnaround times. The change comes as nickel market pricing remained sensitive to logistics constraints, with premiums and spot differentials fluctuating week to week during the shutdown.

headline: Australia graphite producer pauses expansion as permitting delayed by regulators
body:
An Australian graphite producer has paused an expansion project after regulators delayed key environmental and mining permits, the company said, extending timelines for a planned increase in output used in batteries.

The company, which operates a graphite resource in Australia, said it has halted work related to the expansion while awaiting approvals for permits required for construction and related activities. It did not provide a full list of the affected permits, but said the delays were linked to the regulatory review process, including additional information requests.

A number of graphite projects globally have faced permitting and environmental scrutiny as governments tighten assessments of land use, water impacts and emissions. In Australia, regulators typically require detailed studies and public consultation before granting approvals for mine and processing developments.

The producer said it will continue to advance “non-permitting” work where possible, including planning and procurement activities that do not depend on final approvals. It also said it is in discussions with regulators to resolve outstanding items and expects the project schedule to update once the permits are granted.

“We remain committed to the expansion, but we cannot progress construction activities until the relevant permits are secured,” the company said in a statement. It added that the pause is intended to avoid costs associated with building ahead of regulatory clearance.

The delay comes as battery supply chains face tightness in some parts of the value chain. Graphite is used as an anode material in lithium-ion batteries and demand growth has prompted investment in processing capacity and new mining operations. Companies have also worked to expand throughput and reduce reliance on imports, particularly for high-purity graphite required for battery-grade supply.

Industry analysts have said permitting risk can materially affect capital expenditure plans and the timing of new supply. Even projects with approved initial stages may encounter delays when regulators require amendments to environmental management plans or additional mitigation measures.

The company did not disclose the total cost impact from the pause or provide a revised timetable for the expansion. It said further details will be shared after regulators complete their review and final approvals are issued.

headline: South Africa platinum output hits schedule after rail logistics strike escalates
body: South African platinum-group-metal output returned broadly to planned levels after rail logistics disruptions intensified into a strike escalation, according to mine and logistics data cited by traders on March 28, 2026. Producers had flagged earlier that constrained rail capacity from the Rustenburg corridor to processing hubs would delay concentrate and refined shipments, affecting deliveries planned for late March and early April. By the end of the week, shipment tracking showed pickups resuming on main lines, with volumes stabilizing near schedule for refined platinum and palladium and reducing expected shortfalls. Weekly deliveries of concentrates were assessed at roughly 90%–100% of the prior baseline, compared with 60%–75% during the first days of the labor action. Still, additional work stoppages were reported at rail yards and loading terminals, extending dwell times by 12–18 hours for some trains. Traders said price support for spot platinum and palladium was tempered by persistent uncertainty over restart timing and the risk of renewed rail bottlenecks into mid-April.

headline: Russia refinery outage triggers palladium supply squeeze, spreads to dealers
body:
A Russia refinery outage has tightened global palladium supply and pushed shortages into the retail dealer market, industry sources said, as buyers sought alternative deliveries for a metal widely used in catalytic converters and industrial catalysts.

The disruption began after an operational failure at a Russian processing site, according to traders tracking spot flows and refinery run rates. With the outage reducing near-term output, producers and traders began prioritizing contract allocations, leaving fewer volumes for spot purchases and secondary market distribution.

Dealers in Europe and Asia reported that offers for near-dated palladium became harder to obtain and premiums widened versus other precious metals, reflecting constraints on physical availability. One factor was the way palladium is typically distributed: refinery output is often pre-positioned through intermediaries, and a sudden reduction can take time to unwind across trading desks, storage locations and delivery schedules.

Several market participants said the shortage was most visible for small and mid-sized deal sizes, where inventory levels can turn over quickly when producers redirect material to larger contracts or to longer-dated tenders. “We saw spreads widen first for smaller lots, then for standard dealer inventory,” one metals dealer said. “Customers are asking for earlier deliveries and we are having to source more selectively.”

The disturbance also affected pricing mechanics tied to physical availability. In markets where liquidity depends on dealer stock, tighter supplies can lead to wider bid-ask ranges and delays in confirming transactions. Traders said some participants shifted to secondary supplies, including stock held by distributors, but those holdings were not enough to fully offset the refinery shortfall.

Analysts said palladium demand remains sensitive to automotive production and industrial usage, and that the metal’s supply structure—heavily concentrated in a limited number of regions—can amplify outages. While some market participants expected production to resume after repairs, the timing was uncertain and dependended on the duration of the shutdown and the pace of restart operations.

Industry sources said discussions with counterparties in Russia and alternative suppliers were underway to restore flows. However, until restart schedules become clearer, dealers said they were likely to continue requesting firm lead times and charging higher premiums for immediate delivery.

headline: Peru announces customs ban on tin concentrates following document discrepancies
body: Peru’s customs authority has announced a ban on the import and clearance of tin concentrates effective immediately, citing discrepancies in shipment documents, according to an official notice dated 29 March 2026. The measure targets consignments presented with inconsistencies between commercial invoices, certificates of origin, and packing lists, customs said, without specifying which filings were most common. Shipments arriving at ports after the notice time will be held pending “corrected documentation,” while goods already in transit may face inspection delays extending several days. The restriction applies to tin concentrate grades used for smelting and refining and covers both direct imports and transshipped loads through Peruvian free-trade zones. Customs did not provide a duration for the ban, stating that reinstatement will depend on verification of the affected documentation sets. The move comes amid heightened scrutiny of mineral imports in recent months, as authorities in Peru and partner jurisdictions have increased checks for fraud, misdeclared origin, and volume deviations exceeding standard tolerance ranges.

headline: Bolivia declares temporary export quota for antimony as stocks tighten
body:
Bolivia has declared a temporary export quota for antimony, aiming to ease tightening supplies of the strategic metal, according to a government resolution issued this week.

The measure sets limits on outbound shipments while domestic stocks are reviewed, the Ministry of Development Planning said in the resolution. Antimony is used in flame retardants, batteries, ammunition primers and other industrial applications, and Bolivia is among the world’s notable producers of the ore-based metal.

Under the quota, exporters will be required to request authorization for shipments within specified volumes, with approvals issued through the relevant licensing process. The resolution does not set a permanent cap, instead describing the export limits as a transitional step while inventory levels and market conditions are assessed.

The government said the tightening of stocks has raised concerns about uninterrupted supply to downstream operations and the country’s broader industrial needs. It added that the temporary restriction is intended to manage export flows rather than halt them, and to provide clearer visibility on available material.

Bolivia’s state-linked mining sector and private operators have been competing for feedstock as global demand has remained resilient, with prices supported by supply constraints and geopolitical risk in other producing regions. Antimony production is sensitive to mine operations and smelter throughput, and trade flows can shift quickly when inventories decline.

One provision of the resolution calls for compliance checks at the point of shipment, including verification of origin and quantities covered by each authorization. Exporters that exceed permitted volumes may face administrative penalties, according to the text.

The quota is expected to affect commercial contracts that were priced or scheduled before the restriction was published. Market participants typically rely on predictable export schedules for smelting and refining planning, especially for antimony used in specialty industrial products.

Government officials did not provide detailed figures in the public summary of the resolution, and an exact timeline for the duration of the quota was not immediately specified in the document shared by regional authorities. The Ministry said it would issue further guidance if adjustments become necessary based on stock monitoring.

Bolivia’s antimony exports are closely watched by manufacturers and traders as the metal’s supply remains uneven across the globe. The temporary quota is likely to influence near-term trading activity and bargaining on spot and term volumes, traders said, while the broader market continues to track developments in other major producing countries.

headline: Vietnam blocks new tungsten licenses pending environmental review, traders react
body: Vietnam has halted new licensing for tungsten projects while awaiting an environmental review, according to market participants, a move traders said is likely to tighten supplies in the second half of 2026. The block applies to fresh permits for exploration and processing capacity, with authorities requiring additional site assessments and updated impact documentation before approvals resume. Several firms had been preparing submissions tied to 2026 production plans, but applications are now held pending compliance checks that may extend beyond the current quarter. Spot and term trading desks in Hanoi and Ho Chi Minh City reported slower deal flow after the decision, with buyers seeking alternative sources to cover delivery schedules originally set for June and September. While existing operations can continue under prior authorizations, suppliers flagged potential delays in sales contracts that reference new offtake volumes. Tungsten prices were broadly steady on the day the decision circulated, but desks said the uncertainty could translate into higher premiums if licensing does not clear by mid-2026.

headline: China raises scrutiny of vanadium imports after filings mismatch at ports
body:
China has stepped up scrutiny of vanadium imports after discrepancies were found between customs filings and shipments at multiple ports, according to industry sources and documents seen by Reuters.

The Ministry of Commerce and customs authorities have requested additional checks on vanadium-bearing materials and related products, including batches accompanied by permits and product specifications that did not match what arrived, the sources said. The reviews focus on whether imports are being declared correctly by grade, content and intended use, and whether paperwork is consistent with laboratory test reports carried out after discharge.

The trigger for the heightened attention was a mismatch discovered during routine post-clearance verification, when data from import declarations did not align with shipping manifests and, in some cases, supplier documentation, the sources said. In several instances, vanadium pentoxide or intermediate vanadium compounds were declared under categories that should correspond to specific chemical compositions, but subsequent inspections indicated different assay results.

Officials have asked importers to provide supplementary records, including original commercial invoices, bills of lading, loading port certificates and results of third-party testing, the sources said. Where gaps remain unresolved, shipments can face delays in unloading, additional sampling or reclassification for tariff and regulatory purposes. Some firms have been told to keep products in bonded areas pending results, one source said.

The tightening comes as China remains the world’s largest consumer of vanadium, a key input for steelmaking and for emerging energy storage applications. Demand is closely watched because vanadium is typically produced from titanium slag and other feedstocks, with supply reliant on a limited number of mining and refining routes.

An industry association representative said the port checks are intended to ensure compliance with import rules and to prevent mis-declaration. “We have been informed that authorities are verifying documentation integrity and the consistency between declared grades and testing outcomes,” the representative said.

China’s scrutiny could affect near-term import flows, particularly for consignments arriving without fully standardized testing documentation. Several companies have reportedly begun resubmitting filings and arranging fresh samples to avoid further interruptions at customs, according to the sources.

headline: Kazakhstan limits uranium deliveries to one buyer after regulator audit
body: Kazakhstan has restricted uranium deliveries to a single customer after a regulator audit found inconsistencies in prior contracting and shipment documentation, according to industry sources. The decision, effective immediately in March 2026, limits outgoing volumes to one approved buyer, down from multiple counterparties used during parts of 2024 and 2025. The national nuclear regulator concluded its review in early March following audits of production accounting, export licensing records, and inventory reconciliation at sites involved in enrichment feedstock supply. Operators said the restriction applies across all delivery schedules for the remainder of 2026 unless Kazakhstan’s compliance checklist is fully cleared in a follow-up verification. Trade participants indicated the move could tighten spot and term availability into the second quarter, even though Kazakhstan remains one of the world’s largest uranium suppliers. Several suppliers also said the audit has delayed some loading windows originally scheduled for April, with revised shipping manifests expected within 10 to 15 days.

headline: Namibia signs emergency procurement to secure zirconium feedstock for refiners
body:
Namibia has signed an emergency procurement arrangement aimed at securing zirconium feedstock for refiners as global supply chains tighten, according to officials and documents seen by Reuters.

The agreement, concluded this month with counterparties involved in the processing and trading of zirconium-bearing materials, allows Namibia to fast-track purchases outside standard procurement timelines. The government said the move is intended to prevent interruptions to downstream refining activities and to ensure that existing off-take arrangements can be honored.

Zirconium is used largely in nuclear fuel cycle applications and in industrial products such as ceramics, coatings and corrosion-resistant components. Feedstock for refiners typically relies on minerals such as zircon and related byproducts, which are concentrated in a limited number of producing regions and subject to seasonal shipping constraints.

Namibia is among the world’s notable suppliers of zirconium-bearing minerals through operations linked to the extraction of heavy mineral sands. The country’s export flows have been influenced in recent years by higher freight costs, port congestion and shifting demand from processors, prompting refiners to seek longer-term and more reliable inputs.

The government said the emergency procurement mechanism is temporary and designed to cover gaps that could emerge between planned production schedules and deliveries under existing commercial contracts. It also said the arrangement includes safeguards intended to maintain traceability and compliance with applicable environmental and import rules.

“Through this emergency procurement, Namibia will be able to secure additional feedstock volumes needed to keep refiners supplied while normal procurement processes are updated to reflect current market realities,” a senior official at the ministry overseeing the initiative said in a statement.

Industry players said the announcement is likely to be read by refiners as a signal that Namibia intends to reduce delivery risk amid volatile demand and competition for concentrates. They said refiners typically rely on multi-stage logistics, where delays in concentrate loading, customs clearance and shipping schedules can affect production timetables.

The details of the counterparties and volumes were not fully disclosed in the public release. However, the procurement is expected to support near-term deliveries and help stabilize feedstock availability for processors seeking to maintain output levels while they plan for longer-term contracts.

Namibia’s emergency procurement comes as the mineral trading market faces broader uncertainty linked to sanctions risk, inspection backlogs and fluctuating exchange rates. Officials said they will review the arrangement before extending it, if necessary.

headline: Poland forces restart at molybdenum unit after safety shutdown last week
body: Poland ordered a restart of a molybdenum production unit after a safety shutdown last week, according to plant officials and regulators. The restart follows an inspection triggered on March 21 after operators detected abnormal readings in a key process circuit, prompting an automatic stop to prevent equipment damage and environmental releases. The restart authorization, issued March 28, requires additional monitoring of pressure, coolant flow, and temperature at the unit’s roasting and leaching stages, with results reviewed continuously by on-site safety staff. The facility is expected to ramp up operations over several days, targeting partial output by April 1 and full throughput by April 4, depending on test outcomes. Local authorities said no injuries were reported during the March 21 shutdown and that emissions remained within permitted limits. The incident comes as the country seeks to stabilize supply of molybdenum used in steelmaking and industrial catalysts, after recent disruptions in European processing capacity.

headline: China orders bismuth inventory checks after suspected smuggling scheme uncovered
body:
China has ordered inventory checks for bismuth following the discovery of a suspected smuggling scheme involving the metal, according to a notice circulated to relevant authorities and companies.

The order, issued by regulators responsible for customs and commodity supervision, requires companies handling bismuth to conduct comprehensive stock reconciliations, document inbound and outbound movements, and submit reports within a set timeframe. Authorities said the checks are aimed at verifying whether shipments and transfers of bismuth and related products were properly declared and whether records align with physical inventory.

Bismuth is used in alloys, electronics, medical applications and as a substitute in some low-melting materials. While China produces and trades significant quantities globally, the metal is often subject to tighter scrutiny because small misclassifications or under-declarations can affect downstream supplies and pricing.

The suspected scheme was uncovered during an enforcement operation that flagged irregular patterns in documentation and logistics, the sources said. Investigators found discrepancies between reported quantities and warehouse records in some transactions, prompting a broader review of inventory held by entities along the supply chain, including refiners, traders and logistics firms.

Authorities also instructed local units to increase coordination with customs offices at ports and border crossings. The focus, officials said, is to track suspicious consignments and to verify whether bismuth is being rerouted through alternate routes or shipped under incorrect product descriptions.

In a statement, one regulator said the inventory checks are intended to “restore compliance across the bismuth trade and prevent illegal diversion,” adding that enforcement measures would follow the findings. The regulator did not provide further details on the number of companies involved, the scale of the suspected activity, or whether any arrests had been made.

Companies subject to the order are required to retain supporting records, including purchase and sales contracts, customs declarations, production logs and warehouse receipts. Regulators said they would use the submitted reports to determine whether any entities need to face further inspections, administrative penalties or referrals to law enforcement.

The move comes as China continues tightening oversight of critical and industrial materials used in manufacturing and export supply chains. Additional measures could be announced depending on the results of the inventory reconciliations.

headline: Japan halts germanium shipments for two weeks due to transport certification issues
body: Japan has halted shipments of germanium for two weeks starting 2026-03-30, citing transport certification problems, according to industry sources. The suspension affects flows of the semiconductor metal and related refined products moving through certified logistics channels, with importers instructed to pause scheduled deliveries until documentation is verified. Firms involved said the interruption is intended to prevent seizure or delays at points of entry after discrepancies were flagged in transport certificates issued for regulated cargo handling and packaging. The stop is set to remain in place until about 2026-04-13, when shipments are expected to resume subject to reissued or corrected certifications. One impact is tightening spot availability for grades used in fiber-optics and infrared components, as inventories typically roll over on 2–4 week cycles. Several buyers have shifted orders to alternative routes, but those reroutings may extend lead times by 1–3 weeks depending on carrier approvals.

headline: Germany extends force majeure for cadmium refinery after wastewater permit delay
body:
Germany has extended a force majeure declaration for a cadmium refinery after a delay in its wastewater permit, the company and regulators said, allowing continued operations while environmental paperwork is finalized.

The extension applies to the refinery’s discharge and treatment activities at the facility, where operators had previously been granted temporary relief from contractual and regulatory timelines. Under the declaration, the company can cite the permit process as the cause of delays to certain discharge-related obligations, helping to avoid penalties during the administrative review.

The wastewater permit has taken longer than expected, according to the parties involved in the process, delaying the start or resumption of specific wastewater-related measures required for full compliance. Officials have not indicated any change to the underlying environmental requirements, but have said the administrative steps required to issue the permit must be completed before the refinery can fully operate within the final conditions.

A spokesperson for the refinery operator said the company is working with the responsible authorities to close remaining gaps in the permit file and to align ongoing operations with interim guidance where available. “The extension reflects the fact that the permitting process is still ongoing, and it gives us time to complete the wastewater requirements without breaching obligations tied to discharge timelines,” the spokesperson said.

Force majeure declarations are typically used in Germany and across Europe when events outside a company’s control prevent it from meeting contractual duties. While the extension provides temporary legal cover, it does not eliminate environmental obligations, and the permit decision remains subject to the outcome of the review, including any technical assessments or public consultations required under German and European rules.

Regulators have continued oversight of the facility during the permit delay, including monitoring related to ongoing discharges and operational safeguards. The company said it has maintained measures to minimize emissions and ensure safe operation while the permit authority completes its assessment.

The cadmium refinery, which processes materials used in industrial applications, has faced scrutiny in past years over compliance with water and waste rules. Industry observers said delays in wastewater permitting have been an emerging issue for chemical and metals producers, particularly where permit requirements need additional data or engineering adjustments. The latest extension keeps the facility operating under constrained conditions until the wastewater permit is granted.

headline: Kazakhstan restricts gallium exports to maintain domestic supply for semiconductors
body: Kazakhstan has introduced new limits on gallium exports to preserve domestic supplies used in semiconductors, according to a government measure published on 2026-03-29. The rules tighten licensing requirements for shipments and cap monthly volumes for foreign buyers, with priority given to local metal processors serving electronics customers. The ministry said the policy is intended to reduce shortages for high-purity gallium and related compound inputs used in power devices and radio-frequency components. The export caps take effect immediately and will be reviewed after 90 days, with enforcement beginning at customs checkpoints across Kazakhstan’s main logistics routes. Industry participants estimate Kazakhstan supplies a significant share of global gallium output, making the measures likely to affect spot prices and lead times for refiners outside Central Asia. Traders said contracts signed for April shipments may need renegotiation as compliance documentation and origin certificates are updated to reflect the new quotas.

headline: India imposes anti-dumping probe on indium imports amid price volatility
body:
India has initiated an anti-dumping investigation into imports of indium, citing volatility in prices and potential injury to domestic industry, according to a government notification.

The Directorate General of Trade Remedies (DGTR) said it has launched the probe following a petition filed by the Indian domestic industry. The investigation covers indium imports, which are used in applications including semiconductors, flat-panel displays and solar energy components, and is aimed at determining whether imported product pricing has been dumped and whether that has caused material harm to local manufacturers.

DGTR said it will examine the difference between the export price from subject countries and the normal value in their domestic markets during the investigation period. It will also assess injury factors such as changes in import volumes, price undercutting versus domestic prices, and the impact on Indian producers’ profitability, output and market share.

The investigation will cover imports from specified origin countries named in the notification. DGTR said it will seek input from interested parties, including foreign exporters, importers, and domestic producers, and requested that they provide data supporting their submissions by deadlines set in the notice. The authority will also verify information it receives and may conduct further queries before forming preliminary and final determinations.

In its notice, DGTR said the investigation has been opened amid concerns that fluctuating global pricing could mask dumping practices and affect the competitive position of Indian producers. Under India’s trade remedy rules, preliminary findings are typically issued within months of initiation, with final determinations following after additional analysis and hearing processes.

If DGTR’s findings support the petition, the government may impose anti-dumping duties to offset the dumping margin, potentially on a prospective basis. Duties can be structured as fixed amounts or variable rates depending on the outcome and the data submitted by exporters and importers, officials said in earlier similar investigations.

The indium market has been prone to swings driven by demand cycles in electronics and solar supply chains, and by shifts in availability from primary and by-product production. The probe is expected to bring heightened scrutiny to import pricing and volumes as the DGTR collects evidence for its assessment.

headline: Turkey declares state of emergency at iron ore port after flooding disrupts haulage
body: Turkey has declared a state of emergency at its main iron ore port facility after flooding disrupted truck and rail haulage, the government said on 29 March 2026. The measure covers port operations and surrounding logistics corridors used to move concentrates from inland yards to export berths, where delays began late on 27 March following heavy rain and river overflow. Authorities said access to several loading and storage areas was restricted for safety, with temporary stoppages recorded for at least 36 hours and backlog building around vessel schedules for the following 10 days. Several haulage routes were closed intermittently, forcing rerouting that reduced average daily truck throughput by an estimated 20% to 30% while emergency repairs were carried out. Maritime handling also slowed, with berth turnaround times widening from a typical 8–10 hours to 14–18 hours during peak congestion. Officials said emergency powers would allow fast-track dredging, reinforcement of flood-prone sections, and additional labor and equipment to restore normal dispatch ahead of late-April export windows.

headline: Brazil suspends steel scrap deliveries after labor walkout at processing yards
body:
Brazil has suspended steel scrap deliveries after workers walked out at processing yards, disrupting supply operations for months ahead, industry sources and officials said on Monday.

The suspension covers scheduled shipments of ferrous scrap to yards in several producing areas, where workers stopped work following a dispute over pay and overtime, the sources said. Transit firms and scrap buyers were instructed to pause deliveries to affected facilities until a resumption plan is agreed, according to two logistics providers that handle inbound material for steelmakers.

Several yards in Brazil’s southeast and industrial belt near major consuming mills were operating with reduced staffing during the weekend, but the walkout broadened on Monday morning, limiting weighing, sorting and baling of material. Suppliers said the disruptions are likely to affect inventories at mills that rely on imported and domestic scrap feedstock to manage production costs and furnace schedules.

A representative for one of the larger scrap processors said the company had requested mediation and urged workers to return while negotiations continued. “We are working with labor representatives to restart operations as quickly as possible and ensure safe working conditions,” the representative said, without giving a timeline for when deliveries would resume.

Steelmakers, which use scrap in electric arc furnaces and as a supplementary input for blast furnaces, have limited flexibility because scrap grading and availability are typically tied to contracted delivery windows, sources said. Buyers may divert volumes to alternative yards if material grading and certifications remain within contract specifications, but those substitutions can take time because transport capacity and storage space are constrained.

Brazil’s trade and industrial channels are also sensitive to delays in material processing, industry participants said. Disruptions at yards can slow the movement of compacted scrap batches needed for near-term furnace runs, leading to higher spot procurement or increased reliance on other grades from different regions.

The labor walkout follows ongoing pressures in the scrap supply chain, including volatility in feedstock prices and transportation costs. Companies said they would assess whether any deliveries already on the way can be rerouted or stored at unaffected yards, while authorities said they were monitoring the situation and working toward a resolution through mediation channels.

headline: South Africa opens negotiations after platinum union strike idles key shafts
body: South Africa began formal negotiations on March 30, 2026, seeking to end a platinum-sector strike that has idled key shafts and disrupted shipments of the metal used in catalytic converters and fuel cells. The talks were opened by the government and representatives of major producers after workers from a platinum union suspended operations at several mines in the North West province, according to industry accounts. Output disruptions were concentrated in facilities responsible for a combined share of several million ounces of annual platinum capacity, with at least some concentrate deliveries delayed by more than 2 weeks. Companies reported ongoing losses from downtime that typically run into the millions of rand per day per operation, depending on grade and recovery rates. Negotiators said discussions would focus on wages, job security, and changes to shift rosters amid a separate inflation-driven round of pay demands filed earlier in the quarter. The work stoppage follows a pattern of sector-wide labor disputes in the first half of 2026, and the government set a near-term timetable for interim agreements through early April.

headline: China declares temporary suspension of graphite processing lines after inspections
body:
China has temporarily suspended graphite processing lines at some facilities following inspections, according to a government statement released late on Monday.

The move was announced after inspections found issues related to compliance with environmental and safety requirements at facilities involved in graphite processing, a key input for lithium-ion batteries and industrial uses. The statement said the affected lines will be halted while operators complete rectification measures and submit verification materials to regulators.

Regulators said the suspension is intended to prevent further violations and ensure production resumes only after checks are passed. The notice did not specify the number of companies or the locations of the facilities, but it indicated that the inspections covered both operational procedures and adherence to environmental controls, including emissions handling and waste management practices.

Local authorities instructed firms to stop relevant processing operations in an orderly manner, prevent stockpiling that could violate permit conditions, and maintain safe shutdown procedures. Operators were also told to cooperate with on-site teams during follow-up inspections and to report progress within set timelines.

Graphite processing is a sensitive part of China’s battery supply chain, where purification and other downstream steps can be linked to emissions. China has in recent years tightened enforcement in sectors seen as critical to industrial policy, including chemicals and materials used for clean energy manufacturing.

An industry source familiar with the inspections said operators were required to demonstrate that control equipment is functioning and that processes are consistent with permitted operating conditions. “The companies are being asked to address specific non-compliance points found during the checks and to provide documentation before restarting,” the source said.

Regulators did not mention any production targets or potential duration for the suspensions. A separate notice said that penalties could be imposed if companies restart operations without clearance or fail to implement corrective actions.

The temporary halts come as global demand for battery-grade graphite continues to rise, with buyers increasingly focused on supply reliability and environmental compliance. China is the world’s largest producer of natural graphite and a major processor of both natural and synthetic graphite, with output feeding domestic battery manufacturers and exports.

headline: DR Congo cobalt output threatened by cyclone that damages access roads
body: A cyclone that struck eastern Democratic Republic of Congo has disrupted cobalt production by damaging access roads used by mines and traders, according to industry sources and local logistics providers. The storm, which hit late last week and continued into 27 March, felled bridges and washed out sections of main haul routes linking cobalt-bearing sites in Haut-Katanga to aggregation yards and river loading points. Output at several small and mid-sized operations was cut as of 29 March due to fuel shortages and inability to move concentrate trucks on scheduled runs. Transport delays of 3 to 10 days are being reported, depending on whether roads can be cleared for heavy vehicles, with repair teams prioritizing sections closest to warehouses and weighbridges. Several buyers have shifted shipments to alternate routes, raising costs for road transport and increasing demurrage risk for containers staged at depots. Export timing for March and early April cargoes is now uncertain, with some shipments scheduled for April 1-10 potentially slipping into the following week.

headline: Indonesia extends nickel export ban for additional 15 days after refinery fire
body:
Indonesia has extended a ban on nickel ore exports for an additional 15 days after a refinery fire, the government said on Monday, keeping pressure on miners while production at the affected facilities is assessed.

The export restriction, first introduced earlier this month, was due to expire shortly, but the Ministry of Trade announced the extension following the disruption to refining operations linked to the blaze. Indonesia is the world’s top nickel producer and a key supplier of the metal used in batteries, with its export rules designed to push investment in domestic processing.

Under the latest measure, nickel ore shipments are prohibited until the extended period ends, while affected companies work to restore capacity. The government said it would continue monitoring refinery repairs and output, and that updates would be provided through official channels as the situation develops.

The fire, which damaged equipment at a processing site, disrupted refining schedules and reduced throughput at the time the government reviewed the export ban. Indonesia had previously tied the export policy to compliance with domestic downstream production targets, allowing ore to be exported only under specific conditions tied to processing and licensing.

A spokesperson for the Ministry of Trade said the decision was intended to ensure “stability in nickel supply and orderly industrial operations” while repairs were underway. The ministry did not immediately provide details on the number of facilities affected, the scale of the damage, or the expected timeline for full restoration.

Industry participants have said the ban can affect global supply chains, particularly for buyers seeking ore and intermediate nickel products for refining and battery materials. Indonesia’s policy shifts can also influence pricing for nickel and related contracts, as markets adjust to changes in availability.

Separately, Indonesia has been working to stabilize its downstream sector amid broader efforts to encourage investment in battery materials and high-grade nickel production. The country has relied on export restrictions to build refining capacity, while also attempting to manage energy and operational risks that can arise at industrial sites.

The 15-day extension starts from the date of the earlier expiration deadline, according to the ministry’s statement. Further changes would depend on the status of repairs and refinery operations during the extended period.

headline: Chile copper mine declares force majeure as tailings pond breach risks increase
body: A Chilean copper mine operator has declared force majeure after risks increased around a tailings pond that has been damaged and is under heightened monitoring, according to people familiar with the matter. The declaration, made late on March 29, cites elevated probabilities of further releases of stored material and potential impacts on processing and concentrate shipments. The affected facility, located in northern Chile, has faced intermittent seepage since a breach was first detected during routine inspections, with repairs and water diversions undertaken while engineers assess the stability of the impoundment. Operators said environmental authorities have requested expanded sampling and contingency plans, including limits on inflows and additional perimeter controls. Production interruptions are expected to intensify in the coming days as the mine pauses certain tailings-related operations, with logistics and smelter contracts subject to renegotiation. The force majeure is likely to affect deliveries scheduled for April, a period in which Chilean producers typically ramp output ahead of seasonal demand.

headline: Peru tin producer halts operations after smelter fuel supply contract is revoked
body:
A Peruvian tin producer has suspended operations after a fuel supply contract for its smelter was revoked, according to company statements and industry contacts.

The producer, which operates a processing facility in southern Peru, said it halted production as it could not secure an alternative fuel arrangement quickly enough to keep the smelter running. The decision follows the revocation of the contract that had provided the plant with the gasoil and related fuel needed for furnace operations and power generation on site.

Peru’s mining sector relies heavily on steady logistics for key inputs, including fuel, and disruptions can force temporary shutdowns while suppliers reprice contracts or while operators negotiate new delivery terms. In this case, the company said it was unable to bridge the gap between the contract’s cancellation and a replacement supply agreement that met its operational and safety requirements.

In a statement, the company said it would maintain the suspension until fuel supply is restored under a contract that ensures continuity of delivery and complies with environmental and operational standards. “We have temporarily stopped smelting activities because the fuel supply arrangement required for safe operations has been withdrawn,” the company said. It added that it is working with relevant parties to reestablish supply and resume output.

Local mining officials said the situation reflects broader challenges in Peru’s upstream fuel market, where contract terms can shift rapidly based on availability and pricing. They said smelter operators typically seek fuel arrangements well in advance because furnace schedules and maintenance plans depend on predictable delivery windows.

A shutdown also affects downstream concentrate handling and workforce planning. The company said it is managing stockpiles and maintenance schedules during the downtime to reduce the restart burden. It did not provide a timeline for when operations could resume, saying the restart depends on securing a reinstated or replacement fuel contract.

Tin production in Peru has been closely watched by traders because shipments are sensitive to operational continuity in smelting and refining. Market participants will likely look for updates on the company’s negotiations with fuel suppliers and any interim arrangements that could allow parts of the facility to operate while a longer-term contract is finalized.

headline: Spain restricts gallium contracts under government-backed allocation program
body: Spain has moved to restrict gallium supply contracts tied to a government-backed allocation program, according to an internal notice seen by market participants on March 29, 2026. The rules tighten eligibility for producers and traders receiving allocated material for 2026 deliveries, with new documentation requirements covering end-use statements and downstream customer tracing. Under the program, allocations will be capped by contract volume and by facility capacity for the period from April 1 through September 30, narrowing access for buyers that cannot demonstrate active consumption levels in the prior 12 months. Companies seeking allocations for new customer accounts after the cut-off date of May 15 must submit audited records by June 10, the notice said. The measure follows a broader European push to manage scarce critical metals while maintaining continuity for semiconductor and defense-related demand. Market sources said the change may shift some spot volumes to alternative procurement channels during the second quarter, as contracts tied to restricted allocations are renegotiated.

headline: United States expands sanctions compliance on zirconium shipments from flagged operators
body:
The United States has expanded its sanctions compliance requirements for zirconium shipments, tightening screening rules for shipments associated with operators previously flagged by U.S. regulators, according to government guidance issued this week.

The updated measures apply to entities involved in the export, re-export, transfer, and financing of zirconium-containing materials and certain related items, which U.S. authorities say can be used in defense and industrial applications. The changes require additional diligence by compliance officers and heightened documentation efforts to ensure shipments do not involve sanctioned parties or designated facilitators.

Under the guidance, companies are expected to adopt enhanced procedures to verify the identities of counterparties, vessel and shipping information, and end-use information. Firms are also directed to review historical business relationships for any links to operators that have been identified in U.S. enforcement actions or under intelligence-led monitoring programs.

The Department of the Treasury said the expansion targets cases where zirconium supply chains could be vulnerable to diversion or intermediate transfers through third parties. “We are strengthening compliance expectations to help prevent circumvention and ensure controlled materials do not reach prohibited end users,” a Treasury spokesperson said in a statement.

The measures build on existing sanctions regimes that cover a range of restrictions under U.S. export control and financial authorities. Industry participants typically must screen shipping documents against U.S. government lists, retain records for auditing, and report certain transactions if they involve sanctioned persons or otherwise raise compliance concerns.

Companies affected by the guidance are expected to ensure that contractual terms with suppliers, freight forwarders, and customs brokers include verification obligations. The rules also emphasize that firms should be able to demonstrate to regulators how they determined the permissibility of a shipment, including the basis for any determinations that a transaction does not involve listed parties.

U.S. regulators have warned that flagged operators may be used to obscure the true source or destination of controlled materials. The updated requirements are meant to standardize what Treasury and other agencies view as baseline steps for compliance, while allowing enforcement actions to be triggered where companies fail to meet those expectations.

The guidance does not automatically block all zirconium exports, but it increases the compliance burden for transactions that touch supply routes connected to operators previously singled out by U.S. authorities.

headline: Australia graphite offtake re-priced as China imposes new warehouse accreditation rules
body: Australia’s graphite offtake terms have been re-priced after China introduced new warehouse accreditation rules that change the logistics and eligibility for domestic delivery, according to market participants. The update, effective for shipments routed through accredited storage sites, raises clearance requirements and adds documentation steps that can delay loading and weigh on delivered volumes in the first half of 2026. Several Australian suppliers renegotiated contract pricing indexes and settlement schedules, shifting a portion of price exposure from spot benchmarks to delivery-based premiums tied to approved warehouse status. Industry sources said the repricing is most pronounced for higher-grade material that is routinely contracted for battery-anode demand, where buyers may require shorter holding times and faster chain-of-custody verification. One effect is that contracted quantities are being re-profiled around reassessment windows for warehouse approvals, with some contracts extending delivery dates by 30 to 60 days. Traders also reported tighter arbitration timelines for quality claims linked to storage conditions, with penalties adjusted to account for the accreditation transition.

headline: Rare-earth weekly wrap: praseodymium edges up as terbium holds steady
body:
Praseodymium prices in the rare-earth market rose slightly in the latest weekly assessment, while terbium was largely unchanged, according to traders and pricing indications tracked over the past week.

Across the complex, movement was modest as buyers continued to weigh demand signals against constrained supply for some separated materials. Praseodymium, which is used in permanent magnets for electric vehicles and wind turbines, firmed marginally as offers tightened in spot trading. Market participants attributed the uptick to short-covering ahead of near-term procurement schedules and to firmer sentiment in magnet-grade material.

Terbium, another key magnet component, held steady as steady availability offset any marginal shifts in demand. Traders said the absence of significant new orders kept buying interest steady, with sellers maintaining listed levels. In contrast to praseodymium, terbium pricing did not show the same pressure from order flow, leaving the market range-bound.

Other rare-earths in the same weekly wrap showed mixed performance. Some categories reflected the ongoing pattern of selective buying, where downstream buyers focus on specific grades tied to contract commitments. Several traders noted that inventories at intermediaries remain uneven by product and grade, contributing to patchy spot activity rather than broad-based moves.

“Praseodymium ticked up because liquidity was a little thinner at the offer end, but terbium didn’t follow as demand stayed steady,” one trader involved in spot negotiations said. The trader added that week-to-week changes are being driven more by immediate ordering than by major shifts in long-term pricing expectations.

In separated oxides and related product forms, assessed prices were broadly stable where supply was adequate. However, participants cautioned that the market can react quickly to procurement announcements, shipping delays, or disruptions in upstream refining. They said the coming sessions could see sharper changes if buyers accelerate restocking or if sellers adjust quotes in response to emerging contract tenders.

Overall, the week’s pricing signals pointed to a relatively cautious market tone, with praseodymium showing the only clear uptick among the headline-linked elements, while terbium remained steady as participants awaited clearer demand direction.

headline: Rare-earth price wrap: dysprosium and yttrium slip slightly in thin trading
body: Rare-earth prices dipped marginally in thin trading on 2026-03-01, with dysprosium and yttrium easing as liquidity thinned ahead of the next week’s physical demand indicators. Dysprosium fell slightly, slipping by a small single-digit percentage in late-session quotes, according to market participants tracking spot lots. Yttrium also edged lower, with thin turnover limiting the depth of available orders and leaving bids and offers more sensitive to small changes. Several other heavy rare-earths were quoted unchanged to lower by fractional amounts, while praseodymium-neodymium and cerium-related grades held near prior levels. Trading volumes were reported to be well below recent averages, and market depth remained limited across most contracts. Dealers cited a wait-and-see stance among processors and magnet producers, as buyers assessed inventory levels following the prior month’s volatility. The next set of settlement indications was expected to arrive later in the week, with participants watching China export activity and freight cues for direction.

headline: Cerium prices steady week-over-week; lanthanum remains rangebound amid demand
body:
Cerium prices in the rare earths market were largely steady on a week-over-week basis, while lanthanum continued to trade in a narrow band as buyers showed cautious demand, according to market assessments.

In the latest pricing updates, cerium products held their levels across most tracked grades, supported by stable off-take and steady pricing expectations from suppliers. Traders said there was no significant change in the availability of material and no broad adjustment in contract terms during the period, which helped limit volatility in spot negotiations.

Lanthanum, by contrast, remained rangebound. Several market participants attributed the lack of movement to uneven buying patterns, with demand concentrated in smaller volumes tied to downstream processing schedules rather than large speculative restocking. Some sellers maintained offers within established ranges, while buyers continued to seek confirmation on delivery timing and consistency of chemical specifications.

For cerium, the market balance appeared steadier as importers and intermediaries maintained routine replenishment rather than pausing purchases. One source with direct knowledge of the market said pricing was “held in place by consistent availability and incremental buying rather than any rush for inventory,” reflecting a market where volume and terms moved more slowly than usual.

In their ongoing monitoring, analysts said the rare earth complex continues to be influenced by currency movement, freight costs and end-user ordering cycles, factors that can affect near-term price signals even when supply remains stable. They added that any shifts in demand from ceramic, polishing and catalysts applications can quickly translate into short-term buying changes, though such effects were limited in the most recent week.

Overall, the assessments indicate that while cerium is maintaining steady pricing week-over-week, lanthanum is showing less price momentum. Market participants expect the next move to depend on clearer signals from industrial buyers and whether current procurement patterns expand beyond routine orders.

headline: Neodymium weekly wrap: buyers hold offers; praseodymium firm on spot inquiries
body: Rare-earth prices moved unevenly this week as neodymium buyers largely held existing offer levels while praseodymium saw firmer spot demand, market participants said on March 1–2. In neodymium-linked supply, sellers reported limited follow-through on new bids, with trade discussions concentrated around previously quoted ranges and deals paced by week-to-week order timing rather than fresh price resets. Several buyers indicated they were monitoring inventories and payment schedules before raising offers, keeping negotiated volumes at lower weekly run-rates. Praseodymium, by contrast, remained supported by spot inquiries, particularly for prompt material, sustaining tighter availability perceptions despite slower longer-dated call-offs. Dealers said spot buying interest was concentrated in small and mid-lot tenders, with follow-up activity extending across multiple sessions through March 1. Overall, participants characterized neodymium as range-bound on stable offer discipline, while praseodymium stayed resilient on the back of continuing spot coverage needs.

headline: Terbium price wrap: metal quotes steady while powder premiums narrow
body:
Terbium prices were little changed on Monday as spot trading for the metal held steady, while premiums for terbium powder narrowed, according to market participants.

Dealers said demand remained uneven across end-use sectors, with buyers showing particular caution about near-term inventories. Several traders reported that sellers were more flexible on powder pricing than on metal, reflecting tighter availability of certain powder grades and smoother supply terms for metal products.

In spot markets, terbium metal was quoted at levels close to those seen in the previous session, with most quotes clustering in a narrow band. One factor cited by traders was stable pricing for upstream rare-earth compounds, which helped limit further increases in conversion costs. Another was a steadying in shipping and packaging charges for metal forms, which had contributed to last week’s volatility.

For terbium powder, however, the premium over metal eased. Market participants attributed the narrowing spread to improving availability of pre-mixed powder lots and a slowdown in speculative buying. “Powder premiums have come down as sellers adjusted offers to clear more inventory,” one trader said, adding that buyers were placing smaller orders while monitoring prices for the next production run.

Industry activity also appeared restrained. Some participants noted that electronics and optics buyers were completing planned procurement rounds rather than accelerating orders, which kept volumes modest. Others pointed to ongoing contract negotiations in the supply chain, where payment terms and grade specifications were still being finalized.

Despite the steadier tone, traders said liquidity remained a key driver of short-term price moves. With many market participants waiting for more concrete guidance on downstream demand, any uptick in buying could quickly widen spreads again—particularly for higher-purity powder used in lighting, magnets and specialty alloys.

Overall, the session suggested a split pricing pattern: terbium metal remained firm, while terbium powder softened at the margin as sellers competed for near-term business. Dealers said they expect quotes to remain range-bound unless new indications of demand emerge from end markets.

headline: Rare-earth supply chain update: inventory improves at select Hunan processing sites
body: Rare-earth inventories edged higher at a handful of processing facilities in Hunan, according to industry data reviewed on 2026-03-02, easing pressure in a supply chain that has tightened since late 2024. Site-level checks showed stocks increased by low double-digit percentage points over the prior month at selected separation and refining lines, while total output remains constrained by maintenance schedules and permitting limits. The gains were concentrated in midstream oxides and metal intermediates, with inventory turns improving from roughly 60–75 days to about 45–60 days at the best-performing plants. Downstream demand trends were mixed: magnet-related orders held steady into January–February 2026, but sporadic restocking reduced spot volatility only in specific terbium and dysprosium streams. Traders said improvements may be temporary because feedstock sourcing from upstream concentrates is still the binding factor, particularly for late-quarter shipments. Several Hunan operators indicated tighter control of blending inventories through Q2 to manage cost swings from scandium and heavy rare-earth inputs, with normalization not expected before the second half of 2026.

headline: Rare-earth warehouse notes: NdPr stocks steady; Dy and Tb lots remain limited
body:
Supplies of neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr) at a key warehouse in the rare-earth market remained steady, while dysprosium (Dy) and terbium (Tb) lots continued to be scarce, according to fresh warehouse notes circulated to buyers.

The latest listings showed NdPr stocks unchanged from the previous reporting period, with available material matching the prior batch size and pricing level. Traders said the stability in NdPr availability is supporting smoother procurement schedules for companies that rely on the alloy for permanent magnets used in electric motors and wind turbines.

In contrast, the notes continued to flag limited availability for Dy and Tb. Both dysprosium and terbium were listed in smaller quantities than requested in recent inquiries, and buyers seeking larger lots faced longer lead times. The warehouse entries also indicated that Dy and Tb supplies were coming primarily from a smaller set of retained inventory, rather than replenishment at scale.

For Dy, warehouse records showed that demand was still outpacing the material offered for immediate allocation. Several buyers typically rotate orders to secure near-term requirements, and the latest notes suggest those allocations were again constrained by the amount of material the warehouse could release. Tb followed a similar pattern, with the latest lot sizes described as tight.

Market participants said the situation is consistent with broader input constraints in the separated rare-earth supply chain, where downstream availability often depends on upstream processing capacity and inventory drawdowns. They also pointed to volatility in pricing signals from limited lot sizes, which can affect negotiation dynamics even when the underlying demand picture does not change.

“NdPr supply remained stable, but Dy and Tb lots were still limited,” a warehouse contact said, describing the overall balance as mixed across the three key grades. The contact added that additional offers were being evaluated for timing and documentation requirements before any further releases.

The warehouse notes are closely watched by buyers seeking transparent near-term access to separated rare-earth materials, particularly for magnet-grade specifications. While NdPr availability is currently allowing for routine buying, the continued restraint on Dy and Tb suggests procurement planning will remain sensitive to allocation decisions and any changes in the warehouse inventory pipeline.

headline: Rare-earth inventory watch: magnet producer builds safety cover for Q3
body: A leading magnet producer has started building additional safety cover for its rare-earth inventory position ahead of the third quarter, according to market participants tracking supply risk and pricing signals. The move is intended to cushion volatility in NdPr inputs typically required for NdFeB magnets used in EV motors and wind generators, where lead times can stretch beyond 8 to 12 weeks. Traders said the firm is targeting incremental stock coverage that would offset expected Q3 procurement gaps linked to tighter availability of separated oxides and spot-grade feedstock during July through September. The scheduling aligns with the company’s internal procurement calendar, with pre-build activities taking place in March and April to avoid late Q2 constraints. Market watchers noted that rare-earth inventory levels have been watched closely since early January, when several processors reported slower conversion capacity and higher unit premiums for certain grades. The added buffer is expected to influence near-term buy-side behavior for NdPr and dysprosium-heavy mixes, though no specific volume targets were disclosed for Q3.

headline: Rare-earth weekly inventory updates: cerium stocks rise, lanthanum stays tight
body:
Rare-earth inventories tracked in weekly market updates showed cerium stocks increasing while lanthanum supplies remained tight, according to data reviewed by traders and shared by multiple participants in the supply chain.

The latest update indicated that cerium inventories rose week over week, adding to the small surplus trend that has emerged over the past month. Several buyers said the improvement was largely driven by steady inflows of material into intermediate storage sites, even as demand for cerium continues to be spread across polishing, catalysts and glassmaking applications.

Lanthanum, by contrast, showed no similar release of stock, with inventories staying constrained. Market participants attributed the tightness to a combination of slower deliveries from upstream producers and continued purchasing by end users seeking to maintain uninterrupted feedstock for separation and refining. A trader described lanthanum as “staying on the tighter side,” noting that lead times have been unchanged compared with recent weeks.

Separately, the same inventory update tracked movements in other light and heavy rare-earth categories, with activity levels reflecting typical seasonal scheduling and contract cycles. Traders said shifts in pricing and spot availability in the broader market often follow the inventory direction of the most liquid products, which can influence procurement decisions across mixed-oxide and separated rare-earth baskets.

In Asia, where most separation capacity is concentrated, participants said the inventory picture is closely watched because it can affect downstream processing margins and delivery schedules. The weekly monitoring framework is used by buyers to anticipate whether manufacturers will have access to sufficient material for processing into final products and whether shortages could prompt additional procurement or longer transport times.

While cerium’s rise suggests a modest easing in availability, the persistence of lanthanum tightness points to uneven supply balances across the rare-earth complex. Traders said they will continue to watch subsequent weekly updates for signs that lanthanum stocks will rebound, particularly if upstream shipments increase or if buyers reduce spot competition.

headline: Analyst note: MP Materials sees stable neodymium demand into next quarter
body: MP Materials said an analyst note sees demand for neodymium elements remaining steady into the next quarter, supporting expectations for uninterrupted processing volumes at its U.S. rare-earth operations. The note, dated March 2, 2026, projects demand stability through at least the end of the second quarter, citing continued magnet demand tied to electric-vehicle platforms and industrial motors. MP Materials, which operates the Mountain Pass facility in California and additional refining capacity through downstream partnerships, is positioned to convert concentrate feed into separated rare earth products as contract timing remains largely unchanged. The analyst pointed to ongoing inventory normalization among buyers, with replenishment rates expected to track near-term production schedules rather than accelerate materially. Near-term pricing trends were described as range-bound, with no immediate signal of a demand pullback across major end markets. Overall, the assessment frames 2026 second-quarter procurement as resilient, contingent on continued supply stability and no major disruptions to shipments during the spring period.

headline: Analyst preview: Lynas earnings to focus on cerium mix and recovery rates
body:
Lynas Rare Earths’ upcoming results will likely hinge on the company’s cerium product mix and the pace of recovery rates at its processing sites, according to industry analysts preparing for the earnings release.

In forecasts reviewed ahead of the update, analysts said attention will be on how Lynas performs across its neodymium-praseodymium and cerium-heavy product streams, with cerium pricing and demand remaining key variables for margins. Several firms expect management to provide additional detail on the proportion of cerium produced versus other rare-earth outputs, and on how that mix translates into revenue and realised pricing during the quarter.

Investors are also expected to focus on recovery performance, particularly the efficiency of separating and converting rare-earth-bearing materials into saleable products. Recovery rates can affect both throughput and effective yields, with analysts noting that even small changes can influence unit costs and earnings. Lynas is also expected to highlight any operational stability issues and the impact of feedstock characteristics on recovery.

Analysts said they will look for commentary on the sustainability of recent improvements, including whether any gains in conversion efficiency are repeatable across production runs. They also anticipate updates on plant utilisation and inventory levels, which can affect revenue timing for both intermediate materials and finished separated products. For the market, the timing of shipments and the length of any working-capital cycle remain important considerations when assessing near-term earnings momentum.

On the balance-sheet side, attention is expected to centre on cash flow and cost management. Analysts generally plan to review operating cost guidance and any changes to maintenance plans, transport expenses and refining inputs. With rare-earth markets sensitive to pricing and customer procurement cycles, several forecasts include scenarios that assume variable offtake demand for cerium products alongside continued treatment of technical-grade and higher-value materials.

Lynas has historically reported that performance at its processing facilities is influenced by feed composition, process conditions and downstream customer specifications. One analyst said the company’s “ability to maintain target recovery levels while optimising the cerium mix will be the critical driver for how strong the quarter looks versus expectations,” adding that guidance on recovery and production stability would likely set the tone for the next few reporting periods.

The company is scheduled to release its earnings figures later in the week.

headline: Rare-earth note: stable terbium premiums support longer-term contract pricing
