body: Stable terbium premiums were reported to be supporting longer-term contract pricing across rare-earth procurement in early March 2026, according to market participants tracking spot and term negotiations. Premiums for terbium were described as steady following recent normalization in availability, reducing volatility typically seen around annual rebalancing of buyer inventory targets. Several downstream magnet and additive manufacturing buyers said they were able to roll forward contracts with price bands tied to terbium premium benchmarks, rather than renegotiating fixed unit fees every quarter. In discussions dated 2026-02-20 to 2026-03-01, counterparties pointed to improved visibility on supply schedules and fewer emergency cargo diversions, which helped stabilize forward assessments for April–December 2026 delivery. Trading desks noted that while dysprosium and other heavy rare-earth pricing remained more reactive to delivery tightness, terbium’s relative stability helped anchor the blended basket used in long-tenor agreements. One procurement firm indicated that reference pricing had held within a narrower range of low-to-mid single-digit percentage movements since January 2026, supporting continued use of 6–12 month contract structures.

headline: Investor call recap: Arafura updates rare-earth inventory positioning for Q3
body:
Arafura Rare Earths updated investors on its rare-earth inventory positioning for the third quarter, outlining changes to expected supply and stock levels during an investor call, according to company remarks.

The company said it has been tracking inventory and procurement activity to align production and sales timing ahead of Q3. Arafura described its approach as focused on balancing held material across its supply chain while maintaining flexibility to meet customer requirements, particularly for separation and processing stages that affect when material can be delivered.

Management also discussed how quarter-to-quarter movements in product availability relate to downstream demand and customer offtake schedules. The company said it expects the inventory position heading into Q3 to reflect a combination of operational progress, receipt of materials and processing progress, as well as shipment planning and order fulfilment timing.

Arafura indicated that it continues to evaluate working capital and logistics considerations tied to holding inventory, including the pace at which material can be converted into saleable products. The company said it remains in close contact with partners and customers to manage variations in lead times, with an emphasis on maintaining steady availability rather than maximizing stock levels at any single point in the quarter.

In the call, the company highlighted that rare-earth inventory is a key lever for ensuring continuity of supply, given the sector’s constrained processing capacity and the gap that can occur between mining output and product delivery. Arafura said it is working to reduce timing mismatches by coordinating production, processing and shipment schedules across the value chain.

Commenting on the update, Chief Executive Matt Maguire said the company’s Q3 inventory positioning is designed to support consistent offtake delivery while reflecting the realities of processing lead times. “We are managing inventory to ensure we can meet customer demand with the right product forms as we move into Q3,” he said.

Arafura’s update comes as investors continue to monitor the pace of rare-earth processing and the availability of separated products used in magnets and other industrial applications. The company did not provide a specific revised forecast for full-year performance during the call, focusing instead on inventory positioning and operational timing for the coming quarter.

headline: Rare-earth company guidance: MP Materials maintains output plan despite weather headwinds
body: MP Materials said it will keep its 2026 production plan for rare-earth oxides despite weather disruptions affecting mining and processing operations in the second half. The company, which operates a mine in Mountain Pass, California, and a downstream separation facility, reported that adverse conditions have temporarily constrained haulage and processing schedules through late February and into early March. Management reiterated its prior guidance for contained rare-earth output, citing continued access to stockpiles and equipment availability as mitigating factors. It also said energy costs and contractor lead times remain stable compared with internal forecasts, with seasonal variability posing the main risk. MP Materials’ update follows a pattern of operational volatility seen across the U.S. rare-earth supply chain during winter storms and high wind events. Investors have been watching guidance closely as China controls most global processing capacity and as U.S. defense and industrial buyers seek reliable magnet-material supply. The company expects normal ramp-up to resume once weather patterns ease.

headline: Lynas guides rare-earth separation volumes; cerium production expected to trend higher
body:
Lynas Rare Earths said it expects higher cerium separation volumes as it guides production throughput for its rare-earth separation business, citing improving operational performance and stable demand for light rare earth products.

In an update to investors, the Australian company said it is targeting continued growth in separated rare-earth volumes at its processing facilities, with cerium products set to trend higher during the coming months. The guidance reflects a normalisation of separation line utilisation following earlier disruptions and assumes continued availability of feed materials and steady offtake arrangements.

Lynas’s separation segment focuses on converting rare-earth concentrates into separated oxides used across electronics, electric vehicles and industrial applications. Cerium, one of the most widely used rare earths, is typically demanded for automotive catalysts and polishing and industrial compounds, where refiners and manufacturers require consistent supply and specifications.

The company also indicated that it expects to maintain disciplined management of feedstock chemistry and product routing to support yields and reduce variability in output. It said recent improvements in plant stability have helped support higher throughput, while ongoing maintenance activities are planned to minimise interruptions to downstream separation activities.

Lynas reiterated that its manufacturing network includes downstream refining operations in Malaysia and processing capacity in Australia, while it continues to develop its long-term supply chain and product qualification efforts. The company said it works with customers on product requirements, including purity and particle characteristics, and that these factors influence scheduling and production planning across rare-earth streams.

“Cerium volumes are expected to trend higher as separation throughput continues to build and as we maintain production stability,” Lynas said, referring to its near-term operating guidance for separated rare-earth output.

Market conditions for rare-earth materials have remained sensitive to supply constraints, pricing volatility and qualification timelines for new magnet and catalyst applications. Lynas’s guidance adds to the view that light rare earths, particularly cerium and lanthanum-related products, could see tighter demand-supply balance as buyers replenish inventories and industrial users ramp consumption.

headline: Processing update: Solvay-linked rare-earth recovery program schedules commissioning timing
body: A Solvay-linked rare-earth recovery program has moved to the next stage of planning after updating its project schedule for commissioning timing, according to industry sources. The program, which targets the recovery of critical rare-earth elements from secondary feedstocks, is now slated to complete mechanical completion in stages during 2026, with commissioning work set to begin later than initially expected. Fast-tracking of key long-lead items, including ion-exchange modules and solvent handling skids, has pushed first commissioning activities into the second half of 2026, with ramp-up toward design throughput scheduled for early 2027. The updated timeline follows adjustments to process integration testing windows and regulatory documentation deliverables associated with plant utilities and effluent treatment systems. The schedule also reflects extended performance verification runs for upstream filtration and precipitation steps, with additional sampling campaigns planned across 2 to 3 operating batches. No changes to the program’s stated capacity targets were cited, but the sequencing of hot commissioning and integrated operations has been re-baselined to align with commissioning resource availability and safety sign-off milestones.

headline: Quarterly earnings recap: rare-earth segment margins held as dysprosium pricing moderates
body:
Rare-earth producer MP Materials reported that margins in its rare-earth segment held steady in the latest quarter as dysprosium pricing moderated and demand remained broadly stable, according to results published on Monday.

In its quarterly earnings recap, the company said performance in its magnet-materials and related operations benefited from continued cost discipline and contract pricing terms that reduced exposure to day-to-day fluctuations in spot markets. Segment margins were maintained despite a softer pricing trend for dysprosium, a heavy rare earth used in high-performance permanent magnets for electric motors and wind turbines.

Management attributed the stability to a mix of longer-term sales arrangements and an operating profile that allowed the firm to absorb changes in commodity pricing with limited impact on margins. It also pointed to lower energy and processing costs, alongside ongoing efficiency improvements at its processing facilities, as key factors supporting profitability during the quarter.

For the rare-earth segment, the company reported that dysprosium pricing trends shifted from the highs seen earlier in the year, but did not translate into a steep decline in realized pricing. Analysts and industry participants have been tracking dysprosium closely because its supply constraints and pricing volatility can affect downstream magnet producers and industrial customers with inventory needs that vary by contract cycle.

The company did not provide a detailed breakdown of dysprosium volumes by customer category in its filing, but it said total rare-earth output and product shipments were consistent with management expectations. It also reported continued progress on refining and separation throughput, which it said helped reduce variability in product availability and supporting sales continuity.

“While dysprosium pricing moderated during the quarter, our realized results reflected the contractual structure of our sales and continued cost management across operations,” a company spokesperson said in a statement.

Looking ahead, MP Materials said it expects market conditions to remain variable, with pricing influenced by global demand for magnets and the pace of customer restocking. It also indicated that any changes in dysprosium supply and pricing could affect future quarter results, though it expects its margin profile to remain resilient if cost controls and customer contract terms hold.

headline: MP Materials Q1 earnings preview: focus on neodymium-praseodymium and byproduct yields
body: MP Materials is expected to report results for the first quarter after the close of trading on March 3, 2026, with investors focused on performance in neodymium-praseodymium output and the company’s ability to convert byproducts into saleable rare-earth products. Analysts are looking for evidence that processing yields at the Mountain Pass mine and downstream separation operations improved during the quarter, particularly for neodymium-praseodymium oxides that underpin pricing momentum. Estimates center on quarterly byproduct yields from upstream separation streams, which can swing total rare-earth mixed output and downstream recoveries. Guidance attention is also on operating efficiency and planned maintenance that could affect throughput across January, February, and March. In recent quarters, market expectations have been tied to how successfully MP Materials navigates variability in feed chemistry and the timing of rare-earth crystallization and purification steps. Traders will also monitor net sales sensitivity to spot versus contract pricing for neodymium-praseodymium, alongside the timing of shipment schedules that can shift revenue recognition into early April.

headline: Earnings review: magnet materials firm cites steady terbium usage and disciplined procurement
body:
A magnet materials producer reported steady terbium consumption and said it has maintained disciplined purchasing practices as it reviewed quarterly earnings, according to company commentary released in connection with its results.

The firm, which supplies rare-earth-based components used in high-performance motors and other industrial applications, said terbium demand remained stable over the reporting period. It attributed the level of usage to ongoing customer programs and relatively consistent order rates for products incorporating terbium-containing magnet materials.

In its review, the company highlighted that procurement policies are continuing to focus on balancing availability with cost controls. It said it has managed supply commitments through a mix of contracted volumes and spot purchases, aiming to reduce exposure to price swings while keeping lead times within customer requirements. The company did not provide detailed pricing figures in its update.

The review also noted that production scheduling remains tied to input availability and inventory management. The firm said it had preserved critical material availability to support output continuity, while working through existing inventories to meet near-term demand. Analysts typically scrutinize these disclosures for signals on whether inventories are being built ahead of expected demand changes or drawn down as customers adjust orders.

On costs and margins, management pointed to the importance of maintaining sourcing discipline and improving operational efficiency across processing steps. It said the company is continuing efforts to optimize yield and reduce variability in processing, which it described as supporting manufacturing stability during periods of fluctuating rare-earth pricing.

The firm’s update included a brief outlook statement that focused on customer behavior and near-term requirements. “We’re seeing steady use of terbium and we will continue to manage procurement with a disciplined approach to protect supply and costs,” a company spokesperson said, referring to the period’s demand patterns.

Rare earth prices have remained sensitive to changes in global demand for electric vehicles, wind power equipment and industrial motors, where permanent magnet performance is a key factor. Companies in the supply chain have increasingly emphasized inventory and procurement strategies as they seek to align material availability with manufacturing plans and customer commitments.

headline: Guidance update: Brazilian rare-earth supplier targets higher cerium recovery next quarter
body: Brazil’s leading rare-earth processing supplier said it will raise cerium recovery in the next quarter under an updated operating plan, aiming to increase the yield of cerium concentrate from mixed feedstock. The company, which processes domestic and imported light-rare-earth inputs, expects commissioning and run-rate adjustments to take effect in the April–June 2026 period following trials completed in January and February. Management indicated it will tune solvent-extraction residence times and revise impurity-removal steps to reduce losses tied to lanthanum carryover and iron contamination. Internal targets call for higher cerium extraction efficiency at the plant and improved recovery during start-up and steady-state weeks. The update comes as global buyers continue renegotiating rare-earth supply contracts in response to tighter processing capacity and variable feed composition across 2025 and early 2026. If the revised plan holds, the supplier said it expects improved cerium output compared with the first-quarter average, which it characterized as constrained by feed variability and slower-than-planned circuit stabilization.

headline: Company capex call: Australian processor budgets for rare-earth solvent extraction upgrades
body:
Australian rare-earth processors are earmarking capital spending for solvent extraction upgrades as demand grows for heavier lanthanides used in magnets and clean-energy technologies, according to company disclosures and internal budgeting documents reviewed by Reuters.

Several firms said their near-term capex plans include new solvent extraction trains, additional solvent recovery systems and modifications to existing separation circuits to improve throughput and reduce reagent losses. The upgrades are aimed at tightening control of water use and chemical emissions, while increasing the share of high-value separated products such as dysprosium, terbium and yttrium. Companies also cited higher reliability needs as they move from pilot-scale campaigns to longer production runs.

In general, solvent extraction remains a key bottleneck for projects that process mixed rare-earth concentrates. Industry participants said equipment constraints and operating variability have historically affected recovery rates and purity, particularly for late-stage separations of heavy rare earths. The capex calls described by companies focus on improving contact efficiency in extraction stages, adding polishing steps and upgrading stripping and precipitation infrastructure to meet tightening customer specifications.

One operator, Lynas Rare Earths, said it plans to invest in processing upgrades at its sites to support incremental capacity and product quality improvements. “We continue to invest to enhance recovery and processing efficiency in our separation circuits,” a spokesperson said in a statement. Lynas did not provide detailed figures in the materials reviewed, but noted that upgrades are linked to production schedules and supply-chain lead times for critical reagents and equipment.

Other processors, including regional refiners and contractors supporting integrated rare-earth supply chains, are also budgeting for expansion of chemical storage, backup treatment and monitoring systems. Several outlined timelines for equipment commissioning in phases, aiming to minimize downtime by aligning installation work with scheduled maintenance windows. Industry sources said long lead times for specialty valves, pumps, extraction modules and solvent purification units are a factor in the capex planning.

Companies are managing cost pressures from tighter global supply of industrial pumps and stainless-steel components, alongside volatility in chemicals used in extraction and refining. They said funding for the upgrades is expected to come from operating cash flow and, in some cases, targeted project financing tied to offtake arrangements.

Analysts said the spending is likely to be concentrated in stages rather than broad new greenfield builds, reflecting the quicker path to capacity gains from brownfield improvements. They added that the upgrades could support more consistent product output as contracts for magnet-grade rare earths expand among manufacturers of wind turbines, electric vehicles and energy storage systems.

headline: Capex update: facility expansion in Malaysia supports expanded lanthanum separation capacity
body: A planned expansion at a Malaysia facility is set to increase lanthanum separation capacity as the operator ramps capital spending in 2026, according to an investor update circulated this week. The company said capex tied to construction and commissioning is scheduled through late 2026, with first incremental throughput targeted in the third quarter and full capacity expected to follow by early 2027. The project scope includes additional separation trains and related utility upgrades, designed to raise nameplate output by an amount not specified in the materials but linked to higher demand for rare-earth feedstocks used in optical, catalysts and polishing applications. The update described supply-chain preparation steps beginning in the first quarter of 2026, including procurement of key reagents, membranes and solvent handling equipment. Operating assumptions cited include sustained electricity availability and solvent recovery performance at current design levels, with commissioning testing planned over consecutive monthly batches in Q4 2026. The company did not provide revised quarterly financial guidance, but indicated that capex outlays will be weighted toward the second half of 2026.

headline: Conference recap: Perth rare-earth forum tracks ongoing rare-earth processing timelines
body:
A rare-earth processing forum in Perth reviewed updated project timetables and highlighted ongoing work aimed at securing longer-term supplies of critical minerals, according to participants.

The conference, which brought together producers, suppliers and researchers, focused on where rare-earth processing projects stand after years of delays and shifting demand forecasts. Organisers said the sessions tracked progress from feedstock production through to separation, refining and the production of rare-earth oxides and separated elements used in magnets and other technologies.

Speakers described the importance of aligning upstream operations with downstream processing capacity, given that many projects rely on distinct stages occurring in different facilities and countries. Several presentations revisited scheduling assumptions for commissioning, test campaigns and ramp-up periods for separation plants, including the availability of reagents, utilities and specialist equipment required for sustained production.

A recurring theme was the way processing timelines depend on permitting, construction sequencing and commissioning risk. Participants also discussed how supply contracts and offtake frameworks are being used to support financing decisions for separation and refining capacity, as well as how companies are preparing for variability in feed quality. Attendees said that qualification of offtake streams and the ability to meet product specifications can affect how quickly processing lines move from commissioning to steady output.

One keynote address underscored that developers are maintaining delivery schedules by targeting incremental milestones, including trial separations, phased equipment commissioning and stepwise increases in throughput. “The industry is converging on practical sequencing plans—work is progressing, but the critical path remains linked to commissioning and output verification,” a company representative said during the forum.

Beyond processing schedules, the programme covered advances in hydrometallurgy and recycling related to rare-earth recovery. Researchers presented findings on improving separation selectivity and reducing waste streams, while industry representatives outlined efforts to secure rare-earth-bearing residues and refine feed sources for reprocessing. Organisers said these developments are expected to feed into future capacity expansion plans.

The conference concluded with a review of near-term milestones that companies are using to track progress, with participants indicating that timelines will be updated as commissioning results emerge and as demand signals evolve.

headline: Rare-earth conference in Chengdu ends with panel on lanthanum demand signals
body: The Chengdu rare-earth conference concluded on March 3 after two days of industry briefings focused on demand signals for lanthanum and downstream markets for rare-earth compounds. Organizers said more than 240 delegates from mining, refining, and magnet supply chains attended sessions held March 1-3 across multiple venues in Sichuan. Analysts reported that lanthanum-linked demand has remained more resilient than several other light rare earth segments, supported by continued consumption in catalysts, glass polishing, and battery-related feedstock routes. Several panels cited 2025 order-book recovery in China and expectations for steadier second-quarter of 2026 offtake, while also noting that pricing sensitivity remains tied to monthly separation capacity utilization. Participants reviewed supply logistics for lanthanum-bearing concentrates and the pace of rare-earth separation projects scheduled for commissioning in mid-2026, including incremental expansions aimed at reducing rare-earth inventory churn. The final panel said monitoring of substitution dynamics in mixed rare-earth formulations and compliance costs for refinery upgrades could be decisive for demand growth through year-end 2026.

headline: Industry symposium recap: recycling of cerium and lanthanum discussed for 2026
body:
An industry symposium held this week focused on how manufacturers plan to improve the recycling of cerium and lanthanum ahead of 2026, according to presentations and a summary released by participants.

The meeting brought together producers, materials recyclers and downstream component makers to review current supply chains for the two rare-earth elements used in products ranging from automotive catalysts to glass polishing compounds and lighting. Organizers said the discussions centered on recovery rates, feedstock availability and the practical steps needed to scale collection and processing.

Participants said cerium and lanthanum recycling is increasingly tied to end-of-life management for catalysts and polishing materials, where rare-earth-containing fractions are generated during manufacturing and after use. Several speakers described ongoing efforts to standardize sampling and sorting of rare-earth-bearing waste streams, which they said can reduce variability in composition and improve yields in separation processes.

Sessions also covered technical approaches for extracting the elements from mixed oxides and residues, including hydrometallurgical routes that convert rare-earth compounds into recyclable intermediates. Companies and research groups presented lab results on solvent extraction and precipitation methods aimed at lowering chemical consumption and waste volumes, as well as improvements in controlling impurities that can degrade the quality of recycled material.

Industry representatives said they are preparing targets and timelines for 2026 that align with planned investments in processing capacity and equipment upgrades. The symposium summary pointed to a need for traceability across the recycling chain, from the collection of scrap to the production of refined rare-earth salts and oxides that can meet customer specifications.

One organizer, speaking on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to speak publicly, said coordination across the value chain is central to meeting the 2026 timeframe. “Recycling economics depend on consistent feedstock quality and predictable offtake, so the industry is working to close gaps between material recovery and downstream requirements,” the organizer said.

Participants also discussed regulatory and reporting considerations affecting recycling operations, including how recyclers document the recycled content of products and comply with waste-handling rules. Several companies indicated they would publish technical roadmaps during 2025, including milestones for demonstration-scale recovery and evaluations of how recycled cerium and lanthanum perform in commercial formulations.

headline: Podcast episode recap: analysts discuss routine rare-earth price behavior and inventories
body: Analysts reviewing a new podcast episode said rare-earth prices in early 2026 are behaving in ways consistent with routine inventory cycles rather than a structural supply shock. The discussion, dated March 3, focused on price moves across key separated products, including neodymium-praseodymium and dysprosium, where near-term changes have tracked storage drawdowns and replenishment timing instead of sudden mine disruptions. Participants cited inventory data showing tighter balances in specific midstream segments through January-February, followed by stabilization in late February as buyers reduced spot purchases and shifted to contract volumes. Several analysts noted that processing capacity utilization remains the dominant driver of near-term availability, with lead times typically spanning 4 to 8 weeks. They also highlighted that demand signals from magnet makers have been mixed, with orders stabilizing after a mid-quarter slowdown. Overall, the episode framed the market as range-bound into the second quarter, with risks tied to inventory replenishment speed, China-linked export logistics, and monthly pricing resets.

headline: ETF flows update: rare-earth-linked basket sees modest inflows for second straight week
body:
A rare-earth-linked exchange-traded fund basket logged modest inflows for a second consecutive week, according to fresh flow data compiled by market trackers.

The basket, designed to track companies exposed to rare-earth mining, processing and related supply chains, received net buying during the latest reporting period as investors maintained exposure despite continued volatility in raw materials equities. The renewed inflow follows a prior week of positive demand, a relatively uncommon run for the product group in recent months, when flows have more frequently fluctuated around risk appetite and commodity price swings.

Flow patterns suggest the latest purchases were incremental rather than a broad shift into the theme. Holdings did not see major reshuffling across constituent names, indicating that most of the week’s activity reflected continued position building by existing shareholders rather than broad fund rotations. Market participants attributed the steadier bid to investors seeking longer-term supply-chain themes while keeping allocations small due to uneven earnings visibility among rare-earth producers and equipment suppliers.

The figures also point to a cautious approach from investors navigating a complex macro backdrop. Currency moves, interest rate expectations and the direction of industrial metals have influenced sentiment toward materials ETFs more generally, and the rare-earth basket appears to have followed that broader pattern while still benefiting from steady thematic demand.

One source familiar with ETF trading said the inflows were consistent with “typical rebalancing and dollar-cost-type purchases,” rather than a one-off event linked to a single news catalyst. The source added that liquidity conditions in the underlying shares were broadly stable, allowing flows to translate into holdings without significant dislocations.

Analysts said the next driver to watch would be whether inflows persist into the following week, particularly as investors assess corporate updates from rare-earth and magnet supply-chain companies and consider policy developments affecting critical minerals. For now, the basket’s two-week streak of positive flows signals modest but continuing interest in the segment, even as broader commodities-linked sentiment remains mixed.

headline: ETF flows: global rare-earth ETC adds exposure to dysprosium-heavy themes
body: Global rare-earth exchange-traded commodity and trust-linked vehicles added further dysprosium exposure this week, according to new product listings reviewed on 2026-03-04. The issuer launched an ETC structure designed to track a basket with above-average weighting to dysprosium, a component commonly used in high-strength permanent magnets for EV motors and wind generators. The move follows renewed investor demand for “rare-earth-heavy” themes after 2025’s price volatility in several separated rare-earths, with dysprosium and terbium often cited as the most supply-constrained elements in the complex. Market data cited by distributors showed incremental inflows routed into the product during the first two trading days after listing, while outflows from broader metals ETCs remained mixed between 0.2% and 0.8% on a week-to-date basis. Analysts said the basket approach aims to reduce single-commodity tracking risk as procurement spreads and refining lead times continue to shift month by month. Trading desks noted that while liquidity can be thin in early sessions, spreads typically narrow if daily volumes exceed predefined threshold levels over the subsequent 3 to 10 weeks.

headline: CEO profile: MP Materials’ CEO outlines steady ramp plans and near-term pricing view
body:
MP Materials said its chief executive outlined a steady ramp-up strategy for the company’s rare earths operations and offered a near-term view on pricing during remarks to investors, according to company materials and participants.

The Mountain Pass, California-based producer is working to increase output as demand from customers for magnet-grade rare earths remains supported by industrial electrification and the wider push for domestic supply chains. The CEO described the ramp as incremental and operationally driven, focused on maintaining throughput and reliability while scaling key parts of the production process.

In discussing the company’s near-term pricing environment, the CEO said prices should be understood through a forward-looking mix of contract terms and spot dynamics that can vary by product grade and customer specifications. The remarks indicated the company expects pricing to remain subject to normal market fluctuations rather than a rapid, one-directional change, and that it is monitoring indicators such as inventory levels and buyer purchasing behavior.

MP Materials also highlighted progress on sustaining operations at its separation and refining facilities. The CEO pointed to ongoing efforts to manage cost pressures, including electricity and reagent inputs, as well as to reduce downtime and improve utilization. These measures are intended to support consistent deliveries and to limit volatility in quarterly output.

The company’s update comes as rare earth markets continue to be influenced by policy initiatives and supply diversification across the sector. MP Materials has previously said it aims to expand capacity without disrupting quality requirements for high-performance magnets used in electric vehicles, wind turbines and other industrial applications.

“While the ramp is steady, we are focused on delivering consistent volumes and maintaining disciplined execution across the plant,” the CEO said, according to quotes shared by attendees. The CEO added that the company is aligning production plans with customer demand and contract schedules to help stabilize revenue as volumes increase.

Investors have closely tracked MP Materials’ timing for higher production and its ability to secure offtake arrangements. The company’s latest remarks suggest it intends to balance growth targets with operational readiness, while taking a measured approach to near-term pricing expectations as market conditions evolve.

headline: CEO interview: Lynas CEO on operational cadence for praseodymium and terbium
body: Lynas CEO said the company has tightened its operational cadence for praseodymium and terbium output, aiming to stabilize monthly production and reduce variability that has historically affected rare-earth supply. Speaking on 4 March 2026, the chief executive outlined a schedule that links feedstock receipt, cracking and separation runs, and downstream product finishing to weekly throughput targets at processing sites in Malaysia and Australia. The plan targets consistent processing of rare earth concentrates and monazite-derived material, with maintenance windows scheduled in defined blocks to avoid unplanned downtime during the high-demand period spanning Q2 through Q4. The company also described inventory controls designed to keep praseodymium and terbium output aligned with customer offtake volumes, using rolling forecasts updated at least monthly. Lynas indicated it is prioritizing reliability of separation-stage chemistry, including reagent and precipitate-handling steps, to limit batch-to-batch swings. No production figures or cost estimates were provided in the interview, but the CEO said execution metrics will be reported against the cadence through the 2026 calendar year.

headline: Weekly market call recap: dealers discuss neodymium pricing and customer stocking
body:
Dealers and traders in the rare earth market met for a weekly call to review neodymium pricing, customer inventory behavior and near-term supply expectations, according to participants.

The discussion centered on whether neodymium prices can hold firm after recent moves in the downstream chain, with several dealers reporting steady demand signals from magnet and alloy buyers. Participants said customers have been increasingly focused on timing—monitoring how quickly they need to replenish material against production schedules—rather than placing large speculative orders.

Dealers also compared indications from different feedstock and oxide sources, noting that availability remains uneven across product forms. While some participants said offers for separated neodymium did not show broad downward pressure, others highlighted that buyers are pushing for clearer delivery terms and tighter lead-time guarantees. One dealer said the market’s near-term tone is being shaped by “a balance between limited visibility on future supply and customers’ willingness to top up inventories in smaller increments.”

On customer stocking, participants described a cautious approach. Several dealers said buyers appear to be building coverage for specific contract periods but are still reluctant to commit to bigger procurement volumes unless pricing stabilizes. Expectations varied on how long current stock levels can cover production, though most said replenishment could intensify if pricing confidence improves.

The call also touched on how foreign demand factors into pricing negotiations. Dealers referenced ongoing conversations with buyers outside China, including those seeking longer-term supply arrangements and more predictable conversion capacity. Participants said this has contributed to a split in behavior: some customers prioritize contracting and allocation certainty, while spot buyers continue to gauge whether price resets are likely.

Separately, dealers reviewed the broader rare earth environment, including market liquidity and the pace of transactions in related grades. Participants said there have been pockets of activity, but overall volumes remain sensitive to incremental changes in offers and the availability of competing materials.

No participant reported immediate policy changes affecting neodymium specifically. The group said the next key datapoints will likely come from continued trading activity across neodymium products and from confirmations of customer inventory plans over the coming weeks.

headline: Analyst call transcript: rare-earth buyers expect stable yttrium and cerium spreads
body: Participants in a rare-earth market call said rare-earth buyers are signaling a preference for stable pricing in yttrium and cerium spreads following recent volatility tied to supply scheduling and freight disruptions. Buyers expected yttrium and cerium separation premiums to hold within narrow bands for the March 2026 spot cycle, with several traders citing continued inventory coverage at electronics and optics processors through mid-April. The discussion referenced recent assessments showing cerium spreads supported by steady demand for glass polishing and catalysts, while yttrium demand remained concentrated in phosphors and laser components. Sellers, however, were reported to be cautious on forward bookings beyond June 2026, citing uncertainty around China export compliance checks and periodic production downtime at midstream refiners. Analysts said most participants are leaning toward “hold” recommendations on spread direction, while remaining ready to adjust if new tender results emerge in May. Trading desks also flagged that any tightening in cerium feedstock or delays in shipment clearances could widen spreads rapidly, even if overall rare-earth basket prices stay range-bound.

headline: Rare-earth quarterly guidance: processing margin outlook unchanged amid steady volumes
body:
A rare-earth producer said its outlook for processing margins remains unchanged in its latest quarterly guidance, despite continued steady volumes across its operations.

The company, in a trading update, reaffirmed guidance that it expects processing margins to hold at the level previously indicated for the quarter. It attributed the stability to consistent throughput at processing sites and to continued controls on input costs, which it said helped offset incremental costs elsewhere in the value chain.

Management reported that volumes processed during the period were steady, supporting revenue visibility and helping maintain current operating conditions. The firm did not signal any major change in demand trends for downstream customers, saying it continues to see volumes broadly in line with earlier expectations.

In commentary accompanying the guidance, the company said it remained focused on optimizing production schedules, maintaining conversion yields, and sustaining reliability at its separation and refining facilities. It also noted that working capital and related cash-flow dynamics remain linked to customer payment patterns and inventory levels as the business balances processed material between contracted deliveries and stockpiles.

On margin drivers, the update pointed to the continuing relationship between the realized pricing environment for rare-earth products and costs tied to energy, reagents and logistics. While it said market pricing can move from quarter to quarter, it indicated that the current spread between product prices and processing-related expenses is expected to be broadly similar to the prior outlook.

The company’s guidance update is particularly notable given the sensitivity of margins in rare-earth processing to disruptions in feedstock supply, changes in product mix, and volatility in energy and transportation costs. It said it does not foresee operational changes that would materially affect processing margin performance over the quarter.

Executives did not provide additional targets beyond the reaffirmed margin outlook. “We remain comfortable with our current processing margin guidance given the steady volumes and our ability to maintain operating performance,” a company spokesperson said in the statement.

headline: Quarterly earnings roundup: company reports stable rare-earth output across Ce, La, and Y
body: Several rare-earth producers reported stable quarterly performance across key product lines in the March 2026 earnings window, according to results released during the week of Feb. 25–Mar. 3. Output of cerium (Ce), lanthanum (La), and yttrium (Y) held steady versus the prior quarter, with most companies citing steady feedstock availability and routine plant maintenance schedules. In quarter-on-quarter comparisons covering roughly 3 months ended late February, Ce volumes were reported flat within a narrow band of about 0% to 3%, while La and mixed rare-earth fractions tracking those streams moved within similar single-digit ranges. Y output likewise showed limited variation, with several firms pointing to consistent separation yields and no material equipment downtime. Analysts said guidance for the April–June quarter remained largely unchanged, but expectations were tempered by persistent price volatility in specialty oxides and ongoing inventory management across downstream magnets and polishing applications. Overall, the filings suggested that near-term supply risk for Ce/La/Y is low, though sentiment remains sensitive to currency moves and late-cycle demand shifts.

headline: Capex call highlights: new filtration lines to improve rare-earth rare-metal recovery
body:
A capex call from a rare-earth and rare-metal producer has set out plans to add new filtration lines aimed at improving recovery rates from processing streams, according to project documentation reviewed this week.

The company said the investment package—triggered by the capex approval process—will fund the installation of additional filtration equipment across existing hydrometallurgy circuits. The upgrades are intended to reduce losses of valuable elements during solid-liquid separation steps, particularly where the production process generates fine particulates and complex slurries.

Under the proposal, the new filtration lines would be configured to handle higher throughputs and to support tighter control of particle size distribution, which can affect downstream solvent extraction and precipitation performance. The project also includes modifications to associated piping, pumping and monitoring hardware to improve filtration stability and reduce maintenance downtime.

The capex call highlights that the change is focused on recovery rather than increased feed sourcing, with the company arguing that better separation will translate into higher yields of target rare-earth and rare-metal products from the same upstream materials. The company did not disclose the full value of the program in the initial capex communication, but it described the work as a stepwise rollout that would limit disruptions to scheduled production.

A technical briefing referenced in the documentation said filtration performance is a key determinant of overall plant recovery. “Our goal with the added filtration capacity is to improve the capture of rare-earth and rare-metal-bearing solids and minimize carryover into raffinate streams,” a plant engineering manager said in the briefing.

The company said it will conduct commissioning in phases, with trial runs planned to confirm filtration flux stability and to validate the performance of downstream separation stages using process data. It also flagged that the new lines will be designed for operational flexibility to accommodate variation in feed chemistry over time.

Industry players have continued to emphasize process efficiency as companies expand or refurbish plants to meet demand for magnets, electronics and energy-related applications. In that context, filtration upgrades are among the measures producers have used to increase yields without requiring large increases in reagent use or major changes to overall production flows.

The company said a final schedule for procurement and installation will follow after internal approval of the capex request and supplier lead-time confirmations.

headline: Rare-earth podcast: ETF investors weigh liquidity and routine inventory trends
body: Investors in rare-earth exchange-traded products are closely tracking ETF liquidity and routine inventory trends as the March 2026 rebalancing cycle approaches, according to market participants. Liquidity has tightened in recent weeks in several benchmark-bound contracts, with average daily turnover in key platforms falling to the low-single-digit percentage of listed float, while bid-ask spreads widened for select baskets tied to dysprosium and neodymium. Traders said that while long-term demand for permanent magnets remains steady, near-term price signals are being dominated by fund flows and tradeable inventory rather than headline supply disruptions. Warehouse checks show periodic shifts in rare-earth oxide and separated-material stocks, with some operators reporting lead-time consistency over the past 2 to 3 months and reduced same-week replenishment for high-purity grades. ETF investors are also weighing delivery logistics, including batch certification and settlement windows that can run 5 to 10 business days. Analysts expect liquidity to be a primary driver into mid-March, when standard holdings reviews typically trigger incremental adjustments across multiple tiers of magnet-ready material.

headline: Weekly wrap: praseodymium and neodymium prices track broader industrial demand
body:
Praseodymium and neodymium prices moved broadly in line with underlying industrial demand in the latest weekly assessment of rare earth markets, as buyers balanced inventory needs against cautious end-use orders.

Across trading, the rare earth complex continued to reflect sentiment from the wider materials sector, with prices responding to changes in procurement activity from manufacturers of components used in magnets, electric motors and other industrial applications. Dealers said activity remained uneven, with some participants focusing on near-term requirements while others delayed purchases pending clearer signals on downstream production.

In praseodymium, market pricing tracked similar themes to those seen in neodymium, supported where demand for magnet feedstock held steady. Several traders pointed to the continued importance of magnet production schedules, noting that purchasers typically adjust buying based on forecast motor and generator output. Exchange-linked and over-the-counter discussions emphasized that pricing direction remained sensitive to the availability of supply and the pace at which distributors replenished stocks.

Neodymium followed a comparable pattern, with price movement largely tied to broader industrial demand rather than isolated supply disruptions. In conversations with market participants, dealers cited stable contract interest alongside intermittent spot buying, suggesting that procurement was driven more by working-capital considerations than by a sudden shift in end-user demand. While certain lots were reported to clear quickly when offered at competitive terms, overall liquidity remained patchy across platforms.

Analysts also said the market’s behavior continued to be shaped by risk management decisions from buyers. Firms seeking to secure material for production runs weighed price levels against lead times and the cost implications of holding inventory. As a result, order flows fluctuated week to week, even when longer-term demand expectations for technologies that rely on rare earth magnets remained intact.

One trader said that “weekly pricing continues to mirror the cadence of industrial buying rather than any single-issue shock,” adding that participants were watching both order books and sourcing conditions for indications of whether the next procurement cycle would be heavier or more restrained.

By week’s end, both praseodymium and neodymium prices were assessed as moving in tandem with prevailing demand indicators, with market participants expecting continued alignment between rare earth pricing and the broader trajectory of industrial production.

headline: Rare-earth backgrounder: inventory cycles for dysprosium and terbium explained
body: Rare-earth suppliers are nearing the point where dysprosium and terbium inventory cycles, rather than spot pricing alone, are likely to dominate 2026 market direction, according to trade data reviewed this week. Dysprosium availability has been constrained for most of the last 24 months as processing capacity upgrades in China shifted output toward dysprosium-bearing intermediates, tightening refinery-grade material inventories into periodic replenishment windows. Terbium has shown the same structural pattern but on shorter lead times, with inventory turns tracking shipments tied to NdFeB magnet orders for electric vehicles and industrial motors, where procurement often resets quarterly. Analysts said that typical cycle timing for both metals is driven by 2–3 month mine-to-concentrate lags, followed by an additional 3–6 months for separation and saleable oxide conversion, creating a staggered flow that amplifies shortages when demand ramps. After a brief restocking phase in mid-2025, holdings of separated oxides are now expected to tighten again in the second half of 2026 unless contract tonnages expand. Spot moves therefore may lag fundamentals by one to two quarters, while premiums are most sensitive to month-end drawdowns and route constraints in the China domestic supply chain.

headline: Conference recap: Frankfurt rare-earth session reviews routine supply/demand balance
body:
A recent conference session in Frankfurt reviewed the outlook for rare-earth markets, with speakers focusing on the balance between routine supply and demand and the steady progress of new processing and separation projects.

Participants said near-term fundamentals remain anchored by established production routes and long-term offtake agreements, particularly for separated oxides and metals used in magnets, batteries and industrial applications. Several presentations described demand growth as incremental rather than explosive, citing ongoing electrification trends alongside slower-than-expected expansion in some end-use segments.

On the supply side, the session highlighted the continued concentration of upstream refining and separation capacity, which largely determines availability of specific rare-earth elements and compounds. Speakers noted that while additional capacity is being developed in different jurisdictions, ramp-up timelines and qualification of feedstock and product specifications tend to limit how quickly new supply reaches buyers. They said those constraints have helped keep market balances relatively predictable compared with past volatility linked to disruptions in major producing regions.

Several analysts at the event also discussed how trade flows and pricing conventions continue to shape market expectations. They said buyers typically manage exposure through diversified procurement and inventory planning, particularly where magnet-grade materials and certain alloying components require tight tolerances. Conference materials indicated that transparency around inventories and product availability remains limited, which can delay price signals even when physical balances are steady.

A recurring theme was the role of recycling and secondary sources, which speakers described as supportive but not yet large enough to materially alter short-term supply-demand equations. Recycling volumes were characterized as improving gradually, driven by tighter collection and more efficient processing, but still constrained by feedstock availability and the complexity of separating target elements from mixed materials.

“Most of the near-term moves in the market are still being explained by routine changes in procurement schedules and project commissioning milestones rather than sudden shocks,” one industry participant said during a panel discussion, pointing to the steady scheduling of downstream capacity additions.

The conference session concluded that while structural factors—such as industrial policy, supply-chain localisation and processing capacity expansions—remain important, the current market environment is best described as stable. Organisers said the next round of updates would likely focus on how new separation plants progress through commissioning and how demand evolves across magnet and industrial segments through the rest of the year.

headline: Market wrap: lanthanum remains steady as yttrium demand firmed modestly
body: Lanthanum prices held steady on March 5, 2026, supported by stable industrial offtake and limited changes in spot availability across major Asian and European trading hubs. Yttrium demand firmed modestly, market participants said, after incremental buying tied to phosphor and specialty materials schedules, leading to firmer offers in short-dated deals. Across the rare-earth strip, liquidity remained uneven, with bid-ask spreads largely unchanged despite a slightly higher number of inquiry cycles for yttrium compounds. Traders reported no broad shift in end-user purchasing patterns, though buyers adjusted cover levels ahead of mid-month procurement windows. In the balance of light and heavy rare earths, sentiment was mixed: while lanthanum remained range-bound, selective interest in yttrium drew incremental tonnage. Several desks noted that contract renegotiations for downstream users were still in progress, limiting the pace of sustained upside. Overall, the market ended the session with lanthanum stable and yttrium supported by modest demand, without signs of broad price escalation.

headline: Weekly rare-earth price wrap: neodymium edges higher as praseodymium steadies
body:
Rare-earth prices were mixed in the latest weekly assessment, with neodymium edging higher while praseodymium held steady, according to market participants and trade data compiled for the period.

Neodymium, a key component in permanent magnets used in electric vehicles, wind turbines and industrial motors, rose slightly on the week as buyers sought additional supply ahead of expected demand from magnet producers. The metal’s increase was modest compared with the sharper moves seen in other rare-earth segments in recent months.

Praseodymium prices were unchanged, reflecting a balance between steady buying interest and sufficient availability through established supply channels. Dealers said praseodymium demand remained supported by the magnet supply chain, but that pricing pressure eased as sellers were willing to match prevailing benchmark levels.

Other rare-earths showed smaller shifts or remained range-bound over the week, according to the wrap. Market activity appeared concentrated in near-term contracts rather than longer-dated commitments, traders said, as end-users continued to manage inventories and watch currency and logistics conditions.

Despite the steadying in parts of the market, participants said the broader rare-earth complex continued to be influenced by pricing signals from downstream producers and by regional procurement strategies. In particular, buyers have adjusted purchasing schedules in response to production planning at magnet manufacturers, which rely on consistent feedstock for ordered output.

One trader involved in spot deals said the week’s pricing reflected “light tightening in neodymium supply” while praseodymium remained “well covered,” resulting in the divergence between the two grades. They added that trade volumes were generally steady and that negotiations centered on incremental changes to existing contract terms.

The weekly assessment tracks price movements across the rare-earth sector and is designed to reflect the most liquid benchmarks available during the review window. Further direction will likely depend on developments in downstream demand and any changes in sourcing availability, market participants said.

headline: Dysprosium weekly wrap shows flat pricing; terbium rangebound ahead of demand season
body: Dysprosium prices were flat in the latest weekly assessment as liquidity remained thin and buyers kept spot interest limited ahead of the next seasonal demand cycle, with benchmark levels holding steady across the week to March 4, 2026. Trade in terbium stayed rangebound, with sellers citing steady order books but no urgency to move pricing as most participants waited for clearer offtake schedules in the spring. Across both rare-earth categories, weekly deal counts were reported at similar levels to the prior reporting period, reflecting cautious resupply patterns rather than broad restocking. Market participants attributed the lack of directional movement to uneven industrial draw across magnets, motors, and specialty alloy applications, alongside stable availability from contracted supply. No significant disruption was reported in logistics lanes used for rare-earth concentrates and refined oxides, supporting the subdued price action. Analysts said the next signal for terbium would likely come from confirmed procurement milestones in late March and early April, when demand-related nominations typically rise.

headline: Cerium and lanthanum price update tracks stable baselines amid routine market flows
body:
Prices for cerium and lanthanum held close to stable baseline levels in the latest rare-earth market update, as routine trading flows continued to dominate activity, according to data compiled from industry sources.

Cerium, a key element used in catalytic converters and polishing compounds, was assessed around unchanged reference ranges during the most recent assessment window. Trading remained characterized by normal rebalancing between spot purchases and short-term contracts, with no broad disruptions reported in supply logistics or end-user demand.

Lanthanum, which is commonly used in glass polishing and components for cameras and other optical applications, also tracked steady baselines. Market participants reported that offers were largely aligned with the prevailing assessments, limiting the spread between bid and offer levels and keeping price movement modest.

Across both elements, liquidity appeared supported by routine order cycles rather than significant speculative activity. Several processors and traders continued to work through existing allocations, while customers confirmed procurement needs consistent with recent weeks, maintaining a steady tone in physical availability.

Analysts attributed the lack of notable price swings to comparatively balanced conditions between upstream separation output and downstream buying requirements. While individual lots could vary depending on purity grades and delivery terms, the overall pricing framework remained anchored to the established baselines used for tracking changes over time.

“Pricing is following the usual pattern of slow adjustments around the reference levels, with buyers and sellers meeting near established assessments,” one market participant said, describing current activity as orderly and influenced mainly by standard order timing.

The update also reflects ongoing attention to grade specifications, as cerium and lanthanum are traded across multiple product forms and purity levels. Any movement in those specifications, or in freight and payment terms, can affect deal-level pricing even when the headline baseline remains stable.

The market’s direction continues to be monitored for any signs of supply constraints, contract repricing, or shifts in industrial demand. For now, the latest assessments suggest that routine market flows are maintaining the current pricing structure for both cerium and lanthanum.

headline: Weekly rare-earth inventory update: magnet producers report tighter NdPr lead times
body: Rare-earth inventories tightened further during the week ending 2026-03-05, according to industry checks, as neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr) magnet producers reported longer lead times for separated NdPr. Several buyers said available spot material remained limited, with deliverable windows shifting to 3–6 weeks compared with about 2–4 weeks earlier in February, particularly for higher-grade NdPr used in permanent magnet and traction applications. Trade in NdPr with Nd content typically above 30% faced slower fulfillment, while dysprosium and terbium-linked allocations remained comparatively restrictive, reinforcing higher effective costs for heavy rare-earth blends. Chinese suppliers cited scheduling constraints and tighter order books, while at least two exporters reported more shipments moved from spot contracts into term arrangements. Over the past 7 days, warehouse stocks at service centers were described as stable to slightly down, with replenishment cycles lengthening. The overall rare-earth inventory update pointed to a shift from “normal” replenishment patterns to a more cautious procurement stance ahead of mid-March customer call-offs.

headline: Rare-earth inventories: rare-earth oxides stockpiles unchanged at end-March check
body:
Rare-earth oxide inventories held by processors and traders were unchanged at the end of March, according to a check of stockpiles that tracks movements of the key materials used in magnets and clean-energy technologies.

The latest assessment showed that total inventories of rare-earth oxides remained stable as of March 31, with no overall change reported from the previous end-of-February level. The update focused on holdings of separated oxides, which are used as feedstock for downstream refining and magnet production.

Across the monitored basket, the data indicated offsetting shifts in individual categories. Some grades saw modest variation, but those changes were not large enough to move the overall total. The check, conducted for the same grouping of companies and trading desks, aims to provide a consistent read on whether suppliers are building stocks ahead of demand peaks or drawing down reserves.

Separately, the report said that rare-earth metal inventories, which are less common in standard stock reporting than oxides, also did not show a meaningful change in the period. Inventory patterns for metals can differ because fewer facilities handle intermediate metal products and because conversion from oxides to metals depends on specific customer requirements and production schedules.

A company source familiar with the reporting said firms generally keep oxide inventories at steady levels unless there is a clear signal of order changes from magnet and alloy makers. “Most participants adjust holdings based on near-term procurement and conversion capacity rather than short-term price swings,” the source said.

The inventory figures come as manufacturers continue to manage supply for end-use sectors including electric vehicle motors, wind turbines and industrial equipment. Demand for rare-earth materials has been under scrutiny as governments and manufacturers seek more reliable sourcing, particularly for heavy rare earths that are harder to separate.

Market participants have watched inventory snapshots for clues about whether shortages are building in the physical supply chain. With the end-March check showing no aggregate drawdown or build-up in oxide stockpiles, the data suggested that, at least for the monitored inventories, supply readiness remained intact going into April.

headline: Inventory notes: yttrium supply measured as buyers roll forward contracts
body: A market check indicates global yttrium supply availability remains tightly rationed as buyers extend existing delivery terms rather than initiate new spot purchases. Several traders said estimated inventories in key refining and oxide channels were unchanged over early March, with near-term availability measured against forward contract needs through at least mid-June 2026. Contract roll behavior is most evident for oxide and specialty blends used in phosphors, lasers, and defense-linked components, where lead times are being treated as the main constraint. One source put the effective supply coverage at several weeks for confirmed orders, implying that incremental demand is being absorbed by deferred deliveries instead of stock build. Pricing signals are mixed, with index references stabilizing while transaction premiums vary by purity specification and logistics windows. Market participants also cited ongoing batch-level procurement and screening requirements that can shift volumes by single-digit percentage points between monthly lots. Overall, buyers appear to be managing risk by locking tonnage and timing through forward notes into April and May, limiting any near-term relief in physical availability.

headline: Analyst note: steady dysprosium outlook supports cautious bullish stance on Tb
body:
Analysts said a steady outlook for dysprosium is supporting a cautious bullish stance on terbium (Tb), as investors weigh supply constraints against demand uncertainty in the rare earths market.

In a note reviewed by market participants, the analyst linked the near-term price outlook for terbium to broader balance factors across heavy rare earths, including dysprosium. Dysprosium demand has been supported by long-term demand tied to permanent magnet applications, while limited additional supply has helped keep sentiment stable, the note said.

Terbium, another key component in high-performance magnets and related technologies, has seen more sporadic trading as end-user consumption and industrial restocking signals vary by sector and region. The analyst said that while terbium remains sensitive to shifts in magnet production schedules, the absence of a sharp deterioration in dysprosium conditions reduces the risk of an abrupt downside move in the heavy rare earth basket.

The note also pointed to expected supply dynamics. Heavy rare earth output is constrained by the need to process mixed ore streams and by the pace of new separation and refining capacity additions. In practice, disruptions in downstream separation operations and the timing of new commercial volumes can affect terbium pricing faster than headline production data, analysts said.

On the demand side, the analyst described continued support from electric vehicle and wind power supply chains, though growth is uneven across geographies and manufacturers. Magnet makers typically manage inventories to smooth production, which can translate into short-term swings in terbium consumption even when underlying demand remains broadly intact.

“With dysprosium holding up and the broader heavy rare earth market not signaling a major supply release, we see room for terbium to find support, but remain cautious on the pace of any rally,” the analyst said in the note.

Market participants said the takeaway is a bias toward buying terbium on dips rather than chasing aggressive upside, given the sector’s historical volatility. They also noted that currency moves, shipping and processing costs, and reported inventories at separation and magnet stages could influence near-term pricing.

Traders said they will likely monitor updates on dysprosium-linked contracts and any changes in rare earth processing throughput, which can indirectly affect terbium availability and pricing.

headline: Analyst call recap: neutral view on cerium as downstream orders remain gradual
body: Analysts in a weekly check maintained a neutral stance on cerium as demand signals from downstream segments stayed gradual into early March 2026. The most recent assessment pointed to steady spot activity but limited acceleration in offtake volumes, with buyers largely spreading purchases across March rather than front-loading orders. Pricing for cerium-containing products was described as range-bound, supported by manageable inventories at processors, while end-user consumption remained uneven across ceramics, catalysts, polishing materials, and glass applications. Several participants cited improved availability of feedstock supply compared with late 2025, which reduced short-term tightness in procurement cycles. One factor tempering sentiment was the slow pace of new qualification orders, particularly for industrial catalysts where lead times extend beyond 4–6 weeks. Overall, the group characterized 2026 near-term fundamentals as balanced, with incremental order flow likely to continue through mid-March unless downstream purchasing shifts materially.

headline: Company guidance: Lynas updates Q2 throughput targets for cerium and NdPr streams
body:
Lynas Rare Earths updated its second-quarter throughput targets for two of its rare earth processing streams, citing changes to operational planning at its Mount Weld site in Western Australia and processing activities at its downstream refinery.

In a market update issued on Thursday, the company said it expects higher volumes through its cerium (Ce) stream and adjusted throughput for its neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr) stream during the quarter ending in June. The revisions follow adjustments to plant scheduling and feed availability, Lynas said, adding that the targets remain subject to the performance of processing equipment and supply of suitable ore and intermediate materials.

For the cerium stream, Lynas raised its Q2 throughput expectation, reflecting revised assumptions for feed chemistry and recovery rates. The company said it continues to manage product quality parameters at each stage of processing to align with downstream customer specifications, including controls intended to stabilise impurity levels. Lynas did not provide a detailed breakdown of the revised figures in the announcement, but it indicated the update is intended to give investors a clearer view of expected production during the quarter.

For NdPr, Lynas said it has updated its throughput target for Q2, adjusting for operational constraints and the timing of maintenance activities. The NdPr stream is processed through stages designed to separate and concentrate rare earth products used in magnets and other industrial applications. Lynas said the quarter’s performance will also depend on the availability of suitable intermediate material and the operational cadence of upstream leaching and purification steps.

Lynas’ update comes as the company continues to ramp its processing footprint and manage inventory across its supply chain. The company reiterated that it remains focused on meeting market demand while maintaining compliance and process stability across its operations.

“We are updating our throughput expectations for the quarter to reflect the latest operational plan and material conditions across the Ce and NdPr streams,” Lynas said in the statement.

The company did not change its overall guidance in the update beyond the specific revisions to the two throughput streams. It said further updates would be provided in its regular reporting cycle and through additional market communications if material changes occur.

headline: MP Materials issues quarterly production and rare-earth shipment update; pricing remains steady
body: MP Materials reported results for the quarter ending Dec. 31, 2025, along with an operational update on rare-earth production and shipments, stating that pricing remained steady during the period. The company said production of separated rare-earth products continued in line with its internal targets, while quarterly shipment volumes were adjusted to reflect customer delivery schedules and inventory balancing. MP Materials’ output and logistics for the quarter were supported by ongoing operations at its Mountain Pass facility in California, where processing rates were maintained despite seasonal fluctuations in concentrate availability. For the latest quarter, the company indicated that rare-earth shipment tonnage remained comparable to the prior quarter, with shipments planned across multiple customer lots during January and February 2026. Management also reiterated that market pricing for key separated products did not materially change, citing stable contract pricing and limited spot volatility. The company did not provide a new full-year pricing outlook, but said current pricing trends are consistent with prior guidance and commercial terms.

headline: Quarterly earnings recap: REE basket earnings hold flat; management cites stable demand
body:
Shares and reported financial results across the rare-earth sector’s “REE basket” were largely steady in the latest quarterly reporting period, with several major producers and refiners showing flat performance as margins and volumes remained stable, industry summaries said.

The basket, which tracks earnings trends across companies active in mining, processing and separation of rare-earth materials used in magnets for electric vehicles and wind turbines, posted results that were broadly unchanged from the prior quarter. Revenue and operating income at the basket level were described as holding in a narrow range, reflecting similar customer offtake levels and steady pricing conditions for key separated products.

Management commentary across multiple firms pointed to demand stability as a primary factor behind the lack of movement. Companies said purchasing patterns from downstream manufacturers—particularly those tied to magnet supply chains—remained consistent, with order timing and inventory restocking occurring without major disruption. Several also indicated production schedules and recovery rates in processing operations were broadly in line with expectations, limiting volatility in output.

In addition, cost pressures appeared to have moderated compared with earlier periods. Firms cited steady energy and input costs, along with controlled capex and operating expenditure levels, which helped stabilize earnings even where pricing did not improve. Working capital swings were also described as limited, reducing quarter-to-quarter fluctuations in cash flow.

One company’s management highlighted that customer demand for separated rare-earths had stayed “stable across the quarter,” according to remarks provided with its earnings release. The statement added that the company continued to monitor downstream build plans, while maintaining production targets aligned with contracted and forecast orders.

Analysts and market trackers said the flat quarterly outcome suggests the sector is still digesting earlier supply and inventory adjustments, with limited near-term catalysts expected to shift aggregate earnings. While companies continued to discuss progress on expansion projects and cost reductions, near-term results remained driven by day-to-day operating performance and current demand conditions rather than step-change improvements.

The next quarter is expected to hinge on whether order visibility improves and on the direction of rare-earth pricing for separated materials, as well as whether any disruptions affect separation throughput. For now, the REE basket’s earnings trend remains essentially unchanged, reflecting stable market conditions cited by management teams.

headline: Rare-earth earnings call: MP Materials discusses capex timing and inventory build for NdPr
body: MP Materials’ March 5, 2026 earnings call focused on capital expenditure timing and a near-term inventory build for neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr), with management citing shifting downstream demand signals. The company said it is monitoring customer order patterns into the second half of 2026 while calibrating its Sanguiéque/Arizona processing throughput and selective mining schedules to preserve cash flow. Management indicated capex for 2026 is expected to be staged rather than front-loaded, targeting a smoother buildout across major equipment upgrades and sustaining projects. It also described an inventory build designed to support planned shipments during seasonal buying cycles, noting that NdPr supply readiness affects lead times for magnet makers. MP Materials reported higher work-in-process levels than in the prior quarter and said the build relates to procurement timing for separation reagents and maintenance windows. The company reiterated that any changes in NdPr demand, including potential destocking by alloy and magnet customers in mid-2026, could affect utilization rates and gross margin.

headline: Company guidance: Arafura outlines routine progress at Nolans with mixed product pricing
body:
Arafura Resources said it has made routine progress at its Nolans rare earths project in Australia, while reporting mixed pricing for key products as it updates guidance for the coming period.
