Additional detail on the recovered material volumes and the timing of sales was not included in the company’s initial filing.

Elemental Industries said the recovery outcomes and mix improvements were driven by operational initiatives aimed at reducing downtime and improving material handling across its processing steps. It also cited ongoing adjustments to feedstock sourcing and blending designed to stabilize input characteristics and improve output consistency.

In a statement accompanying the results, Chief Executive Officer Mara Linton said the quarter demonstrated “disciplined execution across our product slate and continued progress in cerium recovery from our processing streams.” She added that the company remained focused on sustaining throughput and maintaining quality targets as it moves into the next quarter.

The company did not provide guidance figures in the release. Analysts and investors will be watching for further updates on sales volumes, margins and the pace at which recovered cerium is converted into revenue. Elemental Industries is scheduled to host a call with investors to discuss the quarter’s financials, operational milestones and any changes to expected near-term supply and demand conditions.

headline: Earnings conference call recap: supply outlook for Dy and Tb remains steady
body: A recap of the latest earnings conference call for a rare-earth-focused supplier indicated that near-term demand and pricing trends for dysprosium (Dy) and terbium (Tb) remain steady, according to internal commentary provided during the March 12, 2026 session. Management said its forward supply outlook for Dy and Tb is unchanged versus prior guidance, with contracted deliveries expected to track seasonal patterns seen in the first half of 2025. The company reported that order intake for magnet-grade materials held at levels consistent with Q4 2025, and that lead times for processed outputs continue to compress from roughly 10–12 weeks late last year toward a range closer to 8–10 weeks in early 2026. On costs, it highlighted stable conversion and refining expenses, citing lower spot volatility in input oxides relative to the fourth quarter. For the remainder of 2026, the firm maintained that Dy and Tb availability will be sufficient to meet forecast demand, while pointing to continued monitoring of downstream electric vehicle and wind turbine magnet orders through June 30, 2026.

headline: Analyst notes after earnings: terbium and dysprosium affordability supports demand view
body:
Rare-earth prices and supply dynamics were in focus after a fresh round of earnings reporting led an analyst to reiterate a demand-supportive view for terbium and dysprosium, citing improved affordability as the key factor, according to market commentary reviewed by investors this week.

The analyst said the continued decline or stabilization in some rare-earth inputs has lowered the relative cost pressure for buyers of high-performance magnets, where terbium and dysprosium are used to enhance heat resistance and performance. That affordability, in turn, was seen as supporting purchasing activity from customers that track alloy and magnet supply chain costs closely, the note said.

The update followed earnings disclosures from companies tied to magnet materials and related processing, which prompted renewed attention to near-term procurement plans by manufacturers of electric motors, generators and other applications reliant on rare-earth permanent magnets. The analyst’s note suggested that while end-market demand remains uneven across sectors, the price-to-performance balance for terbium and dysprosium is currently more favourable than in periods when costs rose more quickly than end-product pricing.

In the note, the analyst pointed to the link between rare-earth procurement and magnet production schedules, indicating that lower input costs can enable customers to sustain inventory levels rather than delay orders. That behavior can, over time, translate into steadier offtake for suppliers and processors, especially where contracts are tied to prevailing indices or where buyers seek to reduce volatility risk.

The analyst also cautioned that near-term sentiment remains sensitive to broader market conditions, including industrial output data and the timing of downstream project orders. Any renewed volatility in rare-earth prices, the note said, could affect ordering patterns even if affordability remains an argument for demand.

For market participants, the update lands as traders and industrial buyers continue to monitor both pricing and policy signals affecting rare-earth supply chains. With terbium and dysprosium regarded as higher-cost, higher-sensitivity components in magnet manufacturing, analysts said sustained affordability could be an important determinant of whether demand holds through the next quarter rather than shifting to a more cautious stance.

headline: Conference recap: Rare Earths conference in Singapore reviews ETF demand trends
body: Rare Earths demand and investment positioning were the focus of a 2-day conference in Singapore on 10–11 March 2026, with speakers reviewing how ETF flows are tracking price expectations across key element markets. Participants said exchange-traded products continue to be the fastest channel for retail and wealth-managed allocations, with reported AUM growth accelerating after late January 2026 when liquidity improved in several China-linked benchmarks. Conference attendees reported that physical premiums for dysprosium and terbium remain the main driver of near-term spot spreads, while cerium and lanthanum are trading closer to supply-driven index levels. Panelists reviewed ETF demand trends tied to monthly rebalances, noting higher net inflows in February and early March despite uneven day-to-day volumes. Market strategists also flagged currency and hedging costs, estimating that SGD and USD basis swings have added 30–70 bps to ETF all-in exposure for investors using currency-hedged share classes. The next industry review session is scheduled for 22–23 June 2026, with attendees expecting updated data on recycling yields and long-lead magnet supply.

headline: Conference recap: Perth panel discusses routine inventory flows and warehouse restocking
body:
A panel discussion in Perth on Friday focused on how routine inventory movements and warehouse restocking are being managed across the supply chain, with speakers highlighting practical approaches to keeping stock levels aligned with demand.

The session, held as part of a wider industry conference programme, brought together logistics executives, procurement managers and operations specialists to discuss day-to-day stock movements, replenishment planning and the controls used to avoid gaps in warehouse availability. Organisers said the talks concentrated on operational processes rather than major disruptions, reflecting ongoing emphasis on efficiency and reliability.

Speakers described how inventory is tracked through a combination of warehouse management systems, regular cycle counts and scheduled stock transfers between regional storage sites. They said many companies are using clearer reorder points and tighter lead-time assumptions to reduce the risk of overstocking while still ensuring materials are on hand when orders are confirmed.

On warehouse restocking, panellists said replenishment workflows are increasingly tied to receiving schedules, picking constraints and storage capacity. One presenter noted that restocking decisions often depend on the timing of inbound shipments, the classification of materials by turnover rate, and the need to maintain audit trails for stock movements. “The goal is to make restocking predictable for operations teams and measurable for procurement,” the speaker said.

The discussion also covered how firms manage variability in incoming deliveries, including differences in shipment size, transport timing and documentation accuracy. Participants said operational teams use exception reporting to flag discrepancies between expected and actual arrivals, allowing adjustments to replenishment plans before shortages or excess inventory build up.

Representatives further addressed coordination between procurement and warehouse staff, including processes for confirming supplier release schedules and aligning internal cut-off times for replenishment orders. Several speakers said improved data sharing between departments has reduced delays in receiving and has helped warehouse teams plan labour and equipment for put-away and storage.

In the closing segment, the panellists outlined common performance measures, including inventory turnover, fill rates, stock accuracy and the time taken to complete replenishment from receipt to ready-for-pick status. Conference organisers said the session underscored the importance of routine controls in maintaining supply continuity even in stable market conditions.

headline: Conference recap: Brussels briefing covers rare-earth processing capacity updates
body: A Brussels briefing on March 13 reviewed the latest updates on rare-earth processing capacity, focusing on separation, refining and magnet-feed output. Industry representatives cited incremental expansions through 2027 that would lift European downstream capabilities, but warned that feedstock availability remains the binding constraint across the supply chain. Attendees said new commissioning schedules for solvent extraction and cracking lines in Central Europe are progressing, with targeted utilization rates of 60% to 75% in 2026 and higher in 2027 following ramp-up. Officials also mapped policy timelines for permitting and grid connections tied to rare-earth magnet production, noting that some projects face delays of 6 to 12 months due to environmental review requirements. Several firms presented capacity data by oxide and metal form, including mixed rare-earth concentrates, neodymium/praseodymium streams and dysprosium-heavy fractions. The briefing concluded that near-term risks center on financing and offtake contracts for separated products rather than on announced headline capacity, while monitoring of China-linked processing concentration continued to influence investment decisions.

headline: Webinar recap: investors review lanthanum and yttrium pricing drivers for Q3
body:
Investors participating in a webinar on rare-earth market conditions reviewed what they said are the main pricing drivers for lanthanum and yttrium heading into the third quarter.

The session, organised for investors tracking non-magnetic rare earths used in applications such as catalysts, polishing powders and optical materials, focused on supply expectations, demand signals and the pace of pricing adjustments in spot and contract markets. Speakers said recent price moves have been influenced by both restocking cycles among downstream buyers and ongoing efforts by producers to align output with near-term offtake.

On lanthanum, discussion centered on the availability of feedstock streams and the stability of downstream demand from refinery and chemical-sector customers. Participants also cited the timing of inventory drawdowns by processors and the degree of substitution with other feed components in catalysts and glass manufacturing. Several attendees pointed to how export logistics and shipping lead times can delay physical availability, tightening prompt supply even when longer-term production is unchanged.

For yttrium, the webinar highlighted how thinner supply volumes and more concentrated processing capacity can amplify price sensitivity to incremental changes in buying schedules. Speakers described demand as tied to segments including phosphors for lighting and screens, ceramic applications and specialty ceramics, while also noting that project schedules can affect consumption patterns from quarter to quarter. They said the market’s response to customer inquiries has been shaped by lead times for separation and refining, which can create temporary gaps between contracted volumes and available material.

Market participants reviewed recent transactions and considered how contract renegotiations might proceed into Q3. They said pricing benchmarks are likely to reflect a combination of rebalancing between supplier and buyer positions, continued assessment of inventory levels at separation plants and the downstream tier. Speakers also referenced broader rare-earth sentiment and the knock-on effect from adjacent elements, though they said lanthanum and yttrium can still trade differently due to application-specific demand and supply constraints.

“The expectation is that the next quarter’s pricing will be driven more by prompt availability and processing capacity than by generalized macro assumptions,” one panellist said during the webinar.

headline: CEO profile: MP Materials CFO highlights steady capex cadence and inventory targets
body: MP Materials’ chief financial officer emphasized a steady capital expenditure cadence and inventory-focused execution as the company positions for 2026 output targets for NdPr products. In an updated operational profile shared this week, the CFO pointed to a planned capex rhythm designed to support ongoing mine and processing work through mid-2026, with spending tracked against stage-based milestones rather than episodic boosts. Inventory targets were described as a key lever for margin stability, balancing finished goods build with feedstock availability for the separation and refining chain. The company said its planning framework aims to keep inventory levels aligned with quarterly demand variability, while limiting excess accumulation ahead of scheduled production ramps. For the remainder of 2026, management indicated capex will remain consistent relative to prior run-rate, with periodic reviews tied to equipment uptime and recovery rates at the Mountain Pass site. The approach comes as investors continue to monitor rare-earth price sensitivity and supply chain constraints that can affect conversion timing across the NdPr value chain.

headline: CEO interview: Lynas CEO discusses routine commissioning progress and guidance discipline
body:
Lynas Rare Earths’ chief executive said the company is making steady progress on commissioning activities at its operations, while emphasizing discipline around guidance as it manages the timing and ramp-up of key processing lines.

In an interview, Lynas CEO Amanda Lacaze said commissioning work at the rare earths refinery is proceeding in a “routine” manner, with the company focusing on completing testing, performance verification and gradual capacity increases. She said the company is working through the normal sequence of checks required to move equipment from initial operation to sustained production levels, without citing any major disruptions.

Lacaze also described the current approach to guidance, saying Lynas seeks to align expectations with demonstrable operational milestones rather than forecasting production volumes based on assumptions that may not be confirmed during commissioning. She said the company’s guidance discipline is designed to reflect the realities of startup schedules, including dependencies on commissioning completion, process stability and supply chain readiness.

Asked about how progress is being measured, the CEO said Lynas tracks commissioning against specific technical performance targets, including steady-state outputs, compliance with process specifications and the ability to operate within required operating parameters. She said adjustments are expected during early stages of ramp-up and that the company’s planning incorporates a cautious view of timelines until performance is validated.

The company’s commentary comes as the rare earths producer continues to navigate a market that is sensitive to supply availability and project schedules. Lynas has previously highlighted the importance of bringing capacity online in line with customer demand and of maintaining consistent product quality, particularly for buyers requiring stable sourcing of critical materials.

Lacaze declined to provide new commissioning dates in the interview, but reiterated that Lynas is aiming to convert commissioning progress into measurable production and to update its outlook only when results are confirmed. She said the company will continue to communicate through its regular reporting cycle while commissioning proceeds and operational data becomes available.

headline: Podcast episode: Rare-earth price wrap and inventory checklist for investors
body: Investors listening to a new podcast episode centered on rare-earth markets received a price wrap and an inventory checklist calibrated for the 2026 outlook. The discussion, released on 2026-03-13, surveyed pricing moves across key oxides and magnets, with emphasis on sustained volatility tied to end-demand signals and shipment timing. The episode highlighted that investors should track producer spot offers, monthly contract premiums, and the spread between mixed rare-earth concentrates and separated products, noting that week-to-week swings can exceed 5% during constrained logistics periods. It also laid out a checklist for inventory risk, including conversion capacity for separation, tonnage available at Chinese refineries versus export-eligible stock, and compliance-driven delays at ports. Analysts pointed to lead times that can stretch to 6 to 10 weeks for separation campaigns, while substitution risk was framed around dysprosium and terbium availability for high-grade magnet supply chains. For position sizing, the episode advised stress-testing exposure to both price drawdowns and inventory build-to-order backlogs through quarter-end 2026.

headline: Podcast: weekly rare-earth market diary—NdPr, Dy, and Tb pricing signals
body:
A weekly rare-earth market podcast released by industry publisher Argus examined pricing signals for key permanent magnet elements, focusing on neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr), dysprosium (Dy) and terbium (Tb).

The episode, part of Argus’ “Rare Earths Market Diary,” follows activity in the global supply chain for rare-earth oxides and downstream magnet materials, where contract negotiations and physical availability continue to shape short-term price movements. The discussion highlighted how buyers and sellers are weighing inventory levels against ongoing demand from the electric vehicle, wind power and industrial motor sectors.

In the NdPr segment, the podcast pointed to market participants closely monitoring tightness in separated material supply and the pace of new offtake agreements. It said price indications for NdPr have been influenced by the balance between restocking needs among magnet producers and the timing of shipments into China and other end-user regions. The episode noted that while demand signals have been mixed across product lines, sentiment remains sensitive to lead times for refining and separation capacity.

For Dy and Tb, the podcast said the market has continued to respond to both strategic procurement and the underlying scarcity of heavier rare earths used to improve magnet performance in high-temperature applications. It described Dy and Tb as typically subject to wider spreads between bid and offer levels, reflecting differences in spec requirements, purity, and volumes that can be secured on short notice.

Argus also referenced broader themes affecting all three elements, including the availability of feedstock and the pace of restarts or expansions among processing facilities. The episode said participants are assessing whether recent procurement cycles will extend into the next quarter and how any changes to export licensing, freight costs and contract terms may feed through to spot and negotiated levels.

“Weekly assessments are particularly useful right now because the market is reacting to both physical availability and contracting behavior rather than just steady end-demand,” an Argus analyst said in the podcast.

The episode urged listeners to track price indications for separated NdPr, Dy and Tb, along with updates on supply logistics, as those factors are expected to remain key drivers of weekly movements.

headline: ETF flows: rare-earth-linked funds add modest exposure in latest weekly data
body: Funds tracking rare-earth exposure recorded modest inflows in the latest weekly ETF flow release, according to data compiled through the week ending March 8, 2026. Investors added incremental capital to a small set of strategies tied to rare-earth mining and processing, with net subscriptions concentrated in the smallest share classes and limited to a handful of issuers. Across the category, total net inflows were measured in single-digit millions of dollars, after several weeks of near-flat or marginal outflows. Trading volumes remained low versus broader commodity ETF averages, suggesting the activity reflected hedging rather than broad reallocation. Fund flows also showed exposure skew toward basket-style products holding both rare-earth producers and downstream materials firms, reducing single-name concentration. By regional split, most purchases originated from U.S. brokerage accounts, while European participation was smaller and more volatile week to week. Analysts said the modest addition comes as investors respond to persistent supply-chain constraints and relatively steady spot-price signals in key rare-earth oxides.

headline: ETF flows: materials basket shows mixed demand for NdPr and dysprosium exposures
body:
A basket of exchange-traded funds focused on industrial materials showed mixed demand for neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr) and dysprosium exposures in recent trading, according to market data.

Flows into the materials complex remained uneven as investors shifted exposure across rare-earth-linked funds, with NdPr receiving more consistent inflows than dysprosium. The pattern suggested a preference for segments of the rare-earth supply chain tied to broader demand for permanent magnets used in electric vehicles, wind power and industrial motors, traders said.

Among the NdPr-focused vehicles, inflows were reported in the latest period, extending a streak of net buying that analysts associate with continued interest in electrification themes. Dysprosium-linked exposure, by contrast, drew smaller net purchases and in some cases showed near-flat movement, reflecting investors’ narrower view of dysprosium’s more limited role in magnet formulations.

The data also indicated that flows varied by fund structure and the underlying commodity or futures benchmarks used to achieve exposure. Some ETFs tracking rare-earth basket indexes rely on derivatives or swap arrangements, while others follow holdings of linked instruments, which can affect how quickly net creations or redemptions translate into exposure changes.

“NdPr demand appears to have remained steadier versus dysprosium, reflecting how investors calibrate risk around magnet materials,” one commodities strategist said, noting that dysprosium’s smaller market depth can make it less straightforward to express through liquid ETF wrappers.

Despite the divergence within the basket, overall materials sentiment remained supported by continued investment in broader industrial input exposure. Commodity-linked ETFs were still being monitored closely for signs of demand from institutional allocators, with managers and market participants watching for changes that could influence rare-earth pricing and availability expectations.

Separately, analysts said fund flow data should be interpreted alongside broader macro signals that can move metals markets, including interest-rate expectations and industrial production indicators. They added that rare-earth ETFs can also experience short-term fluctuations tied to rebalancing cycles, which may partially explain the mixed demand between NdPr and dysprosium exposures.

headline: ETF flows recap: Yttrium-focused themes see small inflows amid stable spot quotes
body: Global ETF flows for the week ending March 13, 2026 showed limited demand for theme baskets tied to yttrium exposure, even as physical spot prices were steady across major trading hubs. Data compiled for the period from March 7 through March 13 indicated small net inflows into yttrium-focused ETPs, with total purchases not exceeding mid-single-digit percentage points of assets under management. Spot quotes for yttrium intermediates held near prior levels, supporting flat performance in underlying proxies and limiting investor urgency for new allocations. Flows into broader rare-earth and metals baskets were mixed, with some funds reporting outflows while others absorbed net additions, leaving thematic ETPs with comparatively modest balances. Calendar-month year-to-date figures remained positive for the segment, but growth slowed after early-month inflow streaks ended. Market participants attributed the restrained positioning to tight spreads in liquid lots and cautious risk appetite ahead of mid-quarter rebalancing windows.

headline: Capex call: Lynas management outlines routine spending for separation and purification
body:
Lynas Rare Earths said its management has outlined routine capital expenditure plans focused on separation and purification work at its processing sites, in a capex update provided to investors.

In the company’s latest communication, Lynas set out anticipated spending levels associated with maintaining operations and supporting ongoing production through its separation and purification stages. The spending relates to equipment, sustaining works and minor upgrades designed to keep key parts of the processing flow functioning as designed, rather than to new, large-scale capacity expansion.

The update described separation and purification as a continuing investment priority for the business given the operating profile of its downstream processing activities. Lynas’ messaging indicated that the planned spend would be directed toward reliability, maintenance and process-related improvements intended to reduce downtime and support consistent product output.

Lynas also referenced the timing and cadence of capex, indicating that expenditure would be managed in line with operational schedules and procurement cycles. Management said the planned work includes activities that support plant availability, such as component replacements and routine maintenance tied to operational throughput, as well as capitalised costs for process and plant infrastructure.

The company’s communication was presented as part of regular investor engagement and did not include any new guidance on major construction milestones or the start of a larger project. Instead, it emphasized that the capex programme is intended to reflect normal operating requirements for separation and purification operations.

A Lynas spokesperson said the capex plans are “focused on sustaining and optimising our separation and purification performance” to support reliable production and continued operations at its sites.

Lynas has continued to report that its processing activities are central to its strategy for producing rare earth materials used in magnets and other industrial applications. The separation and purification segment is typically where plants require ongoing maintenance and targeted upgrades to manage feed variability and preserve product specifications.

The capex call follows the company’s broader updates on operational performance and cost management, with investors closely monitoring spending levels given the material and energy-intensive nature of rare earth refining and the impact of component availability on scheduled maintenance.

headline: Capex call recap: MP Materials details procurement timing for rare-earth production lines
body: MP Materials outlined procurement timing for new rare-earth production lines in a capex call held on 2026-03-13, detailing a phased plan for equipment delivery and installation. The company said orders for long-lead items tied to downstream separation and refining capacity are scheduled to be placed in the second half of 2026, with deliveries expected to begin in the first half of 2027. Engineering work supporting line integration is underway through mid-2026, while site readiness activities for utilities, material handling, and commissioning are targeted to complete ahead of equipment arrival. MP Materials added that vendor lead times and qualification testing will govern the pace of commissioning, with first operational ramp steps anticipated later in 2027. Management indicated procurement priorities focus on performance-critical systems required for throughput stability and product specifications, including reagent preparation and solvent handling modules. The update comes as the company continues to advance capacity additions intended to expand rare-earth output and reduce reliance on external processing.

headline: Quarterly earnings: Arafura updates capex timeline; rare-earth price assumptions unchanged
body:
Arafura Rare Earths said it had updated the timing for capital expenditure on its flagship Dubbo separation and processing project, while keeping its assumptions for rare-earth prices unchanged in its latest quarterly update.

The company, which is developing a processing plant in Australia to convert rare-earth concentrate into separated products, said its revised capex timeline reflects ongoing engineering and procurement work, as well as adjustments to activity sequencing across key project work packages. Arafura did not provide a detailed month-by-month breakdown of the new schedule but said the update is intended to align planned spending with the latest progress indicators.

In the quarter, Arafura reported that it continued to progress work toward key project milestones, including engineering activities and procurement planning. It also said it remained focused on maintaining the overall project development plan while working through site and infrastructure requirements associated with the Dubbo project.

The company’s pricing outlook for the products it expects to sell—based on market forecasts for separated rare-earth compounds—remained unchanged, it said. Arafura said it continues to use the same rare-earth price assumptions that it had previously applied for financial modeling, providing continuity in the way the company assesses potential project economics.

Arafura also said it had made further progress toward securing the regulatory and commercial frameworks required for the project, including work related to offtake arrangements and government approvals. The company’s update covers both operational developments and project finance considerations, with an emphasis on how the revised capex schedule affects near-term expenditure expectations.

“The capex timeline update reflects the latest execution plan for Dubbo as engineering and procurement progress, while maintaining our view on pricing assumptions,” Chief Executive Officer Matthew Drummond said in the statement.

Arafura said it will continue to provide updates on the project’s development and funding requirements in future quarterly reports. The company’s rare-earth separation development has been a focal point for investors tracking the industry’s efforts to expand non-Chinese supply of critical minerals used in electronics, wind power and other applications.

headline: Guidance note: company reaffirmed cerium and lanthanum volumes amid stable offtake
body: A company guidance note issued on 13 March 2026 reaffirmed planned volumes for cerium and lanthanum, citing stable demand and unchanged offtake conditions across the quarter. The company said cerium volumes would remain in line with prior guidance for the first half, with lanthanum expected to track the same run-rate despite ongoing normalization in end-market inventory levels. Management linked the outlook to steady customer buying patterns and to contracts that continue to cover a base level of purchases through June 2026. It did not indicate any near-term changes to pricing mechanisms tied to rare-earth basket indices, but confirmed that the volume mix between cerium and lanthanum would follow the existing production schedule. The note said deliveries are spread across multiple shipments to reduce concentration risk, with routine quality checks supporting acceptance timelines. For the remainder of 2026, the company maintained its framework guidance, adding that any deviation would be driven primarily by logistics and rare-earth feedstock availability rather than demand.

headline: Weekly wrap: rare-earth prices hold steady with no supply shocks reported
body:
Rare-earth prices held steady across key products this week, with no reported supply disruptions offsetting normal demand patterns, according to market sources and industry tracking.

Traders said tightness in certain materials remained limited to specific grades and end uses, rather than spreading across the broader sector. Prices for commonly traded rare-earth oxides used in magnets and electronics were broadly unchanged in spot and near-term contract discussions, they said, as buyers continued to rely on existing inventories.

Markets for neodymium and praseodymium products were stable, with participants pointing to steady offtake from magnet makers and compressor and motor supply chains. While some sellers indicated they were maintaining availability discipline, they did not cite shortages or logistical constraints. The absence of fresh disruptions helped keep offers and bargaining ranges within the prior week’s band.

Dysprosium and terbium, which are often monitored due to their heavier use in high-performance magnet applications, were also reported to be steady. One supplier said trading remained “order-led” and that customers were not rushing to secure additional volume despite ongoing negotiations over term pricing, reflecting a cautious but stable view of near-term requirements.

Other separated rare earths showed similar lack of volatility, with suppliers indicating that production schedules and shipping timelines were functioning as planned. Several buyers said they continued to follow procurement plans tied to quarterly demand forecasts, rather than adjusting rapidly in response to any new risk premium.

In the week’s reporting, no supply shock events were flagged, including disruptions to mine output, processing capacity, export permissions, or critical shipping routes. Analysts said that while the market remains sensitive to policy signals and production curtailments, the current pricing environment is being supported by limited new information and incremental buying rather than a reactive shift in sentiment.

Overall, participants characterized the week as a consolidation period, with sellers and buyers aligning on established price levels while waiting for clearer indicators on demand and any potential policy or operational developments that could affect supply over the coming weeks.

headline: Inventory and pricing wrap: rare-earth stocks steady as NdPr and Dy settle
body: Rare-earth inventory and pricing activity stabilized this week as rare-earth buyers and sellers completed end-of-cycle stock adjustments, with neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr) and dysprosium (Dy) both settling into narrower bid-ask ranges. Market participants reported that NdPr supply coverage remained largely unchanged through the 2026-03-10 to 2026-03-13 window, after several lots were re-priced following warehouse reconciliation and third-party weight audits. Dy trading also steadied, with fewer short-dated offers appearing for immediate shipment, pushing成交 activity toward standard lead times. Pricing wrap assessments indicated that NdPr and Dy were most affected by settlement recalculations tied to product mix shifts and revised metal purity documentation, rather than fresh changes in mine output. Across contracts indexed to March 2026 delivery, spreads tightened by single-percentage points compared with the prior wrap period ending 2026-03-07, according to traders tracking spot conversions. Inventory holding patterns suggested moderate restocking rather than aggressive buying, leaving overall stocks steady heading into the next pricing cycle.

headline: Analyst update: market expects stable terbium liquidity; valuation multiples reviewed
body:
A market update from analysts on Friday said expectations for terbium liquidity remain stable, with pricing and availability factors largely unchanged from recent assessments. The note also indicated that valuation multiples used to assess rare-earth-related assets have been reviewed as investors updated their assumptions for supply, demand and inventory levels.

Analysts said physical liquidity in terbium—an element used in magnets for electric vehicles and wind power equipment, as well as in electronics and defense applications—has continued to track broadly steady conditions in the near term. They pointed to ongoing flows tied to industrial orders and restocking cycles, alongside a lack of major disruptions in refining and distribution.

The update attributed the stability to a balance between current purchaser demand and the ability of sellers to source material without significant delays. It also noted that while spot activity fluctuates with end-market buying patterns, broader liquidity indicators have not deteriorated materially.

On valuations, the analysts said they reviewed trading and financial multiples for companies and funds with exposure to rare-earth supply chains. The adjustments reflected updated views on cost inflation, contract pricing mechanisms and the timing of new capacity additions. In particular, they said the review incorporated revised expectations for how quickly inventories could move through the market and how that could affect realized margins for producers and traders.

Several analysts highlighted that multiple frameworks used to value the sector—such as comparisons to peers and discounted cash flow scenarios—continue to rely on assumptions about future rare-earth pricing durability. The latest note said those assumptions were under periodic reassessment as market participants monitor the pace of demand growth, especially in magnet-related supply chains.

“The core message from our checks is that terbium liquidity remains adequate for current buying needs, and we do not see a near-term supply shock,” one analyst said in the update, noting that attention has shifted to pricing sensitivity and the timing of downstream procurement. The analyst added that valuation work is focused on aligning multiples with current liquidity and inventory turnover rather than extrapolating from past spikes.

The analysts did not name specific companies in the update. They said investors are likely to continue tracking policy signals and industrial procurement trends, which can influence both availability and pricing for terbium and other heavy rare earths.

headline: Conference Q&A recap: industry group discusses routine logistics for separated oxides
body: An industry conference Q&A on 2026-03-14 addressed routine logistics for managing separated oxides used in industrial supply chains, with organizers outlining standard handling, warehousing, and transport procedures. Panelists focused on batch traceability across receipt, silo storage, and outbound shipment, citing documentation checkpoints at intake, before packaging, and at loading. Speakers described typical routing timelines of 2–5 days from inland depots to port terminals and additional 3–7 days for ocean or intermodal handoffs, depending on customs and vessel schedules. Several participants noted that oxide consignments are commonly consolidated to reduce dwell time, targeting less than 48 hours between container sealing and departure. They also reviewed contamination-control practices, including segregated storage by chemical grade and monitoring logs kept for regulatory and customer audits. Conference staff said the group expects no major procedural changes through 2026, but emphasized that weather disruptions, container availability, and port congestion remain the main operational risk factors impacting delivery SLAs.

headline: Quarterly earnings: yttrium and lanthanum segments show steady shipment rhythm
body:
Rare earth producer Yttrium and Lanthanum segments reported a steady shipment rhythm in its latest quarterly earnings update, according to company disclosures reviewed by market participants.

The firm said deliveries of yttrium-bearing products continued to follow a relatively consistent cadence through the quarter, with sales volumes tracking close to the levels seen in the preceding reporting period. Management attributed the stability to ongoing demand from end-use customers and maintained production planning across its downstream processing sites.

For lanthanum products, the company reported shipment volumes that were largely in line with the quarter’s earlier guidance, supported by continued orders for polishing and catalyst-related applications. Monthly shipment data released with the results showed no major spikes or gaps, indicating that supply flows and logistics remained broadly predictable.

While overall revenue was shaped by pricing dynamics across the rare earth basket, the company said the operational cadence in both segments helped limit quarter-on-quarter variability in shipment-related costs. Unit economics were also affected by changes in input costs and contracted freight rates, but the company reported that procurement and inventory management remained within expected parameters.

In its commentary on the results, management highlighted that the segments’ shipment patterns reflected steady customer procurement cycles rather than spot-driven buying. “We saw consistent pull from our key buyers in both yttrium and lanthanum during the quarter,” a company spokesperson said, adding that production schedules were maintained without significant disruption.

Market watchers said the results may be read as a signal of near-term demand continuity for separated rare earths used in industrial and specialty applications. They noted that yttrium demand is closely tied to areas such as phosphors, optics and electronic components, while lanthanum is commonly linked to glass, automotive catalysts and refining feeds.

The company’s next update, scheduled with its subsequent quarterly filings, will likely include more detail on order backlogs, inventory levels and pricing assumptions. Analysts said they will also monitor whether the steady shipment pattern persists as customer procurement calendars shift into the next quarter.

headline: Rare-earth weekly wrap: neodymium slips, praseodymium steady as bids firm
body: Rare-earth prices ended the week mixed as demand for separated Nd and Pr held up, while neodymium softened on intermittent spot liquidity. Neodymium offers for 99.0% Nd oxide basis declined over the period, with latest indications down from the prior week’s level as buyers reduced spot coverage during the 8–12 March window. Praseodymium, by contrast, was steady as bid interest firmed, particularly for Nd/Pr separated products tied to magnet supply chains. Dealers cited firmer auction-style buying around 10 March, when restocking activity in Europe and North America supported Praseodymium pricing, limiting downside. Dysprosium and terbium were less active and saw narrower spreads, suggesting traders were waiting for clearer volumes rather than repricing immediately. Overall, weekly sentiment remained cautious ahead of March mid-month procurement, with market participants expecting bids to stay supportive for Pr if Nd demand stabilizes in the next 2 weeks.

headline: Rare-earth weekly wrap: terbium flat, dysprosium unchanged amid orderly trading
body:
Rare-earth prices moved mostly sideways over the past week, with terbium remaining flat and dysprosium unchanged as trading stayed orderly, according to market participants tracking the complex.

Terbium prices held steady across the assessed deals, supported by consistent demand from makers of high-performance magnets and other specialty applications. Several traders said replenishment buying continued at a routine pace, without any signs of sudden shifts in end-user requirements. One dealer described market conditions as “stable and predictable,” with buyers and sellers largely meeting on previously agreed ranges.

Dysprosium also saw little change, with quoted levels holding at the same level as the prior week. Participants attributed the lack of movement to balanced supply and demand, alongside limited urgency from buyers. While some spot transactions were reported, most participants said procurement activity remained focused on maintaining inventory rather than chasing new positions.

Other heavy rare-earths showed more mixed signals, with some contracts reported at levels slightly different from the previous assessment period. Traders said the broader market continued to weigh pricing guidance from recent upstream activity and the availability of material in different product forms, including oxides and metal. Several market participants noted that differences in purity specifications and delivery terms continued to influence deal-by-deal pricing.

Light rare earths were also generally steady, though market sources indicated that liquidity varied by segment. Some participants said firms with longer supply arrangements were less active in spot markets, while others used the week’s stability to complete planned purchases. The overall tone in trading desks was that there were no major disruptions in logistics or procurement, allowing quotes to remain anchored.

Despite the relatively quiet price action, participants continued to monitor signs that could alter near-term sentiment, including changes in export availability, production schedules, and demand from electronics and clean-energy supply chains. Any shift in these factors could affect the next pricing cycle, traders said, but for now the week ended with terbium flat and dysprosium unchanged amid orderly activity.

headline: Rare-earth weekly wrap: cerium rangebound, yttrium steady on continued offtake
body: Rare-earth markets were largely rangebound in the week ending March 13, 2026, with cerium holding steady as buyers kept purchases cautious amid thin liquidity. Cerium moved in a narrow band around the mid-market range for the period, showing limited price expansion across spot lots and short-term term supply. Yttrium was steady on continued offtake, supported by steady demand from applications tied to specialty manufacturing and high-grade optical and phosphor uses. Traders cited improved scheduling of deliveries through late March, which reduced near-term procurement pressure and helped stabilize pricing. Praseodymium and neodymium remained mixed, with fluctuations tempered by slower restocking cycles in Europe and Asia. Despite the steadier tone in yttrium, broader sentiment stayed subdued as market participants awaited clearer signals on industrial demand and upstream supply restarts.

headline: Rare-earth weekly wrap: lanthanum modestly higher as domestic offers firm up
body:
Lanthanum prices moved modestly higher this week as buyers in China increased demand for domestic supply, according to market participants tracking weekly rare-earth quotes.

The rare-earth market saw limited changes across several other separated products, with sentiment tied closely to availability and the pace of new contracting. Traders said recent domestic offers firmed after periods of tighter supply, helping stabilize prices for some mid-range items even as many buyers remained cautious about committing to larger volumes.

Lanthanum, which is widely used in alloys, glass and catalysts, rose in the latest assessment as sellers were more willing to hold firm on asking levels. “Domestic offers have been more supportive, so buyers have had to accept slightly higher prices to secure material,” one trader said, citing a pickup in enquiries from downstream manufacturers.

Praseodymium-neodymium separated oxides also reflected a generally steady tone, with price movement constrained by a balance between working stock levels and the willingness of suppliers to maintain their terms. Some sellers reportedly continued to route offers through contracts rather than spot tenders, which limited visible day-to-day fluctuations but kept spreads relatively narrow.

Other light rare earths were mixed. Cerium and related products largely held near prior levels, with participants pointing to continued demand from polishing compounds and industrial catalysts. However, they said the pace of procurement varied by end-use segment, particularly where customers were adjusting intake schedules to align with factory run rates.

Market activity was shaped by broader supply-and-demand dynamics, including the timing of shipments and the level of inventories held by both traders and end users. Participants said that while there was no clear sign of a major shift in overall demand, the improved firmness of domestic offers contributed to the slight upward tone in lanthanum. Additional weekly assessments are expected to clarify whether this support extends beyond light rare earths or remains confined to specific grades and contract structures.

headline: Inventory update: mixed rare-earth stocks edge up at magnet supply chain nodes
body: Rare-earth inventory across magnet supply chain nodes in Asia and Europe showed small gains on 14 March 2026, according to market checks, with mixed availability for key NdPr and DyTb grades. At sampling points tied to magnet makers, neodymium-praseodymium separated material was reported steady-to-firmer on day-over-day basis, while dysprosium and terbium balances remained tight, limiting spot flexibility for high-performance magnet batches. Warehouse data indicated average fill rates in the mid-single digits for NdPr conversion lots, versus low single digits for Dy/Tb add-ons, with lead times extending from 6–8 weeks to 8–10 weeks at two logistics hubs. On pricing mechanics, buyers focused on replenishment lots linked to end-March production schedules, supporting marginal upward pressure despite stable demand signals for April. In contrast, some downstream processors deferred non-critical receipts after 10 March due to tighter outbound capacity and higher demurrage exposure at ports. Overall, the update suggests improved but still uneven stock visibility, with the tightest bottlenecks concentrated in dysprosium/terbium supply rather than NdPr.

headline: Inventory update: dysprosium look-through supply improves; lead times stable
body:
A fresh inventory look-through update for dysprosium indicates improved supplies at downstream and intermediate stages, while overall lead times remained stable, according to industry participants tracking rare-earth materials.

The update, compiled from reports submitted by traders, processors and buyers involved in rare-earth sourcing, suggested that dysprosium availability has strengthened along the supply chain compared with the prior reporting period. “Improvement is visible in inventories held by intermediaries and during processing, rather than a sudden change at mine level,” one market source said, requesting anonymity because the information was not for public release.

Lead times for dysprosium-related products were described as broadly steady across most contract windows in the latest round of checks. Several buyers said they have not seen the kind of extensions that typically follow supply disruptions, with ordering and delivery schedules continuing to align with recent norms for shipments. Dealers also indicated that pricing volatility has eased in tandem with the improved visibility into stock positions, although they did not provide specific price figures.

The “look-through” concept used in the update seeks to capture inventories that may not be reflected in spot market availability alone, including stocks held by refiners, component manufacturers and traders that are intended to support longer-term demand. In the latest reporting, participants pointed to more consistent holdings for dysprosium, a key element used in high-performance permanent magnets found in wind turbines, electric vehicle motors and industrial motors.

While the overall supply picture improved, the update noted that dysprosium remains a relatively concentrated material with limited alternative sources compared with more widely traded metals. Several participants said buyers are continuing to manage risk through staggered procurement and by monitoring processing capacity, reflecting that changes in refining throughput can still influence availability.

The inventory update did not indicate any new regulatory developments or logistics disruptions that would materially affect dysprosium supplies in the near term. Instead, it portrayed a gradual strengthening in stock coverage at intermediary levels, paired with stable delivery timelines for contracted volumes.

headline: Inventory update: rare-earth separation inventories steady as buyers rotate grades
body: Rare-earth separation inventories held steady in early March 2026, according to industry tracking, as buyers rotated among dysprosium, terbium, neodymium and praseodymium grades to match current magnet and alloy orders. Multiple producers reported no meaningful drawdown during the 10-day period ending March 14, with separation stock levels described as flat versus late February after normalizing for customer reclassification of blended product. Shipments remained uneven: heavy rare-earth lots moved on shorter lead times, while NdPr streams were delayed by 2–4 weeks in several export routes tied to customs clearance backlogs. Prices were largely stable in spot indications, but traders said grade-specific spreads narrowed as downstream processors accepted alternate purities to maintain kiln schedules through March. One factor cited by market participants was steadier upstream oxide supply, limiting forced inventory liquidations. Demand signals for the March quarter were described as mixed, with electronics and battery materials drawing cautious orders while wind and EV magnet demand continued to shift volumes by specification rather than total tonnage.

headline: Inventory update: rare-earth traders report normal warehouse turns after seasonal lull
body:
Rare-earth traders said inventory “warehouse turns” have returned to normal levels after a seasonal lull, according to informal checks across supply chains used for trading and distribution.

Several market participants reported that turnover rates—how quickly stocked rare-earth materials are cycled through storage and into orders—have stabilized following the slowdown typically seen around seasonal demand fluctuations. Traders said replenishment schedules have resumed and that near-term order flow is tracking more closely with recent baseline activity rather than the reduced volumes seen in the period just prior to the current quarter.

The update comes as buyers and processors assess availability across key separated and alloying materials used in magnets, polishing powders and other industrial applications. While some counterparties have continued to emphasize longer lead times for certain grades, the broader comment from traders was that stock is moving at a steadier pace in domestic and regional distribution hubs.

One trader familiar with logistics for separated rare-earths said the turnaround reflects “more consistent order release from end customers and fewer delays in shipping paperwork,” adding that warehouse schedules have been aligned again with normal billing cycles. The trader also noted that replenishment deliveries were not described as urgent, suggesting that inventories remain manageable rather than constrained.

Market watchers said warehouse turns can provide an early signal of shifting demand, particularly for products where inventories are held by distributors or secondary processors instead of end manufacturers. They noted that turnover rates are influenced not only by consumption but also by trading behavior, including how frequently firms rotate inventory to meet contract requirements and quality specifications.

Separate indicators of physical demand have varied across rare-earths sub-segments. Some traders have pointed to ongoing volatility in pricing and contract structures, including spot versus term sales, while others have focused on the ramp of magnet-related procurement ahead of major industrial cycles. Still, the consensus among the traders consulted for this update was that the distribution layer is no longer operating at the reduced pace that characterized the seasonal dip.

Traders said they are monitoring whether the current normalization persists through subsequent weeks, particularly as distributors adjust to incoming orders tied to customer production plans. For now, the latest checks suggest that inventories are being worked through at a rate closer to typical market conditions, with fewer disruptions reported in the movement from warehouse to customer shipments.

headline: Analyst note: Benchmark sees stable demand outlook supporting terbium pricing
body: An industry benchmark issued in March 2026 said stable demand signals should support terbium pricing over the near term, according to an analyst note circulated on 15 March. The view comes as buyers in applications tied to magnet production and electronics maintain steady procurement plans through mid-2026, offsetting recent volatility in spot activity. The benchmark cited limited new downstream ordering surges and a relatively balanced supply situation across Chinese and global channels, with inventory turnover expected to remain consistent during the second quarter. In pricing terms, the note pointed to subdued downside pressure, with market participants continuing to reference prior contract levels rather than repricing for weaker demand. It also highlighted that rare-earth procurement schedules typically tighten ahead of summer production windows, which can stabilize offtake patterns even when exchangeable availability improves. Overall, the note forecast demand staying within a narrow range through 30 June 2026, supporting terbium prices against broader risk factors impacting other heavy rare earths.

headline: Analyst note: Jefferies flags balanced neodymium fundamentals in weekly checks
body:
Jefferies said weekly checks indicate neodymium fundamentals remain balanced, with supply and demand signals holding steady for the rare earth metal used in permanent magnets for electric motors and wind power.

In a note to clients, the bank pointed to stable conditions across key inputs into the neodymium supply chain, including mine production, downstream processing and magnet demand expectations. Jefferies said the current read-through suggests no immediate pressure toward a sustained surplus or shortage, despite ongoing disruptions that have periodically affected rare earth logistics and pricing.

The firm highlighted that pricing and physical market indicators have not shown a decisive trend that would warrant a near-term shift in its assessment. It said demand remains linked to industrial production and the pace of clean-energy and electrification investment, while supply continues to reflect both the ramp-up of separated rare earth production and the pace of downstream conversions into magnet-ready material.

Jefferies also referenced the broader rare earth complex, noting that investor attention to neodymium typically tracks movement in mixed rare earth baskets and the relative economics of separating various lanthanides. While neodymium is often discussed in the context of magnet supply, the bank said weekly checks point to manageable feedstock availability and steady operating rates among relevant processing nodes.

“Weekly indicators continue to point to balanced neodymium fundamentals, with neither a clear tightening nor a build-up emerging at this stage,” a Jefferies analyst wrote in the note.

The bank’s comments come as automakers, industrial equipment makers and renewable energy developers continue to plan for higher magnet intensity, particularly in traction motors, generators and related components. At the same time, policy efforts and inventory management can influence short-term buying patterns in the rare earths market.

Rare earth prices can move quickly when procurement calendars change or when supply disruptions occur at key stages such as separation, refining or magnet manufacturing. However, Jefferies said its latest checks do not indicate an immediate catalyst for a stronger swing in fundamentals, leaving expectations broadly unchanged near term.

headline: Analyst note: RBC reiterates constructive view on praseodymium amid firm orders
body: RBC on March 15 reiterated a constructive view on praseodymium, citing firmer end-market orders and improving liquidity across specialty rare earth supply chains. The bank pointed to steadier procurement from magnet and electronics customers during the first quarter, after uneven demand in the fourth quarter of 2025. RBC said order flow remained supported by inventory restocking ahead of the second half, with exchange-linked and contract prices holding up relative to prior expectations. It also noted that pricing volatility in praseodymium has moderated, as buyers shift from spot purchases to longer-dated supply arrangements. RBC’s note indicated that while downstream utilization varies by sector, near-term absorption looks resilient, helped by continued investment in motors, energy equipment, and advanced manufacturing inputs. The analyst maintained its stance despite ongoing constraints in upstream processing capacity and permitting timelines that continue to cap incremental supply.

headline: Analyst note: Citi expects cerium supply to remain routine with steady conversion rates
body:
Citi expects cerium supply to remain largely routine through the near term, supported by steady conversion rates across upstream processing, according to an analyst note seen by market participants.

The bank said it does not foresee major disruption to cerium availability, noting that conversion capacity and operating performance for cerium-containing feedstocks are continuing at levels consistent with recent supply patterns. Cerium, a key component in catalysts, polishing powders and glass applications, is typically produced alongside other rare earth elements, which can affect output depending on plant utilization and product mix.

In its assessment, Citi focused on the pathway from mined and separated rare earth materials to finished or separated cerium products. The firm said that conversion rates—used to translate feedstock inputs into cerium outputs—are a central factor for near-term balances, and that current performance indicates supply should stay in line with demand expectations.

Citi also highlighted that the broader rare earth sector has been managing variability through contracted feedstock flows and incremental production adjustments, which can smooth near-term shortages even when upstream conditions are uneven. The bank indicated that while spot prices for individual rare earths can move on sentiment and short-term logistics, the underlying physical supply for cerium appears tied more closely to processing throughput than to abrupt changes in mine supply.

The note comes as traders watch for signals from both China, where a significant share of separation and processing capacity is concentrated, and other regions that supply downstream components. Analysts say cerium’s pricing and availability can be influenced by how operators prioritize different rare earth streams and how quickly separated products are converted into more saleable grades.

Citi’s view suggests that participants should expect continued normalcy in cerium supply unless conversion performance changes materially or regulatory and trade-related developments alter processing flows. The bank did not call for immediate supply-tightening scenarios, instead pointing to steady conversion as the main reason it expects cerium availability to remain stable.

Market participants have also been monitoring inventories and the timing of procurement cycles, particularly for industrial customers that require consistent volumes for catalyst manufacturing and materials processing. Citi said the current indicators do not point to sudden procurement stress for cerium, assuming conversion rates continue as projected.

headline: Company guidance: Lynas targets Q3 rare-earth volumes consistent with prior guidance
body: Lynas Rare Earths said it expects third-quarter 2026 volumes for rare-earth products to be consistent with its prior guidance, maintaining the company’s outlook after a mixed start to the year. Management reiterated that Q3 shipments will align with earlier volume expectations, while also keeping focus on steady supply from its processing platform in Malaysia and ore-to-separation output from existing operations. The update comes as spot market pricing for neodymium-praseodymium and dysprosium fluctuated through February and early March, with buyer restocking partially offset by periodic inventory corrections. Lynas did not provide revised quarterly production ranges, instead pointing to continued execution against existing capacity plans and ongoing reliability work at separation facilities. The company’s guidance review is expected to be reflected in its next quarterly reporting cycle following the end of Q3, with further volume detail likely alongside financial disclosures. Investors are watching near-term working-capital impacts tied to sales timing and customer offtake schedules across North Asia and Europe through 2026.

headline: Company guidance: MP Materials updates March-quarter output outlook for neodymium
body:
MP Materials updated its outlook for neodymium production for the March quarter, citing operational and market conditions as it reported changes to guidance for the critical rare-earth metal used in magnets.

In a market update on Monday, the miner said it now expects March-quarter output of neodymium to reflect updated operating assumptions at its Las Vegas-based processing facilities and the company’s downstream separation and refining workflow. The guidance update follows prior expectations issued earlier in the fiscal year and comes as battery and electric-vehicle supply chains continue to track rare-earth availability and pricing.

The company also provided information on how it expects production to align with demand from customers for NdPr oxide and related products, which are used in permanent magnet applications. MP Materials’ guidance includes revised timing for processing and shipments, and it highlighted that rare-earth production can be influenced by feedstock availability, inventory levels and the ability to run at planned throughput during the quarter.

Management said it would continue to monitor performance at the Mountain Pass mine and at processing operations, while adjusting plans as needed to sustain production targets. The company’s update referenced typical quarter-to-quarter variability, including the ramp-up and maintenance schedules that can affect separation rates and the conversion of intermediate materials into finished rare-earth products.

A representative for MP Materials said in the company’s statement that the updated March-quarter guidance “reflects our latest view of operational throughput and supply chain timing” as it balances mine feed, processing capacity and customer delivery requirements.

MP Materials is the largest producer of rare earths in the United States and one of the major suppliers of neodymium and praseodymium outside of China. Its Mountain Pass operation produces rare-earth-bearing concentrates, which are then processed into separated products used by magnet manufacturers. The company has in recent years expanded separation capacity and sought to increase the reliability of deliveries to customers seeking non-China sources of rare earth materials.

The guidance update will be closely watched by magnet and component makers as they plan inventories for the second quarter. Rare-earth pricing and contract terms can shift with changes in export controls, supply from other producers and demand from electronics and clean-energy sectors.

headline: Company guidance: Shenghe guidance on separated rare earths points to steady ramp
body: Shenghe, a key supplier of processed rare earth products, signaled steady progress in its separated rare earths segment, according to guidance released on 2026-03-15. The company indicated that volumes should ramp at a measured pace through the March to June quarter, supported by ongoing utilization of separation capacity and continued offtake arrangements. Management pointed to stable ordering patterns for NdPr and other separated feedstocks, with pricing assumed to remain range-bound versus the 2025 second-half environment. The guidance also suggests that reductions in inventory overhang are continuing gradually, rather than through rapid liquidation, reflecting normal lead times for downstream magnets and alloys. For investors, the update frames throughput growth as incremental, with no clear acceleration signals despite a tighter supply backdrop for certain heavy rare earths. Shenghe did not provide new contract totals in the guidance package, but referenced supply continuity measures that are expected to keep shipments on schedule across Q2.

headline: Company guidance: Arafura outlines capex timeline tied to continued rare-earth throughput
body:
Arafura Rare Earths said it expects its capital expenditure for the restart and scale-up of rare-earth production to follow a defined timeline, contingent on maintaining targeted rare-earth concentrate throughput at its projects.

In an update to investors on Monday, the company linked its capex schedule to continued processing volumes through its planned supply chain. Arafura said the sequencing of spending is designed to align major equipment and commissioning outlays with steady-state production from its processing facilities, rather than front-loading costs before throughput is sustained.

The company did not provide a single end-to-end capex number in the update, but described spending as occurring in phases, with incremental investment tied to performance milestones. It said that if throughput levels are met and sustained, it expects to progress through the next stages of development and related sustaining work on schedule.

Arafura’s guidance also indicated that throughput continuity is a key driver for sustaining costs and working capital requirements associated with ore sourcing, processing, and product logistics. The company said it is monitoring operational parameters that influence recovery rates and plant availability, which in turn affect the volume of rare-earth output that can be produced for offtake and commercial use.

“Maintaining rare-earth throughput is central to how we plan and sequence our capital expenditure,” the company said in its investor update. “We intend to invest in line with production performance, so capex supports manufacturing rather than relying on a single step change in output.”

The update comes as global demand for rare-earth materials used in magnets for electric vehicles, wind turbines and other clean-energy applications continues to be closely watched by suppliers and buyers. For producers, the ability to sustain throughput through ramp-up and commissioning periods often determines the pace of development and the timing of revenue from contracted product.

Arafura has previously outlined steps to move projects through construction and commissioning toward long-term production. Monday’s guidance reiterated that the company will continue to link additional capex to operational readiness and the achievement of ongoing throughput targets.

The company said it would continue to provide further guidance as performance data is compiled and as commissioning proceeds, with updates intended to reflect throughput trends and any changes in project sequencing.

headline: Conference recap: rare-earth sourcing sessions in Tokyo close with policy Q&A
body: Tokyo hosted a 3-day rare-earth sourcing conference ending on 2026-03-16, with industry representatives holding closed-door sessions on procurement of neodymium-praseodymium, dysprosium and terbium for magnets and advanced industrial uses. Delegates reviewed 2025–2026 supply timelines from key producers in China, Australia and Central Asia, focusing on how shipment terms and product specification standards affect landed pricing. Organizers said attendee questions centered on contract structures for separated oxides versus magnet-grade feedstocks, and on volume commitments sized for 1H and 2H 2026 demand cycles. A policy Q&A late on 2026-03-15 addressed export-control compliance, customs classifications, and licensing timelines that can add 2–6 weeks to deliveries when documentation is incomplete. Participants also discussed refining capacity utilization and inventory drawdowns that have tightened availability since mid-2025, with sourcing strategies increasingly split between spot purchases and multi-quarter supply agreements. The final session wrapped with an action list for follow-up data exchanges on lead times, impurity tolerances, and end-use traceability requirements for public and private buyers.

headline: Conference recap: Perth forum panel reviews 2026 demand for NdPr and TbDy
body:
A panel at a Perth conference reviewed expected demand through 2026 for neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr) and terbium-dysprosium (TbDy), two critical rare-earth materials used in permanent magnets.

The forum brought together suppliers, producers and end-users to examine how demand forecasts are shaping investment and procurement strategies across the supply chain. Panelists focused on the balance between accelerating demand from electric vehicles, wind power and industrial electrification, and the constraints imposed by upstream processing capacity and refining lead times.

Speakers said demand for NdPr, which is widely used in high-performance magnets, is projected to remain the primary driver of rare-earth consumption in the coming years. They attributed the outlook to continued adoption of magnet-based traction motors and generator systems, while also noting that magnet demand can fluctuate with technology mix and vehicle production schedules.

For TbDy, the panel said expectations were more conditional because terbium and dysprosium are typically used in smaller amounts to improve magnet performance in high-temperature and high-stress applications. Panelists highlighted that demand is closely tied to the geographic distribution of manufacturing and the design choices of magnet producers, particularly for applications that require stronger coercivity.

Several contributors said that corporate procurement plans increasingly include diversification across processing routes and supplier countries, alongside longer-term offtake arrangements. They also noted that recycling and product substitution can influence future demand, though they said timelines for meaningful material substitution remain uncertain.

“One takeaway is that 2026 demand will be shaped as much by processing and magnet manufacturing constraints as by end-market growth,” a representative from a participating company said during the discussion. Panelists agreed that refining capacity and magnet supply are key determinants of how quickly downstream manufacturers can translate theoretical demand into actual purchases.

The panel concluded by discussing how policy signals and industrial targets in major importing regions may affect consumption patterns. Organisers said the conference sessions also covered market fundamentals, including price dynamics for separated oxides and the role of inventory cycles, as participants assessed planning horizons heading into 2026.

headline: Conference recap: Frankfurt materials conference highlights rare-earth recycling progress
body: Representatives from 28 countries convened in Frankfurt on March 14-15 for a materials conference focused on rare-earth supply risk, with organizers citing new recycling yields and faster separation steps aimed at reducing dependence on primary mining. Technical sessions highlighted progress in recovering neodymium and dysprosium from end-of-life magnets, including pilot lines that reported grade improvements from mixed scrap streams over 6-12 month operating trials. Several papers described hydrometallurgical circuits that cut reagent consumption by 20-35% versus earlier flowsheets, alongside leaching times reduced from days to hours through optimized acidity control. Attendees also reviewed pilot demonstrations of solvent extraction and ion-exchange steps that achieved multi-cycle solvent stability at target recovery rates, while maintaining impurity rejection for iron, cobalt, and nickel. Regulators and industry groups discussed extended producer responsibility frameworks expected to be drafted through 2026, with draft collection targets set for 2027. Conference participants said scaling from lab to commercial processing will hinge on qualifying feedstock quality and securing capacity for magnet-heavy waste currently handled by fragmented operators.

headline: Podcast: Rare-earth market pulse reviews weekly price drivers and inventory trends
body:
A new weekly podcast focused on rare-earths market conditions is reviewing price drivers and inventory trends, highlighting developments across key supply chains and end-user demand.

The episode, published by Argus Media as part of its rare-earth coverage, runs through the main factors affecting prices week by week, including the pace of purchases by magnet and polishing material producers and changes in downstream consumption. It also examines how logistics timing, contract renewals and spot-market availability are influencing trade flows for separated rare-earth products.

In its market update segment, the podcast outlines how inventories held by distributors and processors have shifted, with attention to whether stock levels are tightening or building. Analysts discuss how lead times and warehousing costs can affect near-term purchasing decisions, and how sellers respond when inventory levels move relative to seasonal demand patterns.

The briefing also reviews reported production and processing signals, including changes in output from major refining and separation hubs and any disruptions that can alter the balance between supply and contracting demand. It discusses how feedstock availability, processing yields and buyer negotiations can translate into revised expectations for both domestic and export shipments.

Alongside pricing factors, the podcast looks at trade sentiment and risk considerations, such as regulatory and shipping constraints that can affect the timing of deliveries. It covers how these elements feed into weekly price assessments and whether buyers are shifting between longer-term commitments and spot procurement.

“The rare-earth market remains highly sensitive to inventory visibility and lead times, and this week’s review shows how those variables are shaping the price outlook,” one of the speakers said in the episode.

The podcast’s weekly structure is designed to consolidate information from reported transactions, market intelligence and ongoing price-monitoring for a range of rare-earth products.
